title: JWT verifier accepts future-issued tokens (verify_iat disabled in production)
evidence: _decode() passes options={"verify_iat": False, ...} with the comment "iat may be in the future in tests" (jwt_utils.py:112). This is the only decode path for BOTH access tokens (auth_authorizer.py:50 -> verify_access_token) and refresh tokens (auth_handler.py:405 -> verify_refresh_token), so it runs in production, not just tests. The code requires exp/iat/sub/typ/session_id present and manually rejects expired tokens (jwt_utils.py:124-125), but never validates that iat is not in the future. Signature is still verified (no verify_signature override) and algorithms is pinned to ["HS256"] (jwt_utils.py:108), so this is not a forgery hole — an attacker cannot mint a token without the SSM signing secret.
risk: A token whose iat is set far in the future (only mintable by a holder of the signing secret, or by a clock-skew bug in the issuer) is accepted as valid. With min_acu and a 7-day refresh TTL the practical impact is negligible, but the test-justification comment leaking into prod behavior means iat sanity is silently unenforced; if future logic ever keys off iat (e.g. 'reject tokens issued before a credential-rotation timestamp') it would be bypassable.
recommendation: Drop verify_iat:False for the production decode path (let PyJWT validate iat, with a small leeway), or add an explicit `iat <= now + leeway` check in _decode. Keep the test relaxation behind a test-only flag rather than the shared decoder.

votes.reason: Verified every cited claim against src/collector/auth/jwt_utils.py and the two call sites. The code matches exactly: _decode (line 100-127) passes options={"require": [...], "verify_exp": False, "verify_iat": False} with the comment "iat may be in the future in tests" on line 112. It manually rejects expired tokens (lines 123-125: `if now_ts >= exp`) but never checks that iat is not in the future. algorithms=[_ALGO] with _ALGO="HS256" (line 108) is pinned, and there is no verify_signature override, so the finding correctly characterizes this as NOT a forgery hole. Both production paths confirmed: verify_access_token routes through _decode (used at auth_authorizer.py:50), and verify_refresh_token routes through _decode (used at auth_handler.py:405 and :514). The "test-justification comment leaking into prod" is real. Severity P3 is appropriate: the finding itself acknowledges the practical impact is negligible (a future-iat token is only mintable by a secret-holder or issuer clock-skew bug, and no current logic keys off iat), making this a latent hygiene/defense-in-depth issue rather than an exploitable vulnerability. No misread logic, no missed guard, all line numbers correct.

Technically accurate (verify_iat:False runs in the shared prod decode path) but no plausible trigger or impact for this MVP. (1) Not a forgery hole — the finding concedes the signature is verified and algorithms is pinned to HS256, so only a holder of the SSM signing secret can produce a future-iat token. (2) The issuer never mints future-dated tokens: issue_access_token (jwt_utils.py:48) and issue_refresh_token (line 67) both set iat=int(now.timestamp()) from the caller's now; a future iat would require a clock-skew bug in the issuer Lambda itself. (3) Confirmed via grep that iat is consumed by ZERO production logic — it appears only at issuance and in the require-presence list (line 110). There is no credential-rotation timestamp gate or 'reject tokens issued before X' check anywhere, so the finding's stated risk is explicitly conditional on code that does not exist ('if future logic ever keys off iat'). (4) Even a future-iat token still has exp enforced manually (lines 123-125), typ checked, required claims present, and signature verified — no privilege or expiry bypass. Lenient iat handling is the default many JWT libs take; harmless given self-issued tokens and 7-day refresh TTL. Purely theoretical with no realistic trigger.

Verified accurate at the code level. src/collector/auth/jwt_utils.py:_decode (lines 100-127) is the single shared decode path, called by verify_access_token (used in production by auth_authorizer.py:50) and verify_refresh_token (used by auth_handler.py:405 and :514). It passes options={"verify_iat": False, "verify_exp": False} and only manually rejects expired tokens (now_ts >= exp at lines 124-125) — there is no future-iat check. Signature IS verified and algorithms is pinned to ["HS256"] (line 108), so this is not a forgery hole, as the finding correctly states.

I confirmed via grep that the iat claim is never read for any security decision anywhere in src/collector/ — it appears only at issuance (lines 48, 67), in the "require" presence list (line 110), and the comment. So the future-iat acceptance has zero behavioral impact today.

No constructible exploit path exists from real code: the issuers always set iat=now from the handler clock; there is no path that mints a future-dated token. The finding itself concedes the only sources are (a) a holder of the SSM signing secret (a total compromise where future-iat rejection is moot — they can mint any exp anyway) or (b) a hypothetical issuer clock-skew bug. The stated risk ("if future logic ever keys off iat") is explicitly conditional on code that does not exist.

Not refuted because the code-level claim, location, and non-forgery framing are all correct and verifiable. P3 is appropriate: the finding is accurate, self-describes the impact as negligible, and is a defense-in-depth/hardening note rather than an exploitable vulnerability. It does not overstate severity.

title: Token-bearing auth responses lack Cache-Control: no-store
evidence: _handle_callback and _handle_refresh return 200 JSON bodies containing access_token and spotify_access_token (auth_handler.py:358-369, 495-503) with headers limited to Content-Type and x-correlation-id — no Cache-Control or Pragma. The SPA is served same-origin behind CloudFront (infra/auth.tf:62, infra/frontend.tf). No response sets `Cache-Control: no-store` or `private` on any token-bearing body.
risk: Defense-in-depth gap: token-bearing responses rely entirely on the CloudFront/API-Gateway default of not caching POST/authorized responses. A future CloudFront cache-policy or shared-proxy misconfiguration could cache a response containing a bearer/Spotify token. No evidence this is currently exploited (callback is GET but state-gated; refresh is POST).
recommendation: Add `Cache-Control: no-store` (and `Pragma: no-cache`) to the headers of every auth response that carries a token in its body (callback, refresh) — ideally in the shared _json_response helper for the auth Lambda.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code. _handle_callback (auth_handler.py:379-391) and _handle_refresh (auth_handler.py:486-504) both return 200 JSON bodies containing access_token and spotify_access_token (lines 358-369 and 495-503), with headers limited to Content-Type and x-correlation-id — no Cache-Control or Pragma. The shared _json_response helper (lines 247-257) also omits Cache-Control, so there is no central injection point. The only no-store in the repo (infra/frontend.tf:76) sits on an unrelated CloudFront spa_html_fallback function that emits a 302 redirect for HTML navigations; it does not touch the auth token bodies. The auth routes (/auth/callback, /auth/refresh) live in api_gw_pure_path_patterns and use the CachingDisabled cache policy (4135ea2d...), confirming the finding's premise that token responses rely on the CloudFront/API-GW default rather than an explicit no-store header. Line numbers, logic, and the same-origin-behind-CloudFront framing are all accurate; no missed guard. Severity is correctly P3: short-lived tokens, POST/state-gated responses, CloudFront caching explicitly disabled, no demonstrated exploit, S-effort defense-in-depth hardening. The finding does not overclaim (explicitly notes no current exploitation). Not refuted.

REFUTED. The finding's core premise is factually wrong and the residual concern is purely theoretical with no plausible trigger for this MVP.

1. Not a "default" — caching is explicitly disabled. The finding claims token responses "rely entirely on the CloudFront/API-Gateway default of not caching." In reality infra/frontend.tf attaches the AWS-managed CachingDisabled policy (cache_policy_id = "4135ea2d-6df8-44a3-9df3-4b5a84be39ad") to EVERY API Gateway cache behavior, and the auth routes /auth/callback, /auth/refresh, /auth/login, /auth/logout are all listed in api_gw_pure_path_patterns (frontend.tf:122-140, behavior at :189-201). CachingDisabled forces min/max/default TTL = 0 and does not cache responses regardless of origin headers. So the worst-case the finding wants to prevent (a token body cached at the CDN) is already structurally prevented by an explicit, named policy — not by a fragile default.

2. The "future CloudFront cache-policy or shared-proxy misconfiguration" is hypothetical. The risk text itself concedes "No evidence this is currently exploited." Triggering it requires a future operator to deliberately swap CachingDisabled for a caching policy on the auth routes — an obvious, separately-reviewable infra change, not an accidental gap.

3. No shared-proxy exposure in this architecture. The SPA is same-origin behind CloudFront, HTTPS-only (redirect-to-https, TLSv1.2_2021). There is no intermediary HTTP proxy on the path, so the shared-proxy caching scenario has no realistic trigger.

4. Impact for THIS MVP (small DJ circle, same-origin SPA, CachingDisabled already set) is nil. This is textbook defense-in-depth whose stated worst case is already covered by infra.

The code observation (no Cache-Control: no-store on token bodies) is technically true, but it does not matter for this MVP. Severity is appropriately the lowest tier; leaving as P3.

Finding is factually accurate and the literal claim is reproducible, but I confirm P3 is correct (lowest severity, pure defense-in-depth, non-exploitable today).

CONFIRMED FACTS (read real code):
- src/collector/auth_handler.py:379-391 (_handle_callback) returns 200 JSON with `access_token` + `spotify_access_token` (live Spotify bearer) and headers limited to `Content-Type` + `x-correlation-id`. No Cache-Control/Pragma.
- src/collector/auth_handler.py:486-504 (_handle_refresh) returns 200 JSON with `access_token` + `spotify_access_token`, same minimal headers.
- grep over src/ + infra/ finds zero `Cache-Control`/`no-store`/`Pragma` on any auth response (only hit is infra/frontend.tf:76, an unrelated SPA-fallback 302).
- Single CloudFront distribution (aws_cloudfront_distribution.frontend), same-origin SPA — confirmed.

REPRODUCIBLE PATH (literal missing-header claim): browser GET /auth/callback?code=...&state=... (state-gated by oauth_state cookie) -> CloudFront api_gw_pure_path_patterns behavior -> API GW v2 -> auth Lambda -> 200 JSON containing a real spotify_access_token with no Cache-Control: no-store. The header is genuinely absent and this path is 100% reproducible.

WHY THE RISK IS NOT REACHABLE TODAY (no concrete exploit path):
- Auth routes (/auth/callback, /auth/refresh, etc.) are all in api_gw_pure_path_patterns, whose CloudFront behavior explicitly pins cache_policy_id 4135ea2d-... = AWS managed CachingDisabled (Min/Default/MaxTTL = 0). CloudFront cannot cache these regardless of origin headers. The finding's claim that responses 'rely entirely on the default' is slightly imprecise — they rely on an EXPLICIT CachingDisabled policy.
- API Gateway is HTTP API v2 (infra/api_gateway.tf:3 protocol_type='HTTP'); HTTP APIs have no response-cache capability at all.
- Transport is HTTPS end-to-end (viewer redirect-to-https + origin https-only), so passive shared proxies cannot cache the body without TLS interception (out of threat model).
- /auth/refresh is POST and cached_methods=[GET,HEAD] anyway.

The finding itself concedes all of this ('No evidence this is currently exploited', risk framed as a 'future cache-policy or shared-proxy misconfiguration'). It is honest defense-in-depth hardening, not an exploitable defect. The header truly is missing and adding no-store to the shared _json_response is a valid, low-effort improvement, so I do not mark the finding as false — but its severity is correctly the lowest tier (P3) and it is non-exploitable in the deployed architecture.

title: Spotify token-endpoint error payload echoed verbatim into the auth HTTP error body
evidence: _post_token raises SpotifyOAuthError(f"spotify token endpoint returned HTTP {status}: {parsed}") and SpotifyTokenRevokedError(f"...invalid_grant: {parsed}") embedding the full parsed Spotify response (spotify_oauth.py:151,154). In _handle_callback/_handle_refresh this is wrapped as OAuthExchangeFailedError(str(exc)) (auth_handler.py:292,437), and _error_response copies exc.message straight into the JSON response body (auth_handler.py:236-240). Note this does NOT leak to logs — the AppError log path emits only error_code/status_code/error_type, never message (auth_handler.py:107-116) — and Spotify token-endpoint error bodies normally contain only {error,error_description}, not token material.
risk: Information disclosure of upstream (Spotify) error internals to the API caller. Low: token-endpoint error responses do not contain access/refresh tokens, and the client is the user's own browser. Still surfaces raw third-party payloads through CLOUDER's error envelope, which is unnecessary coupling.
recommendation: Have OAuthExchangeFailedError carry a generic client-facing message and keep the detailed {parsed} only in a server-side log field (which is already redaction-gated), rather than passing str(exc) into the response body.

votes.reason: Verified the full chain in the cited code. spotify_oauth.py:147-155 raises SpotifyOAuthError/SpotifyTokenRevokedError with f-strings embedding the full `{parsed}` Spotify response. Both are subclasses of plain Exception (spotify_oauth.py:19,23), so str(exc) returns exactly that message. In _handle_callback (auth_handler.py:292) and _handle_refresh (auth_handler.py:437), the SpotifyOAuthError is re-wrapped as OAuthExchangeFailedError(str(exc)). OAuthExchangeFailedError (errors.py:150-154) passes that string verbatim as AppError.message, and _error_response (auth_handler.py:236-240) copies exc.message straight into the JSON body. So the raw Spotify error payload is echoed to the API caller — exactly as claimed. The finding's nuances are also accurate: the AppError log branch (auth_handler.py:108-115) emits only error_code/status_code/error_type and NOT error_message, so it does not leak to logs (error_message is only logged in the separate generic `except Exception` branch, which never catches this AppError subclass). The embedding occurs only on non-200 responses; successful 200 returns parsed without raising, so access/refresh tokens are never embedded. P3 is correct: genuine but low-impact info-disclosure/coupling — no token leakage, no log leakage, consumer is the user's own browser. Not refuted; severity unchanged.

Finding is technically accurate (verified spotify_oauth.py:147-157 embeds the parsed Spotify response into SpotifyOAuthError/SpotifyTokenRevokedError; auth_handler.py:292,437 wrap it as OAuthExchangeFailedError(str(exc)); _error_response at 236-244 copies exc.message into the JSON body). But it does not matter for THIS MVP. The disclosed content is Spotify's own OAuth error envelope ({error, error_description} — public, RFC-6749-documented codes like invalid_grant/invalid_client), not secrets. The finding itself confirms: (1) no leak to logs (line 108-116 emits only error_code/status_code/error_type), (2) no access/refresh token material is ever in a token-endpoint error body, and (3) the recipient is the authenticating user's OWN browser — there is no cross-tenant path. An attacker can only ever see the OAuth error for their own failed login attempt, which is functionally identical to Spotify's native error UX. There is no plausible trigger that discloses another tenant's data, secrets, or tokens. The only concrete harm cited ("unnecessary coupling" / surfacing raw third-party payloads) is a code-hygiene nit, not a security exposure. For a small-DJ-circle SaaS this is purely theoretical with no realistic impact. Severity is correctly self-rated at the lowest actionable level (P3), so unchanged.

Confirmed and reproducible from real code. Concrete path: GET /auth/callback -> _handle_callback (auth_handler.py:270) with valid code/state/oauth_verifier -> oauth.exchange_code -> _post_token (spotify_oauth.py:80). When Spotify's token endpoint returns a non-200 whose error is NOT invalid_grant (e.g. {"error":"invalid_client","error_description":"Invalid client secret"} from a misconfigured/rotated secret), spotify_oauth.py:153-155 raises SpotifyOAuthError(f"spotify token endpoint returned HTTP {status}: {parsed}") embedding the full parsed dict. auth_handler.py:291-292 catches SpotifyOAuthError and re-raises OAuthExchangeFailedError(str(exc)); since AppError.__str__ is overridden, str(exc) here is the raw SpotifyOAuthError message, so OAuthExchangeFailedError.message carries the verbatim Spotify payload. lambda_handler (107-116) catches AppError, logs only error_code/status_code/error_type (no message — confirming the no-log-leak caveat is accurate), then _error_response (236-240) copies exc.message verbatim into the JSON body returned with HTTP 502. The same verbatim wrap exists on the refresh path (auth_handler.py:436-437) for non-invalid_grant SpotifyOAuthError; the invalid_grant case correctly routes to a generic SpotifyRevokedError instead. Severity is correctly P3: the leaked branch (status != 200) cannot contain access/refresh tokens (those only appear in 200 responses on the success branch), the disclosure is to the user's own browser only, and it is not logged. It is genuine but minor unnecessary coupling of third-party error internals into CLOUDER's error envelope.

title: bp_token logging safety depends entirely on the ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS allow-list; redaction layer is effectively dead and would not catch a token nested under a future allow-listed field
evidence: log_event sanitizes via _sanitize_fields (logging_utils.py:136-141) which is an allow-list: only keys in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS survive. No allow-listed field is sensitive, so the separate redact_sensitive_data pass (which only matches exact key names in SENSITIVE_KEYS={'bp_token','authorization','token','access_token'}) never actually fires today. The invariant 'bp_token never logged' currently holds ONLY because no log_event call passes a token-bearing field name. If a future field carrying a nested dict with a token were added to ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS, redaction would catch it only if the inner key were literally named one of the four SENSITIVE_KEYS (case-insensitive exact match) — e.g. a key named 'bearer' or 'secret' or 'credentials' would pass through unredacted.
risk: No current leak. Latent risk: the two-layer defense is really one layer (the allow-list). A maintainer adding a new allow-listed field that nests credential data, or renaming a sensitive key, could silently break the no-log invariant with no test catching it, since the redaction layer covers only four exact names.
recommendation: Keep the allow-list as the primary gate (it is sound). Add a unit test asserting that passing bp_token=... (and a nested {'authorization': ...}) to log_event produces output with no token substring, to lock the invariant. Optionally broaden SENSITIVE_KEYS to a substring/regex match (e.g. anything containing 'token','secret','password','authorization','bearer') so the redaction backstop is meaningful.

votes.reason: I read src/collector/logging_utils.py in full and verified every load-bearing claim. (1) _sanitize_fields (lines 136-141) is an allow-list: `if key in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS` — only allowed keys survive, confirmed. (2) None of the four SENSITIVE_KEYS ({'bp_token','authorization','token','access_token'}) appear in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (lines 14-100), so redact_sensitive_data never fires on a top-level sensitive key today — confirmed. (3) redact_sensitive_data (lines 120-133) matches only exact case-insensitive key names (line 124: `key.lower() in SENSITIVE_KEYS`); a nested key named 'bearer'/'secret'/'credentials' would pass through unredacted — confirmed. (4) The order is allow-list-then-redact: redaction only recurses into values of already-allowed fields (line 139-140), so it is effectively a backstop only for nested dicts under future allow-listed fields, exactly as the finding states. The finding correctly notes redaction still runs on nested values of allowed fields (not literally never-called), and frames it as a latent risk with no current leak. I also checked tests: tests/unit/test_logging_utils.py:4 tests redact_sensitive_data directly but does NOT test log_event(bp_token=...) end-to-end, so the recommended invariant-locking test is genuinely missing. The invariant 'bp_token never logged' indeed holds only because no call site passes a token-bearing allow-listed field. The finding's logic, line cites, and reasoning are all accurate. Severity P3 is correct: explicitly no current exploit, defense-in-depth/test-hardening observation, small effort.

Technically accurate but no current defect. The finding self-admits "No current leak" — the bp_token-never-logged invariant holds today via the allow-list. Verified: ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (logging_utils.py:14-100) contains no sensitive field, and redact_sensitive_data (line 120) only matches 4 exact key names, so the redaction pass is a dormant backstop — by design, not a bug. The allow-list is the sound primary gate. The hypothesized leak requires a future maintainer to BOTH add a credential-nesting field to the allow-list AND name its inner key outside SENSITIVE_KEYS — a speculative future-maintenance scenario with no plausible trigger in this MVP. Tests already partially lock the invariant: test_beatport_url_value_is_dropped_from_logs asserts a URL containing token=SECRET is dropped, and test_redact_sensitive_data_recursive covers the redaction layer. The recommendation (add a token-substring test, broaden SENSITIVE_KEYS) is reasonable defense-in-depth hardening, not a real risk for a small-DJ-circle MVP. Per the impact rubric, a purely latent issue with no current trigger is refuted.

All technical claims verified against real code in src/collector/logging_utils.py. (1) _sanitize_fields (lines 136-141) is an allow-list keyed by exact, case-sensitive `key in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS` (line 139); none of the ~90 allow-listed field names are sensitive. (2) redact_sensitive_data (lines 120-133) only redacts a key when key.lower() is in SENSITIVE_KEYS={bp_token,authorization,token,access_token} (line 124), recursing into mapping/list values and returning scalars unchanged (line 133). (3) The redaction pass runs AFTER the allow-list filters top-level keys, so it can only fire on a sensitive sub-key nested inside an allow-listed field's value, and no such case exists today (verified: candidate_labels/candidate_artists/error_message/reason/search carry no nested credential keys; grep for any log_event passing bp_token/token/access_token/authorization returns empty). The redaction layer is effectively dead today, and the no-log invariant holds solely because of the allow-list. (4) The case-sensitivity asymmetry is real (allow-list exact-match line 139 vs redact .lower() line 124); the four-name exact-match backstop would miss a future nested key named e.g. bearer/secret/credentials, and would also miss a token embedded as a substring of a free-form field like error_message (scalar, returned unchanged). The finding explicitly states 'No current leak' and frames this as a latent defense-in-depth / test-coverage risk, which matches reality. The reproducibility requirement concerns a live leak; the finding does not claim one, so its honest framing as a latent hardening gap is appropriate. P3 is correct for a latent, non-exploitable defense-in-depth observation with a sound primary gate still in place. Cannot refute.</parameter>
</invoke>

title: Ingest enqueue failure permanently strands a run at RAW_SAVED with no retry or reconciliation
evidence: `_run_beatport_ingest` writes the S3 snapshot, inserts the `ingest_runs` row at status RAW_SAVED (handler.py:431), then calls `_enqueue_canonicalization`. If the SQS `send_message` raises, the exception is swallowed and converted to `EnqueueResult(ENQUEUE_FAILED)` (handler.py:957-974); the API still returns HTTP 200 with `run_status=RAW_SAVED` (handler.py:463-471). Nothing re-enqueues. A grep for RAW_SAVED across src/ and infra/ shows the status is only ever set (handler.py:431) and read — there is no sweeper, cron, or reconciliation job that re-drives RAW_SAVED runs. RunStatus has no in-progress state (models.py:24-28), so a stranded run is indistinguishable from a worker that is mid-flight.
risk: A transient SQS outage (or the queue being disabled/misconfigured, handler.py:889-919) silently drops the week's data from canonicalization: the raw snapshot sits in S3, the run row says RAW_SAVED forever, but the tracks never enter the catalogue. The admin sees HTTP 200 success and a green-ish run, and the week is silently absent from the catalog with no alarm (DLQ alarms only cover messages that were actually enqueued).
recommendation: On enqueue failure, mark the run FAILED (so GET /runs and coverage surface it) instead of returning 200/RAW_SAVED, and/or add a periodic reconciliation that finds RAW_SAVED runs older than N minutes and re-enqueues them by run_id+s3_key (idempotent because canonicalization replays cleanly). At minimum add a CloudWatch metric/alarm on canonicalization_enqueue_failed.

votes.reason: Verified the finding against the cited code; it is accurate. handler.py:431 inserts the ingest_runs row at RunStatus.RAW_SAVED, then :438 calls _enqueue_canonicalization. On SQS send_message failure, the exception is caught at handler.py:957 and converted to EnqueueResult(FAILED_TO_QUEUE/ENQUEUE_FAILED) (handler.py:958-974); the run row is NEVER updated, and :463/:498 returns HTTP 200 with run_status=RAW_SAVED. RunStatus has only RAW_SAVED/COMPLETED/FAILED (models.py:24-28) — no in-progress state, so a stranded run is indistinguishable from a worker mid-flight. grep across src/, infra/, alembic/, scripts/ confirms RAW_SAVED is only ever set (handler.py:431) and read — no sweeper, cron, EventBridge, or reconciliation re-drives RAW_SAVED runs. The worker (worker_handler.py:112-116) is the only thing that exits RAW_SAVED, and it runs only if a message actually reached the queue. Alarm coverage gap confirmed: the Lambda Errors alarm (alarms.tf:29-49) needs a Lambda Errors metric, but the swallowed exception yields HTTP 200 with zero errors; the DLQ alarm (logging.tf:69-90) counts only ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible for messages that were enqueued — a never-enqueued message never appears; and there are no log metric filters at all on canonicalization_enqueue_failed. So a transient SQS outage or a disabled/misconfigured queue silently strands the week's data with no automated alarm. One nuance the finding understates: coverage_for_year (repositories.py:1070-1116) selects the latest run per cell including its status, so the coverage matrix would display 'RAW_SAVED' for the stranded cell — a manual detection path exists if an admin inspects status. That tempers but does not refute the finding; no automated detection or recovery exists. Lowering to P2: real DATA/silent-failure risk, but it requires a transient SQS failure (rare; SQS is highly available) or a deliberate config-disabled state, blast radius is one style/week per event, and the coverage matrix offers a manual visibility hook.

Verified all technical claims in the code. handler.py:957-974 swallows the SQS send_message exception into EnqueueResult(ENQUEUE_FAILED) and handler.py:448-498 still returns HTTP 200 with run_status=RAW_SAVED. grep confirms RAW_SAVED is only ever written/read (handler.py:431,463; models.py:25) with no sweeper, cron, EventBridge, or reconciliation anywhere in src/ or infra/. RunStatus has only RAW_SAVED/COMPLETED/FAILED (no in-progress state), and useRunPoller.ts treats RAW_SAVED as non-terminal, so a stranded run is genuinely indistinguishable from a mid-flight one — the progress toast spins forever with no timeout-to-error. No CloudWatch metric filter/alarm exists on the canonicalization_enqueue_failed ERROR log line (grep of alarms.tf/logging.tf). The infra lambda_errors alarm (alarms.tf:29) fires on the Lambda Errors metric, but because the exception is caught and a 200 returned, the collector emits no error metric, so it does NOT fire; DLQ alarms only cover already-enqueued messages. The frontend IngestForm.tsx surfaces failure only on mutation.isError (non-2xx), so the 200 + processing_status=FAILED_TO_QUEUE response is treated as success and onStarted fires — the admin sees success.

This is not an accepted-behavior carve-out (not cold-start/refresh-replay/min_acu/YT). The trigger is plausible, not theoretical: besides a transient SQS outage, the same FAILED_TO_QUEUE 200/RAW_SAVED path is taken when canonicalization_enabled=false or queue_url is empty/misconfigured (handler.py:889-919), so a deploy-time misconfig would silently drop every week's ingest with a green-looking response and zero alarm. For a curation product whose core value is the catalogue, a silently-absent week with no recovery and no alarm is real DATA loss. The only mitigation (an attentive admin noticing an indefinitely-spinning toast) is exactly the human-in-the-loop gap the finding flags. P1 is appropriate.

Finding confirmed and reproducible from real code. Concrete path: admin POST /collect -> _handle_collect (handler.py:501-518) -> _run_beatport_ingest (handler.py:438) writes the S3 snapshot (handler.py:414) and inserts the ingest_runs row at status=RAW_SAVED (handler.py:418-436), then calls _enqueue_canonicalization (handler.py:438). With canonicalization_enabled=True and a valid queue_url, it reaches client.send_message (handler.py:933). PRECONDITION: a transient SQS failure (regional outage, throttling, expired IAM creds, or a queue_url env var pointing at a deleted/misconfigured queue) makes send_message raise. The except at handler.py:957 swallows it into EnqueueResult(ENQUEUE_FAILED) and the response is still built with run_status=RAW_SAVED (handler.py:463) and returned as HTTP 200 (handler.py:498).

Verified the structural claims:
1. Status transitions OUT of RAW_SAVED occur ONLY in the canonicalization worker (worker_handler.py:112 set_run_completed, :147 set_run_failed), which is invoked solely by the SQS message. No message delivered => worker never runs => status stays RAW_SAVED permanently. grep confirms set_run_completed/set_run_failed have no other callers.
2. No sweeper/cron/reconciliation exists. The only Terraform event source mappings are SQS->Lambda (infra/lambda.tf); there are zero aws_cloudwatch_event_rule / aws_scheduler_schedule resources. grep shows RAW_SAVED is only set (handler.py:431) and read (coverage / openapi schema).
3. RunStatus has no in-progress state (models.py:24-28: RAW_SAVED/COMPLETED/FAILED), so a stranded run is indistinguishable from a worker mid-flight.
4. No alarm fires. infra/alarms.tf only has Lambda Errors (AWS/Lambda) alarms, but the collector swallows the exception and returns 200, so it reports no Lambda error. There is NO metric filter on the canonicalization_enqueue_failed log event (handler.py:965 is a structured log only, no put_metric). The DLQ (infra/sqs.tf) and its coverage only apply to messages that actually reached the queue.
5. Coverage does NOT surface this as a gap: coverage_for_year (repositories.py:1070) returns r.status raw and treats any non-null run row as a present cell, so a RAW_SAVED run shows as a populated/green-ish cell, masking the absent catalog data — exactly as the risk states.

Severity P1 is appropriate: silent data-integrity loss (a full week of catalog tracks dropped) with no alarm and no automatic recovery, requiring manual detection plus manual re-drive. It is gated on a transient infra fault/misconfig rather than a normal-input bug, and the raw snapshot persists in S3 (recoverable via run_id+s3_key), which is consistent with P1 rather than P0.

title: Worker performs no completeness check between the S3 snapshot and the recorded item_count
evidence: The API records `item_count` (the number of releases fetched) on the `ingest_runs` row (handler.py:404,432) and in `meta`. The worker reads the snapshot via `storage.read_releases(s3_key)` which silently filters to dict items (`[item for item in parsed if isinstance(item, dict)]`, storage.py:107) and never compares `len(raw_tracks)` against the run's `item_count` (worker_handler.py:83-89). On success it overwrites `processed_count` with `result.tracks_processed` and marks COMPLETED unconditionally (worker_handler.py:112-116). There is no assertion that the snapshot it canonicalized is the snapshot that was originally fetched, nor that it is non-empty/non-truncated.
risk: A truncated or partially-overwritten snapshot (see the same-week overwrite finding) is canonicalized as if complete and marked COMPLETED. Because canonicalization only ADDs via ON CONFLICT/identity-map, missing tracks are silently never inserted — gradual, invisible catalog gaps. A 0-item snapshot also marks COMPLETED with processed_count=0, indistinguishable from a genuinely empty week.
recommendation: In the worker, compare `len(raw_tracks)` against the run's persisted `item_count` (fetch the run row first) and fail (transient) on mismatch, or at least emit a warning metric. Reject empty snapshots when item_count>0.

votes.reason:
All cited code claims verified accurate. handler.py:404,432 records item_count (==len(releases)==len(raw_tracks) since fetch returns track-level items written atomically as one gzip blob); worker_handler.py:83 reads via storage.read_releases which silently filters non-dicts (storage.py:107) and never compares len(raw_tracks) against the run's item_count; worker_handler.py:112-116 marks COMPLETED unconditionally with processed_count=result.tracks_processed, including a 0-item snapshot. The risk mechanism is real: canonicalize.py _resolve_track (604-656) is ADD-only (find_identity miss -> create_track; no DELETE/reconciliation), so a dropped track becomes an invisible catalog gap. A get_run method exposing item_count exists (repositories.py:259) but is unused, so the fix is cheap. Not refuted — the code behaves exactly as described and the recommended len(raw_tracks)==item_count check is apples-to-apples (would not false-positive in the normal path; the finding correctly targets the read count, not tracks_processed which can legitimately be lower).

Severity overstated, though. This is a missing defense-in-depth guard, not an independently-firing bug. On the happy path item_count always equals len(raw_tracks) (same atomic S3 PutObject written and read; S3 PutObject is atomic with no multipart/partial write here), so a single run's snapshot is never truncated in isolation. The silent-loss path only fires given a SEPARATE triggering defect — the same-week overwrite race the finding itself references (run A's message processed after run B overwrote the deterministic style/year/week S3 key). The empty-snapshot-marked-COMPLETED case is real but low-impact since a genuinely empty week is also valid. A contingent, non-self-firing DATA hardening gap is P2, not P1.

Refuted as a P1 data risk. The finding's three risk scenarios have no plausible trigger in this MVP. (1) "Truncated snapshot": write_run_artifacts uses a single atomic put_object (storage.py:265, no multipart) of a fully-gzipped body; S3 is read-after-write consistent, so a reader never sees a truncated object. (2) "Partially-overwritten snapshot": overwrites are also full-object atomic put_object calls — never partial. The referenced same-week overwrite only occurs on deliberate admin re-ingest (manual POST /admin/beatport/ingest with admin-supplied bp_token; there is no EventBridge/cron schedule). Each re-ingest writes its OWN complete snapshot to the deterministic style_id/year/week key AND enqueues its OWN worker message, and canonicalization is additive (ON CONFLICT identity-map), so across the two worker runs the union of tracks is inserted — no snapshot goes unprocessed, no silent loss. (3) "0-item snapshot marked COMPLETED": item_count = len(releases) at handler.py:386 and the identical releases list is written to S3 at handler.py:414 with zero filtering between them, so item_count==0 always corresponds to a genuinely empty fetch — which the finding itself concedes is indistinguishable from a real empty week (because it IS one). The proposed check (len(raw_tracks)==item_count) would always pass for empty weeks and detect nothing, and for the one case where the values CAN diverge (a same-week overwrite by a different complete run) it would FALSE-POSITIVE: run A's worker compares run A's item_count against run B's snapshot and fails transiently, cycling a perfectly-processable message toward the DLQ. So the recommended fix is net-harmful at exactly the trigger it targets. This is a theoretical robustness/observability gap, not a P1 data-integrity bug; at most a P3 nice-to-have invariant.

The structural claim is verified against real code. The API records item_count = len(releases) on the ingest_runs row (handler.py:386,432) and in meta (404). The worker reads the snapshot via storage.read_releases(s3_key), which silently filters non-dict items (storage.py:107), and never fetches the run row or compares len(raw_tracks) to item_count (worker_handler.py:81-116). On success it overwrites processed_count with result.tracks_processed and marks COMPLETED unconditionally via set_run_completed (repositories.py:206-225). Canonicalization is additive-only (identity-map + upsert, no DELETE of stale rows; canonicalize.py), so any track missing from the snapshot is silently never inserted, and there is no reconciliation anywhere comparing item_count to processed_count (confirmed by grep across src/collector).

Reproducible path (the only real divergence vector): the S3 key is deterministic per (style_id, year, week) (storage.py:245-248), but each collection mints a fresh run_id (handler.py:360) and enqueues a message carrying (run_id, s3_key) (CanonicalizationMessage, schemas.py:43-48). (1) Collect week W -> run A writes key K with N items, records item_count=N, enqueues {run_A, K}. (2) Before run A's message is processed, re-collect week W -> run B overwrites K with M<N items (upstream gap), records item_count=M, enqueues {run_B, K}. (3) Worker processes {run_A, K}, now reads M items, canonicalizes M, marks run A COMPLETED with processed_count=M. No check fires; run A's persisted item_count=N diverges from what was canonicalized. The 0-item variant (item_count was non-zero, snapshot now empty -> processed_count=0, COMPLETED, indistinguishable from an empty week) is also reachable.

Why severity is downgraded to P2 rather than P1: (a) The finding's headline 'truncated' vector is not real for intra-fetch truncation — BeatportClient raises UpstreamUnavailableError at the 300-page safety cap (beatport_client.py:84) and ends pagination only on a genuinely absent next link; it never returns silently truncated data. Only the same-week-overwrite race produces divergence. (b) Snapshot and item_count are always consistent at write time (both derive from the identical releases list, handler.py:386 & 414); divergence requires a second run to overwrite the deterministic key during the narrow window an earlier message is still in flight — an operator-triggered, infrequent race, not a routine condition. (c) The catalog-gap consequence is largely self-healing: the additive identity-map means any release ever canonicalized stays forever, and run B's own message canonicalizes the latest snapshot; permanent loss only occurs for a release that vanished upstream before any run captured-and-canonicalized it, under a specific message-ordering window. (d) The divergence is persisted on the row (item_count vs processed_count) and logged, so it is detectable post-hoc even absent an automated check. This is a data-integrity hardening / observability gap (defense-in-depth), not a routinely-triggered corruption bug — P2.

title: Re-ingesting the same style/week overwrites the S3 snapshot and spawns a duplicate, racing canonicalization job
evidence: The S3 key is `{raw_prefix}/style_id={style_id}/year={year}/week={week:02d}/releases.json.gz` (storage.py:245-248) — it omits `run_id`. Each ingest POST mints a fresh `run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())` (handler.py:360). Two POSTs for the same style/week therefore produce two distinct run_ids and two `ingest_runs` rows, but write to the SAME S3 key (the second `write_run_artifacts` overwrites the first, storage.py:55-90) and enqueue two canonicalization messages each pointing at that same key (handler.py:921-929). The worker for run A and the worker for run B both call `read_releases(s3_key)` against whatever bytes currently live at the key.
risk: If run A's worker reads the key AFTER run B has overwritten it, run A canonicalizes run B's data yet records its result under run A's run_id and item_count — `processed_count` and run state become inconsistent with the snapshot the row claims to describe. Worse, if run B fetched a smaller/partial set (e.g. an upstream Beatport hiccup), it clobbers run A's complete snapshot, and any later re-read of run A's key (e.g. a DLQ redrive) now sees the degraded data. This is silent, condition-dependent corruption of run provenance.
recommendation: Include `run_id` in the S3 key so each run owns an immutable snapshot (the row already preserves per-run identity); or make the snapshot key content-addressed. This also fixes the overwrite-of-good-data-by-bad-data hazard.

votes.reason:
Verified every link in the finding's chain against the actual code; all claims hold.

(1) S3 key omits run_id: `_base_key` (storage.py:245-248) returns `{raw_prefix}/style_id={style_id}/year={year}/week={week:02d}` and `releases_key = base_key + "/releases.json.gz"` (storage.py:43). Year/week come from meta's iso_year/iso_week (or week_year/week_number fallback) — not run_id.

(2) Fresh run_id per POST: `run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())` (handler.py:360); a new ingest_runs row is created each time (handler.py:418-436) with no prior-run lookup or conflict check.

(3) Second write overwrites first: `write_run_artifacts` calls `_put_object` (storage.py:55-61, 250-265) — an unconditional S3 put_object, no conditional-put, no versioning guard.

(4) Both messages share the key: enqueue payload carries per-run `run_id` but the shared `s3_key` (handler.py:921-929).

(5) Worker re-reads at processing time: `read_releases(s3_key)` runs inside the worker loop (worker_handler.py:83) and records results under the message's own run_id via `set_run_completed(run_id=run_id, processed_count=result.tracks_processed, ...)` (worker_handler.py:109-116). So run A's worker can read bytes written by run B yet record under run A's run_id/processed_count.

(6) No dedup/idempotency anywhere: grep found no get_ingest_run/conflict/already-exists short-circuit on the ingest path. The canonicalization SQS queue is a STANDARD queue (infra/sqs.tf:6-18 — no `.fifo` name, no content_based_deduplication, no MessageGroupId), so no message-level dedup either.

The race and the overwrite-good-with-partial hazard are both real. I could not refute the finding.

Severity adjustment: the bug requires two overlapping POSTs for the SAME style/week, which is an admin-triggered weekly ingest (narrow concurrency window, not a routine user path), and the realistic worst case is provenance/count inconsistency rather than destruction of user-facing canonical data (canonicalization upserts into the shared core keyed by run). Silent condition-dependent provenance corruption is a legitimate DATA issue but the likelihood/impact fits P2 rather than P1.

Mechanism is technically real (S3 key omits run_id at storage.py:245-248; each POST mints a fresh run_id at handler.py:360; second write_run_artifacts overwrites the same key), but the impact does not rise to P1 for this MVP, and the worst-stated harm is largely refuted by the data model.

1) Trigger is admin-only and not concurrent in practice. POST /admin/beatport/ingest plus the run views are all /admin/* routes (handler.py:64,164,168,694). The race requires an admin to fire two ingests for the SAME style/week close enough that the two SQS workers interleave around the overwrite. Re-ingesting a cell is a deliberate, serialized, low-frequency admin action — not organic DJ-circle traffic.

2) The product data the DJs curate cannot be corrupted by this race. Canonicalization writes are all idempotent upserts keyed by SOURCE IDENTITY, not run_id: ON CONFLICT (source, entity_type, external_id) for entities/identities, ON CONFLICT (track_id, artist_id, role) for track-artists (repositories.py:283,455,703). Beatport external_id is a stable property of the track, independent of which snapshot/run it came from. So "run A canonicalizes run B's data" just upserts content-derived canonical rows that converge regardless of which worker read which snapshot. The shared canonical core reflects the union/latest of the upserts; no corruption.

3) The actual residual harm is confined to ingest_runs provenance — processed_count/item_count on an admin run row surfaced only on /admin/runs and /runs/{run_id} (handler.py:644-714). A mismatch there is admin observability noise, self-heals on the next clean ingest, and has zero effect on curation/playlists/playback/tenant data.

4) The "partial snapshot clobbers good snapshot, then DLQ redrive reads degraded data" sub-claim needs a conjunction of independently-rare events (two manual same-cell ingests + second fetch returns a degraded partial set + later DLQ redrive of run A). Even then, upsert-by-identity (DO NOTHING / DO UPDATE on external_id) means already-canonicalized tracks are not deleted by a partial re-read.

The design intends the S3 key to hold the latest snapshot per cell (key omits run_id by design; create_ingest_run even uses ON CONFLICT (run_id) DO UPDATE). This is a real but narrow, admin-only, self-healing provenance hygiene issue with no impact on the MVP's served data. Not P1.

Mechanism fully confirmed from real code, end to end. (1) S3 key is deterministic per style/week with no run_id: storage.py:245-248 `_base_key` returns `{raw_prefix}/style_id={style_id}/year={year}/week={week:02d}`, and storage.py:43 appends `/releases.json.gz`. (2) Each ingest mints a fresh run_id: handler.py:360 `run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())`, reached by BOTH routes — `_handle_collect` (handler.py:518) and `_handle_admin_ingest` (handler.py:553) — with NO uniqueness/dedup/lock guard on style+week. (3) write_run_artifacts unconditionally PUTs to the deterministic key (storage.py:55-90), so a second ingest overwrites the first. (4) DB does not prevent two rows: create_ingest_run ON CONFLICT target is run_id only (repositories.py:169), and idx_ingest_runs_coverage on [week_year, style_id, week_number] is non-unique (alembic 20260509_16:35-39); PK is run_id (init schema:38). (5) Enqueue puts the bare key in the message (handler.py:921-929: `s3_key: releases_key`). (6) Worker reads whatever bytes currently live at that key — worker_handler.py:70,83 `read_releases(payload.s3_key)` — then records the result under payload.run_id via set_run_completed(run_id, processed_count=result.tracks_processed) (worker_handler.py:109-116, repositories.py:206-225). Concrete path: POST ingest style X / week W -> run A writes 200-item snapshot to K, enqueues A; before A's worker runs, a re-ingest run B (e.g. Beatport hiccup, 5 items) overwrites K and enqueues B; A's worker then reads B's 5-item data from K and writes set_run_completed(run_id=A, processed_count=5) while row A's item_count=200 and raw_s3_key=K now describes B's degraded data. Result: silent, condition-dependent corruption of run provenance and loss of the per-run immutable-snapshot guarantee. Finding is NOT refuted. Severity adjusted to P2: trigger is not steady-state (requires two overlapping ingests / a re-ingest-while-in-flight for the SAME style/week, i.e. operator double-submit or deliberate re-run, in a single-admin ingest flow), damage is bounded to that one style/week's run rows + snapshot (canonicalization is run-keyed and source-entity upserts are idempotent, so the shared canonical core is not globally corrupted), and there is no automatic/routine trigger. That matches a silent single-partition data-integrity issue (P2) rather than a P1.

title: S3 snapshot write is non-atomic across the two objects and orphans releases.json.gz on partial failure
evidence: `write_run_artifacts` issues two independent `put_object` calls — `releases.json.gz` first, then `meta.json` (storage.py:56-73). If the second put fails, the first has already committed but the function raises StorageError (storage.py:81-82), so `_run_beatport_ingest` aborts before `create_ingest_run` (handler.py:416-417) — leaving the releases object in S3 with no meta and no `ingest_runs` row. The raw bucket has versioning on and no lifecycle policy (per system map §5.4), so these orphans accumulate.
risk: Orphaned snapshots are never canonicalized (no run row → never enqueued) and never cleaned up — unbounded S3 growth from partial-write retries, plus a snapshot/row pair that can silently disagree if downstream tooling assumes releases+meta are written together.
recommendation: Write meta first (or treat the pair as best-effort and only persist the run row after both succeed, which it already does), and add an S3 lifecycle rule to expire orphaned/old raw objects and non-current versions. Optionally write a single combined object to make the snapshot atomic.

votes.reason:
Verified the cited code; the core mechanic holds. storage.py:56-73 issues two independent put_object calls — releases.json.gz first (56-61), then meta.json (69-73) — and any exception is wrapped as StorageError at 81-82. In handler.py, write_run_artifacts is called at line 414 with no surrounding try/except in _run_beatport_ingest, and both call sites (_handle_collect:518, _handle_admin_ingest:553) just `return _run_beatport_ingest(...)` with no catch. So a failure of the second put (meta) after the first put (releases) has committed propagates StorageError and aborts before create_ingest_run (416-436), leaving releases.json.gz orphaned with no meta and no ingest_runs row. infra/s3.tf confirms the supporting infra claims: versioning is Enabled (s3.tf:5-11) and there is no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration anywhere, so orphans/noncurrent versions are never GC'd. No delete_object/rollback/cleanup logic exists. The defect (non-atomic two-object write that orphans releases.json.gz with no lifecycle expiry) is real, so the finding is not refuted.

Severity should drop to P3, however. (1) The trigger is a partial S3 failure — the second put failing only after the first succeeded — which is an uncommon transient/throttle path, not a routine occurrence. (2) The consequence is dead storage, not data corruption or loss of correctness: the orphaned snapshot is simply never enqueued and never read. (3) The stated "snapshot/row pair can silently disagree" risk is overstated — the run row is only written after BOTH puts succeed (the recommendation itself acknowledges this is already correct), and grep shows meta.json is never read by any downstream code, so there is no real consumer that assumes releases+meta atomicity. The genuine harm is therefore narrow: slow, low-volume S3 growth on a rare failure path, amplified only modestly by versioning. That is a low-priority hygiene/cleanup item, i.e. P3 rather than P2.

Refuted. The finding is backwards on its central claim and has negligible impact for this MVP. (1) Factual error: write_run_artifacts writes releases.json.gz FIRST (storage.py:56-61), then meta.json (storage.py:69-73). On a second-put failure the orphan is meta.json, NOT releases.json.gz as the title states. (2) The orphaned object is harmless: I grepped all of src/collector/ — meta.json/meta_key is never read by any downstream consumer. Workers read only releases.json.gz via raw_s3_key (worker_handler.py:83). So the "snapshot/row pair that can silently disagree if downstream tooling assumes releases+meta are written together" risk has no actual consumer. (3) The run row is already created only after BOTH puts succeed (handler.py:414→416-418), which the recommendation itself concedes ("which it already does"), so the fix is self-contradicting. (4) Trigger is a rare transient: first S3 PUT succeeds, second fails — S3 PUT availability is extremely high, and ingest is an admin-triggered weekly job for a small DJ circle. (5) "Unbounded S3 growth" is not credible: a worst-case orphan is a few-hundred-byte meta.json per rare partial failure; no plausible cost/ops impact at this scale. No lifecycle policy on a low-volume raw bucket is an accepted-cost MVP posture.

Verified against real code; the finding is accurate but P2 overstates impact. Path: POST ingest -> _run_beatport_ingest (handler.py:341) -> write_run_artifacts (handler.py:414) puts releases.json.gz first (storage.py:56-61), then meta.json (storage.py:69-73). If the second put fails, the catch at storage.py:81-82 re-raises StorageError, which propagates before create_ingest_run (handler.py:418), leaving the releases object committed with no meta.json and no ingest_runs row. The orphan is permanent: worker_handler.py:83 reads releases only via the s3_key carried in the SQS message, which is enqueued only after a run row exists, so no row -> never canonicalized. Infra confirms versioning enabled (s3.tf:5-11) with no aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration anywhere in infra/, so orphans and noncurrent versions accumulate. However, two factors lower severity: (1) the trigger requires a partial S3 failure (second put succeeds-then-fails) which is uncommon, and (2) the blast radius is cost/storage-hygiene only — meta.json has zero downstream readers (grep shows it is only written, never read), so the 'snapshot/row can silently disagree' sub-risk does not materialize and no incorrect data ever flows downstream; the aborted run simply produces nothing. Rare trigger + bounded cost-only impact = P3, not P2.

title: Empty/unknown-shaped Beatport pages are silently dropped during pagination with no integrity signal
evidence: `_extract_items` returns `[]` for any page whose item array is not under one of `results/items/releases/data` (beatport_client.py:158-165). The pagination loop blindly `all_items.extend(items)` and continues as long as `next` is present (beatport_client.py:73-78). A page that returns 200 with items under an unexpected key, or a transient upstream returning an empty list while still advancing `next`, contributes zero rows but is not distinguished from a genuinely empty page. The only safety net is the 300-page cap (beatport_client.py:84).
risk: Upstream schema drift or a partial upstream response silently reduces `item_count`, the snapshot is written and canonicalized as complete, and the week ends up with fewer tracks than Beatport actually returned — gradual catalog under-population with no error or alarm.
recommendation: Treat an unexpected/empty page-shape mid-pagination (page with no recognised item key) as an error or at least emit a warning metric; consider cross-checking against an upstream total-count field when present.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code; all claims hold. `_extract_items` (beatport_client.py:158-165) only recognizes item arrays under `results/items/releases/data` and returns `[]` for any other shape. The pagination loop (lines 64-84) does `all_items.extend(items)` unconditionally (line 74) and continues solely on the presence of a non-empty string `next` (lines 76-78), with no per-page check on `len(items)`. Nothing distinguishes a genuinely empty page from a 200 page whose items sit under an unrecognized key or a transient empty list that still advances `next`. The only loop guard is the 300-page cap (line 84). I confirmed the absence of any integrity signal: the only UpstreamUnavailableError triggers are non-dict payload (line 128), malformed next link (line 82), page cap (line 84), and HTTP/transport errors — none cover an unexpected/empty-shaped page. Downstream treats the result as authoritative: handler.py:386 sets `item_count = len(releases)`, persists it as RAW_SAVED (handler.py:404,432) and the worker re-reads the snapshot and canonicalizes with no cross-check (worker_handler.py:83-94). A grep found no `count`/`total` upstream field captured anywhere, so there is currently nothing to validate completeness against. The realistic trigger (upstream schema drift renaming the item key, or a partial upstream response mid-pagination) silently under-populates the catalog with no error or alarm. Severity P2 is correct: real data-integrity risk, latent and gradual, no crash/security/availability impact, requires upstream drift to manifest — more than cosmetic (not P3) but not a guaranteed current data loss (not P1).

Code is accurately described — `_extract_items` (beatport_client.py:158-165) returns `[]` for any unrecognized shape and the pagination loop (beatport_client.py:64-84) silently continues. But the trigger is implausible and the impact is low for this MVP. (1) The Beatport v4 catalog API canonically returns the array under `results`, the first candidate key, confirmed by the test fixtures in tests/unit/test_beatport_client.py. For SILENT under-population both an unrecognized item key AND a well-formed, advancing `next` string must coincide. The loud variants are already guarded: non-dict payload raises UpstreamUnavailableError (lines 127-130), malformed `next` raises at line 82, and missing/non-string `next` simply ends the loop. The realistic schema-drift outcome is a week with ~0 items — glaringly visible to the DJ users browsing that week, not a silent partial reduction. Scenario 2 (empty `results` with non-null `next`) describes a malformed upstream pager; `next` is server-generated from count/page so the terminal/empty page carries `next: null`. No documented or realistic mechanism produces an advancing-but-empty page. (2) Impact is bounded: this is admin-driven ingest into the shared canonical core (handler.py:62 POST /admin/beatport/ingest) — no per-tenant data, no auth surface, no corruption, no cross-tenant leakage. Worst case is fewer tracks in a week, which is user-visible, idempotently re-runnable (repositories.py:169 ON CONFLICT (run_id) DO UPDATE overwrites item_count), and already traceable: `api_pages_fetched` and `item_count` are both persisted in meta (handler.py:404-405) and logged per page, so a "many pages, few items" anomaly is detectable — a partial integrity signal already exists. This is theoretical hardening against a no-plausible-trigger scenario, which the rubric marks refuted.

Confirmed, not refuted. The mechanism is real and the trigger path is reconstructible from actual code. `_extract_items` (beatport_client.py:158-165) returns `[]` for any page whose item array isn't under `results/items/releases/data`, and the pagination loop (beatport_client.py:64-84) blindly does `all_items.extend(items)` then continues as long as `next` is a non-empty string. `item_count = len(releases)` (handler.py:386) is the ONLY count recorded; I confirmed there are zero references to any upstream `count`/`total` field in beatport_client.py or providers/beatport.py, so there is no cross-check. The under-populated result is then written to S3 (handler.py:414), persisted as RAW_SAVED (handler.py:431), and enqueued for canonicalization with no error/metric/alarm.

Concrete path: admin ingest route -> _run_beatport_ingest (handler.py:341) -> fetch_weekly_releases (handler.py:377). Two reproducible sub-scenarios: (A) upstream schema drift renames the items key — `next` is a top-level sibling unaffected by the rename, so the loop paginates to completion yielding item_count=0 with no exception; the 4-key fallback is defensive code that masks drift rather than detecting it. (B) a 200 page with empty `results` but a well-formed non-null `next` advances and contributes zero rows, surviving the malformed-next guard (beatport_client.py:81-82) and capped only by the 300-page limit.

Refutation attempts fail: a total-zero week (A) is eventually human-observable but there is still NO automated integrity signal, which is exactly what the finding claims. The partial-reduction case (B) is genuinely silent. The finding is correctly framed as a missing-integrity-signal / defense-in-depth gap, not a guaranteed live bug, so it does not overclaim.

Severity unchanged at P2: latent data-integrity degradation requiring an external precondition (upstream drift or empty interior pages) with no evidence it has fired; it silently under-populates rather than crashing or corrupting. P1 would need a more direct/likely path; P3 would understate the silent catalog under-population. The recommendation (cross-check the upstream count field the code ignores) is sound and low effort.

title: Concurrent runs of same style/week create duplicate orphaned canonical rows (read-then-mint identity has no concurrency guard)
evidence: Canonical-row dedup is a read-then-mint pattern: find_identity(source,entity_type,external_id) returns None -> mint uuid4 -> create_label/artist/album/style/track with ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING -> queue UpsertIdentityCmd. The clouder_* tables have NO unique constraint on (source,entity_type,external_id) or normalized_name — only the synthetic id PK (init schema 20260301_01:78-138). The identity_map ON CONFLICT key is (source,entity_type,external_id) (repositories.py:455,491). Each ingest mints a fresh run_id (handler.py:360); the canonicalization SQS ESM has no maximum_concurrency cap (infra/lambda.tf:97-101) and the queue is a standard (non-FIFO) at-least-once queue (infra/sqs.tf:6-18). Two concurrent canonicalizations of the same Beatport entity (a normal admin re-run of the same style/week, or an SQS duplicate delivery racing a retry) both read find_identity=None under READ COMMITTED (no advisory lock, no SELECT FOR UPDATE, no SERIALIZABLE — verified absent), both mint distinct uuids, both create_*(...) succeed (different ids, DO NOTHING never fires), and batch_upsert_identities collapses identity_map to ONE row pointing at whichever transaction commits last.
risk: Two clouder_labels/clouder_artists/clouder_albums/clouder_tracks rows are created for the same Beatport entity; identity_map points at only one, so the other is a permanently orphaned canonical row. Downstream FKs (track.album_id, track.style_id, track_artists) and curation overlays can attach to the orphaned id, producing duplicate tracks/artists in the catalogue that survive future re-ingests (find_identity now finds the surviving mapping and never touches the orphan). Silent gradual catalogue corruption that worsens with each concurrent/overlapping ingest.
recommendation: Make canonical creation concurrency-safe: either (a) add a unique constraint on identity_map's natural key path by inserting the identity FIRST with INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (source,entity_type,external_id) DO UPDATE ... RETURNING clouder_id and deriving the canonical id from the winning row, or (b) take a pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(source||entity_type||external_id)) before find_identity within the phase transaction, or (c) serialize the canonization worker (FIFO queue keyed by style/week, or ESM maximum_concurrency=1). Option (a) is the cleanest — it removes the read-then-mint race entirely.

votes.reason:
Verified every cited claim against the code; all hold.

1. Read-then-mint confirmed: _resolve_label/style/artist/album/track (canonicalize.py:443-656) do find_identity -> if None -> uuid4() -> create_*(...) -> return UpsertIdentityCmd.
2. find_identity (repositories.py:413-441) is a plain SELECT — no FOR UPDATE / lock.
3. create_* uses ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING on the synthetic UUID PK (repositories.py:526,549,572,600,627), so two distinct minted uuids never collide and DO NOTHING never fires for the race.
4. No unique constraint on natural keys — verified across ALL 30 migrations. clouder_artists/labels/albums/tracks (init schema:78-138) and clouder_styles (mig 04:21-28) have only id PK; idx_album_match is explicitly unique=False; nothing adds a unique on normalized_name/title.
5. identity_map PK and both upsert paths key on (source,entity_type,external_id) (init:151; repositories.py:455,491) -> concurrent upserts of the same external_id collapse to ONE row (last commit wins), orphaning the loser's minted uuid.
6. No concurrency guard / isolation: transaction() (data_api.py:99-107) is plain begin/commit; begin_transaction (data_api.py:75-81) passes no isolation -> Aurora default READ COMMITTED. No advisory lock / SERIALIZABLE anywhere.
7. Infra confirms open concurrency: canonicalization queue is standard non-FIFO SQS, maxReceiveCount=5 (sqs.tf); ESM canonicalization_queue (lambda.tf:97-101) has no scaling_config/maximum_concurrency; and unlike spotify/vendor/label workers, the canonicalization_worker lambda block has NO reserved_concurrent_executions at all.
8. Fresh run_id per ingest (handler.py:360) and _enqueue_canonicalization (handler.py:878+) has no idempotency/in-progress dedup, so an ordinary admin re-run of the same style/week enqueues a second independent message over the same Beatport external_ids.

The per-phase separate transactions widen the race window further. Downstream FKs (clouder_tracks.style_id, album_id, track_artists) can attach to the orphaned canonical id, and the orphan survives future re-ingests because find_identity then returns the surviving mapping. Severity P1 is correct: silent, cumulative DATA-integrity corruption with no self-healing, triggered by a normal concurrent/overlapping admin re-run — worse than a cosmetic bug, but not an immediate outage (sequential single-pipeline runs are unaffected), so not P0.

Verified the mechanism end-to-end and could not refute it. Schema (alembic 20260301_01:78-152): clouder_* tables have only a synthetic `id` PK; identity_map PK is (source,entity_type,external_id). There is no unique constraint on the natural key or normalized_name. find_identity (repositories.py:413-441) is a plain SELECT with no FOR UPDATE / advisory lock; the RDS Data API transaction wrapper (data_api.py:75-104) uses default READ COMMITTED with no SERIALIZABLE. Each phase commits create_* and batch_upsert_identities in ONE transaction, and create_* uses ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING — so two overlapping runs that both read find_identity=None each mint a distinct uuid4 and both INSERT succeeds (different ids), while identity_map collapses to one row. The orphan is durable: all future ingests resolve via the surviving identity and never touch the orphan.

Trigger is real and admin-reachable, not theoretical: (1) the canonicalization ESM (lambda.tf:97-101) has NO scaling_config/maximum_concurrency — unlike the enrichment workers (lambda.tf:232,282,321) which deliberately cap it — so multiple canonicalization messages run in parallel; (2) there is no idempotency gate on re-ingest — handler.py:360 mints a fresh run_id and enqueues unconditionally, so a second admin ingest of the same style/week reads the same Beatport external_ids; (3) Aurora cold-start (ADR-0014) widens the per-phase window. Two same-style/week ingests overlapping before the relevant phase commits hit the race. Consequence is genuinely user-visible: catalogue list queries read FROM clouder_tracks directly (repositories.py:744,769,882), not via identity_map, so the orphaned duplicate surfaces as a duplicate track/artist to the DJ circle, silently and durably.

Does not match any accepted-behavior carve-out (not cold-start 503, refresh-replay, min_acu, or YT reconnect). It is a real unguarded read-then-mint race producing durable, silent DATA corruption. Not refuted. Severity stays P1: durable silent data-integrity corruption keeps it above P2, but the low per-event probability (requires temporal overlap of two same-key runs; the more common SQS-retry-of-same-message path is self-healing because find_identity finds the committed identity) and bounded blast radius for a small DJ circle (a few duplicate rows, no impact to auth/curation/core loop) keep it below P0. One overstatement in the finding: it does NOT "worsen with each ingest" — only overlapping same-key ingests add orphans; sequential re-ingests are safe.

Could not refute — every load-bearing claim verified against real code, and a concrete trigger path exists.

VERIFIED FACTS:
1. Read-then-mint with no guard: _resolve_label/style/artist/album/track (canonicalize.py:443-656) call find_identity() — a plain SELECT with no FOR UPDATE (repositories.py:413-441) — then on None mint uuid4() and create_*() with ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING on the SYNTHETIC uuid PK (repositories.py:526,549,572,600,627). Two distinct uuids never collide, so DO NOTHING never fires for the natural key.
2. No natural-key uniqueness: clouder_artists/labels/albums/tracks have only PrimaryKeyConstraint('id'); no unique on normalized_name or (source,external_id) (init schema 78-138).
3. identity_map collapses to one row: upsert_identity (455) and batch_upsert_identities (491) use ON CONFLICT (source,entity_type,external_id) DO UPDATE SET clouder_id = EXCLUDED.clouder_id → last committer wins, orphaning the loser's clouder_* row.
4. No concurrency control: begin_transaction (data_api.py:75-81) sets NO isolation level → Aurora default READ COMMITTED; no advisory lock / FOR UPDATE / SERIALIZABLE anywhere on this path. Canonicalization ESM (lambda.tf:97-101) has NO scaling_config/maximum_concurrency — unlike the enrichment ESMs (lambda.tf:232,281,321) which do. Worker Lambda (lambda.tf:36-62) has NO reserved_concurrent_executions. Queue is standard, non-FIFO, at-least-once (sqs.tf:6-18; name has no .fifo suffix, no fifo_queue/content_based_deduplication).
5. No idempotency guard: worker_handler.py:107-116 unconditionally runs process_run then set_run_completed; never checks ingest_runs.status before processing. A redelivered message re-runs canonicalization.

REPRODUCIBLE PATH (single ingest, no admin re-run needed): One admin ingest POST mints run_id R + S3 key K and sends ONE message to the standard queue. The worker invocation is slow (large bundle / Aurora cold-start) and exceeds the queue visibility timeout, so SQS redelivers the same {run_id:R, s3_key:K} message while the first invocation is still in-flight. With no maximum_concurrency, no reserved concurrency, and no status guard, two invocations run process_run concurrently. For each new Beatport entity both read find_identity=None under READ COMMITTED, mint distinct uuid4s, both create_* succeed (DO NOTHING never fires), and identity_map ends pointing at the last committer; the other clouder_* row is permanently orphaned and survives future re-ingests (find_identity then returns the surviving mapping and never reclaims the orphan). Plain SQS at-least-once duplicate delivery reproduces it identically without any timeout overrun; two overlapping ingests of the same style/week (distinct run_ids per handler.py:360) is a second valid path.

SEVERITY: P1 is appropriate. Silent, persistent, self-reinforcing catalogue corruption (duplicate orphaned canonical rows that downstream FKs and curation overlays can attach to) with no detection or self-healing. It is probabilistic (requires a concurrency race, not the happy path) rather than guaranteed every run, placing it near the P1/P2 boundary, but the compounding, undetectable data-integrity damage justifies keeping it at P1.

title: Transient S3 read failure misclassified as permanent error -> message dropped, run marked FAILED, no retry/DLQ (silent data loss)
evidence: read_releases wraps EVERY S3 exception into StorageError: `except Exception as exc: raise StorageError(...)` (storage.py:96-97) — this catches transient S3 conditions (SlowDown/503 throttling, connection resets, read timeouts) identically to genuinely-corrupt payloads. StorageError is listed in _PERMANENT_ERRORS = (ValueError, TypeError, KeyError, StorageError) (worker_handler.py:21). In the handler, is_permanent=isinstance(exc,_PERMANENT_ERRORS) (worker_handler.py:140); permanent -> set_run_failed then `continue` (worker_handler.py:176-179), which deletes the SQS message WITHOUT re-raising, so it never cycles toward the maxReceiveCount=5 DLQ (infra/sqs.tf:14-17). The raw S3 snapshot is keyed by style/year/week with no run_id, so there is no automatic re-enqueue path.
risk: A momentary S3 throttle/timeout during the read_s3 phase permanently abandons that run: the message is deleted, the run sits FAILED, and the already-fetched raw releases are never canonicalized. The catalogue silently misses an entire weekly ingest with no DLQ artifact to replay from — operators only see a FAILED row.
recommendation: Separate transient from permanent S3 failures: in read_releases, re-raise the original boto3 ClientError (or a dedicated TransientStorageError) for retryable S3 codes (SlowDown, RequestTimeout, 5xx, throttling) so worker_handler re-raises and lets SQS redrive/DLQ handle it; reserve StorageError (permanent) only for decode/shape errors (gzip/json/non-list payload, storage.py:99-107). Equivalently, drop StorageError from _PERMANENT_ERRORS and add a narrow permanent decode-error type.

votes.reason:
Mechanically the finding is fully correct — I read every cited line. read_releases (storage.py:92-107) wraps EVERY exception into StorageError at lines 96-97 (the bare `except Exception`), conflating transient S3 conditions with genuinely corrupt/missing payloads. Note the body read at line 95 (`response["Body"].read()`) happens after get_object returns and is on the streaming path, which boto3's request-retry layer does not cover. StorageError is in _PERMANENT_ERRORS (worker_handler.py:21). In the handler, is_permanent=isinstance(exc,_PERMANENT_ERRORS) (line 140), and permanent -> set_run_failed then `continue` without re-raise (lines 176-179), so SQS deletes the message and it never cycles to the maxReceiveCount=5 DLQ (sqs.tf:14-17). The raw key is style/year/week with no run_id (storage.py:245-247), and there is no reconciliation/redrive path for FAILED runs (only beatport_client fetch-retries and data_api DB-retries exist, neither re-drives canonicalization). The unit tests (test_permanent_error_does_not_reraise) explicitly bless treating an S3 read failure as permanent. So the bug is real — NOT refuted.

However, P1 overstates severity, so I downgrade to P2. Two mitigants reduce likelihood and impact: (1) boto3.client("s3") is created with no custom retry Config (storage.py:13-16), so it uses boto3's default retry mode, which already retries SlowDown/throttling/5xx on the get_object call before raising — a momentary throttle is usually absorbed before reaching read_releases; the genuinely-exposed window (streaming body read / sustained outage) is narrower and rarer than the finding implies. (2) The harm is recoverable, not irreversible: the raw releases object persists in S3 and a FAILED run row is visible, so an operator can re-enqueue. This is a real error-classification gap with a missing auto-recovery path and a silent-degradation footprint (FAILED row, no DLQ artifact), but low trigger frequency plus recoverability fit P2 rather than P1.

Technical claim verified exactly as described. read_releases (storage.py:96-97) wraps EVERY S3 exception into StorageError, which sits in _PERMANENT_ERRORS (worker_handler.py:21); a permanent classification hits `continue` (worker_handler.py:176-179) without re-raising, so SQS deletes the message and it never cycles toward the maxReceiveCount=5 DLQ (sqs.tf:14-17). A transient S3 read failure (SlowDown/503/timeout/reset) is therefore misclassified as permanent. The misclassification is genuine: the code's own intent comment says permanent = "corrupted/malformed," which a transient read is not. The failure is also genuinely silent — because `continue` lets the Lambda return successfully ({"processed": 0}), no Lambda Errors metric is emitted, so the canonicalization Errors alarm (alarms.tf, threshold=1) does NOT fire for this path; operators only see a FAILED ingest_runs row, and there is no scheduled reconciliation/sweep to self-heal.

Two facts pull the severity below P1, however. (1) Recoverability: the raw snapshot is durable in S3, deterministically keyed by style/year/week, and the ingest_runs row persists raw_s3_key with FAILED status — so this is a recoverable, manually-replayable gap, not irreversible "silent data loss" as the title states. (2) Trigger probability is low: ingest runs are admin-triggered (roughly weekly per style) with canonicalization_batch_size=1, so S3 sees a tiny request rate against a single same-region GetObject — S3 throttling/SlowDown effectively never occurs at this volume, and a lone transient 5xx/reset is rare. Worst realistic case: one weekly style ingest silently not canonicalized, requiring manual operator action, with no alert.

Real, correctly-located correctness/silent-failure defect with a clean small fix (split transient vs permanent S3 errors), but low trigger probability plus full recoverability put it at P2 rather than P1.

Confirmed — the finding reproduces against real code. Path: API handler (handler.py:414-446) writes raw releases to S3, creates a RUN_SAVED ingest run, and enqueues a CanonicalizationMessage{run_id, s3_key} to the canonicalization queue (batch_size default=1, infra/variables.tf:82). The worker (worker_handler.py:82-83) calls storage.read_releases(s3_key). On a transient S3 read failure during get_object/Body.read() (SlowDown/503 throttle, connection reset, ReadTimeoutError), storage.py:96-97 catches it with a bare `except Exception` and wraps it into StorageError — indistinguishable from a corrupt payload. StorageError is in _PERMANENT_ERRORS (worker_handler.py:21), so is_permanent=True (line 140), the run is marked FAILED (line 147; repositories.py:252), and `continue` (line 179) returns normally → SQS deletes the message. Verified the receive count never advances toward maxReceiveCount=5 (sqs.tf:14-17) and the event source mapping has no function_response_types/ReportBatchItemFailures (lambda.tf:97-101), so no DLQ artifact. No re-enqueue path: raw S3 key is style/year/week-based with no run_id (storage.py:245-248), and grep for FAILED shows no scheduled reprocessor of failed runs. Outcome (a momentary S3 blip permanently abandons a weekly ingest with no automatic replay) is real. Severity stays P1: genuine DATA-dimension data-loss via misclassification with no automatic recovery; mildly mitigated by a visible FAILED row + ERROR log (so not fully silent) and a plausible-but-not-frequent trigger, but the permanent-loss-without-redrive consequence justifies P1.

title: Run-status writes are unguarded blind UPDATEs and follow-up enqueue is outside idempotency, so SQS redelivery re-triggers Spotify fan-out and can flip a FAILED run back to COMPLETED
evidence: set_run_completed/set_run_failed are `UPDATE ingest_runs SET status=...
