WHERE run_id=:run_id` with NO status precondition (repositories.py:211-217, 243-248) — they overwrite any prior status unconditionally. mark_completed (worker_handler.py:112-116) and the Spotify-search enqueue (worker_handler.py:134-138, _enqueue_spotify_search_after_canonicalization) run on EVERY successful message processing with no dedup against run_id and no check that the run was already COMPLETED. SQS standard delivery is at-least-once (infra/sqs.tf:6-18) and the ESM has no concurrency cap (infra/lambda.tf:97-101). A redelivery of an already-COMPLETED run re-runs the full canonicalization (idempotent but wasteful) AND re-enqueues a fresh {batch_size:2000} Spotify search; a redelivery that arrives after a different attempt set the run FAILED will overwrite it to COMPLETED (or vice-versa).
risk: Each duplicate delivery re-fires the Spotify ISRC-search batch (a 5+-calls-per-miss vendor fan-out), wasting Spotify quota; the ingest_runs status can race between COMPLETED/FAILED across concurrent attempts, making the run-status surface (GET /runs/{run_id}, /admin/coverage) unreliable. Not data corruption, but a reliability/observability and minor cost issue under normal at-least-once redelivery.
recommendation: Guard the status transition (e.g. set_run_completed `UPDATE ... WHERE run_id=:run_id AND status <> 'COMPLETED'`, and only enqueue the Spotify follow-up when that UPDATE actually changed a row / when the run was not already COMPLETED). This makes mark_completed and the follow-up enqueue idempotent across redeliveries.

votes.reason:
Code reading is accurate. set_run_completed (repositories.py:209-225) and set_run_failed (repositories.py:241-257) are blind `UPDATE ... WHERE run_id = :run_id` with no status precondition — confirmed verbatim. mark_completed (worker_handler.py:112-116) and the Spotify enqueue (worker_handler.py:134-138 → _enqueue_spotify_search_after_canonicalization, 185-221) run on every successful message with no run_id dedup. The canonicalization queue is standard SQS, at-least-once, maxReceiveCount:5 (sqs.tf:6-18) and the ESM sets only batch_size — no maximum_concurrency/scaling_config (lambda.tf:97-101). So redelivery CAN re-run the path and overwrite status. The mechanism is genuine and the small guarded-UPDATE fix is sound.

However the severity is overstated at P2; it should be P3, because the two stated impacts are weaker than claimed:

(1) The Spotify "fan-out re-fire" is already self-gated. _process_spotify_search (spotify_handler.py:182) pulls from find_tracks_needing_spotify_search, whose SQL filters `WHERE t.spotify_searched_at IS NULL` (repositories.py:748). Once searched, a track is excluded. A duplicate enqueue arriving after the search ran returns `spotify_search_skipped` with ZERO Spotify API calls (spotify_handler.py:183-190). The "5+-calls-per-miss" quota waste only occurs in a narrow window where tracks genuinely still need searching — i.e., the same work that legitimately had to run anyway. Not the unconditional re-fan-out the finding describes.

(2) The status-flip's harmful direction is the uncommon one. The common redelivery path (transient attempt sets FAILED then re-raises → retry succeeds → sets COMPLETED, worker_handler.py:139-180 + 112-116) ends COMPLETED after the work actually completed — which is the correct/desired result, not a bug. The genuinely-bad flip (COMPLETED→FAILED, marking a successful run as failed) requires concurrent in-flight duplicates of the same message, which standard SQS plus a visibility timeout sized to max(visibility, lambda_timeout) (sqs.tf:8-11) makes uncommon. The finding itself concedes "Not data corruption... reliability/observability and minor cost issue." That self-assessment plus the self-gated search puts this at P3.

The code-level claims are accurate (set_run_completed/set_run_failed are unguarded blind UPDATEs at repositories.py:206-257; mark_completed + Spotify enqueue run on every successful message with no run_id dedup at worker_handler.py:111-138; SQS-standard at-least-once with batch_size=1 and no ReportBatchItemFailures). But the finding's headline harm — re-firing the Spotify ISRC vendor fan-out and wasting Spotify quota — is refuted by an existing guard. The Spotify worker consumes the generic {batch_size:2000} message and selects via find_tracks_needing_spotify_search (repositories.py:739-755), which filters `t.spotify_searched_at IS NULL`. After the first search those tracks are marked searched, so a duplicate enqueue finds zero rows and returns early ('spotify_search_skipped', spotify_handler.py:182-190) with NO vendor calls. Worst case from a redelivery is one no-op DB query, not a 5-calls-per-miss fan-out. Reserved concurrency further caps Spotify parallelism regardless. The canonicalization re-run is conceded as idempotent and negligibly costly. The residual FAILED<->COMPLETED status flip is structurally rare: effective visibility timeout = max(180,900) = 900s >= the 900s lambda timeout (sqs.tf:8-11, variables.tf:67-89), so the same message cannot be processed concurrently — a redelivery only becomes visible after the prior attempt's window closes. The normal transient-fail-then-retry sequence correctly lands on COMPLETED; the genuinely-wrong stale-COMPLETED-overwriting-FAILED ordering needs a duplicate of an old success arriving after a later fresh failure, an unlikely path with no data corruption. The only affected surface is admin-only ingest observability (GET /runs/{run_id}, /admin/coverage), not user-facing curation for the small DJ circle. Net: a rare, transient, admin-only observability blip with no functional or meaningful cost impact for this MVP; the primary cost claim is wrong. The guard is cheap to add but does not matter enough to be P2.

The finding's two load-bearing claims are contradicted by the actual code/config, so the P2 reliability+cost framing does not hold.

COST CLAIM (re-fired Spotify fan-out) — mostly refuted. The re-enqueued {batch_size:2000} message is real, but the expensive 5+-calls-per-miss fan-out is self-deduplicating. spotify_handler._process_spotify_search calls repository.find_tracks_needing_spotify_search (repositories.py:739-755), whose WHERE clause filters `t.spotify_searched_at IS NULL`. batch_update_spotify_results (repositories.py:802-832) sets spotify_searched_at = :searched_at for EVERY track in the batch — found and not-found alike (UpdateSpotifyResultCmd is built for all chunk rows in spotify_handler.py:333-343). So once the first search wave persists, a duplicate canonicalization delivery enqueues one cheap SQS message and the Spotify worker finds zero tracks, logging spotify_search_skipped/no_tracks_need_search with ZERO vendor calls (spotify_handler.py:182-190). The fan-out only repeats in a narrow race where the duplicate arrives before searched_at is persisted — not "each duplicate delivery re-fires the batch" as the evidence asserts.

STATUS-FLIP CLAIM — largely refuted. set_run_completed/set_run_failed are indeed unguarded blind UPDATEs (repositories.py:206-225, 227-257), confirmed. But the two harmful directions break down: (1) FAILED→COMPLETED via sequential retry (attempt 1 transient-fails + re-raises at worker_handler.py:180, attempt 2 succeeds) yields the CORRECT terminal status COMPLETED — desired behavior, not a bug. (2) COMPLETED→FAILED, the only genuinely harmful flip, requires two concurrent in-flight attempts of the SAME message. With canonicalization_batch_size=1 (variables.tf:79-83) and effective visibility timeout = max(180, 900) = 900s (sqs.tf:8-11 + variables.tf:67-70,85-89) equal to the Lambda's own hard timeout, the same message is not redelivered while attempt 1 is alive: attempt 1 either returns and SQS deletes the message before the visibility window expires, or it is killed at the 900s Lambda timeout and thus cannot still be writing. The finding's "under normal at-least-once redelivery the status can race" overstates this — it needs a rare SQS-internal duplicate delivery overlapping in real time, and even then the idempotent second attempt typically re-reaches COMPLETED.

RESIDUAL: There is a real but minor latent robustness nit — the missing status precondition means a genuinely concurrent SQS duplicate could momentarily flip COMPLETED→FAILED, and a late stale attempt could overwrite a terminal status. The recommendation (WHERE status <> 'COMPLETED', gate the enqueue on rows-changed) is sound and cheap. But this is a defensive hardening, not a reliability/cost defect that manifests under normal operation. Corrected severity P3.

title: Match-review resolve/accept writes the GLOBAL vendor_track_map keyed only by track_id+vendor — one user's manual resolution overwrites the shared YT Music match all other users see
evidence: get_open_review/resolve_review_accept/resolve_review_reject take only (track_id, vendor) and have NO user_id predicate. resolve_review_accept calls ClouderRepository.upsert_vendor_match with match_type='manual', confidence=1.000 (playlists_repository.py:1040-1056) on the GLOBAL vendor_track_map ((clouder_track_id,vendor) PK), and updates the GLOBAL match_review_queue (lines 1057-1065). fetch_ytmusic_status (lines 963-1020) likewise reads vendor_track_map/match_review_queue per track with no user scope. The handler gates access with _scope_check (curation_handler.py:315-320), so this is NOT a cross-tenant IDOR — but because the underlying tables are user-agnostic, any user who has a shared canonical track in scope can permanently set its vendor match, and that resolution is then shown to every other user who has the same track (e.g. via list_tracks fetch_ytmusic_status). User B's correct match can be silently replaced by user A's manual choice.
risk: In a multi-tenant SaaS the YT Music match shown on a playlist is global, not per-user. User A's accept/reject of a shared track's match mutates state visible to user B with no audit trail and no way for B to know it changed. For a small trusted DJ circle the blast radius is limited, but it is silent shared-state mutation across tenants where the rest of the curation model is strictly per-user.
recommendation: Decide whether vendor_track_map is intended to be canonical/shared (like the catalog) or per-user. If canonical, document it as accepted shared state (it mirrors the ingest-time auto match). If it should be per-user, scope match_review_queue and vendor_track_map by user_id (add a user_id column / composite key) so manual resolutions don't leak across tenants.

votes.reason:
The finding's technical claims are all correct (verified). vendor_track_map and match_review_queue have NO user_id; PK is (clouder_track_id, vendor) — confirmed in migration alembic/versions/20260421_10_vendor_match_tables.py:22-59. resolve_review_accept/_reject/get_open_review take only (track_id, vendor) with no user predicate (playlists_repository.py:1024-1086). resolve_review_accept calls ClouderRepository.upsert_vendor_match, which is a global ON CONFLICT (clouder_track_id, vendor) DO UPDATE (repositories.py:1037-1068) — so a manual resolution overwrites the single shared row. fetch_ytmusic_status reads global rows with no user scope (playlists_repository.py:963-1020). clouder_tracks IS a shared canonical table deduped by spotify_id across users (upsert_imported_track, SELECT-first by spotify_id with no user_id), so the same clouder_track_id can simultaneously be in scope for users A and B (validate_tracks_in_scope, playlists_repository.py:828-844 + _SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE:82-104). The finding correctly notes _scope_check (curation_handler.py:315-320) prevents IDOR, so user A's resolve genuinely can change state user B sees. So I cannot refute it factually.

HOWEVER, severity P2 is too high. This exact behavior is an explicitly documented and ACCEPTED architectural decision, not a latent defect: ADR-0002:21 states "Enrichment results (ai_search_results, vendor_track_map) are also shared"; ADR-0019:25-32 states vendor_track_map is keyed (clouder_track_id, vendor) "so a YT Music match is shared across all users (canonical-core model)," that regular users resolving needs_review "writes the canonical vendor_track_map," and that the mitigation is explicitly "the small DJ circle reviews its own playlists." The finding's own recommendation ("If canonical, document it as accepted shared state") is already satisfied by two accepted ADRs. The remaining residual is a minor observability gap (no resolved_by DB column), and even that is partly mitigated: the match_review_resolved structured log DOES record user_id/track_id/vendor/action (curation_handler.py:379-383), which contradicts the finding's "no audit trail" assertion. Accepted-by-design shared canonical state in a small trusted circle, with a partial log-level audit trail, is a P3 observation, not a P2 data-integrity issue.

Code facts are accurate (vendor_track_map PK = (clouder_track_id, vendor), no user_id; resolve_review_accept/reject and fetch_ytmusic_status take no user scope), but the conclusion that this is a defect is refuted because the table is canonical/shared BY DESIGN, not a per-user table missing a key. Proof: the ingest-time auto-match worker (vendor_match_handler.py:105-164) and canonicalize.py write the SAME (clouder_track_id, vendor) rows globally with match_type='isrc'/'fuzzy'/'auto_create' and ON CONFLICT (clouder_track_id, vendor) DO UPDATE (repositories.py:1037-1068). vendor_track_map FKs to clouder_tracks.id — the shared canonical core of the documented multi-tenant model (admin ingest -> shared canonical core -> per-user overlay). A YT Music videoId for a canonical recording is a fact about the track, not a per-user preference; it belongs in the shared catalog exactly like ISRC/title/auto-match. The manual resolve (match_type='manual') is a human correction of a shared catalog fact, gated by _scope_check (curation_handler.py:315-320) so it is not an IDOR (finding concedes this) and exposes no private data (a public video id is not private). The finding itself frames this as a design-documentation decision ('If canonical, document it as accepted shared state — it mirrors the ingest-time auto match'); the code answers that decision unambiguously in favor of canonical/shared. Trigger requires two users holding the same ambiguous-match track to disagree on the one correct video for one recording — implausible and self-healing for the stated small trusted DJ circle. No data corruption, no cross-tenant private-data exposure, no security boundary crossed. This is shared canonical state behaving as shared canonical state — an accepted behavior. At most a P3 documentation nit, not a P2 data-integrity issue.

The finding's mechanics are accurate and the path is fully reproducible, BUT its central premise — that this is unintended/undocumented cross-tenant shared-state leakage warranting P2 DATA — is refuted by ADR-0002.

VERIFIED MECHANICS (all true):
- vendor_track_map PK = (clouder_track_id, vendor), no user_id (alembic 20260421_10:31-34). match_review_queue also has no user_id (lines 41-52). Confirmed by reading the migration.
- resolve_review_accept (playlists_repository.py:1040-1065) calls upsert_vendor_match (repositories.py:1037-1068) which does INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (clouder_track_id, vendor) DO UPDATE SET vendor_track_id/match_type/confidence/payload — a genuine overwrite of the shared row, set to match_type='manual', confidence=1.000.
- fetch_ytmusic_status (playlists_repository.py:963-1020) reads vendor_track_map/match_review_queue with no user scope.
- clouder_tracks is a shared canonical table (init schema 20260301_01:112-127, no user_id). The same beatport-origin track_id can appear in multiple users' playlist_tracks. _scope_check (curation_handler.py:315-320) passes for any user who has that shared track in their own playlist/category/import (_SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE lines 82-104).

CONCRETE PATH (reproducible): admin ingest creates shared track T -> users A and B each add T to their own playlist -> A calls POST playlists/{A_pid}/tracks/{T}/match {action:accept,vendor_track_id:X} -> _scope_check passes -> ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE overwrites vendor_track_map(T,'ytmusic') to X/1.000 -> B's list_tracks/fetch_ytmusic_status reads the same global row and shows X. So the described overwrite genuinely happens.

WHY REFUTED (severity/framing wrong): ADR-0002 (docs/adr/0002-multi-tenant-overlay.md:21) explicitly states "Enrichment results (ai_search_results, vendor_track_map) are also shared." The shared, global nature of vendor_track_map is an ACCEPTED, DOCUMENTED architectural decision, not an oversight or leak. It deliberately mirrors the ingest-time auto-match (which is also global and overwrites the same row via the same upsert). The finding's own risk text concedes "blast radius is limited" for the "small trusted DJ circle" (also documented in ADR-0002: "small, trusted DJ circle"), and its own recommendation offers "document it as accepted shared state (it mirrors the ingest-time auto match)" as a valid resolution — which the ADR already does. There is no data corruption (the new value is itself a valid match), no data loss, and the finding explicitly states it is NOT an IDOR (gated by _scope_check). What remains is a by-design UX observation (one trusted user can change a shared match another trusted user sees, with no audit trail). That is a P3-level note at most, not a P2 DATA finding. The finding is not false in mechanics, but its premise of unintended cross-tenant leakage is contradicted by the codebase's own ADR.

title: _scope_check validates the track is in the user's overall scope, not in the specific playlist named in the URL
where: src/collector/curation_handler.py:315-320 (_scope_check), used by _handle_match_candidates (323-339) and _handle_resolve_match (349-385); route POST /playlists/{id}/tracks/{track_id}/match-resolve
evidence: _scope_check confirms (1) repo.get(user_id=user_id, playlist_id=pid) is not None (playlist belongs to caller) and (2) the track is in validate_tracks_in_scope (any of the user's categories/playlists/imported tracks, playlists_repository.py:82-104). It never asserts the track is actually a member of playlist {id}. The {id} path param is therefore only an ownership token, not a real constraint linking the track to that playlist.
risk: No cross-tenant breach (both checks are user-scoped), but the route's contract is misleading: a caller can resolve a YT Music match for any track in their library via any playlist they own, even if that track is not in that playlist. Combined with the global vendor_track_map (finding above) this widens who can trigger a shared-state write. Low impact for the MVP audience.
recommendation: Either drop {id} from the match-resolve/candidates routes (they are effectively track-scoped, not playlist-scoped) or add a membership check that the track exists in playlist_tracks for {id} so the URL semantics match enforcement.

It never checks that track_id is a member of playlist pid.

validate_tracks_in_scope (src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py:828-844) runs _SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE (lines 82-104), which keys every EXISTS branch on :user_id only. The playlist branch (lines 92-98) is `JOIN playlists p ... WHERE p.user_id = :user_id AND p.deleted_at IS NULL` — it matches ANY of the user's playlists, never the specific pid. The handlers (_handle_match_candidates:323-339, _handle_resolve_match:349-385) and the actual write (resolve_review_accept:1040-1065 / get_open_review:1024) are keyed solely on track_id+vendor; pid is used only for a log field (line 382), never in any query or guard. So the {id} path param is purely an ownership token, not a real constraint linking the track to that playlist — exactly as claimed. Risk framing is also accurate: both checks are user-scoped, so no cross-tenant breach; the issue is misleading route semantics (a user can resolve a match for any track in their own library via any playlist they own). Only inaccuracy is a trivial path imprecision in the citation (file is at src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py, written as playlists_repository.py), which does not affect substance. Finding is technically accurate: _scope_check (curation_handler.py:315-320) verifies the playlist {id} belongs to the caller and the track is in the user's overall scope (validate_tracks_in_scope: any owned category/playlist/imported track, playlists_repository.py:82-104), but never asserts the track is a member of playlist {id}. The {id} param is an ownership token, not a real constraint. However, the impact does not matter for this MVP: (1) No cross-tenant breach — both checks are user-scoped, as the finding admits. Confirmed accurate and reproducible. _scope_check (src/collector/curation_handler.py:315-320) performs exactly two checks: (1) repo.get(user_id, playlist_id=pid) which runs `WHERE p.id = :id AND p.user_id = :user_id AND p.deleted_at IS NULL` (playlists_repository.py:268-275) — pure ownership token, no join to playlist_tracks; and (2) validate_tracks_in_scope, whose SQL (_SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE, src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py:82-104) checks the track is in ANY of the user's categories OR ANY of the user's playlists OR the user's imports, scoped only by :user_id. title: Concurrent triage move_tracks unconditionally re-inserts into target, duplicating tracks across buckets
where: src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py:448-494 (move_tracks); INSERT at :483-492 inserts the full track_id_list, not the rows actually deleted
evidence: The presence SELECT (:448) and the status/inactive guard (:399-433) run OUTSIDE the transaction; only DELETE+INSERT are wrapped (:465). The DELETE is `WHERE triage_bucket_id=:from_id AND track_id IN (...)` and the INSERT into to_bucket is unconditional over the full track_id_list with `ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING`. The PK is (triage_bucket_id, track_id) so the same track may live in multiple buckets of one block (no per-block uniqueness, alembic/versions/20260428_15_triage.py:137).
risk: Two concurrent moves of the same track from one source bucket to different targets (A: from→X, B: from→Y) both pass the out-of-tx presence check; A deletes+inserts to X, B's DELETE is a no-op but B still inserts to Y. The track now exists in BOTH X and Y. Bucket track_counts inflate, and finalize promotes the track into multiple categories. `moved` is also over-reported. Same TOCTOU lets a move land after a concurrent finalize flips the block (guard read outside tx).
recommendation: Move the guard and presence SELECT inside the transaction; make the INSERT insert only the rows the DELETE actually removed (e.g. DELETE ... RETURNING track_id, then INSERT those), or add a block-scoped uniqueness/move via UPDATE of triage_bucket_id with a single statement.

votes.reason:
Verified every load-bearing claim against the cited code; the finding is mechanically correct and the severity is justified.

CODE CONFIRMED (src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py):
- move_tracks guard SELECT (lines 399-433) and presence SELECT (lines 448-456) run OUTSIDE any transaction — neither passes transaction_id. Only the DELETE+INSERT (lines 466-491) are wrapped in `with self._data_api.transaction()` (line 465).
- DELETE is `WHERE triage_bucket_id=:from_id AND track_id IN (...)` (lines 468-470).
- INSERT is unconditional over the FULL track_id_list, not the deleted rows: line 476 builds value rows from `range(len(track_id_list))`, with `ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING` (line 488). The ON CONFLICT dedupes only within one bucket, not across buckets of a block.
- `moved` is returned as `len(track_id_list)` unconditionally (line 494), so it over-reports when the DELETE was a no-op — also as claimed.

SCHEMA CONFIRMED (alembic/versions/20260428_15_triage.py:137): triage_bucket_tracks PK is (triage_bucket_id, track_id). No constraint forces a track to live in only one bucket per block, so the same track can sit in two buckets simultaneously.

CONCURRENCY VALID: data_api.begin_transaction sets no isolation level → Aurora/Data API default READ COMMITTED. In the A:(from→X) / B:(from→Y) race, both pass the out-of-tx presence check; A deletes from `from` and inserts to X; B's DELETE is a no-op but B still inserts to Y. Crucially B never reads `from` INSIDE its transaction (the check is outside), so even raising isolation to SERIALIZABLE would not catch this — there is no read/write dependency to serialize. Result: track in both X and Y. No application-level locking exists (curation_handler.py:_move_tracks is a thin wrapper; no advisory locks anywhere in curation/).

DOWNSTREAM IMPACT CONFIRMED (finalize_block, lines 667-710): finalize iterates each active STAGING bucket and promotes its tracks into that bucket's category via add_tracks_bulk. category_tracks conflict key is (category_id, track_id) — promoting the same track into two different categories yields two valid rows, no conflict. So a cross-bucket duplicate becomes genuine, persistent corruption of the user-facing curated output (track in two categories), plus inflated bucket track_counts. The status-guard-outside-tx also creates a real TOCTOU vs a concurrent finalize.

SEVERITY: P1 is appropriate. Silent, persistent data-integrity corruption of the product's curated output, no constraint to catch it, no error surfaced. Trigger requires concurrent same-track moves by a single user — realistic given the tap-button mobile+desktop curation UX, double-taps, multi-tab/multi-device, and client retries — which keeps it at P1 (real but not trivially mass-triggered) rather than P0. No basis to downgrade.

The TOCTOU mechanism is technically real: the block/bucket guard (triage_repository.py:399-433) and presence SELECT (:448-456) run outside the transaction, while the INSERT (:483-492) is unconditional over the full track_id_list with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING rather than inserting only the rows the DELETE removed. So two concurrent moves of the same track (from→X and from→Y) could leave it in both buckets. BUT this has no plausible trigger in this MVP:

1. Single-user scope. A triage block is owned by one user (tb.user_id = :user_id in the guard). The only way to race A(from→X) and B(from→Y) for the same track is the SAME human firing two conflicting moves within the Aurora Data API begin→delete→insert→commit window (tens of ms).

2. The UI makes this practically impossible. Moves are per-track-row dropdown clicks (MoveToMenu.tsx; the menu closes on selection), and useMoveTracks.onMutate synchronously removes the track from the source-bucket view (applyOptimisticMove filters it out). Once moved to X, the row disappears from the source bucket — there is no affordance to immediately re-move that same track to Y.

3. No bulk/automated path. The 'quick distribute' path (useBucketDistribute.tsx) moves only the single currently-playing track per click and advances to the successor each time, so it never re-targets the same track. There is no server-side or client-side path that fires overlapping same-track moves.

4. Audience is a small DJ circle on their own per-user blocks; organic concurrency on one user's own block is effectively nil.

Triggering this would require deliberately crafted racing API calls, not normal use — a purely theoretical TOCTOU. Per the rubric (theoretical issues with no plausible trigger → refuted), this is refuted. The data-corruption framing overstates it; at most it is a P3 hardening item (move the guard/presence SELECT into the transaction and DELETE ... RETURNING) for a future multi-device concurrent-edit scenario.

CONFIRMED — not refuted. Every load-bearing claim checks out against real code. (1) The guard SELECT (triage_repository.py:399-433) and presence SELECT (:448-456) run OUTSIDE the transaction; only DELETE (:466-474) + INSERT (:483-492) are wrapped in self._data_api.transaction() (:465). (2) The INSERT builds VALUES rows from the full track_id_list (:475-481) and is unconditional with ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING — it does NOT key off what DELETE removed; the happy-path test (test_triage_repository.py:572-626) confirms this shape. (3) PK is (triage_bucket_id, track_id) per migration line 137 — per-bucket, not per-block. No EXCLUDE constraint, trigger, advisory lock, or SELECT...FOR UPDATE exists (grep clean) to prevent a track from living in two buckets of one block. (4) move_tracks returns moved=len(track_id_list) (:494) regardless of actual DELETE rowcount, so 'moved' over-reports. (5) finalize_block (:682-710) iterates each STAGING bucket and promotes its tracks into that bucket's category_id, so a track duplicated across buckets X(cat C1) and Y(cat C2) is promoted into BOTH categories.

Reproducible path: POST /triage/blocks/{id}/move (route at curation_handler.py:1911 -> _move_tracks :1444 -> repo.move_tracks). Schema (curation/schemas.py:63) permits arbitrary from/to bucket pairs. Precondition: block IN_PROGRESS, track T in bucket 'from', two distinct STAGING buckets X and Y. Two concurrent requests A(from->X,[T]) and B(from->Y,[T]) land in separate Lambda invocations (no shared lock). Aurora defaults to READ COMMITTED; both pass the out-of-tx presence check seeing T in 'from'. Tx A deletes T from 'from' and inserts (X,T). Tx B's DELETE is a no-op (T already gone) but its INSERT (Y,T) still runs unconditionally and succeeds (different bucket, no PK conflict). T now lives in both X and Y; counts inflate; finalize promotes T into both C1 and C2; B reports moved=1 falsely. The fix (DELETE ... RETURNING then insert only returned rows inside the tx, or a single UPDATE of triage_bucket_id) is correct.

Severity P1 is appropriate: silent cross-category data corruption that survives finalize into the user's curated library, with no error surfaced. The only mitigating factor is the narrow trigger (two near-simultaneous moves of the same track to different targets within one block — plausible via double-click/retry, multi-tab, or mobile+desktop for a single user), which could argue P2, but the high, silent, persistent data-integrity impact keeps it at P1.

title: transfer_tracks performs multi-step validate-then-insert with no transaction
where: src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py:496-600 (transfer_tracks) — three separate execute() calls (:504, :521, :564, :590), none wrapped in self._data_api.transaction()
evidence: Unlike move_tracks/finalize_block/create_block which open `with self._data_api.transaction()`, transfer_tracks issues the source-block lookup, target-bucket guard, presence check, and the final INSERT...ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING as independent auto-committed statements. The broad Data API retry (data_api_retry.py) can also replay the final INSERT.
risk: The block-status/inactive checks and the presence check can be invalidated by a concurrent finalize/soft-delete between the guard and the INSERT, allowing tracks to be written into a bucket of a block that just became FINALIZED or whose target category was just inactivated. No atomic rollback if the process dies mid-sequence.
recommendation: Wrap the guard reads + presence check + INSERT in a single transaction (thread transaction_id through all four execute calls), mirroring move_tracks.

votes.reason:
Code description is factually accurate: transfer_tracks (lines 504,521,564,590) issues four auto-committed execute() calls with no transaction_id, while move_tracks/finalize_block/create_block use `with self._data_api.transaction()`. But the finding's risk narrative and recommendation are flawed, so the P2 DATA rating is overstated.

(1) The recommendation ("mirror move_tracks") misreads move_tracks. move_tracks keeps its guard read AND presence check OUTSIDE its transaction (lines 399-463); only the DELETE+INSERT pair is inside (465-492). It uses a transaction solely to make its TWO mutations atomic (don't delete from source without inserting to target). transfer_tracks is a pure additive copy with a SINGLE mutation (one INSERT, no DELETE) — there is no multi-statement atomicity to protect, so the "mirror move_tracks" premise doesn't apply.

(2) The retry-replay concern is the documented, intended safe pattern, not an oversight. data_api_retry.py's docstring (lines 47-52) states writes outside transactions are safe to retry when they use "UPSERT/ON CONFLICT semantics" — and the final INSERT uses `ON CONFLICT (...) DO NOTHING`. Worst case of a replay is the RETURNING-based `transferred` count under-reporting, a cosmetic inaccuracy, not corruption.

(3) The stated concurrency (TOCTOU) race would NOT be closed by the recommended plain-transaction wrapper. RDS Data API transactions run at the DB default isolation (READ COMMITTED on Aurora/Postgres); a guard SELECT in that transaction does not block a concurrent finalize/inactivate UPDATE. Closing the race needs SELECT...FOR UPDATE / serializable, which the recommendation never mentions — so the proposed fix is insufficient for the stated risk. The same TOCTOU window exists in move_tracks (guard read outside its transaction), the very function held up as the correct model.

(4) Blast radius is one benign, idempotent extra row in triage_bucket_tracks in an edge-case race; no destructive op, no cross-table inconsistency, and a mid-sequence process death leaves at most one auto-committed idempotent INSERT (fully consistent). This is a minor robustness nit (P3), not a P2 data-integrity defect, and the recommendation as written wouldn't fix the headline risk.

Structurally accurate (four un-transactioned execute() calls), but both risk mechanisms collapse under scrutiny. (1) Retry-replay angle is refuted outright: data_api_retry.py's documented IDEMPOTENCY CONTRACT explicitly sanctions "UPSERT/ON CONFLICT semantics for writes outside transactions" — and the final INSERT uses ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING, making any replay a no-op by design, not a defect. (2) The concurrency race is technically real but immaterial here. The finding misreads move_tracks: move_tracks ALSO runs its guard + presence check outside the transaction (lines 400-463) and wraps only the DELETE+INSERT mutation pair — the transaction protects two-statement mutation atomicity, not guard-vs-mutation. transfer_tracks is a single additive INSERT with no DELETE, so there is no multi-statement state to make atomic and nothing to roll back if the process dies. Both racing operations (finalize_block, mark_staging_inactive_for_category) are scoped AND user_id = :user_id — no cross-tenant/admin path. Triggering the race requires one DJ firing two concurrent mutating requests against their own block in a sub-millisecond window: implausible for a small DJ circle clicking a UI. Worst case is benign: a few stale rows in the triage_bucket_tracks STAGING table; the FK to triage_buckets.id still holds (soft-delete is a flag flip, the bucket row persists), no constraint breaks, no track is lost, and canonical published data is untouched. Theoretical race, no plausible trigger, cosmetic-at-worst outcome.

The factual code observation is TRUE and reproducible: POST /triage/blocks/{src_id}/transfer → _transfer_tracks (curation_handler.py:1476, route at :1912) → transfer_tracks (triage_repository.py:496-600) issues four independent auto-committed execute() calls (:504, :521, :564, :590) with no transaction() wrapper, unlike finalize_block. However, the finding's risk and severity are overstated, and its core recommendation does not fix its own stated race. (1) Recommendation is technically wrong: Aurora PostgreSQL runs READ COMMITTED (verified — no FOR UPDATE, no elevated isolation anywhere in src/collector). Wrapping the guard SELECT + presence check + INSERT in one transaction gives atomic rollback but does NOT serialize against a concurrent finalize_block: the guard read and INSERT remain separate statements, the committed status flip is visible, and no lock is taken on the block row. The described FINALIZED/inactivation race would survive the recommended fix; only SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the block row or SERIALIZABLE would close it. (2) The 'unlike move_tracks' contrast is false: move_tracks ALSO runs its status/inactive guard (triage_repository.py:399-433) and presence check (:448) OUTSIDE its transaction; only DELETE+INSERT are wrapped (:465). So 'mirror move_tracks' would still leave the guard reads non-transactional. (3) The retry-replay concern is neutralized by design: data_api_retry.py's idempotency contract (lines 47-52) mandates ON CONFLICT for non-transactional writes, and the INSERT uses ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING (:595); a replay is harmless to data (worst case off-by-N transferred count via RETURNING). The genuine residual issue is a rare two-user interleaving (frontend disables the transfer modal while pending and has 422/409 fallbacks per the F3b/F4 specs) producing an orphan triage_bucket_tracks row in a just-FINALIZED block — a missed-promotion robustness gap, not data corruption (finalize snapshot-copies and does not delete triage rows; transfer_tracks is insert-only so a mid-sequence process death leaves nothing half-written). Real but minor: P3, not P2.

title: Spotify import is non-atomic: per-track upsert + separate append, partial failure leaves orphaned imported tracks
where: src/collector/curation_handler.py:1063-1145 (_handle_import_spotify); repo.upsert_imported_track called per-track in a loop (:1103, each its own tx in playlists_repository.py:864) then repo.append_tracks once (:1124, a separate tx)
evidence: Each upsert_imported_track opens its own transaction inserting clouder_tracks + user_imported_tracks. The playlist append_tracks runs in a later, independent transaction. A Spotify get_track failure mid-loop (:1094) or a Lambda timeout (30s, infra/curation.tf) between the import loop and append leaves clouder_tracks/user_imported_tracks rows committed but never added to the playlist.
risk: Orphaned spotify_user_import rows accumulate in clouder_tracks (no cleanup path; FK ondelete on user_imported_tracks is CASCADE only from users). They permanently widen the user's track scope (validate_tracks_in_scope EXISTS on user_imported_tracks) and silently bloat the catalogue. Client sees a 5xx with no record of what was imported.
recommendation: Accept partial import as the documented contract OR wrap the import+append in one transaction; at minimum return the imported-but-not-appended track_ids so the client can retry idempotently.

votes.reason: Verified the cited code; the finding's substance holds. In _handle_import_spotify (src/collector/curation_handler.py:1092-1127), repo.upsert_imported_track is called per-track inside the loop and each call opens its own transaction (src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py:864, `with self._data_api.transaction()` — committing clouder_tracks + user_imported_tracks rows independently). repo.append_tracks runs once afterward in a separate transaction (same file, line 416, called at handler line 1124). The loop only catches SpotifyNotFoundError (handler:1095); any other Spotify failure — SpotifyRateLimitedError, SpotifyApiError (5xx after one retry, or unexpected status), SpotifyNotAuthorizedError (401), SpotifyScopeInsufficientError (403) per spotify_user_client.py:185-216 — propagates and aborts the loop after earlier tracks are already committed, so append_tracks never runs. A 30s Lambda timeout (infra/variables.tf:421 default 30) between the loop and append produces the same outcome. validate_tracks_in_scope uses an EXISTS on user_imported_tracks (playlists_repository.py:100-102), so orphaned imports permanently widen the user's track scope, and grep confirms no DELETE path for clouder_tracks/user_imported_tracks anywhere in src — no cleanup, matching the finding's risk. Two minor location/detail inaccuracies that do NOT change the conclusion: (a) the repo file path is src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py, not src/collector/playlists_repository.py, and the line numbers (864/408 vs the finding's 864/... and :864) are slightly off; (b) the risk claims the user_imported_tracks FK is 'CASCADE only from users', but the migration (alembic/versions/20260512_19_playlists.py:112-119) shows BOTH FKs (to users AND to clouder_tracks) are ondelete=CASCADE. That cascade direction (clouder_tracks→user_imported_tracks) is irrelevant to cleanup because nothing ever deletes the clouder_tracks rows, so the orphan-accumulation mechanism stands. Severity P2 is appropriate: a genuine non-atomicity defect with a real but slow-accumulating, low-blast-radius consequence (catalogue bloat + scope widening on a partial-failure path), no data loss, and the client does receive a 5xx so the failure is not fully silent.

Technical claims are accurate: upsert_imported_track runs per-track in its own transaction (playlists_repository.py:864) and append_tracks is a separate transaction (curation_handler.py:1124), so a Lambda timeout or unhandled Spotify error between the loop and the append leaves clouder_tracks/user_imported_tracks rows committed but not in any playlist. But the impact is materially over-stated for this MVP, so the severity is wrong rather than the finding being valid at P2.

Mitigating facts: (1) The operation is idempotent and self-healing. upsert_imported_track does SELECT-first dedup on spotify_id (line 866) and user_imported_tracks INSERT uses ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (line 905). A client retry of the same import resolves to the same clouder_tracks.id and appends it — no per-retry row accumulation, contradicting the 'orphaned rows accumulate' framing. (2) The 'widened scope' is per-user and scoped to the importing user (validate_tracks_in_scope filters uit.user_id = :user_id); the orphaned marker is the user's own deliberately-imported track, never visible in another tenant's scope. No cross-tenant leak. (3) No real catalogue bloat: orphaned spotify_user_import rows carry a non-null spotify_id, so they are excluded from the admin Spotify-backfill/search views (which filter spotify_id IS NULL, repositories.py:772-795) and surface in no shared browse path. (4) Mid-loop get_track 404s are already handled gracefully (per-track skipped); the only non-atomic gap is a timeout/unhandled error, rare for a small DJ circle importing modest batches, and the success path already returns the added track_ids.

Net: a handful of harmless, per-user, retry-resolvable orphaned rows with no functional or cross-tenant consequence. This is a data-tidiness/documentation nicety, not a P2 data-integrity defect. Correct severity is P3.

Verified against real code; could not refute. Concrete reproducible path: POST /playlists/{id}/tracks/import-spotify (route registered at curation_handler.py:1945) with a body of several valid spotify_refs on an owned, existing playlist. In _handle_import_spotify (curation_handler.py:1063), the per-ref loop (1092-1121) calls repo.upsert_imported_track for each resolved track. That method (curation/playlists_repository.py:846, NOT the path the finding cites — real path is src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py) opens its OWN transaction (line 864) and commits clouder_tracks + user_imported_tracks per track. repo.append_tracks runs only afterward (1123) in a fully separate transaction (playlists_repository.py:416). The dispatcher (lambda_handler:466) wraps the handler in no transaction. The loop catches ONLY SpotifyNotFoundError (1095); any other Spotify failure mid-loop — SpotifyRateLimitedError, SpotifyApiError (5xx-after-retries or 403), SpotifyNotAuthorizedError, SpotifyScopeInsufficientError (spotify_user_client.py:186-216) — propagates to the generic except Exception (curation_handler.py:478) and returns HTTP 500 (line 488). A 30s Lambda timeout (infra/variables.tf:421, default=30; 29s API GW wall) between loop and append produces the identical state. Already-committed user_imported_tracks rows then persist: no DELETE/cleanup path exists anywhere in src (grep confirmed), the user_id FK is ondelete=CASCADE only from users and track_id FK CASCADE only from clouder_tracks (db_models.py:676-695), neither of which ever fires here. Those orphan rows permanently widen the user's track scope via the EXISTS on user_imported_tracks in _SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE (playlists_repository.py:99-102) even though the tracks were never appended, and the client gets an opaque 500 with no list of what was imported. Minor inaccuracy in the finding: the file path omits the /curation/ segment, and 'each its own tx in playlists_repository.py:864' line ref is correct only in the corrected path — these are cosmetic and do not affect validity.

title: Playlist remove_track conflates 'track not in playlist' with 'playlist not found' (404)
where: src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py:498-539 returns False both when playlist missing and when track absent; handler maps False→PlaylistNotFoundError at src/collector/curation_handler.py:943-947
evidence: remove_track returns False at :523 when the track row is absent (playlist exists) and the handler raises PlaylistNotFoundError('Playlist or track not found') for that same False. The optimistic client (useRemoveTrackFromPlaylist) cannot distinguish an idempotent no-op (already removed) from a genuinely missing playlist, unlike the categories path which treats 404 track_not_in_category as idempotent success.
risk: Idempotent re-removal (e.g. retried mutation, double-tap) returns a 404 that the client surfaces as an error and may roll back the optimistic shrink, producing a confusing UI state. No data corruption.
recommendation: Return distinct outcomes (playlist-missing vs track-absent) and have the handler treat track-absent as 204/idempotent, matching the categories remove semantics.

votes.reason:
Verified against the cited code. The behavioral chain holds: src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py:522-523 returns False when the playlist exists but the track row is absent; the handler at src/collector/curation_handler.py:946-947 maps that False to PlaylistNotFoundError("Playlist or track not found") -> HTTP 404 with error_code "playlist_not_found". The playlist optimistic hook frontend/src/features/playlists/hooks/useRemoveTrackFromPlaylist.ts:23-25 has no idempotency catch, so the 404 propagates to onError (lines 40-43) and rolls back the optimistic shrink. The claimed contrast is accurate: the categories optimistic hook frontend/src/features/categories/hooks/useRemoveTrackOptimistic.ts:48-49 explicitly treats a 404 track_not_in_category as idempotent success (no rollback), and there is a regression test asserting this (useRemoveTrackOptimistic.test.tsx:73). So a retried/double-tap track removal on a playlist surfaces a 404 error and rolls back, where the categories path absorbs it cleanly — a real cross-path inconsistency with UX impact and no data corruption.

One imprecision in the finding's wording: the repo does NOT "return False both when playlist missing and when track absent" — a missing playlist RAISES PlaylistNotFoundError() directly at playlists_repository.py:513-514, and only the track-absent case returns False (line 523). However, the net user-facing result is an identical 404 (error_code playlist_not_found) in both cases, so the conflation the finding's risk describes (client cannot distinguish idempotent no-op from genuinely-missing playlist) is real. This imprecision does not change behavior or risk and is insufficient to refute. Severity P3 is appropriate: minor UX inconsistency, no data integrity impact.

Refuted on both premise and impact.

PREMISE IS FALSE: The finding's central claim — that the categories remove path "treats 404 track_not_in_category as idempotent success," unlike playlists — is factually wrong. The categories path is IDENTICAL to playlists. categories_repository.py:650 sets `deleted = bool(rows)` (False when the track is absent but the category exists), and curation_handler.py:749-750 maps that False to `raise NotFoundError("track_not_in_category")` = a 404. This is exactly what the playlist path does (playlists_repository.py:523 returns False; curation_handler.py:946-947 raises PlaylistNotFoundError = 404). Both subsystems return 404 for an idempotent re-removal and both distinguish playlist/category-missing by raising directly in the repo (playlists :514, categories :638). There is no asymmetry. The recommendation to "match the categories remove semantics" would change nothing, because categories does not have the idempotent-success semantics the finding attributes to it.

TRIGGER NOT PLAUSIBLE: react-query mutations are configured `retry: false` (frontend/src/lib/queryClient.ts:17), so there is no automatic retried mutation. The optimistic onMutate filters the removed track out of `items` immediately (useRemoveTrackFromPlaylist.ts:34), so the row disappears from the list on the first tap and a normal double-tap on the same track is not reachable via the UI.

NEGLIGIBLE IMPACT: Even in a rare race, onError rolls back the optimistic shrink and shows a generic error toast, then onSuccess/invalidation refetches and reconciles to true server state. The finding concedes there is no data corruption. For this MVP (small DJ circle), a 404 on an already-removed track is a cosmetic API-semantics nicety with a false comparative basis and no realistic trigger.

Severity unchanged (P3 is already the actionable floor; if anything this is below the bar).

Confirmed and reproducible. remove_track at src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py:516-523 returns False when the playlist exists/is owned but the track row is absent (pos_rows empty); the handler at src/collector/curation_handler.py:946-947 maps that False to PlaylistNotFoundError("Playlist or track not found"). PlaylistNotFoundError (src/collector/curation/__init__.py:162-164) always emits {error_code:"playlist_not_found", http_status:404}, so the client receives an identical 404 whether the playlist is missing or the track is merely absent — they are genuinely indistinguishable.

Concrete path: DELETE /playlists/{id}/tracks/{track_id} on an owned, existing playlist where the track was already removed (retried mutation, double-tap, or a second tab/device). First DELETE returns 204; second finds no row → 404 playlist_not_found. The frontend api() throws ApiError on the non-ok response (frontend/src/api/client.ts:77). useRemoveTrackFromPlaylist (frontend/src/features/playlists/hooks/useRemoveTrackFromPlaylist.ts) has NO idempotency guard, so onError (lines 40-43) restores ctx.previousTracks, rolling back the optimistic shrink — the removed row visibly reappears — and PlaylistDetailPage.tsx:153-154 shows a red generic_error toast. This is exactly the confusing UI state described.

Asymmetry verified: the categories backend returns a distinct error_code "track_not_in_category" (src/collector/curation_handler.py:749-750) which useRemoveTrackOptimistic.ts:48-49 explicitly swallows as idempotent success; the playlist path has no such distinct code or guard.

One minor wording inaccuracy in the finding: "the categories path treats 404 track_not_in_category as idempotent success" describes the frontend hook, not the backend handler (which still raises the 404). This does not affect the validity — the root cause (playlist backend lacks a distinct track-absent error code) and the user-visible impact are real. No data corruption: the DB end-state is correct after the first delete; only the transient UI is affected, and it requires a retry/double-action. P3 is the correct severity.

title: validate_tracks_in_scope runs in a separate statement from append_tracks (TOCTOU on playlist scope)
where: src/collector/curation_handler.py:907-918 (_handle_add_playlist_tracks): validate_tracks_in_scope (:907) and repo.append_tracks (:915) are independent calls; scope SQL at playlists_repository.py:828-844, append at :408-496
evidence: The scope check (EXISTS over category_tracks/playlist_tracks/user_imported_tracks) is an untransacted SELECT; append_tracks then opens its own transaction. Between them a concurrent category/playlist delete could remove the only basis for a track being in-scope.
risk: A track could be appended to a playlist a few ms after losing its in-scope basis. Low impact: scope only governs which catalogue tracks a user may reference, and the track still exists; no cross-tenant leak (user_id is bound in both queries). Mainly a correctness footgun.
recommendation: If strict scope enforcement matters, fold the scope check into the append_tracks transaction; otherwise document it as best-effort.

votes.reason: Verified against the actual code. The TOCTOU described is real and correctly characterized. In _handle_add_playlist_tracks (curation_handler.py:907-918), repo.validate_tracks_in_scope and repo.append_tracks are two independent calls. validate_tracks_in_scope (playlists_repository.py:828-844) issues a standalone SELECT with NO transaction_id — an untransacted autocommit read against the _SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE (lines 82-104: EXISTS over category_tracks/playlist_tracks/user_imported_tracks). append_tracks (lines 408-496) then opens its own transaction via `with self._data_api.transaction() as tx_id`. So a concurrent same-user delete of the sole in-scope basis can land in the gap, and the track is still appended. The finding's risk text is accurate: user_id is bound in both the scope SQL (:user_id in all three EXISTS subqueries) and in append_tracks' owner check (WHERE id=:id AND user_id=:user_id), so there is no cross-tenant leak; the track still exists in clouder_tracks so no FK/orphan issue. It is a low-impact, same-user, self-inflicted correctness footgun. P3 is appropriate. Note: the cited file path omits the `curation/` segment — actual path is src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py — but the cited line numbers (828-844, 408-496, 82-104) all match exactly, so this is a cosmetic path error that does not undermine the finding.

Technically accurate but immaterial for this MVP. Confirmed at src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py: validate_tracks_in_scope (:828-844, untransacted SELECT) and append_tracks (:408-496, own transaction via _data_api.transaction()) are indeed separate statements, so a delete between them could append a track that just lost its in-scope basis. However the impact is benign: (1) No integrity violation — the INSERT into playlist_tracks references clouder_tracks.id (the canonical track still exists), so no dangling row, no FK failure, no crash. (2) No cross-tenant leak — user_id is bound in both the scope SQL (c.user_id/p.user_id/uit.user_id = :user_id at :90,96,101) and the ownership check in append (id = :id AND user_id = :user_id at :419). (3) All three scope branches reference the SAME user's own data (their categories, playlists, imports), so the only way to trigger the race is the same user concurrently deleting the scoping basis while adding the same track — for a small DJ circle the realistic trigger is a double-click, not an adversarial race, and the worst outcome is a track the user could see moments ago lands in their own playlist. Zero data corruption, zero security consequence. The finding itself concedes "Low impact... no cross-tenant leak... Mainly a correctness footgun." No plausible harmful trigger for this MVP — refuted.

Confirmed against real code; the TOCTOU race is genuine and reproducible. In _handle_add_playlist_tracks (curation_handler.py:907-918), repo.validate_tracks_in_scope() runs as an autonomous, untransacted SELECT (playlists_repository.py:828-844 → self._data_api.execute with no transaction_id), and repo.append_tracks() (playlists_repository.py:408-496) then opens its own separate self._data_api.transaction(). They are independent sequential calls.

Concrete path: a single user issues two concurrent API requests — Request A: POST /playlists/{id}/tracks adding track T whose only in-scope basis is, say, membership in one of the user's categories; Request B: deletes that category (removing the category_tracks rows). The scope SQL (_SCOPE_CHECK_SQL_TEMPLATE, :82-104) checks three EXISTS clauses (category_tracks, non-deleted playlist_tracks, user_imported_tracks), all bound by :user_id. If B's commit lands between A's scope SELECT and A's append transaction, T gets appended after losing its in-scope basis. The race window is real.

Severity correctly P3 (lowest). The finding's own impact assessment holds: (1) curation is strictly per-user and user_id is bound in BOTH queries, so there is no cross-tenant leak; (2) the track row in clouder_tracks still exists — only an association was removed, so no orphaned FK or data corruption; (3) the only victim is the same user racing their own requests, and the outcome (a playlist referencing a track whose scope basis just vanished) is self-inflicted, recoverable, and cosmetic. This is a correctness footgun, not a data-integrity or security defect.

Minor inaccuracy that does not refute: the cited path 'data/playlists_repository.py' is wrong — the actual file is src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py — but the quoted line numbers (82-104, 408-496, 828-844) and code content match exactly, so the finding remains actionable.

title: Enrichment worker re-spends ALL paid vendor calls on SQS redrive (no idempotency gate before vendor fan-out)
evidence: The SQS worker calls enrich_label_for_run / enrich_artist_for_run with NO try/except (label_enrichment_handler.py:114, artist_enrichment_handler.py:102). Inside, run_vendors_parallel executes the paid Gemini + OpenAI + Tavily(2 Tavily HTTP + 1 DeepSeek) + DeepSeek-merge calls UNCONDITIONALLY (orchestrator.py:93 then merge_cells at :117). All persistence — insert_cell, upsert_label_info, increment_run_counters — happens AFTER the paid calls (orchestrator.py:105-134). insert_cell has ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING (repository.py:713) which protects the DB row but does NOT gate the already-completed paid call. There is no 'cells already exist for this run+label?' check before run_vendors_parallel, and mark_run_running (repository.py:731) is a no-op when already 'running' but does not short-circuit the vendor calls. Any exception during persistence (Data API ThrottlingException/StatementTimeout, JSON encode, etc.) OR a Lambda timeout (not a Python exception, so the visibility timeout simply re-exposes the message) raises/redrives the message.
risk: Every redrive re-incurs the full vendor spend for that label/artist (~6 upstream LLM/search calls for the default 3-vendor set: gemini/openai/tavily_deepseek + DeepSeek narrative merge). With label/artist queue maxReceiveCount=3 (infra/variables.tf:452,505), a label whose persistence keeps failing pays up to 3x the full vendor cost before reaching the DLQ. A transient Aurora throttle or a 900s timeout window where vendors finished but writes did not turns into duplicate paid LLM/Tavily/DeepSeek billing across the whole batch of claimed items.
recommendation: Make the worker idempotent BEFORE spending: at the top of enrich_*_for_run, check whether cells already exist for (run_id, label_id) (e.g. SELECT 1 FROM clouder_label_enrichment_cells WHERE run_id=:r AND label_id=:l) or whether clouder_label_info.last_run_id already equals this run, and skip the vendor fan-out if so. Alternatively wrap enrich_*_for_run in try/except that, on persistence failure after vendor calls, persists what it can and does NOT re-raise (so the message is not redriven). Lowering maxReceiveCount to 1 for the enrichment queues would also cap re-spend.

votes.reason:
Verified against the cited code — every claim holds.

Handler (label_enrichment_handler.py:114, artist_enrichment_handler.py:102): the worker calls enrich_label_for_run / enrich_artist_for_run inside the per-record loop with NO try/except. Any exception from the call propagates out of lambda_handler, fails the SQS batch (no ReportBatchItemFailures/partial-batch config — confirmed absent in infra), and redrives the message.

Orchestrator (orchestrator.py:91-134 label, 90-125 artist): order is exactly as described — repository.mark_run_running(run_id) (a status-only UPDATE ... WHERE status='queued', repository.py:731-740, no short-circuit), then run_vendors_parallel (the paid Gemini/OpenAI/Tavily+DeepSeek fan-out) at :93/:92 UNCONDITIONALLY, then merge_cells at :117/:113 (paid DeepSeek narrative merge), then ALL persistence (insert_cell, upsert_*_info, project_ai_suspected, increment_run_counters) at :105-134 / :104-125 — strictly AFTER the spend. There is no cells-already-exist check before the fan-out (confirmed: grep finds only mark_run_running, no SELECT/skip/idempotency gate).

insert_cell's ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING (repository.py:713) protects the DB row but runs after the vendor calls, so it does NOT gate the already-completed paid call — exactly as the finding states.

maxReceiveCount default 3 confirmed (variables.tf:452 label, :505 artist), so a persistently-failing-persistence item pays up to 3x the full vendor cost before DLQ. The 900s Lambda timeout (variables.tf:458) with parallel vendor latency makes timeout-driven redrive realistic, and a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception, so the message simply reappears after the 1000s visibility timeout — re-running the whole fan-out for that single label/artist (batch_size=1, one item per message).

The mechanism, locations, evidence, risk, and recommendation are all correct. COST dimension is right. Severity P1 is appropriate: real duplicate paid LLM/Tavily/DeepSeek billing, but bounded (max 3x per affected item, fault-triggered on the persistence/timeout path rather than every invocation), so not P0.

Code-level claim is confirmed. The worker (label_enrichment_handler.py:114, artist_enrichment_handler.py:102) wraps enrich_*_for_run in no try/except, and the orchestrator (orchestrator.py:91-134) runs the paid vendor fan-out (run_vendors_parallel, :93) AND the paid DeepSeek narrative merge (merge_cells, :117) unconditionally, before any persistence (upsert_label_info :120, increment_run_counters :129). A grep confirms there is NO 'cells already exist for run+label?' idempotency gate anywhere in the orchestrator or handler. insert_cell's ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (repository.py:713) protects the row but not the already-spent call; mark_run_running (repository.py:731) is a no-op when already running and does not short-circuit. With label_enrichment_queue_max_receive_count=3 (variables.tf:449-452), a persistently-failing item re-spends the full vendor cost up to 3x before the DLQ. So the mechanism is real, not refuted, and the recommendation is sound.\n\nBut severity is overstated at P1. The finding's most-cited trigger — a transient Aurora ThrottlingException/StatementTimeout during persistence — is largely absorbed: the Data API client decorates execute with retry_data_api (data_api.py:27,51,74), which retries exactly ThrottlingException, StatementTimeoutException, InternalServerErrorException, ServiceUnavailableError, DatabaseResumingException for 5 attempts with full-jitter exponential backoff up to 30s (data_api_retry.py:17-25,38-57). So transient throttles rarely bubble up to redrive. The genuinely-unmitigated path is a Lambda timeout near the 900s worker limit (variables.tf:455-458) where vendors finished but writes are still in flight — not a Python exception, so no try/except helps and the 1000s visibility timeout re-exposes the message. That is plausible but rare, and the blast radius is bounded: enrichment is admin-only (POST /admin/labels/enrich, api_gateway.tf:113), not user-facing or high-volume; re-spend is capped at 3x; and per-label cost is a handful of LLM/search calls measured in cents. A rare timeout/non-transient-fault window costing a few labels 2-3x cents is a real but minor cost-efficiency defect for this small-DJ-circle MVP, i.e. P2, not P1.

Verified end-to-end against real code; the finding is accurate and reproducible.

CONCRETE TRIGGER PATH:
1. Admin POSTs the label-enrichment run route -> routes.py:127 creates run (status 'queued'), routes.py:136 SQS-enqueues one message per label.
2. Event source mapping (lambda.tf:223, batch_size=1, NO function_response_types=["ReportBatchItemFailures"]) invokes label_enrichment_handler.lambda_handler.
3. Handler validates msg -> get_run (repository.py:652, NO status filter, returns run even when 'running'/'completed') -> builds adapters -> calls enrich_label_for_run with NO try/except (label_enrichment_handler.py:114). Artist path identical (artist_enrichment_handler.py:102).
4. enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:91-134): mark_run_running (no-op when already 'running', does NOT short-circuit) -> run_vendors_parallel executes ALL paid vendors UNCONDITIONALLY at line 93 -> persistence (insert_cell/upsert_label_info/increment_run_counters) only AFTER, at lines 105-134. There is NO pre-vendor 'cells already exist for run+label?' check anywhere (confirmed: the only SELECT 1 in repository.py at lines 170/311 are unrelated label-listing queries).
5. A persistence failure that survives the 5-attempt retry decorator (data_api_retry.py:93-94 re-raises ThrottlingException/StatementTimeoutException/etc. after max_attempts) OR a 900s Lambda timeout (not a Python exception; visibility timeout re-exposes the message) redrives it. With maxReceiveCount=3 (sqs.tf:79 label, sqs.tf:100 artist) the message is reprocessed up to 3 times before DLQ.
6. Each reprocess re-runs the full default 3-vendor set ["gemini","openai","tavily_deepseek"] (routes.py:337) plus the DeepSeek narrative merge = duplicate paid LLM/Tavily/DeepSeek billing, up to 3x per affected item.

insert_cell ON CONFLICT (repository.py:713) and upsert_label_info ON CONFLICT only protect DB rows, not the already-completed paid call. increment_run_counters (repository.py:998) is a non-idempotent delta UPDATE, so redrive also double-counts cost/ok/error, but that is secondary to the re-spend. No test covers redrive idempotency. All file:line references in the finding are accurate.

SEVERITY: Genuine but COST-only, no correctness/security/data-loss. Re-spend is bounded (max 3x, only on the subset of items whose persistence fails post-vendor), and the most common transient DB failures are absorbed by the 5-attempt retry, so the post-vendor failure window is the narrower path. For an admin-triggered enrichment run in a small-circle product this is a real but moderate cost leak, not a stop-the-line P1. Recommend P2.

title: Auto-enrichment fan-out per triage block is uncapped (block size is an unbounded paid-call multiplier)
evidence: On triage-block finalize, the dispatch worker calls try_dispatch_for_triage_block + try_dispatch_artists_for_triage_block (auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.py:26-27). label_ids_for_triage_block (auto_repository.py:236) is a plain SELECT DISTINCT with no LIMIT, and claim_labels (auto_repository.py:108-181) chunks in _IN_CHUNK=500 but applies no upper bound on total claimed labels; the same holds for artists. Each claimed label/artist becomes one SQS message → one full vendor run (vendors x (1 generate + 1 merge), ~6 upstream calls for the default 3-vendor set per orchestrator.py + tavily_deepseek.py:100,132,179). The only limiter is the per-item _MAX_ATTEMPTS=2 claim dedup (auto_repository.py:11). By contrast the MANUAL enrich path is explicitly capped at max_length=100 labels (label_enrichment/messages.py:45) — the auto path has no equivalent cap.
risk: A single finalize of a large triage block (hundreds of distinct labels + hundreds of distinct artists across the block's tracks) fans out into hundreds of paid LLM/Tavily/DeepSeek enrichment runs with no ceiling, multiplied again by the redrive re-spend in the related finding. Spend per finalize scales linearly with block size and is not bounded by any config; alarms default to an empty SNS topic so nothing pages.
recommendation: Cap the number of labels/artists claimed per auto-dispatch (e.g. LIMIT in label_ids_for_triage_block/claim_labels or a configurable max_auto_enrich_items_per_block, dropping or deferring the remainder), mirroring the manual path's max_length=100. Add a CloudWatch metric/alarm on enrichment-run creation rate.

votes.reason: Verified the cited code; the finding is accurate. label_ids_for_triage_block (auto_repository.py:236-248) is a SELECT DISTINCT with no LIMIT; claim_labels (108-181) chunks in _IN_CHUNK=500 but applies no upper bound on total claimed labels — _MAX_ATTEMPTS=2 is a per-item dedup, not a total cap. Identical for artists (claim_artists + artist_ids_for_triage_block). The dispatch worker (auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.py:26-27) calls both label and artist dispatch per block; _dispatch_labels (auto_dispatch.py:73,111-119) claims sorted(set(label_ids)) with no cap and emits one SQS message per claimed item. Per-item cost is real: tavily_deepseek.py makes 2 Tavily searches + 1 DeepSeek call per invocation, and with the default 3-vendor set plus a merge step that is ~6 upstream paid calls per label/artist (orchestrator.py:117 merge_cells). The manual path IS explicitly capped at max_length=100 (messages.py:45) with no auto equivalent. alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults to "" (variables.tf:233; alarms.tf:47-48), so the existing Lambda-error/DLQ alarms are inert, and there is no enrichment-run-rate metric at all. finalize_block (triage_repository.py:604-703) iterates all staging-bucket tracks with no size cap, so a block can carry hundreds of distinct labels/artists. The one mitigant the finder did acknowledge correctly is dedup: each label/artist is claimed at most once (NOT EXISTS guards on state row + clouder_*_info), so spend is a first-finalize back-catalog cost rather than a perpetual per-finalize loop. That bounds the worst case to the finite, dedup-protected set of not-yet-enriched entities, which is why I'd put this at P2 rather than P1: real, uncapped one-time spend scaling with block size and missing the manual path's cap, but not a runaway/repeating cost and off the request path.

The code claims are accurate (no LIMIT in label_ids_for_triage_block/artist_ids_for_triage_block, claim_labels/claim_artists chunk at 500 with no total cap, one SQS message per claimed item, no per-block cap unlike the manual path), but the COST impact is far below P1 for this MVP for four reasons. (1) Per-entity global dedup makes this a ONE-TIME cost, not a recurring multiplier: claim_labels/claim_artists only claim items with no clouder_label_info/clouder_artist_info row and no existing state row, and items hitting _MAX_ATTEMPTS=2 are permanently excluded. Lifetime auto-enrich spend is therefore bounded by the fixed count of distinct catalog labels/artists, not by block size repeatedly. Re-finalizing the same block or finalizing overlapping blocks claims nothing new, so the 'scales linearly with block size + redrive re-spend' framing overstates it. (2) Auto-enrich is DISABLED BY DEFAULT: auto_enrich_config.enabled server-defaults to false (alembic/versions/20260525_25_auto_enrich.py:29), and _dispatch_labels returns early at auto_dispatch.py:66 when not enabled. It is an opt-in admin feature, not an always-on path. (3) A worker concurrency cap exists: maximum_concurrency defaults to 8 on both enrichment workers (infra/variables.tf:479-538), throttling burst parallelism. (4) For a small-DJ-circle niche-style catalog the distinct-entity universe is bounded, and the spend is the feature's intended purpose (front-loaded, not wasteful). The max_length=100 comparison is apples-to-oranges: that validator caps labels per admin HTTP request body, not a spend budget. The only real residual risk is a one-time burst on the first large finalize of a fresh backfilled catalog after an admin enables the feature — bounded, opt-in, throttled, and inherent to the feature. A defensive cap is cheap insurance but there is no plausible runaway-cost trigger.

Verified and reproducible. Concrete path: admin enables auto-enrich (PUT /admin/auto-enrich/labels + /artists, enabled=true; default is OFF per alembic 20260525_25 server_default false). A user creates a triage block over a wide date range for a busy style — create_block (triage_repository.py:346-375) populates it via INSERT...SELECT ... WHERE t.style_id=:style_id AND t.publish_date BETWEEN :date_from AND :date_to with NO LIMIT, so a block can hold hundreds/thousands of tracks → hundreds of distinct labels + artists. User calls POST triage-blocks/{id}/finalize → enqueue_block_auto_enrich (curation_handler.py:1534) → auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.lambda_handler:26-27 → try_dispatch_for_triage_block + try_dispatch_artists_for_triage_block. label_ids_for_triage_block / artist_ids_for_triage_block are SELECT DISTINCT with no LIMIT; claim_labels/claim_artists chunk by 500 but apply no upper bound on total claimed. Each claimed item → 1 SQS message → orchestrator.enrich_label_for_run runs len(adapters) vendor calls + 1 merge (tavily_deepseek is itself 2 upstream calls), ~6 upstream calls per item for the default 3-vendor set. The manual path IS capped (EnrichLabelsRequestIn.labels max_length=100, messages.py:45); the auto path has no equivalent. Alarms default to empty SNS (var.alarm_sns_topic_arn default \"\", variables.tf:233). All five mechanical claims confirmed.

Severity correction to P2, not P1: (1) the feature is OFF by default and requires deliberate admin opt-in via the PUT routes — not a default-active runaway; (2) the NOT EXISTS clouder_label_info/clouder_artist_info dedup guard in claim_* means each entity is enriched at most once until a manual re-run, so this is a bounded one-time cost per new entity concentrated at first-finalize of a new style, NOT a recurring per-finalize multiplier that bleeds spend indefinitely; (3) reserved concurrency on the enricher workers (lambda.tf:199,248) caps the spend RATE (does not cap total). The cap-asymmetry vs. the manual path is real and the LIMIT / max_auto_enrich_items_per_block fix is legitimate, but the opt-in gating plus enrich-once semantics (and the small-DJ-circle audience) make this a real cost-control gap worth fixing at P2 rather than a P1.

title: Enrichment ESM has no partial-batch-failure reporting; redrive is the only failure path and always re-spends
evidence: Neither enrichment event_source_mapping sets function_response_types = ["ReportBatchItemFailures"] (grep over infra/lambda.tf returns no such key), and neither handler returns a {batchItemFailures: [...]} payload — lambda_handler returns {"processed": n}. batch_size defaults to 1 (infra/variables.tf:473-477, 526-530), so today one record = one invocation, which limits blast radius, but it means the ONLY way a failure is handled is a whole-message redrive. Combined with the lack of a pre-vendor idempotency gate (see related finding), any post-vendor failure forces a redrive that re-runs the paid vendor calls rather than re-trying only the unfinished persistence step.
risk: If batch_size is ever raised above 1 for throughput, a single failing record would redrive the entire batch and re-spend vendor calls for every label/artist in it. Even at batch_size=1, the missing idempotency means redrive == re-spend, so the design has no cheap retry of just the DB write.
recommendation: Add function_response_types = ["ReportBatchItemFailures"] to both enrichment ESMs and return per-record itemIdentifier failures from the handler, and/or split vendor-spend from persistence so a persistence-only retry never re-hits vendors. Document that batch_size must stay 1 until idempotency exists.

votes.reason:
Verified every cited claim against the code; all are accurate.

1. infra/lambda.tf:223-235 (label ESM) and :272-284 (artist ESM) contain NO function_response_types key. A repo-wide grep for function_response_types / ReportBatchItemFailures / batchItemFailures returns zero hits across infra/, src/, tests/. CONFIRMED.

2. Both handlers return {"processed": processed} (label_enrichment_handler.py:136, artist_enrichment_handler.py:123), never a {batchItemFailures: [...]} payload. CONFIRMED.

3. batch_size defaults to 1 at infra/variables.tf:473-477 (label) and 526-530 (artist), with explicit "Keep at 1" descriptions. CONFIRMED.

4. "ONLY failure path is whole-message redrive": neither handler wraps enrich_label_for_run / enrich_artist_for_run in try/except (handler loop only catches PydanticValidationError for message parsing, lines 85-94 / 73-82). A run-not-found or any persistence exception raises out of lambda_handler, failing the invocation → SQS redrive. CONFIRMED.

5. "post-vendor failure re-spends vendor calls": enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:73-138) calls mark_run_running (no-op on redrive — repository.py:731-740 WHERE status='queued'), then run_vendors_parallel (line 93, the paid vendor fan-out) UNCONDITIONALLY — there is no pre-vendor gate checking whether cells already exist for run/label. The merge_cells call (line 117, another paid deepseek call) also precedes most persistence (upsert_label_info line 120, project_ai_suspected, increment_run_counters). insert_cell uses ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (repository.py:713) and mark_run_running is idempotent, but those only dedup the DB writes — they do NOT short-circuit the vendor/merge spend on redrive. A failure in any persistence step after line 93 propagates and triggers a redrive that re-runs the full paid path. CONFIRMED.

Additional confirming context the finder did not cite: label queue maxReceiveCount=3 (sqs.tf:79 / variables.tf:449-453) and artist=3, so a record that fails only in persistence re-runs the paid vendor+merge calls up to 3 times before hitting the DLQ — a real, quantifiable up-to-3x re-spend today, not purely theoretical.

Severity P2 (COST) is appropriate and stays unchanged. The "missing ReportBatchItemFailures" portion is latent (gated behind batch_size>1, which is pinned to 1 with guardrail comments), so it is not P1. But the redrive-re-spend behavior causes genuine repeated paid vendor calls under a plausible failure mode, so it is not merely cosmetic (P3). The finding does honestly flag the batch_size=1 blast-radius limit and cross-references the related idempotency finding, so there is no overstatement.

Technical facts verified: neither enrichment ESM sets function_response_types=["ReportBatchItemFailures"] (infra/lambda.tf:223-235, 272-284), both handlers return {"processed": n} not {batchItemFailures:[...]}, batch_size defaults to 1 (variables.tf:476, 529), and the orchestrator runs paid vendor calls before persistence (label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:91-134), so a redrive re-spends vendor calls. However the IMPACT does not hold for this MVP. (1) The finding's headline fix is a no-op at the current config: ReportBatchItemFailures is meaningless at batch_size=1 — one record = one invocation = one message, so a per-item redrive and a whole-message redrive are identical. (2) The "if batch_size is ever raised" risk is hypothetical AND already guarded — the variable description literally says "Keep at 1," so it's a documented-against future misconfiguration, not a current-state issue. (3) Residual re-spend is tightly bounded: max_receive_count=3 caps it at ~2 retries before DLQ, maximum_concurrency caps parallelism, and this is admin-side batch enrichment (ingest layer, not a per-user hot path) over a small catalog for a small DJ circle. A DB-write failure after vendor success is rare, and the waste is a few cents of LLM calls on those rare occasions. Real current-state cost is negligible; the only meaningful cost lever (raising batch_size) is hypothetical and guarded. This is a P3 design-hygiene note at most, not a P2 cost finding.

Verified accurate and reproducible against the code.
