(1) No `function_response_types = ["ReportBatchItemFailures"]` exists anywhere in infra — grep over infra/lambda.tf (and all of infra/ + src/) for `function_response_types|ReportBatchItemFailures|batchItemFailures` returns nothing. Both ESMs (lambda.tf:223-235 label, :272-284 artist) omit it. (2) Both handlers return `{"processed": n}` (label_enrichment_handler.py:136, artist_enrichment_handler.py:123), never a partial-failure payload, so a raised exception fails the whole message. (3) batch_size defaults to 1 (variables.tf:473-477 label, :526-530 artist). (4) The only failure path is SQS redrive to a DLQ with maxReceiveCount (sqs.tf:79/100; default 3). (5) Most important — the cost claim holds: in both orchestrators (label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:91-134, artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:90-125) paid vendor calls (`run_vendors_parallel`) and the paid merge LLM call (`merge_cells`) execute BEFORE all persistence, and there is NO pre-vendor idempotency gate. `mark_run_running` (repository.py:731-740) is a no-op once status='running' but does NOT short-circuit the handler, and `insert_cell`'s `ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING` (repository.py:713) de-dupes only the cell row, not the API spend. So a post-vendor persistence failure (transient Data API error in upsert_label_info/increment_run_counters) forces a redrive that re-runs all vendor + merge calls = re-spend.

Concrete path: admin enrichment route (label_enrichment/routes.py:136/179) or auto-dispatch batch (auto_dispatch.py:123) enqueues a message -> ESM with batch_size=1 invokes the worker -> enrich_label_for_run runs paid vendors + merge -> a persistence step raises -> no ReportBatchItemFailures so the message is redriven (up to 3x) -> vendors re-run, re-spending.

The finding is honest about its own scope: it explicitly notes batch_size=1 limits blast radius today and that ReportBatchItemFailures wouldn't help a single-record batch; the live concern it asserts (redrive == re-spend due to missing pre-vendor idempotency) is correct. Severity P2 is appropriate: the loss is real and reproducible but conditional (requires a post-vendor persistence failure, not the common case) and bounded (maxReceiveCount=3, one item per invocation); the batch_size>1 amplification is explicitly hypothetical. Not P1 (no guaranteed high-frequency loss, no correctness/security impact) and not P3 (it is a genuine paid re-spend on a non-exotic failure mode plus a documented config footgun).

title: Enrichment run permanently stuck at 'running' when any per-item worker invocation dies (timeout or DLQ exhaustion)
evidence: A manual/auto run fans out one SQS message per label/artist (auto_dispatch.py:111-123). The parent run is created with status='queued' and cells_total = requested_labels * len(vendors) (repository.py:644). The first worker pickup flips it to 'running' (mark_run_running, repository.py:731-740). The run only flips to 'completed' inside increment_run_counters, whose CASE checks `cells_ok + cells_error + :ok + :err >= cells_total` (repository.py:1020-1027). increment_run_counters runs at the END of enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:129) — i.e. only if the whole per-item invocation completes. A Lambda timeout is NOT a Python exception (per repo MEMORY 'silent Lambda failures'), so a worker that times out mid-vendor-call never reaches increment_run_counters and contributes no delta. Likewise a message that exhausts maxReceiveCount (3) and lands in the DLQ (sqs.tf:77-80) never contributes its delta. There is no sweeper/reconciler anywhere (grep for sweep/reconcile/stuck across enrichment returns nothing). The per-label auto-state row has a 6h stale-queued recovery (auto_repository.py:144-147), but that only re-claims the LABEL for a future run — it never reconciles the already-orphaned run row, which stays 'running' forever.
risk: Any run where even one item's worker invocation times out or DLQs is permanently stuck at status='running' with cells_ok+cells_error < cells_total. The admin runs list (repository.py:420-486) surfaces it as perpetually in-progress; the admin can never tell a run finished. Over time the runs table accumulates phantom 'running' rows. Because Aurora is Serverless v2 (min ACU 0) and vendor calls are slow (300s timeout), worker timeouts at 900s are plausible under vendor latency. Silent, gradual operational corruption of run state with no alarm.
recommendation: Add a reconciler (scheduled Lambda or query in the admin runs view) that flips runs to 'failed'/'partial' when status='running' AND finished_at IS NULL AND created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL (e.g. 2x worker timeout). Alternatively compute terminal status from elapsed time + DLQ depth rather than relying solely on the per-cell counter reaching cells_total. At minimum expose a 'stalled' status in list_runs so stuck runs are visible.

votes.reason:
Mechanism confirmed by reading the cited code. In both subsystems `increment_run_counters` is the final step of `enrich_label_for_run`/`enrich_artist_for_run` (orchestrator.py:129 label / :125 artist), running only if the whole per-item invocation completes. The run flips to 'running' on first pickup (mark_run_running, repository.py:731-740) and only to 'completed' when `cells_ok + cells_error + :ok + :err >= cells_total` inside that final UPDATE (repository.py:1020-1027). The worker loop (label_enrichment_handler.py:114-126; artist_enrichment_handler.py mirrors) calls the orchestrator directly with no per-item try/except that would still emit a delta, so a Lambda timeout (not a Python exception) or a message that exhausts maxReceiveCount=3 (sqs.tf:77-80, variables.tf:449-452 default 3) and lands in the DLQ never contributes its counter delta. I confirmed there is NO reconciler/sweeper for the run row: the only stale recovery is the 6h re-claim in auto_repository.py:135-156, which updates label_auto_enrich_state (re-claims the LABEL for a future run) and never touches clouder_label_enrichment_runs. list_runs (repository.py:420-486) returns raw status with no derived 'stalled' state, so the admin sees a perpetual 'running'. cells_total = requested_labels * len(vendors) at creation (repository.py:644) with status='queued'. All load-bearing claims hold, so the finding is NOT refuted.

However, severity should be P2, not P1. The finding's risk framing ("Silent... with no alarm") is inaccurate: infra/logging.tf:69-82 defines a CloudWatch DLQ-depth alarm on the label_enrichment and artist_enrichment DLQs that fires on ANY message (threshold 0), wired to alarm_sns_topic_arn. A DLQ-exhausted (or repeatedly-timing-out, which also eventually DLQs) run therefore DOES surface an operator alarm for the underlying worker failure — the run-state corruption is observable via that signal, contrary to the finding. Scope is also limited: this is an internal admin-only enrichment subsystem, the consequence is bookkeeping-only (a run row stuck at 'running', phantom 'running' rows accumulating in an admin list), not data corruption — enrichment cells/label_info are written idempotently (insert_cell ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, repository.py:713) and transient failures self-heal within the 3 retries. The pure-timeout trigger is also somewhat less likely than implied: vendors run in parallel via ThreadPool with a 300s per-vendor request_timeout (settings.py:200,216) inside a 900s Lambda budget (variables.tf:455-458), giving ~3x headroom; the DLQ-exhaustion path is the robust trigger. Real reliability gap worth fixing, but admin-only operational/cosmetic blast radius plus an existing DLQ alarm = P2, not P1.

Mechanics confirmed: increment_run_counters (label repository.py:1020-1027 / artist:243-276) is the only path to 'completed' and runs at the END of enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:129); a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception so a timed-out worker skips it; the handler has no ReportBatchItemFailures partial-batch handling, so a DLQ'd message (maxReceiveCount=3) contributes no delta; and grep confirms no reconciler/sweeper exists for the run row. So the run CAN orphan at status='running'. BUT the impact does not warrant P1 for this MVP. (1) The run status is purely cosmetic — it drives only a RunStatusBadge/RunHistoryList in the admin enrichment UI. I confirmed nothing gates on it: no new-run blocking, no auto-enrich blocking, no label/artist lock (grep for status='running' gates returns only mark_run_running). The RunProgressToast polls Beatport ingest, not enrichment runs, so there isn't even a hung spinner. (2) The actual enrichment work self-heals: label_auto_enrich_state has a 6h stale-queued reclaim + _MAX_ATTEMPTS retry (auto_repository.py:127-147), the DLQ retains messages 4 days, and insert_cell is idempotent (ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING) — so a redriven message that succeeds DOES flip the run to completed. No data loss. (3) Trigger plausibility is low: vendor request_timeout_s defaults to 300s with a 900s Lambda timeout and vendors run concurrently in a ThreadPool, so a worker must hang all vendor calls past 900s (3x the per-vendor cap) to orphan — an edge case, not routine. (4) Admin-only, small DJ circle: a stale 'running' badge an admin can recognize is a minor observability gap, not 'operational corruption.' True but cosmetic; P3 at most, not P1.

Confirmed, not refuted. Concrete trigger path: (1) manual/auto run fans out one SQS message per label/artist; run created status='queued', cells_total = requested_labels * len(vendors) (label_enrichment/repository.py:644). (2) First worker pickup flips to 'running' via mark_run_running (repository.py:731-740, no-op idempotent thereafter). (3) The run only flips to a terminal state inside increment_run_counters, whose CASE flips to 'completed' iff cells_ok+cells_error+delta >= cells_total (repository.py:1020-1027) — and that call is the LAST step of enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:129), reached only on full success. (4) With batch_size=1 (variables.tf:473-477) and no ReportBatchItemFailures (grep for batchItemFailures/FunctionResponseTypes returns nothing), a message that persistently fails — 900s Lambda timeout under combined vendor latency (300s/vendor x3 in a ThreadPool; var defaults confirmed at variables.tf:455-459, settings.py:200-201), Aurora cold-start 503, OOM, or any exception after mark_run_running but before increment_run_counters — is redelivered up to maxReceiveCount=3 (sqs.tf:79, variables.tf:449-452) then lands in the DLQ. (5) Its per-label delta is never applied, so cells_ok+cells_error stays < cells_total permanently and status stays 'running' forever.

Verified absence of every recovery mechanism: no sweeper/reconciler (grep sweep/reconcile/stuck/orphan across enrichment finds only the unrelated auto_repository 6h stale-queued LABEL recovery at auto_repository.py:144-147, which re-claims the label-state row, NOT the orphaned run row); no scheduled/cron reconciler Lambda (no schedule_expression/cloudwatch_event_rule/aws_scheduler in any infra/*.tf); no DLQ alarm for the enrichment queues (alarms.tf has no enrichment DLQ depth alarm); list_runs (repository.py:420-486) surfaces the raw status column with no elapsed-time terminal derivation (the only NOW()-INTERVAL is the unrelated staleness_days label filter). Mechanism is identical and parallel for the artist subsystem (artist_enrichment/repository.py:243-276, orchestrator.py:90,125). insert_cell's ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (repository.py:713) makes a *successful* retry safely idempotent, which is exactly why a single timeout that later succeeds does NOT stick — only retry-exhaustion to DLQ does.

Two corrections to the finding. (a) The title overstates frequency: 'when any per-item worker invocation dies (timeout)' is too broad — a lone timeout whose redelivery succeeds within 3 receives completes the run normally; only persistent failure exhausting retries into the DLQ produces the permanent-stuck state. (b) Severity P1 is slightly high. The defect is real but operationally bounded: admin-only observability/state-hygiene (perpetual 'running' rows in the admin list), no canonical/user data corruption, no availability impact, no user-facing breakage, and recoverable by manual SQL. That fits P2 better than P1. The where-locations, evidence, and recommendation (add a reconciler / expose a 'stalled' status / wire a DLQ alarm) are all accurate.

title: artist_enricher and auto_enrich_dispatch workers have no Lambda Errors alarm
evidence: local.worker_lambdas only lists canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, and label_enricher (alarms.tf:14-19). The artist_enricher_worker and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker functions exist (lambda.tf:239-323) but are absent from this map, so the for_each Errors alarm (alarms.tf:30) never creates an alarm for them. The artist path is a near-exact mirror of labels with the same crash surfaces (RuntimeError on missing run, repository writes), and auto_enrich_dispatch is the single entry point that fans out BOTH label and artist enrichment for an entire triage block (auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.py:26-27).
risk: A repeatedly-failing artist enricher or dispatch worker emits errors that page nobody and trigger no alarm. The dispatch worker is the most impactful: if it crashes, the whole block's auto-enrichment silently never happens, with no Errors alarm to catch it. Failures are only indirectly visible via the DLQ-depth alarm (logging.tf:69-90) — and that only fires after maxReceiveCount redrives.
recommendation: Add artist_enricher_worker and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker to local.worker_lambdas (or a dedicated map) so the Errors alarm covers them, matching label_enricher.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code; the finding is accurate. local.worker_lambdas (infra/alarms.tf:14-19) lists only canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, and label_enricher. The lambda_errors alarm uses for_each over local.all_lambdas = merge(api_lambdas, worker_lambdas) (alarms.tf:21, :30), so no Errors alarm is created for artist_enricher_worker or auto_enrich_dispatch_worker. Both functions do exist (lambda.tf:239, :288), and label_enricher's near-identical sibling is covered while artist is not — a real asymmetry. The dispatch handler is confirmed as the single fan-out entry point for both label and artist enrichment, with raise-able lines (auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.py:22-27) that would emit Lambda Errors. A grep of all infra/*.tf confirms no other Errors-metric alarm covers these two functions. Severity assessment: I considered downgrading because (a) both workers have DLQs with redrive (sqs.tf:98-100, :119-121) and the DLQ-depth alarm DOES cover both queues (logging.tf:64-65), giving an indirect backstop, and (b) alarms.tf:25-28 explicitly documents that real paging is deferred for the single-tenant phase. However, the DLQ backstop only fires after maxReceiveCount redrives and the gap is a clear inconsistency with the covered label_enricher sibling on the system's single orchestration entry point. P2 (observability gap, not data loss, S-effort one-line fix) is defensible. Severity unchanged.

Factually the finding is correct: artist_enricher_worker and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker are absent from local.worker_lambdas (alarms.tf:14-19), so the for_each Errors alarm (alarms.tf:30) never covers them. But the risk narrative is largely refuted by the actual code and the existing alarm coverage:

1. The dispatch worker's headline risk ("if it crashes, the whole block's auto-enrichment silently never happens") misreads the code. auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.py:26-27 calls try_dispatch_for_triage_block / try_dispatch_artists_for_triage_block, both wrapped in _safe() which catches `except Exception` and logs at ERROR WITHOUT re-raising (label_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:138-142, artist_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:133-137). A fan-out failure (DB/SQS error, unconfigured Data API RuntimeError) therefore does NOT crash the worker and does NOT emit a Lambda Error metric. The repo comment confirms this: "Only unparseable messages reach the cap — the fan-out itself is best-effort" (variables.tf:586). So an Errors alarm on the dispatch worker would only catch the unparseable-message case (json.loads/msg["block_id"] at handler lines 22-24) — which is ALREADY covered by the auto_enrich_dispatch DLQ-depth alarm (logging.tf:65, threshold>0, same SNS topic) after maxReceiveCount=3.

2. The artist_enricher worker CAN genuinely raise (RuntimeError on missing run at artist_enrichment_handler.py:86; vendor/repository failures in enrich_artist_for_run), so this half is real — but those failures land in the artist_enrichment DLQ after maxReceiveCount=3 and fire the artist_enrichment DLQ-depth alarm (logging.tf:64) on the same SNS topic. The gap is detection latency (a few redrive cycles), not a blind spot — and the finding itself concedes the DLQ alarm covers it.

3. MVP impact: enrichment is best-effort background auto-search explicitly designed to "never break curation," for a small DJ circle — not a user-facing critical path. Both workers already have DLQ-depth alarms routed to the same SNS topic. alarms.tf:25-28 already documents a conscious single-tenant-phase trade-off on alarm completeness. The missing Errors alarm adds only finer attribution/slightly faster detection for one worker and essentially nothing for the dispatch worker.

Because the stated coverage gap is already filled by DLQ-depth alarms and the dispatch-worker "silent crash" is a misread (swallowed+logged, not crashed), this is at most a P3 polish/consistency gap, not a P2 reliability risk.

Finding is factually correct and reproducible. Verified: local.worker_lambdas (infra/alarms.tf:14-19) lists only canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, label_enricher; artist_enricher_worker and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker (infra/lambda.tf:239-323) are absent, and the for_each Errors alarm (alarms.tf:29-49 over all_lambdas) therefore never creates an alarm for them — a grep for any alarm referencing those two functions returned empty. The artist worker has genuine propagating crash surfaces that emit the Lambda Errors metric: src/collector/artist_enrichment_handler.py raises `RuntimeError(f"run not found: {msg.run_id}")` when repository.get_run returns None, plus RuntimeError from _build_clients/_build_merge_client and unguarded enrich_artist_for_run/repository writes. Concrete path: triage finalize enqueues per-artist messages → artist_enricher_worker invoked → message references a deleted/missing run_id → get_run returns None → RuntimeError propagates out of lambda_handler → invocation fails, Errors metric increments, SQS retries to maxReceiveCount=3 (variables.tf:505) → no Errors alarm; only DLQ-depth alarm (logging.tf:64-65) fires after 3 redrives. One overstatement: the finding calls the dispatch worker the most impactful because 'if it crashes the whole block's auto-enrichment silently never happens', but both fan-out calls (try_dispatch_for_triage_block / try_dispatch_artists_for_triage_block) are wrapped in _safe() which swallows all exceptions (auto_dispatch.py:133-137); the variable comment confirms 'Only unparseable messages reach the cap — the fan-out itself is best-effort.' So a dispatch-logic failure does NOT crash the worker; the dispatch crash surface is narrow (malformed JSON / missing block_id only). The no-alarm claim for it is still true. Severity is overstated: this is a monitoring/alerting gap, not a functional defect — failures are still caught by the DLQ-depth alarm covering both queues (with a 3-redrive lag), and CLAUDE.md/alarms.tf:24-28 explicitly accept reduced alarm coverage for the single-tenant phase with paging not yet wired. That defense-in-depth plus the explicitly-accepted posture puts this at P3, not P2.

title: enrich_*_for_run double-counts run counters and re-spends LLM vendor calls on SQS redelivery (non-idempotent)
where: src/collector/label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:93-134 + repository.py:687-729 (insert_cell ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) vs repository.py:998-1037 (increment_run_counters blind +=); artist_enrichment/repository.py:289-298 + :253-276
evidence: enrich_label_for_run runs the vendor fan-out (run_vendors_parallel, orchestrator.py:93) BEFORE writing cells, then calls insert_cell (ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING, repository.py:713) and finally increment_run_counters, which unconditionally does `cells_ok = cells_ok + :ok` / `cells_error = cells_error + :err` / `cost_usd = cost_usd + :cost` (repository.py:1017-1019) with no idempotency guard. SQS at-least-once delivery + batch_size=1 + no ReportBatchItemFailures means a message that is redelivered (worker timed out after committing cells but before the SDK returned, or any transient post-cell error that re-raises) will re-run ALL vendor LLM calls (re-spend) and increment the counters a SECOND time. The cell rows are protected by ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, but the counter UPDATE is not — it has no equivalent guard.
risk: On redelivery: (1) every Gemini/OpenAI/Tavily/DeepSeek call for that item is re-spent (the Tavily adapter alone is 3 upstream HTTP calls); (2) cells_ok/cells_error overshoot cells_total, which can prematurely or incorrectly flip status to 'completed' for OTHER concurrent items, or report counts that exceed cells_total. Uncontrolled vendor spend on retry plus corrupted run accounting.
recommendation: Make the counter update idempotent: derive cells_ok/cells_error by recomputing from the cells table (e.g. SET ... = SELECT counts FROM cells WHERE run_id=...) instead of blind +=, OR gate the increment on insert_cell having actually inserted (use RETURNING / rowcount from the ON CONFLICT). Also consider checking for an existing cell before invoking vendors to avoid the re-spend.

votes.reason: Verified all cited code; the finding is accurate and no guard was missed. orchestrator.py:91-134 runs run_vendors_parallel (LLM fan-out, line 93) BEFORE persisting, then insert_cell (ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING, repository.py:713), then increment_run_counters which does unconditional blind += (repository.py:1017-1019: cells_ok = cells_ok + :ok, etc.) with only WHERE id=:id — no idempotency guard. The artist path is symmetric (artist_enrichment/repository.py:260-262 += , :298 ON CONFLICT; orchestrator.py:90-125 same ordering). Each Data API execute auto-commits independently, so cells from a first attempt are durably committed (making ON CONFLICT engage on retry) while the counter UPDATE re-applies — confirming the double-count is real, not hypothetical. Redelivery is a genuine AWS failure mode here: lambda.tf:223-235/272-284 event source mappings have no function_response_types=ReportBatchItemFailures, the handler (label_enrichment_handler.py:114-136) returns {"processed":N} and re-raises on any post-cell error, batch_size default=1, and the queues have max_receive_count=3 with a DLQ (variables.tf:449-453, sqs.tf:78-79). So on a Lambda timeout (900s) after cells commit, or any transient post-cell exception, SQS redelivers up to 2 more times: each redelivery re-runs every Gemini/OpenAI/Tavily/DeepSeek call (re-spend) and increments counters again, overshooting cells_total and potentially flipping status='completed' incorrectly. The 'single transaction' comment at repository.py:1006-1010 only covers the counter UPDATE, not the orchestrator — it does not protect against redelivery. Mechanics, line numbers, and risk are all correct. Severity P1 is appropriate: not a steady-state constant leak (would be higher), but a guaranteed at-least-once-delivery non-idempotency that both re-spends paid vendor calls and corrupts run accounting on a normal failure mode, with bounded blast radius (<=2 extra re-spends per affected item).

Mechanism confirmed end to end in both label and artist paths. enrich_label_for_run/enrich_artist_for_run call run_vendors_parallel unconditionally (orchestrator.py:93 — no pre-check for existing cells), insert_cell uses ON CONFLICT (run_id,label_id,vendor) DO NOTHING (repository.py:713 / artist :298, idempotent), but increment_run_counters is a blind cells_ok=cells_ok+:ok / cost_usd=cost_usd+:cost (repository.py:1017-1019 / artist :260-262) with no idempotency guard. The handler loop (label_enrichment_handler.py:114) has no try/except, so any exception after cells are written re-raises the batch. The most plausible trigger is a silent Lambda timeout (MEMORY confirms timeout is not a Python exception): 4 vendors + Tavily's 3 HTTP calls + a DeepSeek merge call inside a 900s budget, killed after cells commit but before counters → SQS redelivers → full vendor LLM re-spend + double-count. cells_total = requested_labels * len(vendors) (repository.py:644), so on a multi-label run the overshoot can prematurely flip status to 'completed'. The 'no ReportBatchItemFailures' detail is a red herring with batch_size=1 (single-message batch redelivers identically either way), but the core non-idempotency is real and not an accepted/documented behavior.

P1 overstates impact for THIS MVP, so lowering to P2: (1) re-spend is bounded — maxReceiveCount=3 (sqs.tf:79) caps it at <=2 extra deliveries before DLQ; no unbounded spend loop. (2) Admin-only batch enrichment (/admin/labels/enrich) for a small DJ circle — low volume, not user-facing, fires only on the redelivery edge, not steady state. (3) Data API client has retry decorators (data_api.py) that absorb most transient blips before they escalate to message-level redelivery. (4) Data-correctness blast radius is cosmetic: merged label_info/artist_info is idempotently upserted and cells are ON CONFLICT protected, so enrichment data stays correct — only the run's aggregate counters drift/overshoot, degrading an admin-only status display. Worth fixing (recompute counters from the cells table; gate vendor calls / increment on cell non-existence), but bounded spend + cosmetic blast radius = P2, not P1.

Confirmed against real code on every link. Orchestrator (label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:91-137; artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:88-128) runs run_vendors_parallel UNCONDITIONALLY at the top (no existing-cell check, no run-status gate), then insert_cell with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (cells protected by uq_label_enr_cell/uq_artist_enr_cell — verified in migrations 22 & 26), then increment_run_counters which does a blind `cells_ok = cells_ok + :ok` / `cells_error += :err` / `cost_usd += :cost` with NO idempotency guard (repository.py:1017-1019; artist repository.py:260-262). Each execute is autocommit (no enclosing transaction wraps enrich_*_for_run), so partial-completion-then-redelivery is reachable.

Redelivery is genuinely possible: standard (non-FIFO) SQS, batch_size=1 (var default, lambda.tf:226/275), max_receive_count=3 (redrive enabled, variables.tf:449-453), NO function_response_types=["ReportBatchItemFailures"] anywhere in infra (grep empty), no app-level dedup or processed-message check, visibility_timeout=1000s > Lambda timeout=900s so a worker timeout redelivers ~100s later. Handler (label_enrichment_handler.py:114) has no try/except around enrich_label_for_run, so any post-cell exception also re-raises and redelivers.

Concrete path: create_run sets cells_total = requested_labels * len(vendors) (repository.py:644), i.e. ONE run spans MANY labels with one SQS message per label sharing run_id. Worker A processes label-A message → writes 3 cells + increments counters (+3 committed) → Lambda times out during on_outcome/log before SQS ack → message redelivered → all 3 vendor LLM calls re-run (Tavily adapter = 3 upstream HTTP calls) = re-spend; cells no-op via ON CONFLICT; counters get a SECOND +3. Result: cells_ok+cells_error overshoots cells_total and the status CASE (`>= cells_total`) can flip the run to 'completed' before sibling labels' messages are processed — corrupting run accounting visible via list_runs API (routes.py:351/364). Both label and artist subsystems are byte-for-byte the same pattern.

The diagnosis is fully correct. Lowering severity to P2: the trigger is probabilistic (requires a timeout/exception in a narrow window), bounded by max_receive_count=3, affects only retried items rather than steady-state, and the run-status corruption is informational (no data loss, no irreversible damage). Real bug warranting the recommended fix (recompute counters from the cells table, or gate the increment on insert_cell rowcount via RETURNING), but blast radius/likelihood are P2-level, not P1.

title: Enrichment DLQs have no consumer and alarms default to no SNS topic — failed enrichment is invisible
where: infra/sqs.tf:64-123 (label/artist/auto_enrich DLQs, terminal); infra/logging.tf:69-90 (dlq_depth alarm); infra/variables.tf:230-234 (alarm_sns_topic_arn default "")
evidence: All three enrichment DLQs (label_enrichment_dlq, artist_enrichment_dlq, auto_enrich_dispatch_dlq) have only message_retention_seconds and no redrive/consumer — no Lambda event source mapping targets them and no redrive_allow_policy exists (grep of lambda.tf for DLQ consumers returns nothing). The dlq_depth alarm exists for all six DLQs (logging.tf:59-90) but its alarm_actions/ok_actions are `var.alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" ? [...] : []` and alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults to "" (variables.tf:230-234), so by default the alarm fires into the void and pages nobody. Combined with finding above, a label/artist whose message DLQs is lost (4-day retention for enrichment, sqs.tf:66/87) with no automatic reprocessing and no notification.
risk: An enrichment item that fails maxReceiveCount times silently lands in a terminal DLQ, expires after 4 days, and is never enriched — and its parent run stays 'running' forever (see stuck-run finding). With the default empty SNS topic nothing alerts an operator. Failures are completely silent in the default deployment.
recommendation: Wire alarm_sns_topic_arn to a real topic in the prod tfvars (the alarms are otherwise dead), and add a documented DLQ redrive runbook step or a redrive consumer for enrichment DLQs so DLQ'd items can be replayed before the retention window expires.

votes.reason: All load-bearing claims verified against the cited code. (1) The three enrichment DLQs (label_enrichment_dlq sqs.tf:64-67, artist_enrichment_dlq sqs.tf:85-88, auto_enrich_dispatch_dlq sqs.tf:106-109) have only name + message_retention_seconds, no redrive_allow_policy, and no consumer — grep for redrive_allow_policy returns nothing across infra/, and no event_source_mapping or Lambda targets these DLQ ARNs (only name-local references in main.tf:17/20/23). (2) The dlq_depth alarm covers all six DLQs (logging.tf:58-90, for_each over local.dlq_queues). (3) Its alarm_actions/ok_actions are `var.alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" ? [...] : []` (logging.tf:88-89) and the variable defaults to "" (variables.tf:230-234); terraform.tfvars.example:8 also ships alarm_sns_topic_arn = "", so the default deployment sends DLQ alarms nowhere. (4) Enrichment retention is 345600s = exactly 4 days for label and artist (variables.tf:446/499), matching the sqs.tf:66/87 citation. The finding's logic holds: a failed enrichment message lands in a terminal DLQ, expires after 4 days with no automatic reprocessing, and no operator is paged by default. One minor caveat that does not refute: the runbook (docs/ops/runbook.md:96-104) documents a generic start-message-move-task redrive procedure, but its symptom/diagnosis tables (lines 68-79) list only the four older DLQs and never mention the three enrichment DLQs — so the recommendation to add enrichment DLQs to the redrive runbook is still valid. Severity P2 is correct: this is a default-deployment observability/recoverability gap affecting derived enrichment data (not primary canonical content or a crash), and it only manifests when the operator leaves the SNS topic unset (the default) and a sustained failure occurs.

Technical claims are accurate (verified): the three enrichment DLQs have no consumer and no redrive_allow_policy; the dlq_depth alarm actions are gated on alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" which defaults to "" (variables.tf:230-234, shipped "" in terraform.tfvars.example:8); enrichment retention is 4 days (345600s) / 1 day for auto-enrich. But the impact for this MVP is overstated, refuting the "completely silent / lost forever" core premise. (1) Enrichment is admin-only metadata (POST /admin/labels/enrich, POST /admin/artists/enrich in handler.py:66-77), not the DJ curation path — a lost item degrades metadata completeness, not core function. (2) Failures are NOT invisible: the admin has dedicated visibility surfaces independent of the SNS topic — GET /admin/labels/backlog and /admin/artists/backlog with a status filter that explicitly supports none/failed/outdated/running (repository.py:286-303). A label that lost its DLQ message resurfaces as status=none; a stuck run shows as status=running. (3) Recovery does not need DLQ replay: the backlog source-of-truth is the DB, and POST /admin/labels/enrich re-triggers enrichment, making the 4-day retention window irrelevant to recoverability. (4) A generic, queue-agnostic DLQ redrive runbook already exists (docs/ops/runbook.md:64-104, SQS "Start DLQ redrive"), so the recommendation's "no documented redrive runbook" is only partly true. (5) alarm_sns_topic_arn="" is the documented opt-out, same accept-by-default ops posture as min_acu=0 / cold-start 503. The real residual is a minor ops-hygiene nit (dead alarm = no proactive push), worth P3, not a P2 reliability defect, since the operator can detect and recover via the backlog view.

Central claims verified against real code. (1) label_enrichment_dlq, artist_enrichment_dlq, auto_enrich_dispatch_dlq (infra/sqs.tf:64-123) have only message_retention_seconds — no redrive_policy on the DLQs and no Lambda event source mapping targets them (lambda.tf event source mappings at lines 223/272/315 point at the MAIN queues .arn, not the DLQs); grep finds no redrive_allow_policy. (2) dlq_depth alarm (logging.tf:69-90) gates alarm_actions/ok_actions on `var.alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" ? [...] : []`, and alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults to "" (variables.tf:230-234). No aws_sns_topic resource is defined anywhere in infra, and terraform.tfvars.example:8 ships `alarm_sns_topic_arn = ""`. Reproducible path: deploy with the documented example tfvars → a label enrichment message that fails maxReceiveCount=3 times (var default, variables.tf:449-452) lands in label_enrichment_dlq → the per-DLQ CloudWatch alarm transitions to ALARM but has empty alarm_actions, so nobody is paged → message expires after retention (345600s = 4 days for label/artist; note auto_enrich is 86400s = 1 day, so the finding's blanket "4-day retention for enrichment" is imprecise for the dispatch DLQ).

Two caveats reduce severity below P2: (a) The recommendation to "add a documented DLQ redrive runbook step" is partially already satisfied — docs/ops/runbook.md:96-104 documents the exact `aws sqs start-message-move-task` manual redrive procedure. However that runbook's DLQ symptom table (lines 68,76-79) lists only the four older DLQs and omits all three enrichment DLQs, so enrichment-specific guidance is genuinely missing. (b) The empty SNS topic is an explicit, documented operator opt-out ("Empty disables notifications"), not a code defect — the alarm and DLQ infra both exist; only the notification sink is unconfigured. This is an observability/ops-hardening default gap with an existing manual remediation path, not a hard runtime failure. The "stuck-run forever" amplification depends on a separate unverified finding. Correct level is P3.

title: log_event silently drops diagnostic fields not in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS; enrichment failure paths log no structured context
evidence: _sanitize_fields only keeps keys present in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS and silently discards the rest (logging_utils.py:138-140). label_enrichment_handler logs label_enrichment_completed with label_name (handler.py:133) and artist_enrichment_handler logs artist_name (handler.py:120), but neither label_name nor artist_name is in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (grep count 0) — those fields vanish from the emitted log. More importantly, the 'run not found' branch raises a bare RuntimeError with NO log_event at all (label_enrichment_handler.py:96-98, artist_enrichment_handler.py:84-86): the message goes to the DLQ carrying only a generic stack trace, with no structured run_id/label_id to diagnose which run/item failed (even though run_id and label_id ARE in the allow-list and could have been logged).
risk: When an enrichment worker fails or DLQs, the structured logs that survive the allow-list filter omit the human-meaningful identifiers (label/artist name), and the run-not-found failure path emits no structured diagnostic at all. Operators debugging a stuck/failed run must reverse-engineer it from raw exception traces, slowing incident response for exactly the silent-failure scenarios above.
recommendation: Add a log_event('ERROR', 'enrichment_run_not_found', run_id=..., label_id=...) (or artist_id) before raising in both handlers. Either add label_name/artist_name to ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS or stop passing them so the drop isn't misleading. Consider failing loudly in tests when a passed log field is not allow-listed.

votes.reason:
Core claim verified by reading and by running the sanitizer. _sanitize_fields (logging_utils.py:136-141) keeps only keys in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS and silently discards the rest. Empirical run confirms that of {run_id, label_id, label_name, artist_id, artist_name}, only run_id survives — so label_enrichment_completed (label_enrichment_handler.py:128-134) and artist_enrichment_completed (artist_enrichment_handler.py:115-121) emit logs with label_name/artist_name dropped. The "run not found" branch (label_enrichment_handler.py:97-98, artist_enrichment_handler.py:85-86) raises a bare RuntimeError with NO log_event — confirmed.

Two factual errors in the finding, neither fatal: (1) It asserts "run_id and label_id ARE in the allow-list and could have been logged" — false: label_id and artist_id are NOT in the allow-list (grep + runtime both confirm). Only run_id is. This makes the gap marginally worse than described, not better. (2) The cited auto_enrich_dispatch.py:138-142 is wrong: the real file (src/collector/curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py) is 41 lines and its error path (lines 37-40) correctly logs block_id (allow-listed) + truncated error_message — i.e. it is a counter-example, not support. Neither error undermines the central, verified defect.

Severity P3 is correct: this is a diagnostics/observability gap only — no incorrect behavior, data loss, or failure. run_id (the run's primary diagnostic key) survives in completion logs, the SQS body lands in the DLQ for inspection, and the run-not-found exception string carries run_id in the trace. Real but low-impact. Not refuted.

Technical claims are largely accurate (label_name/artist_name are dropped by the allow-list; the run-not-found branch emits no structured log), but the impact for this MVP is negligible and the finding's own remediation is factually broken.

WHY IT DOESN'T MATTER FOR THIS MVP:
1. Operators are alerted independently of log fields: infra/logging.tf:56-90 defines a DLQ-depth CloudWatch alarm for label_enrichment, artist_enrichment, and auto_enrich_dispatch DLQs (threshold=0, evaluation_periods=1, SNS-wired). Failures surface via alarm, not by grepping log fields.
2. The diagnostic identifiers are NOT actually lost on the failure path. label_enrichment_handler.py:98 / artist_enrichment_handler.py:86 raise RuntimeError(f"run not found: {msg.run_id}") — the run_id is embedded verbatim in the exception string that lands in CloudWatch and the DLQ. And the full SQS message body ({run_id, label_id, label_name, style}) is preserved in the DLQ redrive payload, inspectable from the SQS console/CLI. So the "operators must reverse-engineer from raw traces with no run_id/label_id" risk overstates reality.
3. Trigger probability is very low: neither label_enrichment/repository.py nor artist_enrichment/repository.py exposes a run-deletion method, so 'run not found' requires an orphaned/cross-environment message or manual DB surgery — an anomaly, not a routine failure mode.

FINDING'S OWN FIX IS WRONG: it claims "run_id and label_id ARE in the allow-list and could have been logged." Verified false — ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (logging_utils.py:14-100) contains run_id but NOT label_id or artist_id. The recommended log_event('ERROR', 'enrichment_run_not_found', run_id=..., label_id=...) would have label_id silently stripped by _sanitize_fields — reproducing the very bug it sets out to fix.

Net: minor log-ergonomics nit (drop misleading name fields; optionally add a structured error log keyed on run_id), not a reliability defect. DLQ alarm + redrive payload + run_id-in-exception already give operators what they need for a small-DJ-circle MVP.

Both core claims reproduce on real code. (1) `label_name`/`artist_name` are NOT in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (grep returns no match), yet both are passed to log_event at label_enrichment_handler.py:128-134 (label_name) and artist_enrichment_handler.py:115-121 (artist_name). `_sanitize_fields` (logging_utils.py:138-140) keeps only allow-listed keys, so these are silently discarded — confirmed. (2) The run-not-found branch raises a bare RuntimeError with NO log_event: label_enrichment_handler.py:96-98 and artist_enrichment_handler.py:84-86 both do `if run_row is None: raise RuntimeError(f"run not found: {msg.run_id}")` with no structured log. The exception propagates out of lambda_handler; SQS retries then routes to the DLQ (DLQ confirmed in infra/sqs.tf:64-100, :85-100 with maxReceiveCount), leaving only a raw stack trace for diagnosis. Concrete trigger path: a label/artist enrichment SQS message (enqueued from auto_dispatch.py:111-123 carrying run_id/label_id/label_name) whose run row is absent at processing time (run deleted/rolled-back, or message replayed after cleanup) — repository.get_run() returns None → bare RuntimeError, no structured run_id/label_id field emitted. Two factual errors in the write-up that do not invalidate it: (a) it claims "label_id ARE in the allow-list" — false; label_id and artist_id are NOT allow-listed (only run_id is), which actually strengthens the gap since the completed log also silently drops label_id/artist_id; (b) the cited path src/collector/auto_enrich_dispatch.py:138-142 is wrong — the real file is src/collector/curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py (41 lines) and it logs correctly with allow-listed block_id+error_message, so it is not a valid example. Severity P3 is correct: this is an observability/diagnosability gap, not a functional bug — no data loss/corruption, the exception still surfaces in CloudWatch with the run_id embedded in the message text and lands in the DLQ; impact is limited to operators reading raw traces instead of structured fields.

title: User search input in LIKE patterns has no ESCAPE clause — % and _ act as wildcards (correctness footgun, not injection)
where: src/collector/repositories.py:764,788,873,901,944,963,977,993 (LIKE :search built as f"%{search.lower()}%"); same pattern in curation list endpoints
evidence: find_tracks_not_found_on_spotify/list_tracks/count_tracks/list_artists/list_albums/list_styles all do params["search"] = f"%{search.lower()}%" then SQL `... LIKE :search` with no ESCAPE clause (e.g. repositories.py:763-764, 872-873, 900-901). The search term IS bound as a Data API parameter (no SQL injection), but literal % or _ in the user's search string are interpreted as LIKE wildcards rather than matched literally.
risk: Pure correctness/UX: a user searching for a title containing '%' or '_' gets over-broad matches. No security impact — value is parameterized, never interpolated into SQL text. No data corruption (read-only paths).
recommendation: If literal substring matching is intended, escape %, _ and \ in the user term and append `ESCAPE '\'` to the LIKE clause, or use position()/ILIKE on an escaped term. Low priority; cosmetic for an internal DJ tool.

votes.reason: Verified the finding is correct. All 8 cited locations in src/collector/repositories.py (764, 788, 873, 901, 944, 963, 977, 993) build params["search"] = f"%{search.lower()}%" and use a bare LIKE :search clause with no ESCAPE. The search term is bound through the Data API params dict (never interpolated into SQL text), so the finding's "not injection, pure correctness" framing is accurate. The wildcard behavior is genuinely observable: normalize_text (models.py:148) is only " ".join(value.strip().lower().split()) — it does NOT strip % or _, so those characters survive into both the stored normalized_title/normalized_name columns and the user's search term. The handler's _parse_pagination_params (handler.py:854-875) only .strip()s the search value with no escaping, and a grep for ESCAPE / any like-escape helper across src/collector/ returns nothing. Thus a literal % or _ in a user's search string is interpreted as a LIKE wildcard, producing over-broad matches. No guard was missed. P3 is appropriate: read-only paths, no data corruption, no security impact, cosmetic UX issue for an internal DJ tool.

Technically accurate but immaterial for this MVP. Confirmed at repositories.py:763-764, 872-873, 900-901, 913, 943-944, 963, 976-977, 993: user search is .strip()ed (handler.py:870-873) then bound as `f"%{search.lower()}%"` into `LIKE :search` / `ILIKE :search` with no ESCAPE clause, so literal % and _ act as wildcards. The author correctly scopes this as pure correctness/UX, NOT injection (value is a Data API parameter, never interpolated into SQL text). Impact for this MVP is negligible: (1) all affected paths are read-only listing/count endpoints — no data corruption, no crash; (2) the failure direction is benign — an unescaped % or _ makes LIKE MORE permissive, returning a superset, so the user's target row still appears, just among extra matches (self-correcting by refining the query); (3) the trigger is implausible — searches run against normalized music metadata (track titles, artist/album/style names) for a small internal DJ circle; literal % or _ in a search term is rare and non-adversarial; (4) the codebase already uses the same non-escaped ILIKE pattern in the triage path (tests/unit/test_triage_repository.py:488), confirming this is accepted house style. The author themselves rates it 'cosmetic' and 'low priority.' A real-but-trivial nit with no plausible meaningful trigger for this tool's data and audience — refuted on impact. Severity P3 itself is not mis-set.

Verified accurate and reproducible. Concrete path: GET /tracks?search=foo_bar -> handler._parse_pagination_params (handler.py:848-875) extracts `search` from queryStringParameters and only .strip()s it (no %/_ escaping) -> repository.list_tracks(limit, offset, "foo_bar") -> builds params["search"] = f"%{search.lower()}%" -> executes SQL `WHERE t.normalized_title LIKE :search` with no ESCAPE clause. In Postgres `_` matches any single char and `%` any sequence, so a literal underscore/percent in the user's term becomes a wildcard (over-broad matches). All 8 cited repositories.py locations (764,788,873,901,944,963,977,993) match exactly. Same pattern confirmed in curation list endpoints: categories_repository.py:691-692,785 (ILIKE), triage_repository.py:1055, tags_repository.py:110,125, reachable via GET /categories/{id}/tracks and similar. grep confirms zero ESCAPE clauses and no like-escaping helper anywhere in src/collector/. Severity is correctly characterized: value is bound as a Data API named parameter (:search), never interpolated into SQL text, so NO SQL injection; read-only paths so no data corruption. Pure correctness/UX footgun for the rare user searching a literal % or _ in an internal DJ tool. P3 unchanged is appropriate.

title: Spotify refresh: vendor-token rotation and session rotation are not in one transaction (partial-write window invalidates Spotify access)
where: src/collector/auth_handler.py:444-468 (upsert_vendor_token then rotate_session); src/collector/auth/auth_repository.py has no transaction() usage
evidence: _handle_refresh performs two independent auto-committed Data API statements with no transaction wrapper: (1) upsert_vendor_token re-encrypts and stores the NEW Spotify access+refresh tokens (auth_handler.py:444-455) AFTER oauth.refresh() already consumed/rotated the old Spotify refresh token upstream; (2) rotate_session updates the session hash to the new refresh JWT (auth_handler.py:464-468). grep confirms zero `transaction` references in auth_handler.py and auth/auth_repository.py. If the process dies or rotate_session's UPDATE fails between (1) and (2), the user_vendor_tokens row now holds the rotated Spotify tokens but user_sessions.refresh_token_hash still points at the OLD refresh JWT, and the client never receives the new refresh cookie (handler raised).
risk: Inconsistent auth state: the stored session expects the old refresh JWT while the Spotify tokens have already been rotated. The client retries refresh with the old cookie; hash matches, but a concurrent retry or any later hash mismatch trips replay detection (auth_handler.py:414-417) which revokes ALL sessions (ADR-0015). Net effect under partial failure is a user silently logged out of every session, requiring a fresh /auth/login. Not data loss of catalogue, but a silent integrity gap on the security-critical token path.
recommendation: Wrap the vendor-token upsert + rotate_session in a single self._data_api.transaction() so both commit or neither does, and only return the new refresh cookie after commit. Apply the same to _handle_callback (see separate finding). Order DB rotation before any irreversible upstream side effect where possible.

votes.reason: Factual claims verified and correct, but severity is overstated. Verified: _handle_refresh performs two independent auto-committed Data API execute() calls — upsert_vendor_token (auth_handler.py:444-455) then rotate_session (auth_handler.py:464-468) — with no transaction wrapper; DataAPIClient.transaction() exists (data_api.py:99-107) and is used in canonicalize.py but not in auth; auth_repository.rotate_session/upsert_vendor_token (lines 202-213, 256-287) don't even accept transaction_id, unlike vendor_match/canonicalize repos. Replay detection revokes all sessions (auth_handler.py:415-417, ADR-0015). So the missing-transaction observation is real and not refuted.\n\nHowever the P1 risk chain is overstated. (1) The headline 'silent logout of every session' is NOT a deterministic consequence of the partial write. In the single-client partial-failure case (upsert succeeds, rotate dies), the client retries with the OLD cookie; inbound_hash still equals session.refresh_token_hash (both old) so the replay check at 415 PASSES, and the flow re-reads the now-updated vendor refresh token and re-rotates — it self-heals. The finding itself concedes 'hash matches.' (2) The all-sessions-revocation outcome only arises under a CONCURRENT refresh, which is a pre-existing race that the recommended fix does not address: wrapping the two writes atomically does not serialize concurrent /auth/refresh requests, so two simultaneous calls with the same cookie still trip replay detection regardless of the transaction. The risk is therefore mis-attributed to the missing transaction. (3) The true window is a Lambda crash in the sub-millisecond gap between two Data API calls; the retry contract (data_api_retry.py:47-52) already covers transient-error idempotency via UPSERT. This is a legitimate defensive-hardening item (atomicity on a security-critical path), but low probability, self-healing in the common case, and worst-case is orthogonal — properly P3, not P1.

Factual claim is accurate: upsert_vendor_token (auth_handler.py:444-455) and rotate_session (464-468) are two independent auto-committed Data API writes with no transaction wrapper, and auth_repository.py never opens a transaction (a transaction() context manager does exist on DataAPIClient, so the fix is feasible). But the predicted harm does not follow for this MVP. In the described crash window (die after upsert, before rotate): user_vendor_tokens holds the NEW valid/unused Spotify tokens while user_sessions.refresh_token_hash still holds the OLD JWT hash, and the client still holds the OLD cookie. On retry, inbound_hash == session.refresh_token_hash (both OLD) so replay detection at line 415-416 does NOT fire; the retry reads the new vendor tokens, decrypts the valid unused Spotify refresh token, and oauth.refresh() succeeds. The state the finding calls a corruption is precisely what makes the retry self-heal — Spotify access is not invalidated. The escalation to "logged out of every session" requires a CONCURRENT double-refresh, but frontend/src/api/client.ts:8 single-flights refresh via inflightRefresh, so one client cannot race itself; multiple devices hold distinct session cookies and don't collide. The only path to mass revocation is a deliberately replayed stale cookie, which is the designed, accepted ADR-0015 behavior the transaction wrapper would not change. Also spotify_oauth.py:165 reuses the same refresh token when Spotify omits a new one, so the "old Spotify refresh token already consumed" premise is not even always true. Trigger requires a precisely-timed Lambda death between two writes in a small-DJ-circle MVP, and even then it recovers on the next refresh. No data loss, no silent logout in the realistic path.

The structural claim is accurate: _handle_refresh runs upsert_vendor_token (auth_handler.py:444-455) then rotate_session (464-468) as two independent auto-committed Data API statements with no transaction() wrapper, and AuthRepository never uses transaction() even though the helper exists and is used in playlists_repository.py. So the non-atomicity / partial-write window is real and reproducible: a Lambda timeout or transient Data API failure of the second UPDATE between the two commits leaves user_vendor_tokens rotated while user_sessions.refresh_token_hash still holds the old JWT hash, and no new cookie is sent.

However, the finding's headline P1 impact ("user silently logged out of every session, requiring a fresh /auth/login") does not hold under a concrete single-path trace. After a partial write, the client retries /auth/refresh with the OLD cookie. The session hash is still the OLD hash, so inbound_hash == session.refresh_token_hash and replay detection (414-417) does NOT trip — the finding itself concedes "hash matches." The stored vendor token is now the NEW, unused Spotify refresh token (oauth.refresh at line 431 produced it; note line 165 means Spotify may even keep the same refresh token), so on retry oauth.refresh succeeds, upsert + rotate_session both complete, and a valid new cookie is issued. The path self-heals.

The "revoke ALL sessions" outcome requires an ADDITIONAL precondition the finding smuggles in: a concurrent/racing refresh. That race exists independently of this partial-write window (it is the standard refresh-token-rotation race deliberately handled by ADR-0015's replay-revoke), so wrapping these two statements in a transaction would not fix it. The actual consequence attributable to THIS bug is a single transient failed refresh request that recovers on the next attempt — no logout-everywhere, no catalogue data loss, no persistent integrity gap.

Net: the non-atomicity observation is a legitimate but low-impact robustness nit (worst real outcome = one transient, self-correcting refresh failure), not the security-critical P1 integrity gap described. Downgrading to P3 rather than fully refuting because the underlying "two unwrapped state-mutating writes on the token path" fact is correct.

title: OAuth callback writes user, vendor-token, and session as three separate untransacted statements
evidence: On GET /auth/callback the handler calls upsert_user (303-312), then upsert_vendor_token (316-327), then create_session (339-347) — three independent Data API execute() calls, none sharing a transaction_id (auth/auth_repository.py exposes no transaction path). A failure after upsert_user but before create_session leaves a users row with no session; a failure after create_session-without-vendor-token would leave a session whose later /auth/refresh immediately fails the `vendor_token is None` check and revokes the session (auth_handler.py:420-422).
risk: Partial account-creation state on an unauthenticated entry point: orphaned users/user_vendor_tokens rows, or a session that cannot refresh. Self-healing on the next successful login (all writes are upserts keyed by spotify_id / (user_id,vendor)), so impact is a failed login attempt rather than durable corruption, but the state is inconsistent until then.
recommendation: Wrap the three writes in a single self._data_api.transaction() with transaction_id threaded into upsert_user/upsert_vendor_token/create_session so the user, vendor token, and session are created atomically.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code. The OAuth callback (GET /auth/callback → _handle_callback) performs three independent, untransacted writes: upsert_user (auth_handler.py:303-312), upsert_vendor_token (316-327), and create_session (339-347). AuthRepository (auth/auth_repository.py) implements every method as a bare self._data_api.execute(...) with no transaction_id, so the methods share no transaction — confirmed. The refresh-handler cross-reference is also accurate: auth_handler.py:419-422 revokes the session and raises SpotifyRevokedError when get_vendor_token returns None. The self-healing claim holds: _resolve_user_id (734-736) reuses the existing user_id keyed by spotify_id and all writes are ON CONFLICT upserts, so a retried login repairs the partial state. The platform supports the recommended fix: DataAPIClient exposes begin/commit/rollback and a transaction() context manager (data_api.py:74-107) and execute() accepts transaction_id, so threading a transaction_id through the repo methods is feasible. One minor imprecision: the finding's sub-scenario 'session-without-vendor-token causes refresh to fail' is not reachable on the callback path because upsert_vendor_token (316) runs before create_session (339); the realistic failure mode is an orphaned users/user_vendor_tokens row (user written, session not). This does not invalidate the core finding (untransacted multi-table write with a transient inconsistent intermediate state on an unauthenticated entry point). Severity P2 is defensible: real data-integrity gap, but bounded blast radius (single user's own data, no security boundary, fully self-healing on next login, worst case a failed login attempt). Could arguably be P3 given complete self-healing, but P2 is reasonable for an untransacted auth-path multi-write.

Finding is factually accurate: auth_handler.py:303-347 issues upsert_user, upsert_vendor_token, and create_session as three independent DataAPIClient.execute() calls with no shared transaction_id, and AuthRepository (auth/auth_repository.py) exposes no transaction path even though DataAPIClient.transaction() exists and is used in canonicalize.py/repositories.py. A mid-sequence failure can leave a user row with no session, or a session whose later /auth/refresh hits the vendor_token-is-None branch (auth_handler.py:419-422) and self-revokes. So the claim holds and a transaction is the correct fix — not refuted.

But severity is overstated for THIS MVP. (1) The partial state is fully self-healing and benign: all three writes are idempotent upserts keyed by spotify_id / (user_id,vendor), and _resolve_user_id (auth_handler.py:734) looks the user up by spotify_id and REUSES the same id on re-login — so an orphaned row is overwritten/completed, never duplicated, with zero manual intervention. The finding itself concedes "impact is a failed login attempt rather than durable corruption." (2) No data loss, no cross-tenant leak, no security degradation, no durable corruption. (3) Trigger probability is tiny: it needs a Data API execute() failure or Lambda timeout BETWEEN two of three sequential statements on an already-warm connection (the cold-start cost per ADR-0014 is the first DB call, well before create_session), on a rare low-volume OAuth-callback path for a small DJ circle. Recovery is the user's natural reaction (click "log in" again). This is a textbook low-probability partial-write window with a one-login-attempt blast radius and automatic retry recovery — real hardening, but P3 minor, not P2.

Confirmed and reproducible. Path: GET /auth/callback -> lambda_handler (auth_handler.py:139) -> _handle_callback (270). After OAuth exchange + premium check it issues three independent DataAPIClient.execute() calls with no shared transactionId: upsert_user (303-312 -> auth_repository.py:102), upsert_vendor_token (316-327 -> auth_repository.py:256), create_session (339-347 -> auth_repository.py:154). Each execute() auto-commits independently (data_api.py:34-49 omits transactionId unless passed). A failure between calls (Aurora cold-start 503 per ADR-0014, throttle, or Lambda timeout) commits partial state. The defect is genuine.

Two caveats that temper but do not refute it: (1) The finding's evidence claims 'auth_repository.py exposes no transaction path' — this is wrong. DataAPIClient fully exposes transaction()/begin/commit/rollback (data_api.py:75-107), the repo holds self._data_api, and canonicalize.py already uses this exact pattern, so the recommended fix is trivially feasible with no new infra. (2) Because writes are ordered token-before-session, the specific 'session exists without vendor token -> /auth/refresh revokes via vendor_token is None (420-422)' sub-scenario is unlikely on the natural partial-failure path; the realistic outcome is an orphaned users(+token) row and a failed login that self-heals on the next login (all upserts keyed by spotify_id / (user_id,vendor); _resolve_user_id at 734 re-resolves existing user). So no durable corruption, only a transient inconsistent window.

Severity: self-healing, no durable corruption, low blast radius on an account-creation path. P2 is defensible but borderline toward P3 given the impact is a single failed login attempt rather than persistent corruption. Leaving severity unchanged as the finding and its risk framing are substantively accurate.

title: batch_execute uses the broad retry set: a StatementTimeoutException mid-batch replays the entire batch — safe only by convention
where: src/collector/data_api.py:51-72 (batch_execute decorated @retry_data_api()); src/collector/data_api_retry.py:17-25,47-52 (idempotency contract); call sites e.g. src/collector/spotify_handler.py:283-350 run untransacted
evidence: batch_execute is decorated with retry_data_api() whose TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES includes StatementTimeoutException and InternalServerErrorException — codes the module's own docstring (data_api_retry.py:47-52) says 'can occur after the server has partially applied the statement'. When a batch runs without a transaction_id (e.g. _process_results_chunk in spotify_handler.py:283 calls batch_upsert_source_entities, batch_upsert_identities, batch_update_spotify_results with no tx), a partial-timeout retry re-issues the WHOLE parameterSets batch. This is correct today only because every batch SQL in repositories.py is UPSERT (ON CONFLICT) or UPDATE...WHERE id (idempotent). There is no code-level guard tying batch_execute to a transaction or to idempotent SQL.
risk: Latent double-write hazard: any future batch_execute with a non-idempotent INSERT (plain VALUES, no ON CONFLICT) or a relative UPDATE (e.g. SET position = position - 1, count = count + 1) would silently double-apply on a transient retry, corrupting counts/positions. The discipline is documented but not enforced; nothing fails CI if violated.
recommendation: Either require transaction_id on batch_execute (assert/raise when None for write SQL), or narrow batch_execute's retry to PRE_EXECUTION_ERROR_CODES when no transaction_id is supplied so post-execution timeouts are not auto-replayed outside a transaction. At minimum add a unit/lint check that batch SQL is UPSERT/idempotent.

votes.reason: The finding's technical claims all check out against the code. batch_execute (src/collector/data_api.py:51-72) is decorated @retry_data_api() which uses the broad TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES (data_api_retry.py:17-25) including StatementTimeoutException and InternalServerErrorException, and the module's own docstring (data_api_retry.py:47-52) states these can fire after the server partially applied the statement. _process_results_chunk (spotify_handler.py:283-350) does call batch_upsert_source_entities, batch_upsert_identities, and batch_update_spotify_results with no transaction_id — confirmed untransacted. I enumerated every batch_execute SQL: all untransacted ones are idempotent — INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (source_entities:312, identities:482, source_relations:383), INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (track_artists:720), and an absolute UPDATE ... WHERE id (spotify_results:809). The only non-idempotent batch (plain INSERT at playlists_repository.py:475) and all relative position updates (position-1 at :533, position+offset at :570) run inside with self._data_api.transaction() as tx_id and pass transaction_id=tx_id. There is indeed no code-level guard binding batch_execute to a transaction or to idempotent SQL. So the finding is factually correct and the hazard is real but LATENT (forward-looking) — there is no current double-write bug.\n\nHowever, the P2 severity is overstated. (1) No current code path corrupts data — the finding admits this ('correct today'). (2) The discipline is not merely 'documented but unenforced' as claimed: the contract is documented in THREE places (data_api_retry.py docstring, docs/backend/data-api.md:115 and :196-204, ADR-0001) AND there are unit tests pinning the broad transient set and the narrowed pre-execution set (tests/unit/test_data_api_retry.py:76-84, 117-151) that encode the deliberate design choice. (3) The single non-idempotent batch in the codebase is already transaction-wrapped per the documented mitigation. The precondition for harm (a future engineer adding a non-idempotent untransacted batch) is hypothetical and would itself be the new defect. This is a defense-in-depth / hardening recommendation with no live impact and existing documented guardrails — P3, not P2. The recommendation (assert transaction_id for non-idempotent writes, or a lint/unit check) remains reasonable as hardening.

Verified the claim is technically accurate but presents no live defect. batch_execute (data_api.py:51-72) is indeed decorated with @retry_data_api() whose TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES include the post-execution-unsafe StatementTimeoutException/InternalServerErrorException, and the idempotency requirement is documented (data_api_retry.py:47-52) without code-level enforcement. However, every one of the 7 current batch_execute call sites is safe: repositories.py:312/383/482/720 are UPSERT/ON CONFLICT, repositories.py:809 (batch_update_spotify_results) is absolute UPDATE...WHERE id, and the spotify chain runs untransacted but only over those idempotent statements. The two playlists_repository batches are transacted: line 577 reorder is absolute SET position=:position (idempotent), and line 475 append is a plain INSERT with no ON CONFLICT (non-idempotent) but correctly runs inside transaction_id=tx_id. The finding itself concedes there is no present bug — title says "safe only by convention," risk says any FUTURE non-idempotent batch WOULD double-apply. The genuinely silent-corruption pattern it cites (SET count=count+1) exists nowhere in the repo. The codebase already shows the discipline is real and applied: a dedicated retry_data_api_pre_execution() decorator exists and is used for commit/rollback precisely to avoid post-execution replay. The trigger is doubly conditional (someone writes new non-idempotent untransacted batch code AND a partial-apply timeout fires) — a latent future-code hazard with no plausible trigger in the shipped system. For this MVP (small DJ circle), a documented convention that 100% of current call sites already honor is acceptable; the recommended assert/lint hardening is defensible but addresses no live defect. If retained at all, this is P3 (defensive hardening), not P2.

The finding is factually accurate but mis-severitied. The reproducibility gate cannot be met: there is no concrete code path that triggers an actual double-write today, and the finding itself concedes this ("correct today only because every batch SQL is UPSERT or UPDATE...WHERE id").

Verified facts (all true): batch_execute (data_api.py:51-72) is decorated @retry_data_api() whose TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES (data_api_retry.py:17-25) includes StatementTimeoutException + InternalServerErrorException, and the docstring (47-52) does warn these can occur post-partial-apply. The spotify path _process_results_chunk (spotify_handler.py:283-350) genuinely runs untransacted (no .transaction()/transaction_id).

But tracing every real call site shows no triggerable corruption:
1. Untransacted spotify batches are all idempotent: batch_upsert_source_entities (repositories.py:312, ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE), batch_upsert_identities (482, ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE), batch_update_spotify_results (809, UPDATE...WHERE id= with ABSOLUTE assignments, no relative +=/-=). Replaying the whole batch yields identical final state.
2. The ONLY non-idempotent batch SQL that actually exists — plain INSERT INTO playlist_tracks ... VALUES (playlists_repository.py:475, no ON CONFLICT) and UPDATE SET position=:position (577) — both run INSIDE self._data_api.transaction() with transaction_id=tx_id, i.e. the documented safe mechanism. A server-side statement timeout aborts the transaction, so a replay surfaces as an error rather than silently double-applying.
3. All canonicalize.py batch calls are transacted AND UPSERT.

The "SET position = position - 1 / count = count + 1" relative-UPDATE corruption scenario in the risk section does not exist in any current batch_execute; the one relative UPDATE (playlists_repository.py:533) goes through .execute(), not batch, and is transacted.

The substantive claim — the idempotency invariant is held by convention, not enforced by code or CI (confirmed: no structural test guards batch_execute) — is real but is a defense-in-depth/maintainability gap, not a triggerable data defect. Per the rubric a latent hazard with no reproducible corruption path is not P2; it is a P3 hardening recommendation (require transaction_id or narrow retry codes for untransacted writes).

title: begin_transaction retries on post-execution error codes and can orphan a server-side transaction
evidence: begin_transaction is decorated with the broad retry_data_api(), whose TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES includes StatementTimeoutException and InternalServerErrorException. If the RDS Data API creates the transaction server-side but the response is lost (InternalServerError / timeout after creation), the wrapper retries and calls begin_transaction again, obtaining a SECOND transactionId. The first transaction is never committed or rolled back by this code (the lost transactionId is unknown to the caller), so it lingers until the Data API idle-transaction timeout.
risk: Orphaned transactions hold locks and consume a Data API transaction slot until they time out (Data API auto-rolls-back idle transactions after several minutes). Under a resume/throttle storm this can briefly block conflicting writes and inflate Aurora wake time/cost. Bounded and rare, but a correctness wrinkle in the retry design.
recommendation: Decorate begin_transaction with retry_data_api_pre_execution() (DatabaseResumingException/ServiceUnavailableError/ThrottlingException only) like commit/rollback, since a transaction that may already exist server-side must not be blindly re-created. Post-execution codes here are not safe to retry.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code; the finding is accurate. begin_transaction (src/collector/data_api.py:74-81) is decorated with the broad @retry_data_api(), whose TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES (data_api_retry.py:17-25) includes StatementTimeoutException and InternalServerErrorException — both post-execution codes. By contrast commit_transaction/rollback_transaction (lines 83, 91) deliberately use the narrower @retry_data_api_pre_execution() whose PRE_EXECUTION_ERROR_CODES (lines 27-33) excludes those two codes. The authors clearly understand the distinction: the module docstring, the IDEMPOTENCY CONTRACT comment ("can occur after the server has partially applied the statement"), and dedicated tests (test_pre_execution_retry_skips_statement_timeout, test_pre_execution_codes_are_subset_of_transient) all exist for exactly this reason. The orphaned-transaction scenario is real: if begin_transaction creates the transaction server-side but the response is lost via InternalServerError/StatementTimeout, the wrapper retries and obtains a second transactionId; the first is unknown to the caller and the transaction() context manager (data_api.py:99-107) can only commit/rollback the returned id, so the first lingers until the Data API idle-transaction auto-rollback. The recommendation to switch begin_transaction to retry_data_api_pre_execution() is sound and consistent with the project's own treatment of commit/rollback. I could not find a missed guard. Severity P3 is appropriate: the issue is real but rare (requires server-side success + lost response, not the common pre-execution DatabaseResumingException), bounded, and self-healing via the Data API idle timeout with no data corruption — worst case is brief lock contention / Aurora wake cost under a throttle storm.

Finding is technically accurate: begin_transaction uses the broad retry_data_api() (retries StatementTimeoutException/InternalServerErrorException) while commit/rollback correctly use retry_data_api_pre_execution(). The intentional split is confirmed in src/collector/data_api.py:74-97 and tested in tests/unit/test_data_api_retry.py. But it does not matter for THIS MVP. (1) No plausible trigger at this scale: an orphan requires begin_transaction to succeed server-side yet have its response lost to InternalServerError/StatementTimeout. begin_transaction does no SQL — it only allocates a transaction handle, so a StatementTimeout is essentially a non-event and an InternalServerError on bare handle allocation is vanishingly rare. The dominant retry driver here is DatabaseResumingException (cold-start wake, ADR-0014), which is a pre-execution code and safe to retry with no orphan. (2) Self-healing, tiny blast radius: the Data API auto-rolls-back idle transactions after a few minutes; the orphan is bounded by design. The "block conflicting writes / resume-throttle storm" risk needs concurrent transactions contending for the same locked rows, which a small DJ circle's write volume won't produce. The Aurora wake-cost angle is negligible and overlaps already-accepted min_acu=0 behavior (ADR-0014). The finding itself concedes it is "bounded and rare" — a correctness wrinkle, not a bug: no data corruption, no lost writes, no user-visible failure. Worst case is a few-minute lock on a handful of rows, auto-cleared, under a load profile this MVP will not see. P3 is already the correct floor for a noted-but-deferred hardening item.

Not refuted. The finding is real and reproducible. Confirmed in code: begin_transaction (src/collector/data_api.py:74-81) is decorated with the broad @retry_data_api(), whose TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES includes InternalServerErrorException and StatementTimeoutException (data_api_retry.py:17-25), while commit_transaction/rollback_transaction (lines 83-97) deliberately use the narrower @retry_data_api_pre_execution() that excludes those post-execution codes. The codebase's own docs (docs/backend/data-api.md:117-133, ADR-0001) explicitly justify this split: operations that may have already succeeded server-side must not be blindly retried. begin_transaction is functionally in the same class — a retry after a lost response re-allocates a SECOND transactionId, orphaning the first (which the caller never sees and thus cannot roll back) until the Data API idle-transaction timeout auto-rolls it back.

Concrete path: POST /curation/categories -> CategoriesRepository.create -> self._data_api.transaction() (categories_repository.py:76) -> begin_transaction(). ~20 repository methods across categories/playlists/triage/tags enter this same path. Trigger: the boto3 begin_transaction call returns InternalServerErrorException after the server allocated the transaction but the response was lost (e.g., proxy/connection error). The wrapper (data_api_retry.py:91-98) matches the code, retries, and obtains a new transactionId; the first is leaked.

Two minor inaccuracies in the finding that I considered as refutation grounds but that do not invalidate it: (1) StatementTimeoutException is an implausible trigger for begin_transaction specifically, since BeginTransaction runs no SQL statement — InternalServerErrorException is the realistic trigger, and it alone suffices. (2) The "hold locks" risk is overstated: a freshly-begun transaction with zero executed statements holds no row/table locks, so the impact is essentially one wasted Data API transaction slot for a few minutes, rarely, with no data-correctness or user-lock consequences, auto-cleaned by Aurora's idle timeout. The finding itself already labels this bounded and rare. The recommended fix (swap to retry_data_api_pre_execution, effort S) is correct and exactly mirrors the established codebase pattern. Severity P3 is appropriate — it is a genuine retry-design wrinkle but with no corruption and self-healing cleanup; it sits at the low end of P3 and the finding does not overstate it.

title: transfer_tracks INSERT runs without a transaction (and outside the present-check), unlike the sibling move_tracks
where: src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py:564-600 (transfer_tracks); contrast move_tracks at triage_repository.py:465-492 which wraps DELETE+INSERT in transaction()
evidence: transfer_tracks validates source membership with one SELECT (564-580), then performs the INSERT...ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING RETURNING (590-599) with NO transaction_id and NO surrounding transaction() block. move_tracks, the structurally similar operation, correctly wraps its DELETE+INSERT in `with self._data_api.transaction()` (465-492). Because the present-check SELECT and the INSERT are separate auto-committed statements, a track removed from the source between the two will still be inserted into the target (TOCTOU); and the INSERT itself, decorated with the broad retry via execute, is a single idempotent ON CONFLICT so the only anomaly is the stale present-check.
risk: A transfer can copy a track into the target bucket that was concurrently removed from the source, weakening the 'tracks present in source' invariant the validation is meant to enforce. Single-statement INSERT is idempotent so no double-write, but the validate-then-write is not atomic and diverges from the move path's stronger guarantee.
recommendation: Wrap the membership SELECT and the INSERT in one self._data_api.transaction() and thread transaction_id into both, matching move_tracks, so the presence check and insert are consistent.

votes.reason:
