However, severity is overstated. The practical risk hinges entirely on GitHub branch-protection configuration, which is NOT in the repo and cannot be confirmed absent. In the standard and expected setup for a repo with a dedicated PR-checks workflow, main is PR-only with pr.yml jobs as required status checks — meaning the tests DO gate what merges to main, and re-running them in deploy.yml would be redundant (PR-gates + deploy-on-merge is a common, legitimate pattern). The finding's own risk statement is conditional ("if branch protection ... is not configured", direct push, admin force-merge, hotfix) — i.e. it does not demonstrate the gap is actually open, only that the workflow itself lacks a second gate. Additionally, the migration step does fail loudly on runtime FunctionError / non-ok status (lines 127-131, sys.exit), partially mitigating the "failing migration silently corrupts prod" framing (it would block/fail the deploy, though after terraform apply and not catching successful-but-bad migrations). This is a real defense-in-depth / hardening gap resting on an unverifiable assumption, not a confirmed production-exposure bug, so P2 is the correct level rather than P1.

Finding is factually correct and NOT refuted. Verified deploy.yml (push: [main]) runs Package Lambda -> terraform apply -> migration Lambda invoke -> frontend deploy with zero pytest/lint/typecheck/terraform-plan gate; all test gating lives in pr.yml which only fires on pull_request. Critically, the assumed safety net does not exist: `gh api .../branches/main/protection` returns 404 "Branch not protected" and rulesets is empty []. So the trigger is real, not theoretical — and git history shows the maintainer does push doc/chore commits directly to main (db23cb0 "docs: correct README", c56e067 "fix: correct gitignore"), proving direct-to-main pushes happen. A code-bearing direct push would deploy untested and run an Alembic migration against the single shared multi-tenant Aurora DB. Recommendation (require the tests job as a status check, effort S) is sound. Downgrading to P2 rather than P1: this is a solo-maintainer repo (tarodo is the only collaborator) whose observed practice is PR-per-code-change (last 20 first-parent commits are nearly all merge commits; the only direct pushes were trivial docs), and the deploy fails closed on migration FunctionError/non-ok status (deploy.yml:127-131), so a failing migration is caught. That makes this a real but latent process/hardening gap (untested code CAN reach prod) rather than an active P1 defect with a high-probability trigger.

Could not refute; every load-bearing claim verified against real code and live repo config. deploy.yml (lines 3-6) triggers on push to main and the single `deploy` job runs Package Lambda -> `terraform apply -auto-approve` (line 83) -> migration Lambda invoke with `{"action":"upgrade","revision":"head"}` (line 107) -> frontend deploy (line 148), with NO pytest/lint/typecheck/build/terraform-plan/validate anywhere (independent grep returned no matches; validate+plan exist only in pr.yml). All gating lives in pr.yml, which fires only on `pull_request` (pr.yml:3-6), and its `tests`/`terraform` jobs are even conditional on path filters. migration_handler.py:53 confirms the Lambda runs raw `command.upgrade(config, "head")` against the real Aurora URL — no dry-run. Concrete reproducible path: a push to main (direct push, admin/hotfix merge, or PR merged while checks are red/skipped) → terraform apply + Alembic upgrade against the single shared multi-tenant prod Aurora with zero test verification. Crucially, the hypothesized mitigating control is confirmed ABSENT: live `gh api repos/:owner/:repo/branches/main/protection` returns HTTP 404 "Branch not protected", so no required-status-check enforcement exists and nothing forces pr.yml to pass before a deploy. Git history shows normal flow is PR-based, so this requires a non-standard commit to reach main — that keeps it at P1 (serious process/data-integrity gap with a real trigger) rather than P0 (not the default path).

title: openapi.yaml freshness is never gated — CI diffs schema.d.ts against a possibly-stale committed openapi.yaml
evidence: The frontend job runs `pnpm api:types` (which is `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml -o src/api/schema.d.ts`, frontend/package.json:22) then fails if `src/api/schema.d.ts` drifts (pr.yml:170-176). But nothing regenerates `docs/api/openapi.yaml` itself from its source of truth `scripts/generate_openapi.py` (142KB script, exists at scripts/generate_openapi.py) and diffs it. `grep -rn generate_openapi .github/workflows/` returns no match. CLAUDE.md gotcha #8 states openapi.yaml is generated and must be regenerated after editing routes in infra/*.tf or generate_openapi.py:ROUTES. If a dev adds a route but forgets to regenerate openapi.yaml, the committed (stale) openapi.yaml and committed (stale-derived) schema.d.ts still agree, so the diff check passes — the frontend ships typed against a contract missing the new route.
risk: Backend route additions/changes can merge with an out-of-date OpenAPI contract; the frontend's generated types silently lag the real API surface, producing untyped/incorrect client calls that typecheck green. Drift compounds because the only consistency check compares two artifacts that are both derived from the same stale file.
recommendation: Add a CI step (backend job) that runs `PYTHONPATH=src python scripts/generate_openapi.py` to a temp file and `git diff --exit-code docs/api/openapi.yaml`, failing if the committed openapi.yaml is not freshly generated. Trigger it on changes to infra/**, scripts/generate_openapi.py, or src/**.

votes.reason: Verified all factual claims against the cited code. pr.yml:169-176 runs `pnpm api:types` (= `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml -o src/api/schema.d.ts`, frontend/package.json:22) then fails only if `src/api/schema.d.ts` drifts. Both sides of that diff derive from the committed `docs/api/openapi.yaml`, so the check is circular. `grep -rn generate_openapi .github/` returns no match — neither pr.yml nor deploy.yml ever regenerates openapi.yaml from scripts/generate_openapi.py or diffs its output. The script's own docstring confirms it is the source of truth and must be manually kept in sync with infra/*.tf routes (matches CLAUDE.md gotcha #8). No backend test gates freshness either: the only test mentioning openapi.yaml (tests/unit/test_label_enrichment_repository.py:316) is an unrelated comment. The drift scenario is real: add a route, forget to regenerate openapi.yaml, the committed openapi.yaml and committed schema.d.ts still agree and CI passes green, shipping frontend types missing the new route. No guard was missed. Severity P2 is correct: this is a genuine CI/TEST coverage gap that defeats the one consistency check present, but it requires a developer mistake to trigger and yields missing/incorrect types (caught later by typecheck on the call site or at worst untyped calls) rather than an active runtime fault — too severe for P3, not an active defect for P1.

Mechanism verified and real. CI (pr.yml:169-176) regenerates frontend/src/api/schema.d.ts from the committed docs/api/openapi.yaml and diffs only that; nothing regenerates openapi.yaml from its source of truth scripts/generate_openapi.py (ROUTES is hand-maintained at line 1222, openapi.yaml is a tracked committed artifact, 163KB). No workflow runs generate_openapi.py as a freshness gate (deploy.yml has none either). So a forgotten regenerate leaves both committed artifacts agreeing on a stale contract and the diff passes. The committed openapi.yaml is currently in sync (regenerated to a temp and git diff was empty), so no live drift today. The recommended fix (run generate_openapi.py in CI and git diff --exit-code openapi.yaml, effort S) is correct and worthwhile. NOT refuted because the gap is genuine and the fix valid. BUT severity P2 overstates impact for THIS MVP: (1) the only two contributors already follow CLAUDE.md gotcha #8 and the git history shows they commit the regenerated openapi.yaml in the same commit as route/ROUTES edits (e.g. 92ba864, 07891ae); (2) even when triggered the failure is benign — the frontend gets types MISSING a route, not WRONG types, and pnpm typecheck (which IS gated, pr.yml:177) fails the moment any frontend code calls the new endpoint, so a stale contract surfaces at first use in CI rather than shipping a broken typed call; (3) no runtime/data/security impact — the real API surface is Terraform routes + Lambda handlers deployed independently; openapi.yaml is only codegen/doc input. Real-but-low-impact CI hardening, appropriately P3 not P2.

Confirmed, not refuted. All factual claims verified against real code: (1) frontend/package.json:22 `api:types` = `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml -o src/api/schema.d.ts` — it derives schema.d.ts FROM the committed openapi.yaml; (2) .github/workflows/pr.yml:169-176 regenerates schema.d.ts then fails only if schema.d.ts drifts; (3) `grep generate_openapi` across .github/ and deploy.yml returns nothing — neither pr.yml nor deploy.yml ever runs scripts/generate_openapi.py; (4) no .pre-commit-config.yaml, no Makefile, no other guard regenerates/diffs openapi.yaml; (5) scripts/generate_openapi.py:3808-3812 is the real source-of-truth writer (output docs/api/openapi.yaml); I ran it and it produced byte-identical output to the committed file (git diff --exit-code clean), confirming the two derived artifacts (openapi.yaml, schema.d.ts) currently agree.

Concrete reproducible drift path: a dev adds/changes a backend route (edits ROUTES in generate_openapi.py and/or a pydantic schema it imports, plus infra/*.tf) but forgets to regenerate openapi.yaml. On PR: the frontend job's `pnpm api:types` regenerates schema.d.ts from the unchanged stale openapi.yaml → no diff → check passes; the tests job runs only pytest (no openapi diff); the terraform job never touches openapi.yaml. So the route change merges with a stale OpenAPI contract and the frontend types silently lag. The only consistency check compares two artifacts both derived from the same stale file — exactly as the finding states.

Severity: P2 is correct (unchanged). It is a genuine CI quality-gate gap that defeats the stated purpose of the freshness check, with an easy fix (S). It is not P1 because it has no direct effect on running-code correctness or security — live API routes are defined by infra/*.tf, not openapi.yaml, so production behavior is unaffected; only the OpenAPI doc and the frontend's generated client types lag, and only when a developer forgets a manual step. It is not P3 because it nullifies an existing gate and the drift compounds silently in a contract-first codebase (CLAUDE.md gotcha #8 treats openapi.yaml freshness as load-bearing).

title: No real-Postgres / RDS Data API fidelity test — all repo SQL is validated only as string content against fakes
evidence: Every integration test uses an in-memory FakeRepo (tests/integration/test_curation_handler.py:26 `class FakeRepo`, test_handler.py:11-30 FakeS3/FakeSQS) and every repository unit test drives a captured-SQL fake DataAPIClient asserting only string fragments (e.g. test_triage_repository.py:249-261 asserts `'INSERT INTO triage_bucket_tracks' in sql`). No moto, no psycopg, no conftest fixture spins up Postgres for handler/repository tests (`grep -rl moto tests/` is empty; only the alembic-check CI job uses a real postgres service, and that only runs `alembic upgrade head` twice, pr.yml:73-79). test_triage_handler.py:1-27 explicitly documents: 'We do not have a psycopg-backed Aurora fixture in this repo... A future task (T28 - real-DB integration tests) could add psycopg-backed end-to-end verification.' So the dynamic IN-list/WHERE SQL, ON CONFLICT upsert semantics, and DataAPIClient._to_field type coercion (datetime tz-stripping data_api.py:152-156, Decimal->stringValue, dict/list->JSON) are never executed against a real engine.
risk: A SQL syntax error, a wrong ON CONFLICT target, a column-type mismatch, or a Data-API parameter-coercion bug passes the entire test suite because no test runs the SQL through Postgres or the Data API. The first detection point is production (the runtime DB path the system can never psycopg-bypass).
recommendation: Add a real-Postgres integration tier (the T28 task already scoped in the comment): run the alembic schema into the pr.yml postgres service and execute a handful of representative repository writes/reads (canonicalize upserts, triage move, a dynamic-WHERE list query) end to end. Even a thin psycopg-backed adapter mirroring DataAPIClient's parameter coercion would catch the most likely class of bugs.

votes.reason: Verified every cited fact in the read-only repo and all hold. (1) No moto and no psycopg-backed Postgres fixture for handler/repository tests: `grep -rli moto tests/` is empty; the only `psycopg` string in tests is the documentary comment in test_triage_handler.py:1-27; there is no conftest.py, no create_engine, no env-gated real-DB tier, and the three test dirs (unit/contract/integration) contain only fake-driven tests. (2) Repository unit tests assert only captured-SQL string fragments, confirmed at tests/unit/test_triage_repository.py:249 (`assert "INSERT INTO triage_bucket_tracks" in sql`) plus the surrounding fragment asserts. (3) Handler integration tests use in-memory fakes (FakeTriageRepo, FakeRepo at test_curation_handler.py:26, FakeS3/FakeSQS). (4) DataAPIClient._to_field coercions are present as cited — datetime tz-strip at data_api.py:152-156, Decimal->stringValue at 150-151, dict/list->JSON at 159-162. These coercions ARE unit-tested in tests/unit/test_data_api.py, but only via a hand-rolled FakeRdsDataClient (lines 178-200) that records kwargs and returns canned responses — never executed against a real Postgres engine or the actual RDS Data API, exactly as the finding states. (5) Only the alembic-check CI job touches real Postgres, running `alembic upgrade head` twice and no repository SQL (pr.yml:73-79). The finding's risk (a SQL-syntax error, wrong ON CONFLICT target, column-type mismatch, or Data-API coercion bug surviving the whole suite because no test round-trips through Postgres/the Data API) is real and the runtime DB path is the un-bypassable Data API. Minor nuance: _to_field output-shape is unit-tested, but the finding scopes its risk specifically to execution against a real engine, which is genuinely absent, so this does not undermine the finding. Severity P2 is correct: a notable coverage gap covering the system's only runtime DB path, but no concrete defect is demonstrated, so not P1, and the blast radius is too high for P3.

Finding confirmed factually and on impact. Verified: (1) `grep -rl moto tests/` is empty; no psycopg/handler fixture exists. (2) The postgres CI service in .github/workflows/pr.yml (lines 46-79) runs only `alembic upgrade head` twice — it never executes any repository SQL. (3) Every repo test asserts SQL string fragments against a captured-SQL fake DataAPIClient; every integration handler test uses FakeRepo. (4) test_triage_handler.py:1-27 self-documents the gap and the deferred T28 remediation. Impact is real for this MVP, not theoretical: there are 41 `ON CONFLICT` clauses and ~19 repository files with dynamic IN-list/WHERE SQL, and `DataAPIClient._to_field` (data_api.py:141-163) does non-trivial coercion (datetime tz-stripping, Decimal->stringValue, dict/list->JSON+typeHint) that only the fake ever sees. The runtime path is the Data API by architectural mandate (ADR-0001) and can never be psycopg-bypassed, so no fallback masks a Data-API-specific bug. deploy.yml pushes main -> terraform apply -> migration Lambda -> production with no staging tier and no SQL-executing smoke test, so the risk's "first detection point is production" is accurate. A wrong ON CONFLICT target, bad dynamic IN-list, or column-type mismatch passes the whole green suite and surfaces as a prod 500 against the real DJ users. This is not one of the accepted-behavior carve-outs. Severity P2 is correct: partial compensating controls exist (alembic schema does run in CI catching DDL errors; static-SQL string asserts catch gross typos; deploy re-runs alembic before traffic), so not P1; but the gap is structural across all write paths with a production-first failure mode on the mandated runtime path, so not P3.

Verified against real code. Reproducible CI path: a backend PR (changes.outputs.backend == true) triggers three independent jobs in .github/workflows/pr.yml — `tests` (line ~146-149) runs `pytest -q` with NO postgres service attached; `alembic-check` (line ~41-79) has a postgres:16 service but its only step runs `alembic upgrade head` twice (migration idempotency, zero data-path SQL). No conftest.py exists in tests/ at all (find returned nothing), so no fixture spins up Postgres or a Data API for handler/repo tests. Every integration test uses an in-memory FakeRepo and every repository unit test drives a captured-SQL fake asserting only string fragments — confirmed: `"... in sql"` assertions appear across 17 unit test files, with 29 in test_triage_repository.py alone (e.g. line 249 `assert "INSERT INTO triage_bucket_tracks" in sql`). The only moto/psycopg token in tests is inside the test_triage_handler.py docstring (lines 3-27) documenting the missing fixture and the deferred T28 task. data_api.py:_to_field coercion (datetime tz-strip lines 154-156, Decimal->stringValue, dict/list->JSON) and _to_parameter typeHints are never executed against a real engine. Concrete failure: a dynamic IN-list/WHERE SQL syntax error, wrong ON CONFLICT target, column-type mismatch, or a _to_field coercion bug passes the full suite (string-fragment asserts don't execute SQL) and is first detected in production on the Data-API runtime path the system can never psycopg-bypass. All evidence claims (file paths, line refs, grep-moto-empty, pr.yml:73-79 alembic-only) check out. Severity P2 is correct: a real, material TEST/CI coverage gap on the most safety-critical runtime path, but a latent absence-of-tier risk rather than an active P0/P1 defect, and the SQL is partially guarded by string-content + Python-mirror classify unit tests.

title: Admin-route gating completeness has no regression test — _ADMIN_ROUTES coverage of /admin/* is unguarded
evidence: test_handler_admin_gating.py verifies a few individual routes: `/collect_bp_releases` returns 403 without admin (lines 42-59) and `/tracks/spotify-not-found` requires admin (lines 62-76), plus a non-admin list route stays 200 (lines 79-105). No test asserts that the `_ADMIN_ROUTES` frozenset (handler.py:60-86) covers every `/admin/*` route registered in the API Gateway integrations. The system map flags exactly this as a weak-authorization footgun: 'A new /admin/* gateway route omitted from that frozenset is reachable by any authenticated (non-admin) user' because the JWT authorizer admits any valid token regardless of is_admin and admin enforcement is application-level only. There is no cross-check test comparing the gateway route table to _ADMIN_ROUTES.
risk: Adding an /admin/* route to infra/api_gateway.tf and the handler dispatch but forgetting to add it to _ADMIN_ROUTES makes an admin-only operation reachable by any authenticated tenant user, and no test catches the omission — a silent privilege-escalation regression on a multi-tenant SaaS.
recommendation: Add a unit test that enumerates the handler's admin-intended routes (or parses the gateway route definitions) and asserts each is present in `_ADMIN_ROUTES`, plus a parametrized test that every admin route returns 403 for is_admin=False. Fail CI when a new admin route is added without admin gating.

votes.reason: Verified every claim. (1) tests/unit/test_handler_admin_gating.py covers only a handful of individual routes: /collect_bp_releases 403 without/with non-admin (lines 42-59), GET /tracks/spotify-not-found requires admin (62-76), GET /tracks stays 200 (79-105). (2) _ADMIN_ROUTES frozenset is exactly at src/collector/handler.py:60-86 as cited, and the only gate is `if route_key in _ADMIN_ROUTES: _require_admin(event)` at handler.py:158-159. (3) grep for _ADMIN_ROUTES across tests/ and src/ returns ONLY handler.py — no test imports or asserts against the frozenset. (4) No test parses infra route tables: grep for `.tf`/terraform/`infra/` across tests/ returns nothing. (5) The authorizer claim is correct — src/collector/auth_authorizer.py:59-66 always returns isAuthorized:True for any valid token and merely passes is_admin into context; admin enforcement is purely application-level. (6) The footgun is real and structural: infra/api_gateway.tf registers 23 /admin/* routes, and a cross-check (comm) confirms all 23 are present in _ADMIN_ROUTES today, so no LIVE escalation exists, but nothing in CI prevents a new /admin/* route from being added to the gateway + dispatch while being omitted from the frozenset. The only related per-route 403 checks are scattered and single-route (test_artist_handler_dispatch.py:115-120 tests just GET /admin/artists/backlog; test_label_enrichment_api.py:81 tests just POST /admin/labels/enrich), with no enumerated/parametrized coverage of all admin routes. Severity P2 is correct: this is a genuine regression-test gap (TEST dimension) on a multi-tenant SaaS, not an actively exploitable bug (which would warrant P0/P1), since current coverage of _ADMIN_ROUTES is complete.

Verified the finding is technically accurate: there is no test cross-checking _ADMIN_ROUTES (handler.py:60-86) against the gateway route table, and test_handler_admin_gating.py only spot-checks 3 routes. The threat model holds — the JWT authorizer admits any valid token and admin enforcement is application-level (_route gates on `route_key in _ADMIN_ROUTES`, handler.py:158-159), so a forgotten frozenset entry on a new /admin/* route would be reachable by any authenticated tenant user, with no test catching it. The vector is active: git history shows admin routes added frequently, and the repo has a known "route = three places" omission pattern.

However, this is a TEST/defense-in-depth gap, not a live defect. I confirmed all 23 current gateway /admin/* routes ARE present in _ADMIN_ROUTES (the 2 extra entries — POST /collect_bp_releases, GET /tracks/spotify-not-found — are intentional non-/admin admin ops), so nothing is currently unguarded. The enforcement mechanism itself is already tested (403 paths + 200 non-admin), and generate_openapi.py already independently annotates every admin route with a 403/admin_required response (CI diff-checked per CLAUDE.md gotcha #8), giving a partial review signal. For this MVP (small, trusted DJ circle, token still required to reach any route), the realistic blast radius of a future forgotten entry is exposing admin enrichment/coverage ops to known authenticated users — not anonymous escalation. The recommendation (parametrized coverage test) is sound and worth doing, but the P2 severity overstates a missing-test hardening item with no present bug and a small trusted audience. Not refuted, but severity should be P3.

Finding is accurate and reproducible. Verified all core claims against the code:

1. tests/unit/test_handler_admin_gating.py is exactly 105 lines and tests only individual routes (collect_bp_releases 403 at 42-59, spotify-not-found admin at 62-76, /tracks stays 200 at 79-105). No test imports _ADMIN_ROUTES (grep confirms zero references), and no test reads infra/*.tf — so no cross-check between the gateway route table and the frozenset exists.

2. _ADMIN_ROUTES frozenset (handler.py:60-86, 25 entries) and the application-level gate (handler.py:158-159: `if route_key in _ADMIN_ROUTES: _require_admin(event)`) are exactly as described.

3. The security architecture claim holds: src/collector/auth_authorizer.py:59-66 returns {"isAuthorized": True, ...} for ANY valid token and merely passes is_admin into context. Admin enforcement is purely application-level. So a /admin/* route added to infra + handler dispatch but omitted from _ADMIN_ROUTES would be reachable by any authenticated non-admin tenant user, with no test catching it.

4. I diffed all /admin/* route_keys in infra/ against the frozenset — currently zero gaps, confirming this is a regression-prevention (TEST) gap, not an active bug, which matches the finding's framing.

Minor inaccuracy that does not refute: the finding understates existing per-route 403 coverage. Scattered 403 tests do exist (test_artist_handler_dispatch.py:115, label enrichment test files, etc.) covering most current admin routes individually. This partially mitigates the regression risk for routes copy-pasted into existing subsystems, but the central claim — no systematic test guarantees frozenset coverage of the gateway route table — is fully correct; a new admin route in a new subsystem would have no automatic guard.

Severity P2 is correct: this is a missing-regression-test / defense-in-depth gap (TEST dimension), not an active privilege-escalation vulnerability (current code has no gap). It guards a high-impact failure mode (silent privilege escalation on multi-tenant SaaS), which justifies P2 over P3, but it is not an active P0/P1 bug.

title: Frontend quality gates (typecheck/lint/test/build) skip entirely on backend-only PRs
evidence: The `frontend` job is `if: needs.changes.outputs.frontend == 'true'` (pr.yml:152-153), and the `frontend` paths-filter only matches `frontend/**`, `docs/api/openapi.yaml`, and `.github/workflows/pr.yml` (pr.yml:25-28). A backend PR that changes `src/**` routes (and regenerates docs/api/openapi.yaml) will trigger the frontend job via the openapi path — good — but a backend change that alters the API contract without touching docs/api/openapi.yaml (the exact staleness case in the openapi finding above) never runs `pnpm typecheck`/`api:types`, so the schema-drift gate is bypassed. Also note browser tests (`pnpm test:browser`) are excluded from CI entirely (confirmed: no `browser` reference in workflows), so visual/CSS/focus regressions are never gated (documented as intentional in CLAUDE.md #11, but it means jsdom-green is the only automated signal).
risk: Backend contract changes that should ripple into the typed frontend client are not validated on backend-only PRs unless openapi.yaml is also touched; combined with the missing openapi-freshness gate, frontend/backend contract drift can land green. Visual regressions are never machine-checked.
recommendation: Couple the frontend schema-freshness check to backend changes: add src/** and scripts/generate_openapi.py to the frontend paths-filter (or run the openapi-regeneration+diff in the backend job). Consider a lightweight browser-test job in CI on a runner with a headless browser to gate the most critical visual flows.

votes.reason: Verified against .github/workflows/pr.yml. The `frontend` job is gated `if: needs.changes.outputs.frontend == 'true'` (lines 152-153), and the `frontend` paths-filter only matches `frontend/**`, `docs/api/openapi.yaml`, and `.github/workflows/pr.yml` (lines 25-28). `src/**` and `scripts/generate_openapi.py` are in the `backend` filter, NOT the frontend one. So a backend-only PR that alters an API route without regenerating/committing `docs/api/openapi.yaml` skips the entire frontend job — `pnpm typecheck`, `lint`, `test`, `build`, and the `api:types` schema-drift check (lines 169-180) never run. I confirmed the schema-freshness gate's weakness: `api:types` runs `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml` (frontend/package.json) against the *committed* openapi.yaml, and nothing in any workflow (pr.yml or deploy.yml) ever invokes `scripts/generate_openapi.py` to regenerate it from code — so even the committed openapi.yaml is itself ungated for staleness (no CI/pre-commit diff against generated output; `.pre-commit-config.yaml` does not exist). Browser tests: grep for `browser` across `.github/workflows/` returns nothing, confirming `pnpm test:browser` is never run in CI. Every factual claim and line citation in the finding is correct, and the recommendation (add `src/**` + `scripts/generate_openapi.py` to the frontend filter, or run the regenerate+diff in the backend job) is sound. Severity P3 is appropriate: this is a genuine but defense-in-depth CI/test-coverage gap (not a live bug), requiring a specific maintainer mistake to manifest, and the finding already notes the browser-exclusion is intentional per CLAUDE.md #11.

Technically accurate on all sub-claims. Verified: the `frontend` job is gated on `needs.changes.outputs.frontend == 'true'` (pr.yml:151-153), the `frontend` paths-filter only matches `frontend/**`, `docs/api/openapi.yaml`, `.github/workflows/pr.yml` (pr.yml:25-28), and there is NO CI job anywhere that regenerates `docs/api/openapi.yaml` from `scripts/generate_openapi.py` to check it for staleness (grep confirms only the frontend `api:types` step touches openapi, and it only diffs `schema.d.ts` against the committed yaml, not the yaml against Python source). So a backend contract change that does not manually regenerate openapi.yaml runs zero frontend typecheck/api:types. Browser tests are confirmed excluded from CI (no `browser` reference in workflows; `test:browser` exists in package.json but is never invoked).

However, impact for THIS MVP is low and correctly bounded at P3. (1) The browser-test exclusion is an explicitly accepted behavior — CLAUDE.md #11 documents it as intentional ("run locally," CI runners have no headless browser), so that half is refuted as a known tradeoff. (2) The openapi-coupling half is a real but marginal process-hygiene gap: drift requires a developer to ship a backend contract change AND forget the documented regeneration step; the worst outcome is a stale TypeScript client, not a production outage or data corruption. The DB is reached via Data API and the frontend consumes only a small set of curation/enrich endpoints; any mismatch surfaces as a tsc error on the next frontend-touching PR or as a visible 4xx during the small DJ circle's manual use — no silent user-facing breakage with a plausible automatic trigger. It also substantially overlaps/restates the separate openapi-staleness finding from a CI angle. Legitimate P3 (TEST/ci, effort S), severity unchanged; not refuted as false, but impact stays within documented/accepted MVP tradeoffs.

Verified and reproducible against real code in .github/workflows/pr.yml and deploy.yml.

Concrete path that triggers it:
1. paths-filter `frontend` (pr.yml:25-28) matches ONLY `frontend/**`, `docs/api/openapi.yaml`, `.github/workflows/pr.yml`. The `frontend` job (pr.yml:151-181) is gated `if: needs.changes.outputs.frontend == 'true'` (pr.yml:152-153). Confirmed exact.
2. The schema-drift gate (`pnpm api:types` + `git diff src/api/schema.d.ts`, pr.yml:169-176), plus `pnpm typecheck/lint/test/build`, all live INSIDE that job. `api:types` = `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml -o src/api/schema.d.ts` (verified in frontend/package.json) — it reads the committed static openapi.yaml.
3. There is NO openapi-freshness gate anywhere: grep across both workflows shows `scripts/generate_openapi.py` is never invoked in CI; openapi.yaml (163k, 5812 lines, committed) is only ever hand-regenerated. So if a backend PR changes a route/contract via `src/**` + `scripts/generate_openapi.py:ROUTES` but the dev forgets to regenerate, openapi.yaml stays untouched → paths-filter yields backend=true, frontend=false → the entire frontend job skips. Schema drift is never caught.
4. Deploy does not save it: deploy.yml runs `scripts/deploy_frontend.sh`, which only does `pnpm build` (= `tsc -b && vite build`). tsc typechecks against the stale committed schema.d.ts/openapi.yaml, so it passes green.

Browser-test exclusion is also confirmed: zero `browser` references in workflows; `test:browser` exists in package.json but is never wired into CI (documented intentional per CLAUDE.md #11).

Severity assessment: this is a genuine CI gate-coverage hole, but it is a missing safety net rather than a defect. It only causes harm when the developer ALSO forgets to regenerate openapi.yaml (the compounding case the finding itself scopes), has no runtime/security/data impact, and the browser-test gap is explicitly intentional. P3 (low-severity TEST/process gap) is correct.

title: Two unguarded /auth/refresh callers race and trip replay detection, revoking all sessions
evidence: Two independent refresh paths POST /auth/refresh with the same cookie. The client's silent 401-retry uses tryRefreshOnce() whose module-level inflightRefresh promise dedups ONLY client-path callers (client.ts:18,59). AuthProvider.refresh() calls api('/auth/refresh') directly (AuthProvider.tsx:158) — a 200 response, so it never enters tryRefreshOnce and is invisible to inflightRefresh. The scheduled proactive refresh (scheduleRefresh -> refreshRef.current(), AuthProvider.tsx:133-135) and the bootstrap refresh (AuthProvider.tsx:201) both go through AuthProvider.refresh. The bootstrapStarted ref (AuthProvider.tsx:115,196) only guards the StrictMode double-bootstrap; it does NOT cross-coordinate with the client's 401-retry path. If a background request 401s (waking the silent retry) at roughly the same moment the scheduled timer fires AuthProvider.refresh — both POST /auth/refresh with the same cookie. Backend rotates the session hash on the first (_handle_refresh rotate_session, auth_handler.py:464-468); the second presents the now-stale hash, hitting `if session.refresh_token_hash != inbound_hash: revoke_all_user_sessions` (auth_handler.py:414-417).
risk: A timing coincidence between the proactive refresh timer and a 401-driven silent refresh revokes every session of the user (ADR-0015 all-sessions revocation), forcing a full re-login across all devices. Not malicious replay — a self-inflicted false positive the bootstrap guard does not cover.
recommendation: Route AuthProvider.refresh() through the same inflightRefresh dedup as tryRefreshOnce (single shared refresh singleton), or have AuthProvider listen for auth:refreshed/auth:expired only and never POST /auth/refresh itself except via the shared path. At minimum, suppress the scheduled timer while a client refresh is inflight.

votes.reason: Verified all cited code; the finding is correct. Two independent refresh paths POST /auth/refresh with the same Path=/auth/refresh cookie: (a) the client 401-retry via tryRefreshOnce (client.ts:17-40,58-74), and (b) AuthProvider.refresh which calls api('/auth/refresh') directly (AuthProvider.tsx:158). The module-level inflightRefresh promise (client.ts:8,18,59) is read/written ONLY inside tryRefreshOnce, so it deduplicates client-path callers but is invisible to AuthProvider.refresh — confirmed, no shared lock exists (grep found inflight only in client.ts; AuthProvider.refresh has no in-flight guard). The proactive timer (scheduleRefresh -> setTimeout -> refreshRef.current(), AuthProvider.tsx:133-135) and bootstrap (line 201) both route through AuthProvider.refresh. The bootstrapStarted ref (lines 115,196) only guards the StrictMode double-bootstrap, not cross-path coordination — explicitly confirmed by ADR-0015 line 15 and docs/frontend/auth.md line 26, which describe the bootstrap guard as covering only StrictMode. Backend logic is exactly as cited: auth_handler.py:414-417 does `if session.refresh_token_hash != inbound_hash: repo.revoke_all_user_sessions(...)` and :464-468 rotate_session on the winner. So if the proactive AuthProvider.refresh and a 401-driven tryRefreshOnce both send the same (pre-rotation) cookie concurrently, the second hits replay detection and revokes ALL user sessions (ADR-0015). The maintainers already guard the identical 'two POST /auth/refresh, same cookie' failure mode for StrictMode, proving the mechanism is real and uncovered here. Every line reference checks out. Severity P1 holds: blast radius is every device of the user logged out, but the trigger is an intermittent timing coincidence (30-min access TTL with 5-min leeway means the proactive timer normally fires while the token is still valid; the race needs an overlap such as a deferred timer + focus/reconnect refetch after sleep, or a slow/cold-start refresh round-trip) rather than a deterministic occurrence — consistent with P1 (high impact, lower frequency, no guard), not P0.

Verified the finding against the code; it holds up and is not an accepted behavior.

Code confirms two independent /auth/refresh paths with NO shared mutual exclusion:
- client.ts:8,18,36 — inflightRefresh dedups ONLY the silent 401-retry path (tryRefreshOnce). It is entered solely on a 401 response to some OTHER request (client.ts:58-59).
- AuthProvider.tsx:158 — refresh() calls api('/auth/refresh', {method:'POST'}) directly. That POST returns 200, so api() never enters tryRefreshOnce and never touches inflightRefresh. Confirmed: the proactive timer (scheduleRefresh -> refreshRef.current(), lines 133-135) and bootstrap (line 201) both route through this unguarded path.
- Backend confirms the blast radius: auth_handler.py:464-468 rotates the session hash on the first refresh; auth_handler.py:414-417 sees the stale hash on the second and calls revoke_all_user_sessions -> RefreshReplayDetectedError. Per ADR-0015 this kills every session on every device.

The authors already consider this exact class of bug real, not theoretical: the bootstrapStarted guard + comment (AuthProvider.tsx:109-115) exists specifically to stop two same-cookie refreshes from tripping replay detection under StrictMode. The finding correctly notes that guard does NOT cross-coordinate with the client 401-retry path.

Trigger is plausible, not purely theoretical:
- Access token TTL = 1800s (auth_settings.py:40), proactive timer fires 5 min early (REFRESH_LEEWAY_MS), and jwt_utils.py:124 rejects exactly at exp with zero leeway.
- usePolling (usePolling.ts:19-21) fires playback refreshDevices on a 30s interval AND on every window 'focus' — a recurring, real source of background requests routed through api()/tryRefreshOnce.
- Background-tab setTimeout throttling defers the proactive timer past the 30-min token expiry; on refocus, the focus-driven poll 401s (silent refresh fires) at the same instant the un-throttled proactive timer finally fires (AuthProvider.refresh fires). Two same-cookie POSTs race. DJs leaving the app open in a tab during sets and refocusing is exactly this pattern, and 7-day refresh TTL means the 25-min boundary is crossed constantly.

This is self-inflicted, distinct from the accepted ADR-0015 replay behavior (which is a security response to an ACTUAL cookie replay; here there is no attacker). Impact is severe (full multi-device re-login) with a known cheap fix (single shared refresh singleton). Window is narrow so it won't hit every session — consistent with P1, not P0. Not refuted.

Confirmed the finding against real code. Two independent refresh paths POST /auth/refresh with the same cookie and share NO dedup:

1. Path A (proactive/bootstrap): scheduleRefresh's setTimeout -> refreshRef.current() -> AuthProvider.refresh() (AuthProvider.tsx:133-135, 156-158) calls api('/auth/refresh', {POST}) directly. Backend returns 200, so api() never enters the res.status===401 branch (client.ts:58) and never touches inflightRefresh.
2. Path B (silent 401 retry): a background api() call gets 401 with a truthy token -> tryRefreshOnce() (client.ts:58-59), guarded only by the module-level inflightRefresh (client.ts:18), which Path A is invisible to.

The bootstrapStarted ref (AuthProvider.tsx:115,196) guards only the StrictMode double-bootstrap, not cross-path coordination. Backend confirms the harm: _handle_refresh rotates the session hash on the first request (auth_handler.py:464-468); the second request presents the now-stale hash and hits `if session.refresh_token_hash != inbound_hash: revoke_all_user_sessions(...)` then raises RefreshReplayDetectedError (auth_handler.py:414-417; auth_repository.py:225-235 revokes ALL sessions WHERE user_id matches). This matches ADR-0015 all-sessions revocation.

Concrete reproducible path: authenticated user, access TTL 1800s (infra/variables.tf:380), proactive refresh scheduled at expiresAt-5min (REFRESH_LEEWAY_MS, AuthProvider.tsx:100,132). In a long-lived/background tab the setTimeout is throttled and fires LATE — after the access token has actually expired. At that moment (a) an outstanding/new background request 401s on the genuinely-expired token -> tryRefreshOnce() dispatches fetch #1 to /auth/refresh, and (b) the delayed proactive timer fires AuthProvider.refresh() -> api('/auth/refresh') dispatches fetch #2 — both carrying the original cookie because the rotated Set-Cookie from the first response hasn't been applied yet. Backend processes them serially: first rotates, second sees stale hash -> revoke_all_user_sessions -> every device forced to full OAuth re-login. The auth:refreshed handler's scheduleRefresh reschedule (AuthProvider.tsx:252) only fires after Path B's response lands, so it cannot prevent the already-dispatched concurrent fetch. No test covers the cross-path case (client.test.ts only exercises the single 401-retry path). The recommendation (route AuthProvider.refresh through the shared inflightRefresh singleton) is correct.

Severity: borderline P1/P2. The timing window is narrow and a natural 401 is unlikely exactly when the timer fires during normal operation (token still valid 5 min before expiry), so the realistic trigger depends on background-tab timer throttling aligning a real expiry-driven 401 with the delayed proactive fire. But the impact is severe and user-facing (all-session revocation across all devices, full re-login), the trigger is a normal usage pattern for a cross-device 'pro tool', and the team already engineered the bootstrapStarted guard against the identical double-refresh-> replay mechanism — evidence they treat it as serious. Keeping P1.

title: Revoking a session does not invalidate already-issued access tokens (stateless authorizer)
evidence: auth_authorizer.lambda_handler only calls verify_access_token (auth_authorizer.py:50) and returns the identity context — it never consults user_sessions (grep confirms no repository/session lookup in auth_authorizer.py). Therefore revoke_session (logout auth_handler.py:521, DELETE /me/sessions/{id} auth_handler.py:618) and revoke_all_user_sessions on replay (auth_handler.py:416) mark the session revoked but the already-minted access JWT keeps verifying until its own exp. Access TTL defaults to 1800s (auth_settings.py:39-41). API Gateway additionally caches the authorizer result for 300s keyed on the Authorization header (infra/auth.tf:163, variables.tf:413-417), so even token-expiry propagation lags. The ADR-0015 'revoke all sessions on replay' trip-wire is meant to neutralize a stolen cookie, but a stolen/leaked access token survives that revocation for up to ~30 minutes.
risk: An attacker holding a stolen access token retains full API access for up to the access TTL after the user (or replay detection) revokes all sessions. Logout and per-session revocation are likewise not immediate. This materially weakens the security guarantee ADR-0015 is designed to provide.
recommendation: Either shorten the access TTL substantially (e.g. 5 min) to bound the exposure window, or have the authorizer (or each downstream handler) validate session_id against an active-session/revocation check (e.g. a short-TTL revocation list keyed by session_id). Document the residual window in ADR-0015 if a stateless authorizer is retained.

votes.reason: All cited code verified and behaves as claimed. auth_authorizer.lambda_handler (auth_authorizer.py:39-66) only calls verify_access_token (line 50) and returns identity context including session_id; grep confirms no repository/user_sessions/session lookup in the authorizer. The three revocation paths mark sessions revoked in the DB only — replay revoke_all_user_sessions (auth_handler.py:416), logout revoke_session (line 521), DELETE /me/sessions/{id} revoke_session (line 618) — none invalidate the already-minted access JWT. Access TTL default is 1800s (auth_settings.py:39-41, JWT_ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS default "1800"), and the API Gateway authorizer result cache TTL default is 300s keyed on the Authorization header (auth.tf:163 -> variables.tf:413-417, default 300). Critically, I confirmed no compensating control downstream: curation_handler._user_id_or_none (curation_handler.py:171-181) reads user_id straight from event.requestContext.authorizer.lambda with no get_active_session/session_id re-validation, and the session_id placed in the context is never consumed for a revocation check anywhere in the request path. The refresh flow does check get_active_session (line 410), but that is the cookie path and does not help the stolen-access-token case the finding targets. A stolen access token therefore keeps full API access for up to the access TTL (~30 min by default) after revoke-all-on-replay, which directly undermines the ADR-0015 trip-wire. Severity left unchanged: the technical claim is fully correct and it weakens an explicitly documented security control; it sits at the P1/P2 boundary (deliberate stateless-JWT pattern with a finite, self-healing window and requires the attacker to already hold a token), but P1 is defensible given the documented guarantee it breaks.

Code-level claim is TRUE and verified: auth_authorizer.py (lines 39-66) is stateless — it only calls verify_access_token and returns identity context, never consulting user_sessions. A stolen access JWT keeps verifying until its own exp. TTLs confirmed in infra/variables.tf: access=1800s (377-381), authorizer cache=300s (413-417). Access exp is hard-enforced (jwt_utils.py:123-124).

But the P1 severity and the central risk argument do not hold for this MVP:

1. ADR-0015's guarantee is NOT weakened. ADR-0015 is explicitly about a stolen HttpOnly REFRESH COOKIE; its trip-wire forces all-session revocation so the stolen cookie can no longer mint tokens. That holds fully: POST /auth/refresh checks get_active_session (auth_handler.py:410), so after revocation the stolen refresh cookie is dead. The finding's claim that this 'materially weakens the security guarantee ADR-0015 is designed to provide' is wrong — ADR-0015 never promised instant access-token invalidation.

2. The finding constructs a DIFFERENT threat: a separately-stolen, still-valid access JWT. That presupposes the attacker already holds a live bearer token, which lives only in browser memory (never persisted — CLAUDE.md gotcha #5; no localStorage/sessionStorage found). An attacker who can read in-memory tokens (XSS/device compromise) already controls the live session; the residual window adds little.

3. The exposure is bounded and self-healing: max ~30 min (access exp) + up to 5 min cache, then the attacker is locked out because refresh is session-checked. No standing bypass, no persistent compromise.

4. Stateless authorizer + short-lived access token + session-checked refresh is the standard industry pattern; a bounded access-token window is its deliberate, accepted tradeoff — analogous to the accepted cold-start 503 (ADR-0014) and min_acu=0 behaviors here. Audience is a small DJ circle with no high-value assets.

Real, correctly-described residual window, but it is a documentation/minor-hardening item (note the window in ADR-0015 and/or shorten access TTL), not a P1. Refuting the severity to P3.

Finding is technically accurate and reproducible from real code — NOT refuted. Verified every claim:

1. auth_authorizer.lambda_handler (auth_authorizer.py:39-66) is stateless: it calls only verify_access_token (line 50) and returns the identity context (user_id/session_id/is_admin). grep confirms zero session/repository lookups in the file.
2. All revoke paths touch only the DB session row, not issued access JWTs: _handle_logout -> repo.revoke_session (auth_handler.py:521), _handle_revoke_session -> repo.revoke_session (line 618), replay -> repo.revoke_all_user_sessions (line 416).
3. Access TTL default 1800s confirmed (auth_settings.py:39-41).
4. API Gateway authorizer cache default 300s, keyed on $request.header.Authorization (auth.tf:162-163 -> variables.tf:413-417 default 300).
5. ADR-0015 explicitly frames revoke-all-sessions as the cookie-theft trip-wire ("the attacker's stolen token becomes useless"); the finding correctly notes this guarantee does not extend to an already-minted access token.

Concrete reproducible path: attacker holds a valid (leaked/XSS-exfiltrated) access JWT -> user or replay detection revokes all sessions (auth_handler.py:416) -> session row marked revoked -> attacker hits GET /me; API Gateway invokes the stateless authorizer, which verifies signature+exp only and returns isAuthorized:true; _handle_me trusts ctx user_id/session_id without an active-session check. Window bounded by access TTL (≤~1800s), marginally extended by 300s authorizer cache.

Severity correction P1 -> P2: This is the standard, documented stateless-JWT revocation trade-off, not a novel defect. The precondition is that an access token is ALREADY compromised; the genuinely long-lived credential (HttpOnly refresh cookie) IS immediately revoked, and the replay trip-wire still fully protects it. What survives is only a short-lived (≤30 min), non-persisted access token. That bounded residual window for an already-stolen credential is a real hardening gap worth fixing (shorten TTL or add a session_id revocation check), but it is not an acute, independently-exploitable vulnerability warranting P1. The finding itself offers "document the residual window" as an acceptable resolution, consistent with P2.

title: Refresh handler mutates vendor token and session non-atomically (no transaction)
evidence: _handle_refresh performs three separate Data API auto-commit statements with no enclosing transaction (grep shows no transaction()/begin_transaction in auth_handler.py or auth_repository.py): upsert_vendor_token writes the new Spotify access+refresh tokens (auth_handler.py:444-455), then issue_refresh_token mints a new CLOUDER refresh JWT (auth_handler.py:457-463), then rotate_session updates the session hash to the new JWT's hash (auth_handler.py:464-468). If rotate_session fails after the vendor-token upsert commits, the persisted Spotify refresh token has advanced but the session still holds the OLD CLOUDER hash; the client receives an error. A subsequent retry with the old cookie still matches the old hash and re-runs, decrypting the just-persisted new Spotify refresh token — recoverable, but if Spotify already invalidated the prior refresh token mid-flight, state can drift. There is no compensating action and no atomicity across the Spotify-side and CLOUDER-side rotations.
risk: Partial failure between the vendor-token write and session rotation can leave the user's stored Spotify refresh token and CLOUDER session out of sync, occasionally requiring a full re-login. Low frequency (depends on a mid-call Data API failure) but silent when it happens.
recommendation: Wrap the vendor-token upsert and rotate_session in a single DataAPIClient.transaction(), threading transaction_id into both repo calls. The Spotify HTTP refresh cannot be in the DB transaction, but the two DB writes that depend on it should commit atomically.

votes.reason: Verified all cited claims against the code. auth_handler.py:444-468 performs three sequential operations in _handle_refresh: upsert_vendor_token (444-455, writes new Spotify access+refresh tokens), issue_refresh_token (457-463, in-memory JWT mint), rotate_session (464-468, updates session hash). Confirmed each repo call (auth_repository.py:256-287 upsert, 202-213 rotate_session) issues a standalone data_api.execute() with no transaction_id, and these methods don't even accept a transaction_id parameter. data_api.py:28-49 shows execute() auto-commits when no transaction_id is passed; a transaction()/begin_transaction() context manager EXISTS (data_api.py:99-107) but grep confirms it is never invoked in auth_handler.py or auth_repository.py. So the non-atomic three-write claim is factually correct. I traced the recovery path: on a rotate_session failure after the vendor upsert commits, the session keeps the OLD hash, the client retries with the OLD cookie which still matches (line 415, not a replay), and the retry decrypts the just-persisted NEW Spotify refresh token and re-refreshes — usually self-healing, exactly as the finding states. The non-recoverable drift requires a compound rare event (Data API failure between the two writes AND Spotify invalidating the prior token mid-flight), worst-case outcome being a single re-login. This matches a reliability defect that is real, low-frequency, recoverable, with no security/data-loss impact: a correct P2. Severity and recommendation (wrap the two dependent DB writes in DataAPIClient.transaction()) are both sound. Cannot refute.

The non-atomic two-write claim is technically accurate (upsert_vendor_token at auth_handler.py:444-455 and rotate_session at 464-468 are separate auto-commit Data API calls with no enclosing transaction), but the impact does not warrant P2 for this MVP.

Refutation:
1. Built-in clean recovery. On a rotate_session failure the 200 response (with the new cookie) is never returned (auth_handler.py:486-504), so the client keeps its OLD cookie and the DB keeps its OLD session hash — the two stay mutually consistent. The next refresh retry matches the old hash (auth_handler.py:414-415), reads the freshly-persisted Spotify token, and succeeds. The finding itself concedes this is "recoverable."

2. The "state drift" scenario is implausible. It requires Spotify to have rotated AND invalidated the just-issued NEW refresh token before the retry. Spotify invalidates the OLD refresh token when minting a new one, not the new one — and the retry uses the NEW (persisted, valid) token. Moreover spotify_oauth.py:165 (`refresh = payload.get("refresh_token") or fallback_refresh`) means Spotify frequently does NOT return a new refresh token on the refresh grant, so the stored token is unchanged and there is zero drift surface in the common case.

3. The trigger is heavily guarded. DataAPIClient.execute is wrapped with @retry_data_api() (data_api.py:27), giving 5 attempts with full-jitter exponential backoff on exactly the transient codes (DatabaseResumingException, StatementTimeout, InternalServerError, ServiceUnavailable, Throttling) that are the only plausible mid-flight failures on Aurora min_acu=0. The IDEMPOTENCY CONTRACT (data_api_retry.py:47-52) is satisfied here: upsert uses ON CONFLICT (auth_repository.py:269-275) and rotate_session is an idempotent UPDATE by PK. A failure surviving 5 retries AND landing in the inter-statement window is far rarer than "low frequency."

4. Worst-case outcome is an accepted posture. The absolute worst case is one failed refresh that self-recovers on the next request, or pathologically a re-login — which ADR-0015 already establishes as the accepted recovery for the refresh subsystem. No data corruption, no security boundary crossed, no lost user content; auth tokens are by-design reconstructable via /auth/login.

The transaction() mechanism exists (used in canonicalize.py) so the hardening is feasible, but a retry-guarded, self-recovering, re-login-worst-case window on reconstructable auth state is a P3 hardening note, not a P2 reliability defect for a small-DJ-circle MVP.

Reproduction path is real and constructible. Route POST /auth/refresh -> _handle_refresh (auth_handler.py:392-504). Preconditions: valid CLOUDER refresh cookie whose sha256 matches the active session hash (line 414-415), and a stored Spotify vendor token with a non-null refresh_token_enc (line 419-420). Sequence: upsert_vendor_token commits the new Spotify access+refresh tokens (line 444, auth_repository.py:256-287), then issue_refresh_token mints a new JWT in-memory (line 457), then rotate_session does a separate auto-commit UPDATE of the session hash (line 464, auth_repository.py:202-213). I confirmed by grep that neither auth_handler.py nor auth_repository.py threads a transaction_id or opens DataAPIClient.transaction() (which exists, data_api.py:99-107) — every execute auto-commits independently. Trigger: rotate_session raises after the vendor upsert commits — e.g. a non-transient ClientError, or a transient code (StatementTimeoutException/InternalServerErrorException) that exhausts all 5 retries in data_api_retry.py, or a Lambda timeout between the two calls. Then the persisted Spotify refresh token has advanced while user_sessions.refresh_token_hash still holds the OLD value, and the client gets a 500 (lambda_handler catch-all, auth_handler.py:117-130). So the non-atomicity is factually present and the trigger path is real — not refuted.

Severity is overstated, though. (1) The window is self-healing in nearly all cases: a retry with the OLD cookie still matches the old hash (line 415 does NOT trip replay detection), re-reads the now-persisted new vendor token (line 419), decrypts it, and refreshes against Spotify again — the finding itself concedes "recoverable". (2) The codebase has a documented, sanctioned non-transactional idempotency contract (data_api_retry.py:47-52): writes outside a transaction MAY use UPSERT/ON CONFLICT instead. upsert_vendor_token uses INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE and rotate_session is an idempotent absolute-value UPDATE, so this code conforms to the project's own atomicity policy rather than violating it. (3) Spotify commonly does NOT rotate the refresh token on every refresh; spotify_oauth.py:165 explicitly falls back to the prior refresh token when the response omits one, so in the typical case the 'new' persisted token equals the old one and there is zero drift. Genuine unrecoverable drift requires a triple coincidence: rotate_session fails AND Spotify rotated the refresh token this call AND Spotify invalidated the old one immediately. Worst realistic outcome is an occasional single-user re-login — no data corruption, no security exposure, no cross-user blast radius. That is a hardening nit (P3), not a medium-likelihood/moderate-impact P2.

title: JWT verification disables iat checking, accepting future-issued tokens in production
evidence: _decode sets options={'verify_iat': False} with comment 'iat may be in the future in tests' (jwt_utils.py:112). This disables iat validation for all production tokens, not just tests. Expiry is checked manually with a strict `if now_ts >= exp` (jwt_utils.py:124) with no clock-skew leeway. Combined: a token whose iat is in the future is accepted, and there is no nbf/leeway handling — the only temporal gate is exp, checked exactly against the authorizer's wall clock.
risk: Disabling iat verification removes a defense against tokens minted with manipulated/future timestamps and is a test-only concession leaking into production verification. Strict exp comparison with zero leeway can also reject a just-valid token under minor clock skew between the issuing auth Lambda and the verifying authorizer Lambda, intermittently rejecting fresh tokens.
recommendation: Inject `now` in tests instead of disabling iat globally; enable verify_iat in production. Add a small leeway (e.g. a few seconds) to the manual exp check, or use PyJWT's leeway parameter, to absorb cross-Lambda clock skew.

votes.reason: Code reading is accurate: jwt_utils.py:109-125 does set options={'verify_iat': False, 'verify_exp': False} and re-checks expiry manually with a zero-leeway `if now_ts >= exp` (line 124), with no nbf/leeway. However, the P2 SEC severity is overstated. (1) iat: PyJWT 2.12.1's `_validate_iat` (verified via source inspection) only rejects tokens where `iat > now + leeway` — i.e. future-issued tokens. Disabling it has no security impact here because: tokens are HS256 and can only be minted by a holder of the shared signing secret (verified: only auth_handler.py issues them); a `grep` shows `iat` is never consumed for any security decision (no token-age, no replay-window, no password-change invalidation — it is only in the `require` list, so its presence is still enforced); and `exp` is still enforced. A 'manipulated/future timestamp' attack as described requires already possessing the HMAC secret, at which point iat validation is moot. So this is defense-in-depth hardening, not an exploitable vuln. (2) The zero-leeway exp check: both issuer and verifier run on AWS Lambda with NTP-synced clocks (sub-second skew); access TTL is 1800s and refresh 604800s (auth_settings.py:40,43). Skew only matters in the final ~1s of validity, where rejecting a just-expired token is correct and the client refreshes transparently — the 'intermittently rejecting fresh tokens' risk is not realistic. This is a legitimate minor cleanup (a test concession leaked into prod config, slightly brittle exp check) with no demonstrated security impact, so it belongs at P3, not P2.

Refuted on impact. Both sub-claims lack a plausible trigger in this single-issuer HS256 system.

(1) Future-iat acceptance: Tokens are HS256-signed with a server-held secret (resolve_jwt_signing_key), and the only issuer is the auth Lambda itself (auth_handler.py:331,349,457,470), which sets iat=int(now.timestamp()) from its own clock. The signature — not iat — is the security gate. An attacker who could forge a token with an arbitrary future iat would already possess the signing secret, and with it could set iat to any value (including the past), so verify_iat provides zero marginal defense. There is no path by which a legitimately-signed token carries a meaningfully malicious future iat. The "test-only concession leaking into production" framing is cosmetic: in this architecture verify_iat protects against nothing.

(2) Zero-leeway exp rejecting fresh tokens: Issuer (auth_handler) and verifier (auth_authorizer) are both Lambdas on the same NTP-synced AWS infrastructure with sub-second clock alignment. The strict `if now_ts >= exp` only bites at the exact expiry boundary — i.e. a token in its final second of life, which is indistinguishable from normal expiry. It does NOT intermittently reject freshly-minted tokens (those have exp minutes/hours out). At worst it forces a refresh one second early, which the SPA handles transparently. No user-visible harm for the MVP (small DJ circle).

Both the leeway hardening and enabling verify_iat are reasonable nits, but neither is exploitable or operationally impactful here. P2 (functional/security defect worth scheduling) overstates a no-trigger hardening item; P3 at most.

Refuted. The headline security claim does not hold for this architecture, and no reproducible exploit/failure path exists.

THREAT MODEL (traced from real code):
- Tokens are HS256 / symmetric (jwt_utils.py:12 `_ALGO = "HS256"`), minted ONLY by the auth Lambda via issue_access_token/issue_refresh_token (auth_handler.py:331,349,457,470) and verified by the authorizer with the SAME server-held secret resolve_jwt_signing_key() (auth_authorizer.py:34,49-50). There is no untrusted/third-party token issuer.
- _decode (jwt_utils.py:104-114) keeps signature verification ON (verify_signature defaults True) and pins algorithms=[_ALGO], so an attacker cannot forge ANY token without the signing secret.

WHY iat IS NOT A SECURITY GATE HERE:
- For self-issued symmetric tokens, `iat` carries no security value. An attacker who could set a "manipulated/future iat" would need the secret — and with the secret they could mint a fully valid token with any iat/exp anyway. So disabling verify_iat removes no real defense. There is no route/queue-message/input where a future-iat token from an untrusted source is accepted, because every accepted token is signed with the server's own secret. The "defense against manipulated/future timestamps" premise is false; refuted at the security level.
- If anything, enabling verify_iat would make the system MORE fragile (reject just-minted tokens when the issuer Lambda's clock runs slightly ahead of the verifier's), not more secure — opposite of the finding's framing.

CLOCK-SKEW / STRICT exp CLAIM — NOT TRIGGERABLE:
- exp check is `if now_ts >= exp` (jwt_utils.py:124). Access TTL defaults to 1800s/30min (auth_settings.py:39-40, JWT_ACCESS_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS default "1800"). A FRESH token has ~30 min of headroom against exp. To reject a fresh token, the verifying authorizer's wall clock would have to be ~30 minutes AHEAD of the issuing Lambda's. Both run on AWS Lambda with NTP-synced clocks (sub-second skew in practice). The "intermittently rejecting fresh tokens" scenario cannot be constructed from real code. Zero-leeway exp only rejects genuinely-expired tokens, which is correct behavior.

CONCLUSION: No concrete attack path and no realistic failure path. The recommendation (inject `now` in tests; small exp leeway) is reasonable hygiene/hardening, not a P2 vulnerability. At most this is a P3 cleanliness note (a test-only comment/concession in prod config, and a marginal skew-fragility tradeoff). Not P2.

title: No test coverage for replay detection / revoke-all or the authorizer signing-key cache
evidence: test_auth_authorizer.py forces JWT_SIGNING_KEY env (line 17) so resolve_jwt_signing_key never exercises the SSM path, and there is no test for the _cached_signing_key TTL/expiry logic (_SIGNING_KEY tuple, auth_authorizer.py:27-36) nor for Authorization header case/whitespace variance beyond the happy path. The replay branch revoke_all_user_sessions (auth_handler.py:416) and the highest-impact security behavior in the subsystem are not asserted by any unit test that drives _handle_refresh with a stale hash (test files exist for callback/login/logout/me/refresh/ytmusic, but the refresh test does not cover the hash-mismatch -> revoke-all path per its content). jwt_utils tests omit the future-iat acceptance and the require-claims (missing session_id) rejection.
risk: The most security-critical and most operationally painful path (replay -> revoke all sessions) can silently regress. The signing-key cache (a correctness/perf seam that, if broken, could serve a stale key after rotation) is untested.
recommendation: Add unit tests: (1) _handle_refresh with a stale inbound hash asserts revoke_all_user_sessions is called and RefreshReplayDetectedError raised; (2) authorizer signing-key cache returns cached key within TTL and re-resolves after expiry; (3) jwt require-claims rejection (missing session_id) and the future-iat behavior to lock in intended semantics.

votes.reason:
The finding's primary claim is false. Its title and risk center on "No test coverage for replay detection / revoke-all," and recommendation (1) asks to add a unit test that drives _handle_refresh with a stale hash, asserts revoke_all_user_sessions is called, and RefreshReplayDetectedError is raised. That exact test already exists: tests/unit/test_auth_handler_refresh.py:139-158 (test_refresh_replay_revokes_session_family) sets repo.get_active_session to return a session with hash_str="WRONG" (the stale/mismatch case), drives lambda_handler end-to-end, and asserts body["error_code"] == "refresh_replay_detected" (the RefreshReplayDetectedError branch at auth_handler.py:417) plus repo.revoke_all_user_sessions.assert_called_once() (the call at auth_handler.py:416). The same path is also covered as integration in tests/integration/test_auth_flow.py:277-288. The finding's evidence claim "the refresh test does not cover the hash-mismatch -> revoke-all path per its content" is a direct misread of the file. The most security-critical behavior the finding worries about is in fact asserted.

Secondary claims have minor truth but do not carry P2: (a) the authorizer signing-key cache TTL/expiry branch (_cached_signing_key, auth_authorizer.py:27-36) is genuinely untested because the fixture forces JWT_SIGNING_KEY and resets the cache each test — but resolve_jwt_signing_key's env AND SSM paths are directly tested in tests/unit/test_auth_settings.py:80-98, and the cache is a thin 300s memoization (low-impact regression); (b) jwt_utils omits missing-session_id require-claims and future-iat assertions — minor edge gaps. These residual gaps are low-value hardening nits (at most P3), not the P2 "most security-critical path can silently regress" the finding describes, since that path is fully covered. Refuting because the cited code/test content contradicts the headline claim and the surviving gaps do not justify the severity.

The finding's central, "highest-impact" claim is factually wrong. It asserts the replay -> revoke-all path "is not asserted by any unit test that drives _handle_refresh with a stale hash" and "the refresh test does not cover the hash-mismatch -> revoke-all path per its content." But tests/unit/test_auth_handler_refresh.py:139 (test_refresh_replay_revokes_session_family) does exactly this: it stores hash="WRONG" (line 144) to force the stale-hash/replay condition, drives auth_handler.lambda_handler, and asserts statusCode 401, error_code "refresh_replay_detected", and repo.revoke_all_user_sessions.assert_called_once() (line 158). That is precisely recommendation (1), already implemented. It is additionally covered by tests/integration/test_auth_flow.py:277-288 (end-to-end 401 + refresh_replay_detected) and tests/unit/test_auth_errors.py:40 (RefreshReplayDetectedError -> 401). So the "most security-critical, can silently regress" path that justifies the severity is in fact double-covered.

What remains true is minor: auth_authorizer._cached_signing_key TTL/expiry (lines 27-36) is not exercised because the fixture forces JWT_SIGNING_KEY env, and test_jwt_utils.py lacks future-iat and missing-session_id rejection cases. These are low-impact: the cache is a trivial 300s monotonic check, key rotation is rare, and the env path actually used by the authorizer IS tested. With the primary justification refuted and only small low-trigger gaps left, the finding does not stand as written at P2.

The finding's load-bearing claim is false. It asserts "the refresh test does not cover the hash-mismatch -> revoke-all path" and "the most security-critical path (replay -> revoke all sessions)... is not asserted by any unit test," but tests/unit/test_auth_handler_refresh.py:139 (test_refresh_replay_revokes_session_family) drives _handle_refresh with a stale inbound hash (get_active_session returns a session with hash_str="WRONG"), and asserts both body["error_code"] == "refresh_replay_detected" (the RefreshReplayDetectedError -> 401 path) AND repo.revoke_all_user_sessions.assert_called_once(). This is exactly the finding's recommendation (1). The path is additionally covered end-to-end in tests/integration/test_auth_flow.py:277-288. The finding's claim that the SSM path is never exercised is also wrong: tests/unit/test_auth_settings.py:86 (test_resolve_jwt_signing_key_via_ssm) tests resolve_jwt_signing_key's SSM branch — the authorizer test forcing JWT_SIGNING_KEY env does not create a coverage gap because that branch is covered elsewhere. Two residual gaps in the finding are real but minor: (a) no test for the auth_authorizer._cached_signing_key TTL/expiry seam (auth_authorizer.py:27-36) — but the cache only memoizes for 300s and the env-var resolution path makes a stale-key window self-healing; (b) jwt_utils tests omit the require-claims (missing session_id, jwt_utils.py:110) and verify_iat=False (jwt_utils.py:112) edge cases. These remaining gaps are low-impact and do not support the P2 "most security-critical path can silently regress" framing, since that path is in fact tested twice.

title: Docs claim VITE_API_BASE_URL drives API requests but the runtime client ignores it
evidence: api/client.ts computes baseUrl from window.location.origin (client.ts:5-6) and never reads VITE_API_BASE_URL/lib/env.ts. CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 and the dev-setup instructions state the SPA requires frontend/.env.local with VITE_API_BASE_URL pointing at the terraform api_endpoint to drive requests; docs/frontend/auth.md describes apiClient behavior without noting it is same-origin only. The env var is referenced only in tests and the vite-env type decl.
risk: A developer following the documented setup may point VITE_API_BASE_URL at a cross-origin API endpoint expecting requests to go there; they silently go to window.location.origin instead, breaking local dev against a remote API and producing confusing auth-cookie/CORS behavior (refresh cookie is Path=/auth/refresh same-origin).
recommendation: Update CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 and docs/frontend/auth.md to state the SPA is same-origin and the client uses window.location.origin; either remove VITE_API_BASE_URL or wire it into client.ts as the intended override.

votes.reason: The finding's literal code claim is correct: client.ts:5-6 derives baseUrl from window.location.origin and never reads VITE_API_BASE_URL (the var lives only in vite-env.d.ts:4, lib/env.ts, tests, and vite.config.ts). But the finding's risk narrative — the part that makes it actionable — is factually wrong and rests on a misread of the architecture. VITE_API_BASE_URL IS required and DOES drive where dev requests go: vite.config.ts:30-58 reads it via loadEnv and builds a dev proxy (`server.proxy = target ? proxy : undefined`) that forwards every API prefix (/auth/login, /me, /tracks, etc.) to that target; the browser makes same-origin calls to 127.0.0.1:5173 which Vite proxies to the remote API. So a developer following the documented setup is NOT silently misrouted to window.location.origin — requests reach the remote API exactly as intended. The docs the finding cites as misleading are in fact accurate about the mechanism: frontend/README.md:117-128 ("the Vite proxy forwards every API request to ${VITE_API_BASE_URL}") and docs/frontend/features.md:67 ("the dev server proxies API calls to VITE_API_BASE_URL... without the variable, proxy is undefined and all API routes fall through to the SPA") both correctly describe the proxy, not a client baseUrl. Neither CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 nor any doc claims the runtime client reads the var. The finding's recommendation to "remove VITE_API_BASE_URL" would break local dev. The only sliver of truth is that docs/frontend/auth.md:32 describes apiClient without explicitly stating it is same-origin — a trivial nit, not the documented-vs-runtime contradiction the finding asserts. The substantive claim ("docs say the var drives API requests but it's ignored / setup is broken") is refuted by vite.config.ts and the README.

Refuted: the finding's central claim — that VITE_API_BASE_URL is "dead at runtime," "drives nothing," and is wrongly documented as driving API requests — is false. The var is consumed by frontend/vite.config.ts, which reads env.VITE_API_BASE_URL via loadEnv() and uses it as the dev-server proxy `target` (and only enables the proxy when set: `proxy: target ? proxy : undefined`). client.ts using window.location.origin is complementary by design: the browser issues same-origin requests to localhost:5173, and the Vite proxy forwards /auth/*, /tracks, etc. to the prod API Gateway at VITE_API_BASE_URL. This is documented correctly and intentionally in docs/frontend/features.md:67-68 ("Without the variable, proxy is undefined and all API routes fall through to the SPA, breaking /auth/login"), frontend/README.md:118-120 ("The Vite proxy forwards every API request to ${VITE_API_BASE_URL}"), and docs/archive/specs/2026-05-06-...:25. So CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 ("pnpm dev ... Requires frontend/.env.local with VITE_API_BASE_URL=...") is literally accurate. The finding's risk scenario (requests "silently go to localhost") is the intended same-origin-via-proxy behavior, which is precisely why CORS/cookie issues are avoided. The proposed fix (remove the var or wire it into client.ts) would break the documented, working dev flow. The only kernel of truth — lib/env.ts/parseEnv is unused at runtime (only loadEnv reads the var, not parseEnv) — is a trivial dead-helper nit that does not support the finding's thesis. For this MVP's local-dev-hits-prod setup that already works, no doc change is warranted.

The finding's core claim is factually wrong. It asserts VITE_API_BASE_URL is "dead at runtime" / "referenced only in tests and the vite-env type decl" and that docs falsely claim it "drives requests." But VITE_API_BASE_URL IS read and used: frontend/vite.config.ts:31-32 loads it via loadEnv and sets it as the dev-server proxy `target` (lines 34, 58). The architecture is intentional and coherent: the Vite dev server runs on 127.0.0.1:5173, the browser sees same-origin, client.ts:5-6 deliberately uses window.location.origin so requests hit the dev server, and the dev server proxies them to VITE_API_BASE_URL (the remote API GW) with cookieDomainRewrite:'' rebinding upstream Set-Cookie to localhost. So putting VITE_API_BASE_URL in frontend/.env.local — exactly as CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 instructs — is precisely what routes local-dev requests to the API. frontend/README.md:117-121 ("The Vite proxy forwards every API request to ${VITE_API_BASE_URL}") and docs/frontend/features.md:67 both correctly document this; vite.config.ts:58 even disables the proxy (proxy: target ? proxy : undefined) when the var is absent, confirming gotcha #9's "required." The finding's reproduction is backwards: it claims pointing VITE_API_BASE_URL at a cross-origin endpoint would "silently go to window.location.origin instead, breaking local dev" — but that cross-origin endpoint is exactly where the proxy sends requests, and it works. No concrete broken path exists. The only residual nit (auth.md:32 doesn't explicitly say "same-origin") is a trivial doc-completeness gap already covered by features.md and README.md, not the documented-contradiction defect alleged. The central premise is false, so it does not rise to even P3.

title: Synchronous Beatport fetch (up to 300 pages, no deadline guard) blows the 29s API Gateway budget; side effects persist after client 504
evidence: POST /collect_bp_releases and POST /admin/beatport/ingest call _run_beatport_ingest synchronously inside the API Gateway request. BeatportClient.fetch_weekly_releases loops up to max_pages=300 (beatport_client.py:52,64), each _request_page has timeout_seconds=15.0 and max_retries=4 with exponential backoff (beatport_client.py:33-35,100,136-146). There is NO deadline/remaining-time guard (unlike spotify_client.py which checks remaining_ms<60_000). The API Gateway integration sets no timeout_milliseconds, so it uses the 29s default; the Lambda timeout is 120s. A genuinely large week (or one slow/retrying page) can exceed 29s — API GW returns 504 to the client while the Lambda keeps running to 120s and only THEN writes the S3 snapshot, ingest_runs row, and SQS message (handler.py:409-446). Even the happy path of ~300 pages at minimal latency (300 × backoff/network) can exceed both 29s and 120s.
risk: On any non-trivial style/week the operator gets a 504 with no run_id, while the run can still complete server-side (S3 + ingest_runs + enqueue) up to the 120s Lambda cap — or be killed mid-fetch by the 120s timeout, leaving nothing persisted and wasted Beatport calls. The 504 is indistinguishable from a real failure, encouraging blind re-runs (each re-run re-pays the full Beatport call volume and overwrites the S3 snapshot).
recommendation: Make ingest async: have the API route validate, write a 'started' ingest_runs row, enqueue a fetch job, and return 202 with run_id immediately — move the Beatport fetch+S3 write into a worker Lambda with the 900s budget.
