One evidentiary error: the finding says the handler returns 500, but SpotifyApiError and SpotifyRateLimitedError are CurationError subclasses with http_status=502 (curation/__init__.py:205-222), caught at curation_handler.py:476-477, so the response is 502, not 500. This does not change the orphan/duplication outcome. P2 (REL) is appropriate: a real correctness/reliability defect with bounded blast radius (user-visible Spotify clutter, no local data loss), gated on a >100-track first publish plus a transient Spotify failure, and aggravated by retry-multiplication.

The mechanism is real and accurately described — the code at playlists_publish_service.py:110-146 confirms set_publish_state() runs only after the full track-write loop, so an append_tracks failure on a >100-track first publish propagates out, the handler (_handle_publish, no try/except) returns an error, target_id is never persisted, and a retry re-enters the create_playlist branch producing a second orphaned playlist. However, this is an EXPLICITLY ACCEPTED MVP behavior. The official backend design spec (docs/archive/specs/2026-05-11-playlists-backend-design.md:271-273, "Partial-publish failure") documents this exact class of failure: "First publish: create_playlist succeeded but [track write] failed → DO NOT persist spotify_playlist_id. The new (empty) Spotify playlist becomes orphan; next attempt creates a fresh one. Acceptable for MVP." The finding's variant (failure on a later append_tracks chunk rather than replace_tracks) is the same design decision. On IMPACT for this small-DJ-circle multi-tenant MVP, the blast radius is cosmetic: a few empty/partial playlists sitting in the user's own Spotify account (user-deletable), with no CLOUDER data loss, no corruption of shared canonical state, and no cross-tenant effect — the CLOUDER record simply stays unpublished and the next retry yields a correct playlist. The trigger also requires a narrow conjunction (first-time publish + >100 tracks + a Spotify 429/5xx persisting past the single retry on a specific chunk). This matches the accepted-tradeoff pattern (cold-start 503, refresh-replay revocation) and is refuted.

Confirmed by code trace. Path: POST /playlists/{id}/publish -> _handle_publish (curation_handler.py:1178) -> PlaylistsPublishService.publish(). Precondition: first-time publish (playlist.spotify_playlist_id is None, so target_id is None at line 91) and >100 playable tracks (so the append_tracks loop at playlists_publish_service.py:123-124 runs). Trace: line 110 'not target_id' is True -> create_playlist (line 111) creates the Spotify playlist and assigns target_id to the local var (line 117) only; replace_tracks succeeds (line 122); a later append_tracks hits a persistent 429/5xx. In spotify_user_client.py:197-216 with _MAX_RETRIES_429=1 / _MAX_RETRIES_5XX=1, _request raises SpotifyRateLimitedError or SpotifyApiError after one retry. The exception propagates out of publish() BEFORE set_publish_state (line 142) runs. set_publish_state (playlists_repository.py:759-794) is the SOLE persister of spotify_playlist_id on the publish path — verified there is no try/finally and no earlier write. So the created playlist's id is discarded. A retry re-enters the create branch (target_id still None) and creates a SECOND Spotify playlist, orphaning the first; repeated failures multiply orphans. One factual nit in the finding: SpotifyRateLimitedError/SpotifyApiError map to http_status=502 (curation/__init__.py:207,222), so the handler returns 502 not 500 — but this does not change the bug or its consequence (set_publish_state still never runs, exception still escapes). Severity P2 is correct: genuine but narrow-window (needs >100 tracks plus a persistent Spotify outage landing precisely after replace_tracks and within the append loop), no CLOUDER-side data corruption, impact is user-visible orphaned/duplicate playlists in the external Spotify account that the user can delete manually. No existing test or mitigation covers this case for the Spotify path.

title: YouTube Music publish orphans a partially-filled playlist on add_items failure or 30s Lambda timeout
evidence: publish() calls create_playlist (line 116) then add_items(target_id, video_ids) (line 119); add_items issues one playlistItems.insert POST per video (youtube_data_api_client.py:90-102, 'Data API v3 has no bulk insert'). set_ytmusic_publish_state (line 152) is only reached after the entire add loop completes. _request raises YtmusicApiError on any non-2xx incl. 403 quota-exceeded (youtube_data_api_client.py:163-165). A failure (or Lambda timeout) partway through the per-video loop means the YouTube playlist exists with some items but ytmusic_playlist_id is never stored locally. The curation Lambda timeout is 30s (infra/variables.tf:419-422); 100 sequential inserts can approach that window, and a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception so no cleanup runs.
risk: Retry re-enters the create branch (line 115, target_id still None) and creates a duplicate YouTube playlist, leaving the orphaned partial behind. Worse than the Spotify case because each insert is its own API call (50 quota units) and YouTube's default 10k/day quota means a few large republishes can also exhaust quota mid-loop, guaranteeing the orphan.
recommendation: Persist ytmusic_playlist_id immediately after create_playlist returns, before adding items, so retries resume on the same playlist via the incremental-diff path (which is already idempotent). Mark needs_republish on partial add failure rather than dropping the id.

votes.reason: Verified the cited code; all load-bearing claims hold. In ytmusic_publish_service.py the first-publish path (target_id is None) calls create_playlist (line 116) then add_items(target_id, video_ids) (line 119), and set_ytmusic_publish_state (line 152) is the ONLY place ytmusic_playlist_id is persisted — confirmed by grep across the curation subsystem — and it is reached only after the full add loop returns. add_items (youtube_data_api_client.py:90-102) issues one playlistItems.insert POST per video ("Data API v3 has no bulk insert"), and _request raises YtmusicApiError on any non-2xx including a 403 quota error (line 165). So a failure or Lambda timeout partway through the loop leaves the YouTube playlist created but ytmusic_playlist_id unstored. The handler _handle_publish_ytmusic (curation_handler.py:1148-1163) runs publish() synchronously inside the curation Lambda, whose timeout is 30s (infra/variables.tf:419-422), and a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception so no cleanup runs. On retry, target_id = playlist.ytmusic_playlist_id is still None (line 96), so the edit_meta/get_existing_items block is skipped and control re-enters create_playlist (line 115-119), producing a duplicate YouTube playlist and leaving the partial orphan. The "worse than Spotify" claim is correct: PlaylistsPublishService uses bulk replace_tracks/append_tracks (100 URIs/call) while YouTube is one call per video, widening the failure window and enabling mid-loop quota exhaustion. The recommended fix (persist id right after create, then resume via the idempotent incremental-diff branch at line 126-134, which adds only the missing delta) is sound. No guard or recovery mechanism was missed, and the unit tests have no coverage for partial add_items failure. Severity P2 is appropriate: a real, recurring reliability defect, but impact is bounded — an orphaned playlist on the user's own YouTube account plus a duplicate and wasted quota, all manually recoverable, with no corruption of CLOUDER's canonical state.

Code confirms the mechanism: ytmusic_publish_service.py:116-119 runs create_playlist then add_items (each a separate playlistItems.insert per youtube_data_api_client.py:90-102), and set_ytmusic_publish_state (line 152) only runs after the full loop — so a mid-loop failure or 30s curation Lambda timeout (infra/variables.tf:419-422) orphans a partial YT playlist with no stored id, and a retry re-enters create (line 96 target_id None) producing a duplicate. All three claims are technically true. But the IMPACT does not clear the bar for this MVP. (1) The trigger is implausible at small-DJ-circle scale: at ~150-400ms per sequential insert you need roughly 75-200 tracks to approach 30s, and the 403-quota path needs thousands of inserts/day against the default 10k quota — neither realistic for a small circle's typical 20-80 track playlists. (2) No CLOUDER-side data loss and full self-healing: the orphan lives on the user's own YouTube account, CLOUDER state stays 'not published', and a single republish produces a complete playlist; residue is a stray partial the user can delete. (3) Retries are user-driven (manual re-click of POST /playlists/{id}/publish-ytmusic), not an automatic SQS/Lambda retry loop, so no duplication storm. (4) This is a deliberately accepted design property — the design doc (2026-05-31-youtube-music-publish-design.md step 5) explicitly mirrors the already-shipped Spotify PlaylistsPublishService create→add→persist ordering, and the finding itself concedes it mirrors the Spotify case; the team already accepted this behavior in production. The net real-world consequence is a rare, recoverable, cosmetic stray playlist on an external account, matching accepted behavior — refuted on impact.

Confirmed against real code; not refuted. Concrete reproducible path: POST /playlists/{id}/publish-ytmusic (registered route -> curation_handler._handle_publish_ytmusic, curation_handler.py:1951 and :1148) calls svc.publish synchronously inside the curation Lambda (30s timeout confirmed at infra/variables.tf:419-422, default 30). On a first publish (no stored ytmusic_playlist_id), ytmusic_publish_service.py:96 sets target_id=None, line 115 enters the create branch, line 116 create_playlist succeeds (YouTube playlist now exists), line 119 add_items loops one playlistItems.insert POST per video (youtube_data_api_client.py:90-102). A non-2xx mid-loop — e.g. 403 quota-exceeded — hits youtube_data_api_client.py:165 and raises YtmusicApiError (401/404 are special-cased; 403 falls through to the generic raise). There is NO try/except/finally around lines 115-119, so the exception propagates uncaught and set_ytmusic_publish_state (line 152) never runs. I verified set_ytmusic_publish_state (playlists_repository.py:796-824) is the ONLY writer of ytmusic_playlist_id. Thus the id stays NULL while a partial YouTube playlist exists. On retry, repo.get returns ytmusic_playlist_id=None, so line 63 overwrite-guard is skipped and line 115 re-enters create -> duplicate YouTube playlist, original orphaned. The Lambda-timeout variant is also valid (timeout is not a Python exception, no cleanup), though whether 100 inserts truly exceed 30s is latency-dependent and not load-bearing — a single mid-loop 4xx/5xx suffices. Severity P2 is correctly calibrated: genuine reliability bug with retry amplification and quota waste, but confined to a low-frequency manual admin republish, no canonical-data corruption, no cross-user/security impact, manually recoverable. Recommendation (persist id immediately after create, route retries through the idempotent incremental-diff path; ytmusic_needs_republish column already exists per playlists_repository.py:56,812) is sound.

title: move_tracks reports inflated 'moved' count when tracks already exist in the target bucket
evidence: move_tracks deletes the requested ids from the source bucket then INSERTs them into the target with 'ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING' (lines 483-492), but returns MoveResult(moved=len(track_id_list)) (line 494) — the full requested count, not RETURNING-counted inserted rows. If a track already exists in the target bucket, the insert is a no-op but it is still counted as moved. Contrast transfer_tracks which uses RETURNING track_id and counts actual inserts (triage_repository.py:590-600).
risk: The handler returns and logs an overstated moved count (curation_handler.py:1467); the frontend optimistic decrement of source track_count can drift from the true server state when destination duplicates exist. Cosmetic/correctness drift, not data loss (the move itself is transactional and correct).
recommendation: Add RETURNING track_id to the INSERT and report moved = number of rows actually inserted (matching transfer_tracks), or document that 'moved' counts requested tracks regardless of target-side duplicates.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code. move_tracks (triage_repository.py:483-492) runs INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING with NO RETURNING clause, then returns MoveResult(moved=len(track_id_list)) at line 494 — the full requested count. The validation at lines 448-463 only checks that requested tracks exist in the SOURCE bucket; it does NOT check absence from the TARGET bucket. So a track present in both source and target passes validation, is DELETEd from source, and its INSERT into target is a silent no-op (ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING) — yet still counted as moved. The contrast claim is accurate: transfer_tracks (lines 590-600) uses RETURNING track_id and returns len(inserted_rows), counting only real inserts. The handler (curation_handler.py:1452-1472) logs and returns out.moved directly to the client. No missed guard; no misread logic. The move's end-state is correct (track is removed from source, exists in target), so this is purely an inflated count / observability + frontend track_count drift, not data loss. P3 is the correct severity for this cosmetic correctness drift in an edge case (destination-side duplicates).

Finding is technically accurate (move_tracks returns moved=len(track_id_list) at triage_repository.py:494 without RETURNING-counting actual inserts, unlike transfer_tracks at :590-600), and the duplicate-in-target scenario IS reachable since a track can live in multiple buckets of the same block (PK is per-bucket (triage_bucket_id, track_id); data-model.md:231 documents ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING as intentional idempotency). However, refuted on impact for this MVP. The finding's stated risk is mechanistically wrong: the frontend never consumes `moved`. useMoveTracks.ts computes the optimistic source-count decrement from input.trackIds.length (lines 57/68/71), NOT from the server response, and onSuccess (lines 164-172) discards the response data entirely and invalidates the affected queries, refetching true server state. So (a) the inflated count produces zero UI drift, and (b) any optimistic divergence is self-healing via invalidation independent of `moved`. No test or component renders/asserts on `moved` (only a mock value). The move itself is transactional and correct (finding concedes no data loss). The sole real effect is a single structured-log field (triage_tracks_moved.moved) being occasionally high in a narrow edge case (moving a track into a bucket where a copy already exists) — purely log-cosmetic, no user-facing or data-integrity consequence for a single-user small-DJ-circle internal tool.

CONFIRMED, severity unchanged at P3.

Factual claim verified in src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py:
- move_tracks INSERTs into the target with `ON CONFLICT (triage_bucket_id, track_id) DO NOTHING` (lines 483-492) and returns `MoveResult(moved=len(track_id_list))` (line 494) — the full requested count, never RETURNING-counted.
- transfer_tracks (lines 590-600) uses `RETURNING track_id` and counts `len(inserted_rows)`, confirming the inconsistency the finding cites.
- The pre-insert validation (lines 448-463) only checks tracks are present in the SOURCE bucket; it never checks the target. So a target-side duplicate is silently no-op'd yet still counted.

Reproducible path (precondition IS reachable):
The PK on triage_bucket_tracks is (triage_bucket_id, track_id) — a track may live in multiple buckets of the SAME block. The seed CASE (lines 346-378) assigns one bucket per track, so seed alone never duplicates. But transfer_tracks inserts a track from a different source block into ONE target bucket of block X with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING and no guard that the track isn't already in another bucket of block X. Concrete trace:
1. Block X (style S) has track T in bucket B.
2. transfer_tracks(src_block=Y, target_bucket=A-in-X, [T]) inserts T into bucket A. Now T is in both A and B of block X.
3. POST /triage/blocks/X/move with {from_bucket_id: A, to_bucket_id: B, track_ids: [T]} → _move_tracks (curation_handler.py:1444). Validation finds T in A (passes); DELETE removes T from A; INSERT into B is a no-op (T already there); returns moved=1 though 0 net rows were added to B.
The response (curation_handler.py:1471) and log line (curation_handler.py:1467) both report the inflated count.

Severity assessment — P3 is correct (not higher):
The move itself is transactional and correct (delete+insert in one TX, lines 465-492); no data loss. The frontend never consumes the server `moved` value — useMoveTracks.ts applyOptimisticMove (lines 43-77) computes the source/target track_count deltas purely from input.trackIds.length, and onSuccess (lines 164-172) invalidates triageBlockKey + bucketTracks queries, forcing a refetch that reconciles any drift to true server state. grep found no consumer of `.moved` in frontend/src/features/triage. So real-world impact is limited to a cosmetic/log inaccuracy and a slightly misleading API response field — exactly a P3 correctness nit. Finding stands; severity unchanged.

title: No test covers the publish partial-failure / orphan-playlist path for either Spotify or YouTube Music
evidence: Both publish-service suites cover first-publish, republish, orphan-on-edit-404, cover failure, and nothing-to-publish, but neither has a test where create_playlist succeeds and a subsequent track-write (append_tracks / add_items) raises. There is no assertion that the playlist id is or is not persisted on partial track-write failure, so the orphan/duplicate behavior in the two findings above is unguarded by tests.
risk: The most damaging publish failure mode (orphaned/duplicated external playlists) can regress or be 'fixed' incorrectly with no test catching it. Coverage gap masks a real reliability bug.
recommendation: Add tests that make the second track-write call raise (e.g. SpotifyRateLimitedError / YtmusicApiError) and assert the desired persistence behavior (id saved, needs_republish set) so retries don't create duplicates.

votes.reason: Verified against both test files and both service implementations. Spotify suite (test_playlists_publish_service.py, 10 tests): no test sets sp.replace_tracks.side_effect or sp.append_tracks.side_effect to raise; the two failure-injection tests target update_playlist (test_repub_orphan_falls_back_to_create, test_publish_non_404_spotify_error_propagates) and set_cover (test_publish_cover_failure_marks_dirty), never the track-write step. The >100-track append_tracks pagination path (service lines 123-124) is entirely unexercised. YT suite (test_ytmusic_publish_service.py): FakeClient.add_items (lines 83-84) cannot be made to raise — only edit_raises and cover_raises exist; test_non_404_edit_error_propagates injects on edit_meta, not add_items/remove_items. Confirmed no other test file (handler tests, integration test_playlists_flow.py) injects a track-write failure — integration only injects set_cover (line 634); its line-155 append_tracks is the repository method, not the Spotify client. The claimed gap is real: in playlists_publish_service.py, set_publish_state (line 142) runs after the unguarded track-writes (122-124), so a raise there propagates and the new target_id is never persisted — the exact orphan behavior the finding says is untested. The finding does not overclaim (it explicitly states no assertion exists either way). Severity P3 is correct: this is a TEST-dimension coverage-gap finding on a secondary failure path, deliberately scoped not to double-count the underlying-bug severity captured in the referenced findings.

Factual premise verified: in playlists_publish_service.py, create_playlist (L111) then replace_tracks/append_tracks (L122-124), and in ytmusic_publish_service.py, create_playlist (L116) then add_items (L119), both call set_publish_state only afterward (L142 / L152). If the track-write raises, the new external playlist id is not persisted, and a user retry creates a duplicate/orphan. Neither test suite (both pass, 25 tests) covers a track-write failure after a successful create. So the coverage gap is real.

However, refuted on IMPACT for this MVP. (1) The finding is a TEST-dimension observation that the author already self-rates at the floor (P3); it does not claim the underlying behavior is severe — it merely notes the guarding test is missing, and explicitly defers the bug itself to "the two findings above," so it is a dependent/derivative item, not a standalone defect. (2) The trigger is implausible at MVP scale: publish is a synchronous user-triggered HTTP POST (/playlists/{id}/publish, curation_handler.py:1178/1148) with NO SQS/auto-retry, so the failure requires create-succeeds-then-next-write-fails AND a manual re-click. (3) Impact is cosmetic and self-healing: the orphan is an empty playlist in the user's own Spotify/YT account (no canonical/shared-data corruption, no cross-tenant leak, no security impact), the user can delete it, and the next successful republish edits/replaces tracks in place. For a small DJ circle this is a rare, recoverable nuisance. Severity unchanged at P3 (already the floor); the practical importance is below P3 but P3 is the lowest level available.

Verified against the actual test files and source. Confirmed accurate and not refuted.

Spotify suite (tests/unit/test_playlists_publish_service.py): all 11 tests either error before create_playlist (NothingToPublish, ConfirmOverwriteRequired, NotAuthorized, PlaylistNotFound), exercise the republish-update path before create (test_publish_non_404_spotify_error_propagates makes update_playlist raise, not the track-write), or run the happy path. The closest, test_publish_cover_failure_marks_dirty (L207), makes set_cover raise — that happens AFTER replace_tracks succeeds and is explicitly caught. No test makes replace_tracks (L122) or append_tracks (L124) raise. grep for replace_tracks.side_effect/append_tracks.side_effect returns nothing.

YtMusic suite (tests/unit/test_ytmusic_publish_service.py): the FakeClient only supports edit_raises and cover_raises (L55). add_items (L83) unconditionally appends and can never raise. The edit_raises paths (L205/220/231) all hit the republish edit_meta step before create. No mechanism to make add_items (L119) raise after create_playlist.

Integration (tests/integration/test_playlists_flow.py): replace_tracks is only ever asserted-called (L470-471, 546); the only publish-path side_effect raise is set_cover (L634), the cover-failure case (L615-651), again after the track-write succeeds.

The path is reproducible: POST /playlists/{id}/publish where create_playlist succeeds but the subsequent track-write raises a transient error. In playlists_publish_service.py the exception at L122/124 propagates before set_publish_state (L142); in ytmusic_publish_service.py add_items at L119 raises before set_ytmusic_publish_state (L152). The external playlist is created but the id is never persisted -> orphan on first publish, duplicate on retry. This exact behavior has no test guarding it.

Severity unchanged at P3: this is a pure TEST-dimension coverage gap (not itself a runtime defect). The finding correctly frames it as the orphan/duplicate behavior being unguarded by tests rather than over-claiming a bug. Real, low-effort (S) gap worth a regression test, but not higher severity since the absence of a test does not by itself break anything at runtime.

title: import-spotify and publish run a synchronous per-item vendor-call loop inside the 30s curation Lambda timeout
evidence: _handle_import_spotify loops sp_client.get_track(sid) once per submitted ref (lines 1092-1094); ImportSpotifyTracksIn caps refs at 50 (curation/schemas.py:135) and each get_track may sleep+retry on 429/5xx (spotify_user_client.py:197-211). _handle_publish/_handle_publish_ytmusic run the multi-call publish services synchronously. The curation Lambda timeout is 30s (infra/variables.tf:419-422). A Lambda timeout is not a Python exception, so partial work (imported clouder_tracks rows, partially-populated external playlist) commits with the client receiving a gateway 5xx and no record of what succeeded.
risk: On a slow vendor or large playlist, the request can time out mid-loop. Import is idempotent on retry (upsert_imported_track), so import is mostly safe, but publish timeouts compound the orphan-playlist findings above and the client gets no actionable partial-result envelope.
recommendation: For publish, persist the external playlist id before the per-track loop (see findings above). For import, consider chunking/returning a partial result, or move large publishes to an async worker to escape the 30s synchronous window.

votes.reason: All cited code verified accurate. _handle_import_spotify loops sp_client.get_track(sid) per ref (curation_handler.py:1092-1094); ImportSpotifyTracksIn caps refs at max_length=50 (schemas.py:135); get_track routes through _request which sleeps on Retry-After (429) and 0.5s (5xx) and retries, bounded at 1 retry each (curation/spotify_user_client.py:197-211, constants line 26-27). Publish runs PlaylistsPublishService.publish synchronously with multiple sequential vendor calls: create/update_playlist, replace_tracks + N append_tracks (100-track batches, lines 122-124), set_cover, then DB set_publish_state (playlists_publish_service.py:91-146). Critically, create_playlist (line 111) precedes the DB persist of target_id at set_publish_state (line 142) by the entire append+cover loop, confirming the orphan-playlist-on-timeout window. Curation Lambda timeout is var.curation_lambda_timeout_seconds default 30 (variables.tf:419-422) wired at infra/curation.tf:12. Handlers dispatch synchronously behind API Gateway via the route table (lines 1945-1953), no async/SQS escape. Lambda timeout is indeed not a Python exception, so the service try/except blocks won't catch it and partial work persists. The finding is correct on every load-bearing claim. It correctly notes import is largely idempotent (upsert + append dedup), scoping the residual risk to publish orphans and the missing partial-result envelope. P3 is the right level: bounded retries (1 each) and a 50-ref cap make timeout under normal latency unlikely; the genuine concerns (orphan playlist, no partial-result envelope) are low-impact and partly cross-referenced to other findings, with no data corruption.

The finding's core technical claim is partly wrong and the residual risk is an accepted MVP behavior. PUBLISH does NOT run a per-item vendor loop: both publish services use BULK endpoints. Spotify replace_tracks/append_tracks send up to 100 URIs per single HTTP call (spotify_user_client.py:122-141 — confirmed no inner loop), so the per-track loop at curation_handler.py:122-124 iterates once per 100 tracks, not per track; YTMusic add_items/remove_items take whole lists. For a typical sub-100-track DJ playlist, publish is ~3-5 vendor calls total regardless of length. The "synchronous per-item vendor-call loop" headline overstates publish vendor-call count by ~100x and does not hold for the publish path it names.

Only IMPORT has a genuine per-item loop, and it is bounded and self-protecting: capped at 50 refs (schemas.py:135), each get_track retries at most once (_MAX_RETRIES_429=1, _MAX_RETRIES_5XX=1), and an exhausted 429 raises SpotifyRateLimitedError that is NOT caught in the import loop (only SpotifyNotFoundError is, line 1095) so it propagates and aborts fast rather than grinding to the timeout. The finding itself concedes import is idempotent (upsert_imported_track), so a timeout-then-retry is safe.

The remaining "client gets a gateway 5xx, no record of what succeeded" outcome is an explicitly accepted behavior class: API Gateway's hard 29s timeout sits under the 30s Lambda and already produces 503 envelopes the MVP treats as "retry in a few seconds" (ADR-0014). Publish writes DB state (set_publish_state) only after vendor calls succeed and uses treat_404_as_orphan recreate + idempotent replace_tracks PUT, so republish converges; the narrow first-publish orphan window belongs to the separate orphan finding, not this throughput one. For a small DJ circle with sub-100-track playlists and infrequent publishes, there is no plausible trigger. Refuted.

The core claim holds and is reproducible, strongest on the YouTube Music publish path. Concrete path: a user with a curation playlist whose 100+ tracks are `matched` to YT video_ids (AddTracksIn allows up to 1000 track_ids/call, list_tracks limit=10_000, so large playlists are reachable via API) calls `POST /playlists/{id}/publish-ytmusic` (synchronous API Gateway route, _ROUTE_TABLE curation_handler.py:1951). _handle_publish_ytmusic -> YtmusicPublishService.publish (ytmusic_publish_service.py): create_playlist (1 call) then add_items(target_id, video_ids), which at youtube_data_api_client.py:90-102 loops one synchronous HTTP POST per video ("Data API v3 has no bulk insert: one playlistItems.insert per video"); remove_items likewise loops one DELETE per item (line 86-88). At ~hundreds of ms per call this exceeds the API Gateway 29s / Lambda 30s timeout (infra/variables.tf:419-422). The Lambda timeout is not a Python exception, and set_ytmusic_publish_state (line 152) runs only AFTER the loop — so a mid-loop timeout leaves an external YT playlist with partial tracks and NO DB record, and the next retry creates a fresh orphan. The import path is also a real per-item synchronous loop (sp_client.get_track per ref, capped at 50 by ImportSpotifyTracksIn schemas.py:135) with each upsert_imported_track committing its own transaction (playlists_repository.py:864), so partial imports persist but are idempotent on retry.

Caveats / inaccuracies in the finding (not enough to refute, do not change severity): (1) cited path src/collector/spotify_user_client.py is wrong — the file is src/collector/curation/spotify_user_client.py. (2) The Spotify *publish* service is NOT a per-item loop: it batches 100 URIs per replace_tracks/append_tracks call (playlists_publish_service.py:122-124), so the "per-item" characterization only truly fits the YTMusic publish and the import get_track loop. (3) The "sleep+retry on 429/5xx" wall-clock argument is overstated: in production _build_spotify_user_client passes no sleep fn, so SpotifyUserClient._sleep is a no-op (constructor line 62) and retries (_MAX_RETRIES_429/_5XX = 1) are immediate; the real cost is per-round-trip network latency, not the sleeps.

Severity unchanged at P3: the orphan/no-DB-record consequence is a genuine reliability defect, but it requires a large playlist (100+ matched tracks) or a slow vendor to trigger, import is idempotent, and the YTMusic publish feature is a niche Testing-mode/test-users-only surface. The bug is real and the recommendation (persist external playlist id before the per-track loop; chunk/async large publishes) is correct.

title: curation_handler.py is a 1960-line module mixing categories, triage, tags, playlists, publish, match-review, and client construction
evidence: A single handler file holds 44 route handlers across five distinct subdomains (categories, triage, tags, playlists, match-review) plus inline S3/Spotify/YTMusic client-builder helpers (e.g. _build_spotify_user_client, _build_ytmusic_user_client near lines 1860-1893) and a late mid-file 'import re as _re' (line 1576). The _ROUTE_TABLE dispatch dict (lines 1895-1959) is a clean single source of truth, but the handlers and helpers it references are all colocated in one file.
risk: High cognitive load and merge-contention surface; mixing transport handlers with vendor-client construction makes the publish/import flows harder to test in isolation and obscures the partial-failure paths flagged above. Drift risk as new spec routes append.
recommendation: Split per-subdomain handler modules (categories/triage/tags/playlists) and extract the vendor-client builders into a small factory module, keeping _ROUTE_TABLE as the aggregation point. No behavior change required.

votes.reason: Every factual claim in the finding is verified against the actual file. The module is exactly 1960 lines. `import re as _re` is indeed a late mid-file import at line 1576 (used functionally for `_HEX_COLOR_RE`, so cited correctly as an organizational smell, not a false bug claim). `_ROUTE_TABLE` spans lines 1895-1959 and contains exactly 44 route entries, matching the "44 route handlers" claim. The inline vendor-client builders `_build_spotify_user_client` (line 1804) and `_build_ytmusic_user_client` (line 1851) plus `_build_s3_storage` (1790) are colocated with transport handlers, as stated. Handlers do span five subdomains: categories (~496-768), playlists (769-1219, including publish/import at 1063-1219), triage (1220-1571), tags (1574-1773), and match-review (_handle_match_candidates line 323, _handle_resolve_match line 349). This is an ARCH/maintainability finding making no overstated correctness claim; the cited evidence and the no-behavior-change recommendation are accurate. P3 is the correct severity for a pure cognitive-load/merge-contention concern with no functional, security, or behavioral impact.

Facts are accurate (1960 lines, 44 routes across 5 subdomains, inline _build_spotify_user_client/_build_ytmusic_user_client/_build_s3_storage, late `import re as _re` at line 1576, clean _ROUTE_TABLE at 1895). But the finding's RISK rationale is false and the IMPACT is nil for this MVP. (1) The central claim — colocation "makes publish/import flows harder to test in isolation" and "obscures partial-failure paths" — is contradicted by the codebase: the actual business logic is already extracted into dedicated, independently-tested modules under src/collector/curation/ (playlists_publish_service.py, ytmusic_publish_service.py, categories_service.py, triage_service.py, playlists_service.py) plus resolver/client modules (spotify_token_resolver, spotify_user_client, ytmusic_token_resolver, youtube_data_api_client). The handler's vendor builders are ~30-line thin wiring that delegate to those classes. (2) There are 36+ dedicated test files, including per-subdomain handler tests (test_curation_handler_playlists/tags/triage/match_review.py, test_handle_publish_ytmusic.py) — so the predicted test-isolation problem does not exist; tests already import individual handlers (e.g. _handle_list_tracks) and exercise services directly. (3) The recommended refactor is already done at the layer that matters; what remains colocated is transport/dispatch glue, which _ROUTE_TABLE keeps as a clean single source of truth (the finding concedes this). (4) The finding explicitly states "No behavior change required" — it is a pure code-organization preference with no correctness, security, or operational consequence. "Merge-contention surface" is theoretical for a small DJ-circle team. This is a cosmetic style preference with a partly-false risk justification; no MVP impact. Severity unchanged (already lowest tier, P3).

Verified against the actual file at /Users/roman/Projects/clouder-projects/clouder-core/.claude/worktrees/full_review/src/collector/curation_handler.py. All core claims hold: 1960 lines (wc -l); exactly 44 route entries in _ROUTE_TABLE (grep -cE of HTTP-method keys = 44) and 44 handler-style defs; route table at lines 1895-1960 aggregating categories/triage/tags/playlists factories; vendor-client builders _build_spotify_user_client (line 1804) and _build_ytmusic_user_client (line 1851) are inline and genuinely mix transport handling with token-resolver/KMS-envelope/OAuth-client construction (lazy imports of boto3, SpotifyTokenResolver, KmsEnvelope, etc.); mid-file 'import re as _re' confirmed at line 1576; module docstring confirms it is the single Lambda handler for the whole user-curation surface (spec-C/D/E) and notes 'spec-D and spec-E will append entries' — corroborating the drift risk. This is an architecture/maintainability finding, not a runtime bug, so the relevant 'reproducibility' is structural: the monolithic module exists exactly as described and any maintainer/editor confronts all five concern-areas in one file. Minor framing inaccuracies do not undermine it: the builders are at 1804/1851 (the cited 1860-1893 is inside the ytmusic builder body, not its start), and 'five subdomains' is loose since match-review/publish are folded into the playlists factory (four factories). These are cosmetic and do not change the substance. P3 is the correct level: high cognitive load / merge-contention / test-isolation friction with no functional defect — precisely an architectural smell. Effort 'L' is reasonable for a per-subdomain split plus extracting a vendor-client factory module while keeping _ROUTE_TABLE as the aggregation point.

title: All-vendors-failed enrichment re-run overwrites previously-good label/artist info with a failure placeholder
evidence: enrich_label_for_run() calls merge_cells() then upsert_label_info() UNCONDITIONALLY. When every vendor cell fails to parse (quota/outage/429/timeout), aggregator.merge_cells() returns the Case-1 placeholder LabelInfo(summary="All vendor sources failed.", confidence=0.0) with default status (aggregator.py:393-411). repository.upsert_label_info() (repository.py:759-809) is an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (label_id) DO UPDATE SET merged=EXCLUDED.merged, tagline/country/founded_year/primary_styles/ai_content/... = EXCLUDED.* — i.e. it overwrites ALL columns of an already-enriched row. There is no guard that skips the write (or preserves prior data) when all cells failed. project_ai_suspected() then also re-projects the 0.0-confidence result. Same code shape in artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:113-126 / upsert_artist_info.
risk: A re-enrichment (manual admin re-run, or auto-dispatch on a triage block) that hits a transient Gemini/OpenAI/Tavily outage or shared quota exhaustion silently clobbers a good, previously-enriched label/artist with an empty 'All vendor sources failed' stub (tagline/country/styles wiped, confidence 0.0). This is silent gradual corruption of curated catalogue metadata that no error surfaces — the run is marked 'completed', cells_error just increments.
recommendation: In enrich_label_for_run/enrich_artist_for_run, skip upsert_label_info/upsert_artist_info (and project_ai_suspected) when merge meta has all_failed=True (or merged.confidence==0.0 and ok==0), OR change the upsert to COALESCE-merge non-null fields so a failed run never nulls out existing data. Keep recording the failed cells + run counters for observability.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code; finding holds. (1) orchestrator.py:117-128 calls merge_cells then upsert_label_info + project_ai_suspected UNCONDITIONALLY — no all_failed/confidence==0.0/ok==0 guard. (2) aggregator.py:86-95 _filter_parseable returns empty when every cell has error set or parsed=None (the exact transient outage/quota/429/timeout case), triggering Case-1 placeholder LabelInfo(summary="All vendor sources failed.", confidence=0.0) with meta["all_failed"]=True (aggregator.py:393-411). Per schemas.py:47-76 all other fields default to None/[]/"unknown" (country, founded_year, tagline, primary_styles, status, ai_content). (3) repository.py:774-789 ON CONFLICT (label_id) DO UPDATE SET overwrites merged/tagline/country/founded_year/primary_styles/ai_content/ai_confidence/status from EXCLUDED.* with no COALESCE — previously-good values are nulled. (4) The re-run path is real and unguarded for MANUAL admin re-runs: routes.py:99-138 (batch) and handle_post_enrich_auto routes.py:141-188 (admin button) resolve any label_id and create a run with NO check for an existing clouder_label_info row. (5) Artist mirror identical (artist_enrichment/aggregator.py:368-379, orchestrator.py:113-126). One nuance: the AUTO-dispatch path IS partly mitigated — claim_labels (auto_repository.py:166-173) excludes ids that already have a clouder_label_info row from new claims, and only reclaims failed/stale-queued state rows (which by then already hold the placeholder, not good data); so the finding's secondary "auto-dispatch on a triage block" trigger is weaker than stated. But the primary stated trigger — manual admin re-run during a transient vendor outage — fully clobbers curated metadata, run still marked completed, only cells_error increments. DATA/P1 severity, location, and COALESCE/skip-when-all-failed recommendation all stand.

Technical claim verified. enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:117-127) calls merge_cells() then upsert_label_info() unconditionally. On total vendor failure, aggregator.merge_cells() Case-1 (aggregator.py:392-411) returns LabelInfo(summary="All vendor sources failed.", confidence=0.0) with all denormalized fields empty/None. repository.upsert_label_info() (repository.py:743-809) does ON CONFLICT (label_id) DO UPDATE SET merged/tagline/country/founded_year/primary_styles/ai_content/... = EXCLUDED.*, so a previously-good row is overwritten with the empty stub. No guard skips the write. project_ai_suspected re-projects the 0.0 result. Same shape confirmed in artist_enrichment. The run is marked completed; only cells_error increments — silent.

However P1 overstates impact for THIS MVP, for two reasons that narrow the trigger:

1. The auto-dispatch path (triage-block fan-out, the high-frequency trigger named in the risk) is already structurally guarded against this. AutoEnrichRepository.claim_labels (auto_repository.py:108-181) inserts new work only WHERE NOT EXISTS a clouder_label_info row, so an already-enriched label is never auto-re-claimed; the reclaim branch only touches 'failed' or stale-'queued' rows (capped at _MAX_ATTEMPTS=2), and a 'failed' row by definition has no good data to clobber; 'completed' rows are skipped. So auto-dispatch cannot regress a good row.

2. That leaves only the manual admin re-run (POST /admin/labels/enrich, /enrich-auto in routes.py:90-188), which genuinely has no guard. But triggering corruption requires an admin to deliberately re-run an already-good label AND all configured vendors (gemini + openai + tavily/deepseek — 3 independent providers) failing in that single run simultaneously. The joint probability for a small-DJ-circle MVP with infrequent manual re-runs is low.

The damage is also recoverable: the clobbered fields are admin-curated catalogue metadata (not user data), and the very next successful enrichment run on that label repopulates the row. It is not an accepted ADR behavior and the missing guard is real, so it is not refuted — but the realistic trigger is a narrow manual action coinciding with a total 3-provider outage, with single-re-run recovery, which fits P2 rather than P1.

Confirmed and reproducible, not refuted. Verified the full code path. enrich_label_for_run (orchestrator.py:117-128) calls merge_cells() then upsert_label_info()/project_ai_suspected() UNCONDITIONALLY — there is no all_failed/confidence guard. When every vendor cell has a non-None error (429/timeout/quota/outage all set error via run_vendors_parallel's except block, lines 59-65), _filter_parseable() (aggregator.py:86-95) drops them all, so merge_cells() hits Case 1 (aggregator.py:393-411) and returns LabelInfo(summary="All vendor sources failed.", confidence=0.0) with all other fields at schema defaults (country=None, tagline=None, status="unknown", primary_styles=[], ai_content=UNKNOWN — schemas.py:42-77) plus meta["all_failed"]=True. upsert_label_info (repository.py:759-809) is INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (label_id) DO UPDATE SET merged/tagline/country/founded_year/primary_styles/ai_content/status/... = EXCLUDED.*, so it overwrites every column of an already-enriched row with the stub/nulls. Reproducible trigger: admin re-enriches an already-enriched label via POST /admin/labels/enrich (handle_post_enrich, routes.py:74-138) or POST /admin/labels/{label_id}/enrich-auto (routes.py:141-188) — neither checks for an existing clouder_label_info row. This re-run workflow is intended: the backlog exposes an "outdated" status (repository.py:321-322, 365-368) listing already-enriched labels with stale prompt versions specifically for re-running. Message -> SQS -> label_enrichment_handler -> enrich_label_for_run. Artist path mirrors exactly (artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:113-125, upsert_artist_info). One nuance that bounds severity: the AUTO-dispatch path is NOT vulnerable — claim_labels (auto_repository.py:159-178) has AND NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM clouder_label_info ...), so auto-enrichment never re-runs an already-enriched label. So the trigger is specifically admin-initiated re-runs (incl. the "outdated" backlog workflow and the enrich-auto admin button), coinciding with a total/transient vendor failure. Downgrading P1->P2: it is genuine silent data loss with no surfaced error (run marked completed, only cells_error increments), but (a) requires the compound precondition of an admin re-run AND simultaneous all-vendor failure, (b) the auto path is guarded so it is not self-triggering at scale, and (c) the loss is recoverable — the per-cell run rows are preserved and re-running once vendors recover restores the metadata (derived AI data, not user-authored content). The recommendation (skip upsert/project when meta["all_failed"] is True, or COALESCE-merge non-null fields) is correct and low-effort.

title: LLM enrichment vendors bypass the providers/ registry and VENDORS_ENABLED gate entirely (ADR-0004 not honored)
where: src/collector/label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:159-197 (build_adapters_from_run_config) and src/collector/label_enrichment_handler.py:106-111; artist mirror in artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:155-157; cf. providers/registry.py:_BUILDERS and docs/adr/0004
evidence: ADR-0004 states third-party music services are wrapped behind role Protocols (incl. EnrichProvider) in providers/, gated by VENDORS_ENABLED, so 'disabling a vendor is a single env var change'. But the live label/artist enrichment path constructs GeminiAdapter/OpenAIAdapter/TavilyDeepSeekAdapter directly in build_adapters_from_run_config (orchestrator.py:167-194) from run_row['vendors'], never touching providers.registry or _enabled_vendors(). The merge client is likewise a raw OpenAI/DeepSeek client built in the handler (_build_merge_client, label_enrichment_handler.py:53-56). The registry's only EnrichProvider is SpotifyEnricher, which is dead code (providers/spotify/enrich.py:5). grep confirms artist_enrichment_handler/orchestrator reference no registry symbols at all.
risk: The dominant cost driver in the system (labels|artists x vendors x (generate+merge), Tavily internally 3 HTTP calls) has NO VENDORS_ENABLED kill switch. Disabling/cutting off a misbehaving or key-revoked LLM vendor requires removing it from each run's stored 'vendors' list or a code/SSM change — defeating ADR-0004's stated cost-control and revoked-key purpose. Tooling and tests that assume the registry governs all vendor calls are wrong for the highest-spend path.
recommendation: Either route enrichment adapters through providers/registry as EnrichProvider implementations behind VENDORS_ENABLED, or add an explicit per-vendor enable gate read from settings inside build_adapters_from_run_config so an operator can disable gemini/openai/tavily_deepseek without a deploy. Update ADR-0004 to reflect the actual boundary.

votes.reason: Every cited claim verified against the source. build_adapters_from_run_config in src/collector/label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:159-197 instantiates GeminiAdapter/OpenAIAdapter/TavilyDeepSeekAdapter directly from run_row['vendors'], never touching providers.registry, _enabled_vendors(), or VENDORS_ENABLED. The merge client is a raw OpenAI/DeepSeek client (_build_merge_client, label_enrichment_handler.py:53-56). The artist path mirrors this exactly (artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:147-177; the cited imports are lines 155-157). grep confirms the enrichment dirs/handlers reference zero registry symbols (only unrelated 'prompt registry' matches), and VENDORS_ENABLED appears ONLY in providers/registry.py across all of src/. SpotifyEnricher is confirmed dead code by its own docstring ('Currently NOT wired to any handler') and by grep showing no caller of get_enricher() anywhere. ADR-0004 (docs/adr/0004-provider-abstraction.md) does state EnrichProvider is one of the four role Protocols gated by VENDORS_ENABLED and that 'disabling a vendor is a single env var change' for cost-control/revoked-key reasons — exactly the promise the enrichment path bypasses. Settings (settings.py:193-212) expose only raw API keys for enrichment vendors with no per-vendor enable flag. The one mitigant — auto_enrich_config.enabled — is a per-kind all-or-nothing master toggle that cannot disable a single misbehaving vendor, and per-run vendor lists are stored in the DB row, so cutting one revoked-key vendor needs a DB/config or code change, not an env var. ADR-0016 corroborates 'vendor cost scales with labels × vendors' as the dominant cost driver. Finding is factually accurate; it is an architecture/consistency gap (ADR not honored for the highest-spend path), not a runtime correctness bug. P2 is the correct level: not P1 (no active outage/correctness break and coarse workarounds exist), not P3 (real operational cost-control/revoked-key consequence on the dominant cost path).

Technical claim is TRUE: the live label/artist enrichment path builds GeminiAdapter/OpenAIAdapter/TavilyDeepSeekAdapter directly in build_adapters_from_run_config (orchestrator.py:167-194) and the merge client in _build_merge_client (handler:53-56), never touching providers.registry or _enabled_vendors(); the enrichment Lambdas don't even set VENDORS_ENABLED (infra/lambda.tf:188-258 — only api/spotify/vendor_match set it), and the registry's only EnrichProvider (SpotifyEnricher) is unrelated. So ADR-0004's "EnrichProvider gated by VENDORS_ENABLED" does not describe the live path — a real doc/architecture drift.

But the IMPACT for this MVP is overstated, so I refute the severity. The "no kill switch" risk is mitigated three ways: (1) Vendors are per-run operator choice from an admin enqueue form (handle_get_options / handle_post_enrich take `vendors` from the request), not a fixed pipeline — a misbehaving vendor is simply not selected next run; enrichment is admin-triggered batch work, not a user hot path. (2) A revoked/misbehaving vendor is already non-fatal: run_vendors_parallel wraps each adapter in try/except (orchestrator.py:59-65), recording an error cell instead of failing the run. (3) The actual single-knob disable still exists — API keys come from SSM (GEMINI_API_KEY_SSM_PARAMETER etc.); blanking/rotating an SSM param cuts a vendor off with no deploy, which directly satisfies ADR-0004's stated cost/revoked-key goals and contradicts the finding's claim that disabling needs a code change. Cost is also naturally bounded by finite admin-enqueued batches for a small DJ circle. The 'tooling/tests assume the registry governs all vendors' claim is unsupported — no cost-governance tooling reading the registry for enrichment was shown.

Net: a genuine ARCH-hygiene / ADR-accuracy gap worth fixing (update ADR-0004 or add an explicit per-vendor gate), but not the operational kill-switch failure described. P2 overstates it; correct level is P3.

Could not refute — every factual claim verified against the code. Reproducible path: create_run (repository.py:619) stores vendors=["gemini","openai","tavily_deepseek"] in run_row; SQS → label_enrichment_handler.lambda_handler reads run_row['vendors'] (lines 102-104) and passes it to build_adapters_from_run_config (lines 106-111), which directly instantiates GeminiAdapter/OpenAIAdapter/TavilyDeepSeekAdapter (orchestrator.py:176-194) with no registry/VENDORS_ENABLED check; the merge client is a raw DeepSeek-via-OpenAI client (handler lines 53-56). grep across src/collector/label_enrichment and src/collector/artist_enrichment returns ZERO references to providers.registry, VENDORS_ENABLED, _enabled_vendors, or get_enricher. The artist mirror is identical (artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:147-177). The registry's only EnrichProvider is SpotifyEnricher, which its own docstring marks dead ("Currently NOT wired to any handler", providers/spotify/enrich.py:5). ADR-0004 (docs/adr/0004-provider-abstraction.md:11,17,22) explicitly lists EnrichProvider as a gated role Protocol and promises "disabling a vendor is a single env var change" via VENDORS_ENABLED — so the enrichment path does violate the documented contract. ADR-0016/0017 contain no alternate-boundary carve-out (grep for disable/registry/VENDORS_ENABLED returns nothing), so this is not an intentional documented exception. Operational risk is real: to cut off a key-revoked LLM vendor an operator must blank the SSM secret (and wait for cold-start/lru_cache reset) or edit each run's stored vendors JSON — not the single-env-var change ADR-0004 advertises. Severity P2 is correct: it is an ARCH/ops-control inconsistency on the highest-spend path, not a crash/security bug, and a vendor can still ultimately be killed by blanking its key (a clumsy lever exists), which keeps it below P1.

title: vendor_match worker has no negative cache: no_match/ambiguous tracks re-run full YT Music lookups on every re-enqueue
where: src/collector/vendor_match_handler.py:79-87 (cache check) vs :178-197 (no_match/review writes); repositories.py:1003-1035 (get_vendor_match), :1172-1202 (mark_no_match), :1141-1170 (insert_review_candidate); producer vendor_match/enqueue.py:41-60
evidence: _process_one() short-circuits only on repository.get_vendor_match(), which SELECTs from vendor_track_map (repositories.py:1009-1017) — populated only by upsert_vendor_match on a positive isrc/fuzzy hit. A no-match outcome writes to a DIFFERENT table match_review_queue via mark_no_match (status='no_match'), and an ambiguous outcome via insert_review_candidate (status='pending') — neither writes vendor_track_map and neither is consulted by the cache check. enqueue_vendor_matches (enqueue.py:41-60) sends one message per track unconditionally with no dedup against prior outcomes. So a track that previously got no-match/review re-runs the full _try_metadata path = 2 YTMusic searches (providers/ytmusic/lookup.py:64-67), each retried up to 3x (vendor_match_handler.py:214), every time it is re-added/re-imported.
risk: Unbounded repeat vendor work and quota burn for tracks that legitimately have no clean YT Music match — the exact tracks most likely to be re-attempted by frustrated users. No staleness/TTL either way: a track that DID match positively is never re-checked even if the vendor id changes. Bounded today only because vendor_match_enabled defaults false; turning it on exposes the repeat-lookup cost with no negative-cache backstop.
recommendation: Have the cache check also consult match_review_queue for a recent terminal no_match (and optionally pending review) and short-circuit; or persist no_match into vendor_track_map with a sentinel match_type so get_vendor_match covers it, with a matched_at-based recheck window. Dedup at enqueue against existing outcomes.

votes.reason: The finding's central causal claim is wrong because it missed a load-bearing producer-side guard. It asserts "enqueue_vendor_matches sends one message per track unconditionally with no dedup against prior outcomes" so no_match/ambiguous tracks "re-run the full _try_metadata path ... every time it is re-added/re-imported." But the only two enqueue call sites (curation_handler.py:924 and :1132, both via _enqueue_ytmusic at :388) never call enqueue with raw track lists — they first call repo.fetch_unmatched_match_inputs (playlists_repository.py:914-961), whose SQL does `LEFT JOIN match_review_queue mrq ... WHERE ... mrq.clouder_track_id IS NULL` (lines 939-943). That excludes ANY track that already has a match_review_queue row — both status='no_match' (from mark_no_match) AND status='pending' (from insert_review_candidate). It also excludes positively-matched tracks via the parallel vendor_track_map join (lines 937,942). So a track that previously got no-match or ambiguous is filtered out at the producer and is NOT re-enqueued on re-add/re-import; the worker's _try_metadata (2 YTMusic searches, retried 3x) does not re-run. Even the reject flow (resolve_review_reject, playlists_repository.py:1067-1086) always leaves a no_match row behind, keeping the exclusion in force. The verified-true part is narrow and cosmetic: the WORKER's own cache check (get_vendor_match) only reads vendor_track_map, but that defense-in-depth gap is masked because the producer never feeds already-outcome'd tracks to the worker in the first place. The 'unbounded repeat vendor work and quota burn' risk thus does not materialize for the described flows — the negative-cache dedup the finding recommends ('dedup at enqueue against existing outcomes') is in substance already implemented at fetch_unmatched_match_inputs. The only real residual is the finding's weaker secondary point (no TTL/staleness recheck; positively-matched tracks never re-checked), which is a deliberate design choice, not the described quota-burn defect.

The finding's central claim — that there is "no dedup against prior outcomes" and that no-match/review tracks "re-run the full _try_metadata path ... every time" — is factually wrong. It is built on the bare `enqueue_vendor_matches` helper (enqueue.py:41-60), but that helper is never called with raw track IDs in production. Both real producers (curation_handler.py:924 and :1132) go through `_enqueue_ytmusic`, which first calls `repo.fetch_unmatched_match_inputs(track_ids=..., vendor=YTMUSIC_VENDOR)` (curation_handler.py:407-409). That query (playlists_repository.py:914-947) LEFT JOINs BOTH `vendor_track_map` AND `match_review_queue` and filters `AND vtm.clouder_track_id IS NULL AND mrq.clouder_track_id IS NULL`. So any track with a prior `no_match` row OR a `pending` review row is excluded from the enqueue list and never produces a message — exactly the negative cache the finding says is missing. It is enforced at the producer (enqueue) layer via `match_review_queue`, not at the worker's `get_vendor_match` short-circuit, which is why the finding (focused only on the worker cache check) missed it.

The negative cache also survives review rejection: `resolve_review_reject` (playlists_repository.py:1067-1086) deletes the `pending` row but immediately re-inserts a `no_match` row via `mark_no_match`, so the `match_review_queue` row persists and continues to exclude the track from re-enqueue. There is no backfill/cron/re-drive path that bypasses the filter — `enqueue_vendor_matches` is the sole producer to the vendor-match queue and its sole reachable caller is the filtered `_enqueue_ytmusic`.

The only residual true statement is that the worker's own `get_vendor_match` short-circuit (vendor_match_handler.py:79-87) does not itself consult `match_review_queue`. But that is harmless defense-in-depth, not the actual gate: a duplicate message would have to be produced first, and the producer filter prevents that for already-attempted tracks. The "unbounded repeat quota burn" risk therefore has no plausible trigger in this MVP. The secondary point (positive matches never re-checked / no TTL) is accepted-by-design caching, not a defect, and again irrelevant to the stated quota-burn risk. Claim refuted.

The finding's central causal claim is false, so the asserted risk (unbounded repeat YT Music lookups / quota burn for no_match/ambiguous tracks on re-enqueue) does not occur. The finding states "enqueue_vendor_matches (enqueue.py:41-60) sends one message per track unconditionally with no dedup against prior outcomes" and concludes a re-added no_match/review track "re-runs the full _try_metadata path = 2 YTMusic searches ... every time it is re-added/re-imported." That path cannot be constructed from real code.

Reproduction trace attempted: User re-adds track T that previously got a no_match (or ambiguous review). The ONLY enqueue entry points are curation_handler.py:924 and :1132, both calling _enqueue_ytmusic(repo, result.added_track_ids, ...). _enqueue_ytmusic (curation_handler.py:407) does NOT pass the raw track list to the producer — it first calls repo.fetch_unmatched_match_inputs(track_ids=[T], vendor='ytmusic'). That query (playlists_repository.py:937-943) LEFT JOINs BOTH vendor_track_map (vtm) and match_review_queue (mrq) and filters WHERE vtm.clouder_track_id IS NULL AND mrq.clouder_track_id IS NULL. Since T has a no_match (or pending) row in match_review_queue, it is excluded → inputs=[] → enqueue_vendor_matches receives an empty list → 0 SQS messages → worker never runs → no YTMusic lookup. The negative cache the finding says is "missing" exists at the enqueue layer.

Durability of the terminal row was verified: mark_no_match (repositories.py:1185) and insert_review_candidate (:1153) are the only writers besides resolve paths; resolve_review_accept (playlists_repository.py:1057) leaves the mrq row as 'resolved' AND writes vendor_track_map; resolve_review_reject (:1070) deletes the 'pending' row but immediately re-inserts a 'no_match' row. So after every terminal outcome the track has a row in vendor_track_map OR match_review_queue and is permanently excluded from re-enqueue. enqueue.py / VendorMatchMessage construction exists only in the worker, schema, and producer — no batch/re-import path bypasses fetch_unmatched_match_inputs (grep confirmed). This dedup is explicitly tested: tests/unit/test_playlists_repository_ytmusic_status.py:46 test_fetch_unmatched_excludes_already_attempted_in_sql asserts the SQL anti-joins both tables.

The finding's own recommendation ("Dedup at enqueue against existing outcomes") is already implemented. The only true residual is the secondary remark that a positively-matched track is never re-checked if the vendor id later changes (no TTL on vendor_track_map) — a minor staleness nuance, not the unbounded-quota-burn risk that drives the P2. That nuance alone does not meet P2.

title: Tavily API key transmitted in the request JSON body instead of an Authorization header
evidence: Both Tavily POSTs send the credential inside the JSON body: json={"api_key": self._tavily_key, "query": ...}. The key (resolved from SSM at runtime, settings.py:253) is therefore part of the request payload rather than a header. On the second (social-domain) pass the same body-embedded key is sent again. Tavily's current API expects Bearer auth in the Authorization header; body api_key is the legacy form. Note: the value is NOT logged (vendor errors go to cells, not logs; ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS in logging_utils.py:14 has no key field), and httpx error messages do not include the request body, so cell-error leakage is not demonstrable here.
risk: Secrets in request bodies are more likely to be captured by intermediary/proxy/CDN body-logging than header auth, and body-embedded keys are a deprecated Tavily auth mode that can break on vendor API changes. Lower impact than header exposure but a needless secret-handling footgun.
recommendation: Move the Tavily credential to the Authorization: Bearer <key> header (httpx headers={...}) and drop api_key from the JSON body, matching the current Tavily API contract.

votes.reason: Verified the cited code: tavily_deepseek.py lines 100-113 and 132-146 both POST to https://api.tavily.com/search with json={"api_key": self._tavily_key, ...}, so the credential is in the request body, not an Authorization header — exactly as claimed, including the duplicate send on the social-domain pass. The key is runtime-resolved from a secret (settings.py: tavily = _resolve_simple_secret("TAVILY_API_KEY", "TAVILY_API_KEY_SECRET_ARN")). The finding's non-leakage caveats are accurate: ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS in logging_utils.py contains no key/api_key field (key never logged), and the error path is f"tavily error: {type(exc).__name__}: {exc}" which does not embed the request body — so no in-repo leak path exists, matching the finding's own statement. Tavily's current API reference documents Bearer-header auth and lists no body api_key, confirming the body form is the legacy mode. Minor citation imprecisions that do not affect substance: the finding labels the resolver "SSM" (it is _resolve_simple_secret over env/Secrets-Manager ARN) and gives root-relative cites for settings.py:253 and logging_utils.py:14 (those files live under src/collector/, not label_enrichment/). Severity P3 is correct and conservative: a real secret-handling/contract footgun with no demonstrable exploit, no logged-secret path, and TLS-protected transport either way.

Finding is factually true (verified tavily_deepseek.py:100-113 and :132-146 — api_key is in the JSON body for both POSTs; key resolved from SSM at settings.py:253), but the impact does not matter for this MVP. The request goes over TLS via a direct Lambda→api.tavily.com outbound HTTPS call — there is no MITM proxy/CDN/gateway in that path, so the "body-logging by intermediary" risk has no trigger in this architecture. The finding's own evidence concedes every concrete leakage vector is closed: the key is NOT in ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (confirmed logging_utils.py:14), vendor errors go to cells not logs, httpx errors don't include the body, and "cell-error leakage is not demonstrable here." That leaves (a) a stylistic preference for header vs body auth and (b) speculative forward-compat if Tavily deprecates body auth — the latter would surface as an obvious vendor auth error with a trivial fix and zero secret exposure. This is an admin-side label_enrichment worker, not user-facing tenant code. No plausible trigger for actual harm; purely a best-practice nit. P3 is the right ceiling, but for this MVP it is refuted.

Verified and confirmed accurate, including the severity-limiting caveats. The code at tavily_deepseek.py:100-113 and :132-146 sends the Tavily credential in the JSON body (`json={"api_key": self._tavily_key, ...}`) on both the general and the social-domain pass — exact match. The key is a real secret resolved at settings.py:253 via `_resolve_simple_secret("TAVILY_API_KEY", "TAVILY_API_KEY_SECRET_ARN")`. Reproducibility path is concrete and live (not dead code): an SQS message to the label_enrichment worker → label_enrichment_handler.lambda_handler → build_adapters_from_run_config → TavilyDeepSeekAdapter(tavily_api_key=settings.tavily_api_key) → run() → two httpx.post() calls embedding the key in the body. The finding's own honesty checks hold: ALLOWED_LOG_FIELDS (logging_utils.py:14) has no key field so the value is never logged, vendor errors are stored as f-string `tavily error: {type(exc).__name__}: {exc}` in cells, and I empirically verified that httpx HTTPStatusError/ConnectError string representations do NOT contain the request body — so there is no demonstrable cell-error or log leakage. Against current Tavily docs, Bearer auth in the Authorization header is the documented/recommended method and the request body no longer lists an api_key field, so the body form is indeed the legacy/deprecated mode (forward-compat risk). Net: this is a real but non-exploitable secret-handling hygiene / forward-compat issue (no demonstrated leak vector; credential only travels to Tavily's own TLS endpoint). P3 is the correct, lowest-tier severity for a defense-in-depth nit with no proven exploit.

title: Tavily adapter test masks the two-call (general + social-domain) behavior with a single mocked response, hiding the merge/dedup path
evidence: test_tavily_deepseek_happy_path sets http.post.return_value = tavily_resp (one response) but the adapter calls self._http.post TWICE — the general search (lines 100-113) and the social-domain second pass with include_domains=SOCIAL_DOMAINS (lines 132-146). With return_value, both calls return the SAME object, so the seen_urls dedup (lines 153-161) just collapses identical results and the test still passes. The test never asserts call count, never asserts the include_domains payload, and never exercises the real merge of two distinct result sets. The failure test (lines 57-71) only covers the first-post exception. So the documented '3 upstream calls per cell / social second pass' behavior is effectively untested.
risk: Regressions in the social-domain pass (wrong domains, dropped second call, broken dedup, double-counting citations/cost) would not be caught by the unit suite — provider mocking fidelity does not match the real two-stage call shape that drives both result quality and per-cell spend.
recommendation: Use http.post.side_effect=[general_resp, social_resp] with distinct URLs, assert http.post.call_count==2, assert the second call carries include_domains=SOCIAL_DOMAINS, and assert merged/deduped citations from both sets.

votes.reason: Verified every claim against the cited code. The adapter (src/collector/label_enrichment/vendors/tavily_deepseek.py) makes two http.post calls: the general search (lines 100-113) and a social-domain second pass with include_domains=SOCIAL_DOMAINS (lines 132-146), then merges/dedups by URL (lines 153-161). The happy-path test (test_label_enrichment_vendor_tavily.py:32-33) sets http.post.return_value = tavily_resp, so BOTH posts return the same object whose results list has a single URL https://example.com; the seen_urls dedup collapses the duplicate, so citations == ["https://example.com"] (line 53) passes despite two identical calls. The test asserts only error/parsed/citations/cost_usd — it never asserts http.post.call_count, never asserts the include_domains=SOCIAL_DOMAINS payload, and never feeds two distinct result sets to exercise the real merge. The failure test (lines 57-71) uses side_effect = RuntimeError which raises on the FIRST post, so the social second pass's best-effort try/except (lines 149-150) is never exercised. I confirmed no other unit/integration test drives the real TavilyDeepSeekAdapter.run two-call HTTP path — other references to "tavily_deepseek" in the suite are vendor-name strings or stub adapters (orchestrator/config tests). The finding is accurate on all points. Severity P3 is correct: this is a test-fidelity/coverage gap, not a defect in shipping code — the adapter logic itself reads correctly, so nothing is broken today; only future regressions in the social pass, dedup, or cost/citation counting would go uncaught.

Technical claims all verified: TavilyDeepSeekAdapter.run() calls self._http.post twice (general search lines 100-113, social-domain pass with include_domains=SOCIAL_DOMAINS lines 132-146). The happy-path test (tests/unit/test_label_enrichment_vendor_tavily.py:33) sets http.post.return_value to a single response, so both calls return the same {"url":"https://example.com"} object and the seen_urls dedup (lines 153-161) trivially collapses them to one citation — the test passes (confirmed: 4 passed) without ever asserting call_count, the include_domains payload, or a real merge of two distinct result sets. The failure test (line 59) uses side_effect=RuntimeError on the first post, so only stage-1 failure is covered. I also confirmed no other test exercises the real HTTP path: orchestrator/e2e tests only pass "tavily_deepseek" as a vendor name through fully-mocked adapters. And the adapter is genuinely production-wired (orchestrators, IAM/SSM in infra, selectable routes), not sandbox-only — so the "no trigger" refutation does not apply on those grounds.

However, on IMPACT for THIS MVP it refutes. This is a P3 TEST-dimension gap describing only a hypothetical future regression, not any current defect — the code works correctly today. Critically, the second Tavily call is wrapped in a bare try/except that explicitly degrades gracefully ("second call is best-effort; failure leaves general results intact", line 149): the highest-risk untested regression (dropped/broken social pass) cannot break enrichment, it silently falls back to general results. Blast radius of an untested regression is at worst slightly degraded result quality or a marginally different per-cell Tavily spend on an admin-triggered/auto-background curation path serving a small DJ circle — negligible at this scale, not a correctness or availability issue. The merge/dedup is trivial set-based logic. This is a reasonable test-fidelity nice-to-have, not a must-fix for the MVP.

Verified against real code. The adapter src/collector/label_enrichment/vendors/tavily_deepseek.py calls self._http.post twice: the general search (lines 100-113) and a social-domain second pass with include_domains=SOCIAL_DOMAINS (lines 132-146), then merges + dedups by URL (lines 153-161). The test tests/unit/test_label_enrichment_vendor_tavily.py:33 sets http.post.return_value = tavily_resp, so BOTH posts return the same single-URL object. Concrete trace of test_tavily_deepseek_happy_path (line 49 run): query built, Stage 1 returns results=[{url: example.com}], Stage 1b returns the identical object so social_results=[{url: example.com}], merge iterates [example.com]+[example.com] and dedup collapses the duplicate to citations=["https://example.com"], which is exactly what line 53 asserts. The assertion passes only because dedup collapsed identical data — the real two-distinct-result merge is never exercised. Confirmed the test asserts neither http.post.call_count (no such assert, lines 51-54) nor the include_domains payload nor the SOCIAL_DOMAINS list. The failure test (lines 57-71) uses http.post.side_effect=RuntimeError which raises on the FIRST post; Stage 1 catches it and returns at lines 116-125, so the second-pass best-effort failure path (lines 149-150) is never tested. Grepped all 14 Tavily-referencing test files: none other touches http.post, include_domains, SOCIAL_DOMAINS, or api.tavily.com, so this is the sole HTTP-path test and the two-call behavior is genuinely untested. The two-call behavior is real and documented (docs/reviews/2026-06-10-mvp-review/system-map.md:271 "POST .../search ×2 (general + social-domain pass)"). Every factual claim in the finding holds; the recommendation (side_effect=[general,social], assert call_count==2, assert include_domains, assert merged citations) is correct. Severity P3 is appropriate: a real test-fidelity gap on a best-effort secondary enrichment pass and per-cell cost accounting, no production defect, low blast radius.

title: Canonicalization does one serial Data API round-trip per entity (N+1 find_identity + per-row create/update)
evidence: Every label/style/artist/album/track is resolved one-by-one: each phase loops entities and calls self._repository.find_identity(...) (canonicalize.py:451/486/521/558/604), which issues a single-row SELECT via DataAPIClient.execute (repositories.py:413-434). On a cache hit for a track, conservative_update_track issues another single-row UPDATE (repositories.py:647-694). There is NO batch identity-lookup method — only find_identity (single row) exists (grep of repositories.py shows find_identity + batch_upsert_identities only). A weekly run of up to 300 pages x 100 tracks (beatport_client max_pages=300, per_page=100) plus their artists/albums means thousands of serial RDS Data API calls. Only the source_entity/identity UPSERTs and track-artist inserts are batched; the lookups and the existing-track updates are not.
risk: Each find_identity/create/update is a synchronous Data API round-trip (~10-50ms), so a large run serializes into thousands of calls. This (a) drives Data API request cost and repeated Aurora resume churn under min_acu=0, and (b) inflates worker wall-clock toward the 900s Lambda timeout; a timeout is not a Python exception, so the run is left silently at RAW_SAVED with partial committed data (per the silent-Lambda-failure memory and §2.9).
recommendation: Batch the identity resolution: pre-load all existing identities for a phase with a single SELECT ... WHERE (source,entity_type,external_id) IN (...) before the loop (mirroring the existing IN-list pattern used in propagate_release_type_to_albums), and collapse existing-track updates into a batch_execute UPDATE keyed by id. This turns each phase from O(N) round-trips into O(1)+batch.

votes.reason: Verified all cited code; the finding is accurate on every claim. find_identity (repositories.py:413-441) is a single-row SELECT, and each of the five _resolve_* helpers (canonicalize.py:451/486/521/558/604) calls it once per entity inside plain synchronous for-loops (canonicalize.py:139/184/229/278/378) — there is no asyncio.gather/concurrency and no batch identity-lookup method (only find_identity + batch_upsert_identities exist). On a track cache hit, _resolve_track additionally issues conservative_update_track (canonicalize.py:611), which is a single-row UPDATE WHERE id = :track_id (repositories.py:647-694). Only the writes (batch_upsert_source_entities/identities/track_artists/source_relations) are batched; the lookups and existing-track updates are not. Volume claims check out: beatport_client.py:52/60 set max_pages=300 and per_page=100, and worker_handler.py calls canonicalizer.process_run(run_id, bundle) which processes the entire run's bundle in ONE Lambda invocation (not chunked across invocations), with canonicalization_worker_lambda_timeout_seconds default=900 (variables.tf:85-89; alarms.tf comment confirms 900s canonicalization timeout). So a large run serializes into thousands of Data API round-trips in a single 900s-bounded invocation, driving request cost + Aurora resume churn (min_acu=0) and timeout risk; a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception, consistent with the silent-failure memory. The cited IN-list batch precedent (propagate_release_type_to_albums, repositories.py:834-862) exists exactly as described, making the recommendation actionable. This is a real cost/latency inefficiency, not a correctness bug or data-loss/security issue — P2 / COST is the correct severity.

Technical mechanism confirmed: each _resolve_* (canonicalize.py:451/486/521/558/604) calls find_identity (single-row SELECT, repositories.py:413-434) per distinct entity, and cache-hit tracks add a single-row conservative_update_track (611, repositories.py:647-694); the write-side upserts are batched but lookups/updates are not. However, the IMPACT for this MVP does not hold. (1) Scale is overstated: one canonicalization run = one (style_id, week) ingest writing a single S3 key/run (handler.py:414-446); the cited 300pp x 100 = 30k is beatport_client.py:52 max_pages — a hard UpstreamUnavailableError safety ceiling, not an operating point. Real weekly new-releases per genre are hundreds-to-low-thousands of DISTINCT entities (the phases iterate deduped bundle collections, not raw track rows). (2) Timeout headroom is large: ~few-thousand serial round-trips at 10-50ms = tens of seconds to ~2 min vs the 900s worker timeout (variables.tf:88). Reaching 900s would need ~18k-90k round-trips, i.e. essentially the 30k-track ceiling that errors out upstream first — no plausible trigger. (3) The silent-RAW_SAVED leg is self-healing, not data loss: per-chunk transactions + ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE make re-processing idempotent (ADR-0001, docs/data/canonicalization.md:61), and SQS redelivers on visibility timeout, so a rare timeout resumes cleanly on retry. (4) Cost leg is marginal: RDS Data API has no per-request charge; the round-trips all run inside one already-warm worker invocation (no per-call Aurora resume churn), so incremental ACU cost for a weekly batch over a small DJ circle is negligible.
