Real efficiency smell with a sound IN-list/batch-update fix, but no operational cost or reliability impact at the actual operating point — correctly a low-priority cleanup, not a P2.

Verified every load-bearing claim against real code. (1) N+1 lookup: each _resolve_label/style/artist/album/track loops entities and calls find_identity (single-row SELECT = one synchronous Data API execute_statement round-trip) at canonicalize.py:451/486/521/558/604 -> repositories.py:413-434; data_api.py:28-49 confirms execute() = exactly one boto3 execute_statement. (2) Per-row update on cache hit: conservative_update_track is a single-row UPDATE called per existing track inside the loop (canonicalize.py:611; repositories.py:647-694), so steady-state weekly runs (tracks already exist) do 2 round-trips/track. (3) No batch identity-lookup and no in-memory cache exist: only find_identity (single) + batch_upsert_identities; grep shows the only IN(...) is the unrelated propagate query at repositories.py:858. (4) Volume is real and per-run: beatport_client.fetch_weekly_releases accumulates up to max_pages=300 x per_page=100 = 30,000 tracks into one releases.json.gz S3 object (storage.py), which becomes one canonicalization run; normalize dedups artists/albums/labels/styles but tracks are ~unique, so a single run serializes ~30k-60k track round-trips plus thousands more for deduped entities. (5) Silent failure mode confirmed: canonicalization_worker timeout default=900s (infra/variables.tf:88); a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception so worker_handler's try/except (and set_run_failed) never runs, leaving the run at RAW_SAVED (models.py:25, the pre-canonicalization status) with partially committed per-phase-transaction data. Concrete trigger path: weekly ingest -> fetch_weekly_releases(style_id, week) returns up to 30k tracks -> storage.write_releases -> SQS -> worker_handler.lambda_handler -> Canonicalizer.process_run -> per-entity serial find_identity + conservative_update_track round-trips. Minor caveat (does not refute): the per-phase open transaction keeps one connection warm within a run, so the 'repeated Aurora resume churn' sub-claim is overstated; but the dominant cost (thousands of serial Data API round-trips driving wall-clock toward the 900s timeout, plus request volume) is fully real. P2 is correct: real cost/timeout risk on the largest runs, not a guaranteed every-run break.

title: Worker classifies ValueError/TypeError/KeyError as permanent, silently dropping transient DB-shape failures with no DLQ
evidence: _PERMANENT_ERRORS = (ValueError, TypeError, KeyError, StorageError) (worker_handler.py:21). In the handler, is_permanent = isinstance(exc, _PERMANENT_ERRORS); permanent errors log + continue, deleting the SQS message with no DLQ cycle (worker_handler.py:140,176-179). But these exception types are not exclusively 'corrupt data' signals from the runtime DB path: find_identity reads row['clouder_entity_type']/row['clouder_id'] by hard key (repositories.py:439-440), and _to_rows builds rows defensively (drops columns past the metadata list, data_api.py:183-185), so an unexpected/partial Data API result shape during canonicalize raises KeyError — which is then classified PERMANENT and dropped. The same applies to any int()/str() coercion (e.g. int(rows[0]['cnt'])) that raises ValueError on a malformed transient row.
risk: A transient Data-API/Aurora response anomaly that surfaces as KeyError/ValueError during the canonicalize phase is treated as permanent: the message is deleted (no retry, no DLQ), the ingest_runs row is marked FAILED, and the raw data is never reprocessed — silent loss of a run despite the data being intact in S3.
recommendation: Narrow the permanent set to only truly data-shape errors raised by the normalize/parse layer (e.g. a dedicated MalformedSourceDataError), or wrap normalize/parse failures in that explicit type. Treat unclassified exceptions (incl. bare KeyError/ValueError from the repository/Data API layer) as transient so they redrive to the DLQ rather than vanishing.

votes.reason:
Code cited accurately. worker_handler.py:21 sets _PERMANENT_ERRORS=(ValueError,TypeError,KeyError,StorageError); lines 140,176-179 classify by isinstance and `continue` (return normally) on permanent → SQS deletes the message. There is NO ReportBatchItemFailures config, so returning normally deletes the message and only a re-raise redrives to the DLQ (infra/sqs.tf:14-17, maxReceiveCount=5). Critically, the ONLY alarm for this worker is DLQ depth (infra/logging.tf:69-90); there is no alarm on the permanent-failure log or on ingest_runs FAILED — so a permanent-classified error genuinely evades all alerting. The mechanism is real: find_identity reads row["clouder_entity_type"]/row["clouder_id"] by hard key (repositories.py:437-440), called 5x in the canonicalize path (canonicalize.py:451/486/521/558/604), and other reads do Decimal(str(row["confidence"])) / int(rows[0]["cnt"]) — any KeyError/ValueError from these is misclassified permanent and silently dropped. Moreover normalize.py is fully defensive (_as_positive_int/_as_non_empty_str return None, .get() everywhere, no hard-key access), so the stated rationale "data is corrupted → retry pointless" rarely matches the actual trigger; the realistic trigger is a repository/schema-drift/code bug (e.g. a renamed clouder_id column) that would silently fail-and-drop EVERY run with no DLQ alarm.

However the finding's headline framing overstates the trigger: ordinary TRANSIENT Aurora/Data-API anomalies do NOT surface as KeyError/ValueError. Transient infra failures throw botocore ClientError with codes like InternalServerErrorException/ServiceUnavailableException, which the retry layer (data_api_retry.py) retries and, if exhausted, re-raises as ClientError — NOT in _PERMANENT_ERRORS — so they correctly redrive to the DLQ. The RDS Data API does not return successful-but-misaligned columnMetadata/records, so the specific "partial Data API result shape → KeyError" pathway in the evidence is largely hypothetical. Also the data is preserved in S3 and the run is recorded FAILED in ingest_runs, so it is recoverable by re-enqueue, not unrecoverably lost. The cited int(rows[0]["cnt"]) is in API/spotify count methods, not the canonicalize worker. Net: the design flaw (over-broad type-based permanent classification bypassing the only DLQ alarm) is genuine, but it bites on code/schema-drift bugs rather than routine transient DB blips, and S3 retains the raw data — a real observability/reliability gap of lower likelihood/impact than P2.

Refuting on trigger-implausibility (the finding's own hypothesized mechanism is foreclosed by the code), with impact as a secondary reason.

1. The finding's central claim is that a TRANSIENT Data-API/Aurora anomaly surfaces as KeyError/ValueError during canonicalize and gets misclassified as permanent. But genuinely transient Aurora/Data-API conditions are not KeyError/ValueError — botocore raises them as ClientError with codes DatabaseResumingException / StatementTimeoutException / InternalServerErrorException / ServiceUnavailableError / ThrottlingException (data_api_retry.py:17-25). These are retried 5x at the Data API layer (data_api.py:27 @retry_data_api) and, if they persist, re-raise as ClientError — which is NOT in _PERMANENT_ERRORS (worker_handler.py:21). So the worker treats them as transient and re-raises (worker_handler.py:180) → message redrives to the DLQ. The real transient path is handled correctly.

2. The specific hypothesized trigger — a 'partial/unexpected Data API result shape' producing a KeyError on row['clouder_entity_type'] — cannot occur. _to_rows is fully defensive (data_api.py:166-187): if records isn't a list → returns []; if columnMetadata isn't a list → returns []; extra fields past column count are dropped (never raises). A malformed/partial SUCCESS response therefore yields an empty row list, and find_identity guards it with `if not rows: return None` (repositories.py:435-436). Every int(rows[0]['cnt']) site is guarded by `if rows else 0` (repositories.py:800,905,935,968,998). For row['clouder_entity_type'] to KeyError, the engine would have to return a result for a different query than `SELECT clouder_entity_type, clouder_id` — a deterministic schema/code bug, not a transient blip, for which 'permanent → don't retry' is the correct classification.

3. Even granting the claim were true: raw data stays in S3 (finding concedes), the run is marked FAILED with error_code + phase, and an ERROR-level canonicalization_failed structured log is emitted (worker_handler.py:164-175). For an admin-driven ingest pipeline serving a small DJ circle, this is observable and recoverable by re-enqueuing — not silent unrecoverable loss. No plausible production trigger and bounded, recoverable, observable impact.

Code facts in the finding are accurate: _PERMANENT_ERRORS includes ValueError/TypeError/KeyError (worker_handler.py:21); permanent errors mark the run FAILED and `continue`, deleting the SQS message with no DLQ (worker_handler.py:140,147,176-179); find_identity does hard-key access row["clouder_entity_type"]/["clouder_id"] (repositories.py:439-440) and is on the canonicalize hot path (canonicalize.py:451,486,521,558,604); int(rows[0]["cnt"]) coercions exist (repositories.py:800 etc.). HOWEVER the central RISK — that genuine transient Data-API/Aurora failures get misclassified as permanent and silently dropped — does not hold. Real transient failures (throttling, StatementTimeout, ServiceUnavailable, Aurora resuming, connection reset) surface as botocore ClientError/BotoCoreError. The retry decorator (data_api_retry.py: TRANSIENT_ERROR_CODES, _retry) retries retryable codes and re-raises ClientError on exhaustion/non-retryable codes. ClientError is NOT in _PERMANENT_ERRORS, so in the handler isinstance(exc, _PERMANENT_ERRORS) is False → `raise` → SQS redrive → DLQ. That path is already correct. For the finding's specific KeyError trigger to fire, boto3 must return a SUCCESSFUL (HTTP 200) response whose columnMetadata is present-but-malformed (fewer/renamed columns than the SELECT projects) while records is non-empty, so _to_rows (data_api.py:166-187) builds a row dict missing the key. AWS RDS Data API emits records and columnMetadata atomically from one engine response when includeResultMetadata=True; a partial/renamed metadata block on a 200 response is not a documented or observed AWS behavior. NULL values do not trigger it (the key is present; _from_field returns None → str(None)). The reproducibility requirement (trace a concrete triggering path from real code) cannot be met: the asserted "transient DB-shape anomaly → KeyError → silent drop" path is speculative, not constructible. There remains only a minor latent code-smell: the permanent set is broad and a future refactor could change which exception a real transient surfaces as — a defensive-coding nit, not an actionable reliability bug.

title: Repository-pattern leak: raw SQL and direct DataAPIClient use in token resolvers and publish service
evidence: Three non-repository modules hold a DataAPIClient and embed SQL directly: spotify_token_resolver.py executes SELECT (line 70) and 'UPDATE user_vendor_tokens SET ...' (line 121-123); ytmusic_token_resolver.py does the same (lines 70, 117-119); playlists_publish_service.py runs "SELECT spotify_id FROM users WHERE id = :id" inline (lines 172-173). The codebase's stated convention is that all raw SQL lives in *_repository.py / repositories.py (§4); these services bypass that and own SQL against user_vendor_tokens and users.
risk: SQL against the security-sensitive user_vendor_tokens table (KMS-encrypted vendor tokens) is scattered outside the repository layer, so schema changes, retry/transaction discipline, and audit of token-table access are no longer centralized. Increases the chance a future edit forgets the parameterization/transaction conventions the repositories enforce.
recommendation: Move these queries into the existing auth/auth_repository.py (or a dedicated vendor-token repository) and have the resolvers/publish service depend on that, keeping all SQL — especially user_vendor_tokens access — in the repository layer.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code. All three modules genuinely hold a DataAPIClient/data_api and embed raw SQL outside the repository layer: spotify_token_resolver.py runs an inline SELECT (lines 70-81) and UPDATE on user_vendor_tokens (lines 121-137); ytmusic_token_resolver.py does the same (SELECT 70-81, UPDATE 117-133); playlists_publish_service.py's UserSpotifyIdReader.get_spotify_id runs inline "SELECT spotify_id FROM users WHERE id = :id" (lines 172-176). The repository convention is real and observable — every other table is accessed via a *_repository.py class, and auth_repository.py (AuthRepository) already owns user_vendor_tokens SQL via upsert_vendor_token/get_vendor_token/delete_vendor_token, so these resolvers duplicate access to the same security-sensitive table outside the established layer. The finding's exact line-number spans are slightly imprecise (it cites the opening line of multi-line SQL strings, e.g. "121-123" where the UPDATE actually spans 121-137), but the substance — non-repository modules embedding SQL against user_vendor_tokens and users — is fully accurate. P3 is the correct severity: the SQL is parameterized and functionally correct, so this is a layering/maintainability (ARCH) concern, not a runtime defect.

Finding is factually accurate: spotify_token_resolver.py (SELECT line 70, UPDATE 121-128), ytmusic_token_resolver.py (SELECT 70, UPDATE 117-124), and playlists_publish_service.py UserSpotifyIdReader (SELECT 172-173) all hold a DataAPIClient and embed raw SQL against user_vendor_tokens/users, bypassing the convention that SQL lives in *_repository.py. There is even a concrete duplication: auth_repository.py already exposes get_vendor_token()/upsert_vendor_token() doing the same SELECT/UPSERT the resolvers re-implement inline. So the architectural observation is real.

But the stated risks do not matter for this MVP: (1) Injection — every query is fully parameterized (:user_id, :id), so no exposure regardless of layer. (2) Retry discipline — @retry_data_api() is applied at DataAPIClient.execute, so these inline calls already get the same retry behavior as repositories; moving them changes nothing here. (3) Transaction discipline — each resolver is a single SELECT then a single UPDATE, and the publish reader is a single SELECT; none require multi-statement atomicity, so there is no transaction to lose. (4) Audit/security of the KMS-encrypted token table — the SQL only moves ciphertext blobs; no plaintext touches SQL. The remaining concern ("increases the chance a future edit forgets conventions") is explicitly speculative maintainability, with no plausible trigger that affects a small-DJ-circle MVP. Per the impact rubric (purely theoretical issues with no plausible trigger -> refuted), this is refuted on impact. Severity P3 is the correct floor for an ARCH hygiene/DRY observation and should not be elevated.

Finding is accurate and reproducible; could not refute. All three code claims verified line-by-line: (1) spotify_token_resolver.py holds a DataAPIClient and runs inline SELECT (line 70) + UPDATE on user_vendor_tokens (lines 121-128); (2) ytmusic_token_resolver.py mirrors it (lines 70, 117-124); (3) playlists_publish_service.py's UserSpotifyIdReader runs inline "SELECT spotify_id FROM users WHERE id = :id" (lines 172-173).

The premise is real, not invented: AuthRepository (auth/auth_repository.py:256-313) already centralizes user_vendor_tokens SQL with get_vendor_token / upsert_vendor_token / delete_vendor_token, and get_vendor_token's projection (access/refresh/data_key enc + expires_at) is the exact shape SpotifyTokenResolver re-implements inline. The review's own system-map states "All raw SQL in scope lives in repositories.py" (system-map.md:351) and explicitly calls out "the token resolvers/publish service" as off-pattern exceptions (system-map.md:395). The ytmusic design doc (2026-05-31, lines 64-75) confirms the split is deliberate-by-omission: the OAuth-callback WRITE goes through auth_repository.upsert_vendor_token, but the resolver READ re-implements its own inline SQL — exactly the inconsistency flagged.

Reproducible path: POST /playlists/{id}/publish (curation_handler.py:1948) -> _handle_publish (1178) -> _build_spotify_user_client (1804) -> SpotifyTokenResolver.resolve(user_id) executes the inline SELECT/UPDATE on user_vendor_tokens; the same request constructs UserSpotifyIdReader(repo.data_api) (1193) whose get_spotify_id runs inline SQL on users. ytmusic mirror via POST /playlists/{id}/publish-ytmusic -> _build_ytmusic_user_client -> YtmusicTokenResolver.resolve.

Severity is correct at P3. This is a maintainability/consistency (ARCH) issue, not a runtime defect: all queries use parameterized named binds with decode()/encode() for the bytea columns, so there is no SQL-injection or correctness bug today. The risk is purely future-facing (scattered access to the KMS-encrypted token table, duplicated read logic, decentralized schema/transaction discipline). A localized convention violation on a sensitive table with no current functional impact is appropriately P3.

title: Spotify result persistence writes four independent un-transacted batches per chunk
evidence: _process_results_chunk persists each chunk as four separate calls with no transaction_id: batch_upsert_source_entities (326), batch_upsert_identities (329), batch_update_spotify_results (343), propagate_release_type_to_albums (350). Each auto-commits independently. batch_update_spotify_results sets spotify_searched_at (repositories.py:802-832), and find_tracks_needing_spotify_search filters WHERE spotify_searched_at IS NULL (repositories.py:747-748). If the worker times out/crashes between the identity write and the searched_at write, the chunk simply reruns (idempotent). All four are UPSERT/UPDATE so a broad-retry replay is safe — but there is no atomic boundary tying the spotify_id/identity_map row to the searched_at flag.
risk: A crash partway through a chunk can leave clouder_tracks.spotify_id / identity_map inconsistent with spotify_searched_at across runs; because each write auto-commits, there is no single point that guarantees the searched flag and the match row land together. Low blast radius (idempotent reprocessing self-heals on the next batch), but the invariant is enforced only by ordering, not transactionally.
recommendation: Wrap the per-chunk writes in a single with repository.transaction() block (the methods already accept transaction_id), so source_entities + identity_map + tracks.spotify_searched_at commit atomically per chunk.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code; the finding is accurate. spotify_handler.py:_process_results_chunk (L283-350) makes four repository calls with NO transaction_id: batch_upsert_source_entities (L327), batch_upsert_identities (L329), batch_update_spotify_results (L343), propagate_release_type_to_albums (L350). data_api.py batch_execute only adds a transactionId when one is passed (L69), so each call auto-commits independently. batch_update_spotify_results sets spotify_searched_at (repositories.py:813) and find_tracks_needing_spotify_search filters WHERE spotify_searched_at IS NULL (repositories.py:748) — confirmed. A repository.transaction() context manager exists (repositories.py:1000 → data_api.py:99-107 begin/commit/rollback) and every method accepts transaction_id, so the recommended single-transaction fix is feasible. The finding's mitigants also hold: searched_at is the LAST write before propagation, the writes are idempotent UPSERT/UPDATE, and a mid-chunk crash leaves spotify_searched_at NULL → the chunk is re-selected and self-heals. One nuance the finding under-states (which strengthens, not refutes, it): propagate_release_type_to_albums runs AFTER searched_at commits, so a crash between L343 and L350 permanently skips album release_type propagation (the track won't be re-selected) — a genuine non-self-healing gap that the proposed atomic boundary would also close. Severity P3 is correct: tiny blast radius, primary track/identity invariant self-heals via ordering, only a secondary denormalized album field can be permanently stale, and the trigger is a low-probability mid-chunk crash. Not P2 (no data loss of primary records, no user-facing failure) and not lower (it is a real, fixable consistency gap enforced only by write ordering rather than transactionally).

Mechanics are accurately described (four auto-committed batches, no transaction_id), but the risk does not hold. The write order makes spotify_searched_at the LAST data write (line 343) before the re-derivable album propagation, and find_tracks_needing_spotify_search filters WHERE spotify_searched_at IS NULL (repositories.py:748). The dangerous direction — searched_at set without the identity/spotify_id — cannot occur, because the identity/source_entity UPSERTs (lines 327, 329) commit strictly BEFORE searched_at. Any crash before the searched_at write leaves the flag NULL, so the track is re-fetched on the next SQS batch and all earlier rows are re-UPSERTed via ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (lines 321, 491) with identical deterministic data. This "flag-last + idempotent replay" pattern is the standard idempotent-checkpoint idiom and is functionally equivalent to a transaction for the self-healing invariant the finding cares about. The finding itself concedes the chunk "self-heals on the next batch" and "broad-retry replay is safe" — there is no incorrect state that persists. For this MVP (small DJ circle), the worst realizable outcome is a redundant re-UPSERT of a few rows on redelivery: no data corruption, no user-facing effect. The transaction wrapper is a tidiness nicety, not a reliability fix.

Verified all structural claims against the code. In src/collector/spotify_handler.py, _process_results_chunk (lines 283-350) issues four persistence calls with no transaction_id: batch_upsert_source_entities (327), batch_upsert_identities (329), batch_update_spotify_results (343), propagate_release_type_to_albums (350). Confirmed every one of these repository methods accepts an optional transaction_id (repositories.py:308, 478, 805, 837) but the handler passes none, so each underlying DataAPIClient.execute/batch_execute runs with transactionId=None and auto-commits independently (data_api.py — only transaction() context manager batches commits; it is not used here). A repository.transaction() context manager exists (repositories.py:1000-1001 → data_api.py:99-107) and would make the recommended atomic-per-chunk wrapping trivial, so the recommendation is sound and effort is genuinely small.

Reproducible path: SQS spotify-search message → handler runs ISRC search → _persist_results loops chunks (handler line 252-254) → a Lambda timeout or crash lands between the identity write (329) and the searched_at write (343). Result: identity_map holds a spotify→track row while clouder_tracks.spotify_id / spotify_searched_at remain NULL. There is no single transactional boundary tying the match row to the searched flag — the invariant is enforced only by call ordering, exactly as stated.

The finding is honest about blast radius: find_tracks_needing_spotify_search filters WHERE spotify_searched_at IS NULL (repositories.py:747-748), so a partially-written track is simply re-found and fully replayed on the next batch; all four ops are UPSERT/UPDATE, so replay is idempotent and self-heals. spotify_id and spotify_searched_at are written together in the same batch_update (so those two never diverge); only the separately-committed identity_map row can transiently lead the flag, and that window heals on rerun. No data loss, no user-visible corruption, low probability (requires crash inside a sub-second window mid-chunk). This is a legitimate but minor robustness/invariant nit. Code path is real and the P3 severity is appropriate.

title: Retry path is unit-tested only on the bare decorator, never wired through DataAPIClient or the canonicalizer N+1 path
evidence: test_data_api_retry.py exercises the decorator in isolation with synthetic ClientErrors, and test_data_api.py covers transaction commit/rollback with a fake client — but no test asserts that DataAPIClient.execute/batch_execute/begin_transaction actually retry on a DatabaseResumingException end-to-end (the decorators are applied at data_api.py:27,51,74,83,91 but never triggered through a real method call in tests). There is also no test for the canonicalizer's per-entity resolve path's behavior under a mid-loop transient failure (e.g. find_identity raising), nor for the broad-retry-replays-a-whole-batch idempotency claim documented at data_api_retry.py:43-56.
risk: The decorator-to-method wiring (which decorator guards which method, and that pre-execution vs broad sets are applied to the right calls) is not regression-protected; a refactor could swap retry_data_api_pre_execution for retry_data_api on commit/rollback (re-introducing the double-apply hazard the split was designed to prevent) with all tests still green.
recommendation: Add tests that call DataAPIClient.execute/commit_transaction with a stub rds-data client raising ClientError('DatabaseResumingException') / ('StatementTimeoutException') and assert retry-vs-no-retry per method, plus a canonicalizer test where find_identity fails mid-chunk to lock in the transaction-rollback + classification behavior.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code. data_api.py applies @retry_data_api at lines 27 (execute), 51 (batch_execute), 74 (begin_transaction) and @retry_data_api_pre_execution at 83 (commit_transaction), 91 (rollback_transaction) — exactly as the finding states. test_data_api_retry.py exercises the decorators only on synthetic local op() functions; grep confirms every `raise _client_error(...)` lives there, never on a DataAPIClient method. test_data_api.py instantiates DataAPIClient with FakeRdsDataClient, which never raises a ClientError, so no test drives a transient error end-to-end through execute/batch_execute/begin/commit/rollback. No integration test covers this path. Thus the decorator-to-method wiring is genuinely not regression-protected: swapping retry_data_api_pre_execution for retry_data_api on commit/rollback would re-introduce the double-apply hazard with all tests still green. One minor overstatement: test_canonicalize_transactions.py:134 test_transaction_rolled_back_on_failure DOES cover a mid-process failure (create_label raising RuntimeError) with a rollback assertion — but it uses a generic RuntimeError, fails on create_label not find_identity, and does not exercise retry classification, so the finding's narrower point (no transient-ClientError/retry-classification test mid-loop) still holds. This is a TEST-dimension missing-regression-test finding over currently-correct code; no live defect exists, so P3 is appropriate.

Verified factually accurate. tests/unit/test_data_api_retry.py exercises both decorators (retry_data_api, retry_data_api_pre_execution) only against synthetic throwaway functions, never through a real DataAPIClient method. test_data_api.py's FakeRdsDataClient never raises ClientError, so it covers parameter encoding and transaction commit/rollback ordering but never triggers a retry. No test in tests/unit or tests/integration drives execute/commit_transaction with a stub rds-data client raising DatabaseResumingException/StatementTimeoutException, so the decorator-to-method wiring (retry_data_api on execute/batch_execute/begin_transaction at data_api.py:27,51,74 vs retry_data_api_pre_execution on commit/rollback at :83,91) is unprotected against a refactor that swaps them and re-introduces the double-apply/partial-commit-replay hazard the split was designed to prevent. test_canonicalize_transactions.py confirms phases run inside a transaction but does not assert behavior under a mid-loop find_identity ClientError. The finding correctly scopes this as a regression-protection/test-quality gap, not a live bug, and the underlying retry logic is well unit-tested — so impact is genuinely low. The dominant real-world trigger for this MVP (Aurora min_acu=0 cold-start) raises DatabaseResumingException, which is a pre-execution code that retries safely on every method regardless, limiting practical exposure. P3 is the correct severity: a legitimate, correctly-classified low-priority test gap, not refuted.

Verified the finding against the actual code; it is accurate and not refutable.

(1) End-to-end retry gap CONFIRMED: In tests/unit/test_data_api.py the FakeRdsDataClient (lines 178-200) never raises a ClientError. Its execute_statement/begin_transaction/commit_transaction/rollback_transaction always return normally. So no test ever drives a real DataAPIClient method (execute/batch_execute/begin_transaction/commit_transaction/rollback_transaction) into the retry/except branch. All retry-branch coverage in test_data_api_retry.py is against synthetic local op() functions decorated inline (lines 29,47,63,90,102,122,136,158), never through DataAPIClient. A grep for DatabaseResumingException|StatementTimeoutException|ClientError across tests/ returns hits only inside test_data_api_retry.py.

(2) Wiring-not-regression-protected CONFIRMED: data_api.py applies retry_data_api() to execute/batch_execute/begin_transaction (lines 27,51,74) and retry_data_api_pre_execution() to commit_transaction/rollback_transaction (lines 83,91). The split exists precisely because broad retry on commit/rollback could replay a post-execution operation. The only tests touching commit/rollback (test_data_api.py:254-278) assert WHICH client method is invoked (commit vs rollback ordering) — not which decorator wraps it, and not any retry behavior. A refactor swapping retry_data_api_pre_execution -> retry_data_api on commit_transaction would leave every test green (the pre-execution skip test at retry test lines 117-129 decorates a local op(), not DataAPIClient.commit_transaction), re-introducing the double-apply/post-execution-replay hazard the split was designed to prevent. This is exactly the risk the finding states.

(3) Canonicalizer mid-loop transient gap CONFIRMED: test_canonicalize_transactions.py::test_transaction_rolled_back_on_failure (lines 134-152) triggers rollback via create_label.side_effect=RuntimeError, not via find_identity raising mid-chunk and not via a transient ClientError/classification path. The specific scenario the finding names is absent; the finding correctly acknowledges the existing generic rollback test.

The finding makes no false claim of a current production bug — it asserts a latent-regression test gap, which is real and reproducible as described. Severity P3 (TEST dimension, no live defect, effort S, reduced regression safety on a deliberately-split safety-critical decorator) is appropriate; not P2 because no current incorrect behavior exists and the retry logic itself is well unit-tested.

title: Deploy applies new Lambda code (Terraform apply) BEFORE migrations run — schema/code skew window
evidence: deploy.yml runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` (line 83) which republishes dist/collector.zip to ALL runtime Lambdas (canonicalization_worker, curation, spotify, etc.) BEFORE the 'Run DB migrations via Lambda' step (line 102) invokes the migration Lambda. New code therefore runs against the OLD schema during the entire window between apply completion and migration success — and permanently if the migration step fails. Concrete instance: alembic/versions/20260531_30_track_key.py:21-28 adds clouder_tracks.key_name/key_camelot, and conservative_update_track (repositories.py:647-694, per system map) writes key_camelot. A deploy shipping that canonicalizer build before migration 30 lands makes every canonicalization UPDATE/INSERT referencing key_camelot fail with 'column does not exist'.
risk: Between Terraform apply and migration completion, worker/curation Lambdas executing SQL that references not-yet-created columns fail (canonicalization stuck at RAW_SAVED, curation 5xx). If the migration step fails, the new code is live against the stale schema with no automatic rollback. SQS-driven workers retry into DLQ; user-facing curation returns errors.
recommendation: Re-order deploy.yml so migrations run BEFORE the Terraform apply that swaps Lambda code (expand-then-migrate, or migrate-then-deploy-code), or split into two terraform applies: apply infra+migration-Lambda first, run migrations, then apply the rest. At minimum gate the code swap on migration success. Adopt expand/contract migration discipline so code and schema overlap is always backward-compatible.

votes.reason: Verified every load-bearing claim against the code. (1) Ordering confirmed: deploy.yml runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` at lines 81-100 BEFORE the "Run DB migrations via Lambda" step at lines 102-132; corroborated by docs/ops/deploy.md (step 3 apply, step 4 migrate). (2) "Apply republishes code to ALL runtime Lambdas first" confirmed: every Lambda in infra/lambda.tf (collector, canonicalization_worker, spotify_search_worker, vendor_match_worker, label/artist enrichers, auto_enrich_dispatch) uses `filename = local.lambda_zip_file` with `source_code_hash = filebase64sha256(local.lambda_zip_file)`; package_lambda.sh builds one shared dist/collector.zip, so a new build changes the hash and republishes all functions during apply. (3) Concrete instance verified exactly as stated: alembic/versions/20260531_30_track_key.py:21-28 adds clouder_tracks.key_name/key_camelot (this is the head migration), and both repositories.py create_track (INSERT, lines 618-628) and conservative_update_track (UPDATE, lines 672-673) write key_name/key_camelot as literal SQL column names. canonicalize.py:587-644 `_resolve_track` reaches both paths unconditionally (no feature flag) on every track resolution, so during the skew window canonicalization UPDATE/INSERT fail with 'column does not exist'. (4) Failure mode verified: deploy.yml:127-131 fails the workflow on migration error, but the already-applied Lambda code stays live with no rollback step, leaving new code permanently against the stale schema until manual remediation. No guard or mitigation was missed. The only caveat (skew only bites deploys that bundle an un-applied schema change, and worker failures are DLQ-recoverable after the migration lands) affects frequency, not the mechanism. P1 is appropriately calibrated: a real reliability defect with a concrete trigger and no automatic rollback, but gated on a specific deploy shape rather than every deploy.

The ordering claim is factually correct and verified: deploy.yml runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` (line 83), which swaps in-place Lambda code on all runtime functions via `filename`/`source_code_hash` (infra/lambda.tf, curation.tf), BEFORE the "Run DB migrations via Lambda" step (line 102). New code therefore runs against the old schema during the apply→migration window, and permanently if migration fails (no auto-rollback). The concrete example checks out: migration 30 (alembic/versions/20260531_30_track_key.py) adds clouder_tracks.key_name/key_camelot, and canonicalize.py:397-398 writes key_camelot via _resolve_track. So this is not refuted — it is a real ordering defect, and the expand/contract recommendation is sound.

However, P1 overstates the impact for THIS MVP for several verified reasons. (1) The skew window is seconds, not the implied long window: the migration Lambda is invoked in the very next workflow step (one `terraform output` + one `aws lambda invoke`). (2) The window self-heals on the happy path: the canonicalization queue has redrive maxReceiveCount=5 (infra/sqs.tf:14-17), so any message failing on a not-yet-created column retries across SQS backoff (minutes), by which point the migration has long completed — DLQ is only reached if the migration itself failed. (3) There is no automatic ingest timer — no EventBridge/cron exists for canonicalization; it is enqueued only by admin-initiated ingest (handler.py:_enqueue_canonicalization). For the window to be hit, the same small admin operator group must be actively ingesting at the instant of their own deploy push to main. (4) The migration-failure case (the genuinely permanent risk) is loud and operator-attended: the deploy step does `sys.exit` on FunctionError/non-ok status (deploy.yml:127-131), turning the deploy job red. (5) Migrations and their consuming code ship in the same push (migration 30 + db_models in commit bfceed1; canonicalize.py write path additive), and new read paths degrade gracefully (curation_handler.py:277 uses getattr default). Net: realistic impact is a transient, self-healing retry blip with a worst case that is immediately surfaced to a small DJ-circle operator. Worth fixing with expand/contract discipline, but not an MVP-blocking P1. P2.

Verified reproducible. deploy.yml runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` at line 81-100 BEFORE the "Run DB migrations via Lambda" step at line 102-132. All runtime Lambdas in infra/ share a single zip via `local.lambda_zip_file` with `source_code_hash = filebase64sha256(local.lambda_zip_file)` (infra/lambda.tf:6-10, curation.tf:11-15, etc.), so a deploy republishes new code to every function in the same apply that precedes migrations. Concrete failing path: (1) push to main triggers package_lambda.sh -> new collector.zip -> hash changes -> apply swaps all Lambda code; (2) during the window before migration head lands, a Beatport ingest SQS message hits the canonicalization worker -> canonicalize.py:389-404 _resolve_track -> repositories.py:613-694 create_track / conservative_update_track, both of which write key_name/key_camelot (lines 620-624, 672-673) added only by alembic/versions/20260531_30_track_key.py:21-28 -> SQL fails 'column does not exist'; (3) concurrently a user GET on a playlist runs playlists_repository.py:604-609 `SELECT t.key_name, t.key_camelot` -> 5xx. No feature flag or column-existence guard gates the new-column references (grep found none). Migration-step failure exits non-zero (deploy.yml:127-131) but does NOT roll back the already-applied Lambda code, so a permanently failing migration leaves new code live against stale schema with no auto-rollback. Mitigations exist but are partial: canonicalization queue has maxReceiveCount=5 (infra/sqs.tf:16) so async workers retry into success once the migration lands during a normal deploy, bounding that window to seconds–minutes; however the user-facing curation read path is not self-healing and 5xxes during the window, and Aurora cold-start (ADR-0014) on the migration Lambda invoke can widen the window. The cited key_camelot migration is already head (dated 2026-05-31), but the finding is a structural deploy-ordering defect that recurs for every future additive migration, correctly using it as an illustration. Real defect with a verified path and no compensating control; severity P1 stands.

title: Migration step has no rollback — a failed migration leaves new code live against old schema
evidence: The migration step fails the workflow on FunctionError or status!='ok' (deploy.yml:127-131) but only AFTER 'Terraform apply' (line 81) already published the new Lambda code. There is no compensating step to revert the Lambda code or terraform state on migration failure; the job simply exits non-zero. The frontend deploy (line 146) is skipped, but the backend code is already swapped.
risk: Any migration failure (Aurora won't wake within the timeout, lock contention, a bad DDL, IAM token/role issue) leaves production running new code against the prior schema indefinitely until a human intervenes — silent partial outage of whichever subsystem depends on the missing schema.
recommendation: Run migrations before the code-swapping apply (see prior finding), or add an explicit rollback/alarm path: on migration failure, re-apply the previous Lambda artifact or fail closed before the apply that changes runtime code. Page on the failure (alarm_sns_topic_arn currently defaults empty).

votes.reason:
All cited facts verified against the actual file. The migration step (deploy.yml:102-132) runs AFTER `terraform apply` (line 81-100), and apply publishes new Lambda code: infra/lambda.tf:6,10 use `filename = local.lambda_zip_file` + `source_code_hash = filebase64sha256(...)`, and the workflow packages a fresh zip at lines 37-39 before apply, so changed code is swapped pre-migration. The migration step fails the workflow on FunctionError (line 127-128) or status != "ok" (line 130-131) with no compensating revert of Lambda code or terraform state — the job simply exits non-zero. Frontend deploy (line 146) is skipped on failure (default fail-fast), but backend code is already live. The `alarm_sns_topic_arn` paging claim is also correct: infra/variables.tf:233 defaults it to "", the deploy workflow never passes it, and infra/alarms.tf:47-48 gates alarm_actions on a non-empty value, so failures are not paged. The finding is technically accurate and not refutable.

Severity adjustment: this is a latent/conditional reliability risk that only manifests when a migration actually fails, and the blast radius depends on the migration content (additive/backward-compatible migrations would not break the running new code). It is not a guaranteed-impact defect that fires on every deploy. Combined with the standard expand/contract mitigation and the requirement of a triggering failure, P2 is more defensible than P1, though the missing rollback + absent paging make it a legitimate, actionable reliability finding.

Technically accurate: deploy.yml runs "Terraform apply" (line 81) which publishes new Lambda code BEFORE "Run DB migrations via Lambda" (lines 102-132), and a migration failure exits the job non-zero with no compensating rollback of the Lambda code/state — leaving new code against old schema. The alarm topic does default empty (infra/alarms.tf, terraform.tfvars.example:8). However, the P1 severity and "silent partial outage indefinitely" framing don't hold for this MVP. The deploy triggers ONLY on push to main (no schedule/cron/dispatch) — it is always operator-initiated and attended. A migration failure fails the workflow LOUDLY (red CI) on a push the single operator just made, so it is self-announcing, not silent; "indefinitely until a human intervenes" overstates it because the human is already watching the red job. The cited triggers (Aurora won't wake within timeout, lock contention, bad DDL, IAM issue) are plausible, but recovery is re-running the deploy job or fixing the DDL and re-pushing — the code is already live, so the next migration run brings schema forward. Recent migrations are uniformly additive (add_column/create_index/create_table in revisions 28-30), so the practical failure is errors on one subsystem during an attended deploy for a small DJ circle, not a silent production-wide outage. Migrate-before-apply is the correct pattern and worth doing, but for this MVP this is a process/ordering improvement with a loud, attended, recoverable failure mode — P2, not P1.

Confirmed and reproducible. The deploy workflow runs `Terraform apply` (deploy.yml:81) BEFORE migrations (deploy.yml:102). Lambda code is published directly in apply via `filename`/`source_code_hash` (infra/lambda.tf:6-10 etc.), so new code is live the instant apply finishes. The migration step then invokes the migration Lambda and fails the job on FunctionError or status!='ok' (deploy.yml:127-131) with no compensating rollback of Lambda code or terraform state — the job just exits non-zero and the frontend step is skipped while backend code stays swapped.

Concrete forward-incompatible path: migration 20260531_30_track_key.py adds columns key_name/key_camelot to clouder_tracks (with a JSONB backfill UPDATE that is a plausible failure point — timeout/lock/Aurora-cold-start within the Lambda invoke). The new worker code already references those columns: canonicalize.py:639/397 -> repositories.py create_track issues `INSERT INTO clouder_tracks (... key_name, key_camelot ...)`, and conservative_update_track (repositories.py:686-687) does the same. Trigger: any SQS canonicalization message hits worker_handler.py -> Canonicalizer.process_run -> create_track/conservative_update_track -> Postgres `column "key_name" does not exist` -> every ingest canonicalization fails. There is no automated detection: alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults "" (infra/variables.tf:230-234) and is NOT passed in the deploy `-var` list, so the DLQ alarms (alarms.tf/logging.tf gate on that var) never page.

Severity P1 is correct: silent partial subsystem outage (ingest/canonicalization) on a non-exotic trigger, with no alarm and no rollback. Not P0 because it requires a migration to actually fail, only affects the subsystem depending on the new schema (API reads/curation keep working on old schema), and is recoverable by re-running the migration/deploy. The finding's 'indefinitely until a human intervenes' slightly overstates recoverability (a deploy re-run heals it) but the no-rollback + no-page core claim holds.

title: Migration Lambda connects to Aurora with no connect/lock/statement timeout and no resume retry on cold cluster
evidence: migration_handler builds a psycopg URL with only `?sslmode=require` (migration_handler.py:98,114) and calls command.upgrade (line 53) with no connect_timeout, lock_timeout, or statement_timeout. alembic/env.py:41-45 uses engine_from_config with NullPool and no connect_args. The whole upgrade runs in one transaction (env.py:55 begin_transaction). The Data API resume-retry (data_api_retry.py DatabaseResumingException) does NOT cover this psycopg TCP path. With min_acu=0 / 300s auto-pause (rds.tf), the migration Lambda's first TCP connect must wake a paused cluster; there is no application-level retry around the connect, and a long backfill (e.g. the full-table UPDATE in 20260531_30_track_key.py:34-51) plus cold-start can approach the 900s Lambda timeout.
risk: On a cold/paused Aurora the connect may hit libpq's default timeout or the resume may be slow, failing the migration step nondeterministically; a long DDL+backfill holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE locks with no lock_timeout can block runtime writers indefinitely or be killed only by the Lambda's 900s hard timeout (a timeout is not a Python exception, so the failure is opaque).
recommendation: Add connect_timeout and a SET lock_timeout/statement_timeout via connect_args/options in env.py; add a small retry loop around the initial connect to absorb the Aurora resume; consider a pre-warm Data API ping before invoking the migration Lambda in deploy.yml.

votes.reason: All cited claims verified against the code. migration_handler.py:53 calls command.upgrade with a psycopg URL carrying only `?sslmode=require` (lines 98, 114) — no connect_timeout/lock_timeout/statement_timeout. alembic/env.py:41-45 uses engine_from_config with pool.NullPool and no connect_args; the whole upgrade runs inside one transaction (env.py:55, context.begin_transaction). data_api_retry.py confirms the resume-retry catches only botocore ClientError on RDS Data API calls (DatabaseResumingException etc.), so it does NOT wrap the psycopg/SQLAlchemy TCP connect used by migrations — psycopg raises OperationalError, not ClientError. Infra defaults confirm the cold-cluster premise: aurora_serverless_min_acu default 0 (variables.tf:130), aurora_auto_pause_seconds default 300 (variables.tf:142), migration_lambda_timeout_seconds default 900 (variables.tf:100), and deploy.yml does not override min_acu. The full-table UPDATE backfill plus ADD COLUMN DDL (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE locks) exists at alembic/versions/20260531_30_track_key.py:21-51 (finding's path/lines were slightly off — file lives under alembic/versions/ not alembic/ root, and the backfill spans 34-51 wrapped by op.execute starting line 34, with the ADD COLUMN DDL at 21-28 — but the substance is correct). One nuance the finding overstates: deploy.yml invokes the Lambda synchronously (RequestResponse) and checks FunctionError/non-ok status, so a 900s timeout is surfaced to the deploy pipeline rather than being fully opaque; however this does not address the missing lock_timeout/statement_timeout or the cold-connect resume gap, so it does not warrant a downgrade. P2 is appropriate: deploy-time path (bounded blast radius) but genuine reliability risk of blocking runtime writers via unbounded locks and nondeterministic cold-connect failures.

Technical claims are accurate (no connect/lock/statement timeout in migration_handler.py:98,114; NullPool with no connect_args in env.py:41-45; single-transaction upgrade; Data API resume-retry does not cover this psycopg path; 900s timeout; min_acu=0 in rds.tf). But the IMPACT for this MVP does not justify P2:

1. Deploy-time only, human-supervised. The migration Lambda is invoked exactly once per deploy by `aws lambda invoke` in deploy.yml:105 — never by users, SQS, or any hot path. The small DJ circle never touches it.

2. Cold-paused precondition is weak. `terraform apply` (deploy.yml:81-100) runs immediately before the migration invoke and reconciles the Aurora cluster, so the cluster is effectively warm by the migration step. A deploy is not a 300s-idle steady state.

3. Failure is NOT opaque. The deploy step inspects FunctionError and `status != "ok"` and `sys.exit`s (deploy.yml:127-131). A synchronous invoke surfaces even a 900s Lambda hard timeout as FunctionError in invoke metadata, which the step catches and fails on. The finding's "a timeout is not a Python exception, so the failure is opaque" is refuted by the CI invoker checking invoke metadata, not just the response body.

4. "Blocks runtime writers indefinitely" is wrong. The 900s Lambda timeout bounds any hang; when the client TCP connection dies, Postgres rolls back the open transaction and releases locks. Worst case is a failed deploy, not an indefinitely-locked production DB.

5. Tiny backfill. A single full-table UPDATE on clouder_tracks for a small DJ circle is nowhere near 900s; the "approaches 900s" claim has no plausible trigger at this data volume.

6. Idempotent / re-runnable. `command.upgrade(head)` is revision-idempotent; a flaky connect just means re-run the deploy — standard CI remediation, no data loss, no user-facing outage (runtime uses the Data API path with its own ADR-0014 resume-retry).

Worst realistic outcome: a flaky deploy step that fails loudly in CI and is re-run. That is deploy-tooling hardening, not a runtime reliability risk. Recommendations (connect_timeout/lock_timeout) are reasonable but low value here. Severity should be P3, not P2.

Code facts all verified. env.py:41-45 uses engine_from_config with NullPool and no connect_args; alembic.ini, env.py, and migration_handler.py have zero connect_timeout/lock_timeout/statement_timeout (grep confirms only an unrelated migration filename matches). The upgrade runs in one transaction (env.py:55). data_api_retry.py only catches botocore ClientError on the Data API client, so it genuinely does not cover the psycopg TCP connect in env.py — confirmed. Infra defaults confirmed: aurora_serverless_min_acu=0 (variables.tf:130), aurora_auto_pause_seconds=300 (:142), migration_lambda_timeout_seconds=900 (:100). The backfill in 20260531_30_track_key.py:34-51 is a full-table UPDATE in the same transaction as two op.add_column ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDLs.

A concrete path exists: deploy.yml:105 invokes the migration Lambda with {"action":"upgrade","revision":"head"} -> command.upgrade (migration_handler.py:53) -> run_migrations_online -> connectable.connect() opens a raw psycopg connection with no timeouts.

NOT refuted — the missing timeouts and missing connect retry are real and the recommendation is sound. But severity is overstated for two reasons. (1) The stated failure mechanism is partly wrong: libpq's default connect_timeout is 0 (infinite wait), not a short default, so a slow Serverless v2 auto-pause resume causes the connect to WAIT, not fail nondeterministically; a real failure would need a TCP-level reject, which is far less likely than implied. (2) The deploy ordering makes a paused cluster at migration time improbable: terraform apply (deploy.yml:81-100) contacts/modifies the RDS cluster immediately before the apply->output->invoke sequence, which runs well under the 300s auto-pause window, so Aurora is typically already warm when the migration runs. The lock_timeout concern is valid but bounded — single full-table UPDATE on a small-tenant DB, during a low-traffic deploy window, capped by the 900s Lambda timeout, not "indefinite." This is a real latent hardening gap with an improbable trigger and bounded impact, so P3 rather than P2.

title: docs/ops/deploy.md is badly stale — documents removed Perplexity/ai_search secrets and wrong deploy order/vars
evidence: deploy.md step 2 lists SSM syncs for `/clouder/perplexity/api_key` (and step 3 `-var ai_search_enabled=true`, `perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter`), but deploy.yml syncs gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify/ytmusic (lines 56-79) and passes no perplexity/ai_search vars. deploy.md GitHub-Secrets table (lines 79-81) lists PERPLEXITY_API_KEY as required; it is unused. deploy.md 'Force-update env var' example (line 101) targets `beatport-prod-ai-search-worker`, a function that no longer exists (ai_search tables were dropped in migration 20260518_21).
risk: An operator following the runbook would create dead SSM parameters, look for a non-existent ai-search worker, and trust an inaccurate step-by-step — masking the real (and risky) apply-before-migrate ordering that the doc also describes incorrectly as steps 3→4.
recommendation: Rewrite deploy.md to match deploy.yml's actual secrets (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify/ytmusic), variables, and the true step ordering; remove all Perplexity/ai_search references.

votes.reason:
Verified all core claims against the cited files; the finding holds.

deploy.md (docs/ops/deploy.md):
- Line 34: SSM sync `/clouder/perplexity/api_key` — CONFIRMED stale. deploy.yml lines 56-79 sync gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify/ytmusic only; no perplexity.
- Lines 41, 43: `-var="ai_search_enabled=true"` and `-var="perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter=/clouder/perplexity/api_key"` — CONFIRMED. deploy.yml apply (lines 83-100) passes neither; grep for `ai_search_enabled`/`perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter` in infra/ returns nothing. deploy.md also omits the real vars deploy.yml passes (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/ytmusic SSM params, vendor_match_enabled, enable_secretsmanager_vpc_endpoint, spotify_oauth_redirect_uri, admin_spotify_ids, allowed_frontend_redirects).
- Line 79: `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY` listed as a required production secret — CONFIRMED unused. deploy.yml secrets are GEMINI/OPENAI/TAVILY/DEEPSEEK/SPOTIFY/YTMUSIC_API/CLIENT + AWS_GITHUB_ROLE_ARN; no PERPLEXITY_API_KEY. deploy.md also omits the actually-required GEMINI/OPENAI/TAVILY/DEEPSEEK/YTMUSIC secrets from its table.
- Line 101: `--function-name beatport-prod-ai-search-worker` — CONFIRMED dead. No ai-search lambda in infra (only spotify-search-worker per main.tf/lambda.tf/outputs.tf), and migration 20260518_21_drop_ai_search_results.py drops the ai_search_results table/index.

The doc is genuinely, substantially stale and would mislead an operator (create dead SSM params, hunt for a nonexistent worker, miss required secrets). 

One minor flaw in the finding's risk narrative: it says the doc "describes incorrectly as steps 3→4" the apply-before-migrate ordering. In fact deploy.md step 3 (apply) precedes step 4 (migrate), which MATCHES deploy.yml's actual order (apply at lines 81-100, migrate at 102-132). So the ordering is NOT misdescribed; the doc agrees with reality there. This is a small inaccuracy in the risk wording, not in the four substantive defects, and does not warrant refutation.

Severity P3 is correct: pure DOCS/ops-runbook staleness, no automatic code or runtime impact, manual-operator misleadance only.

Factually confirmed: deploy.md still documents the removed Perplexity/ai_search surface while deploy.yml does not. Verified deploy.yml syncs gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify/ytmusic SSM params (lines 56-79) and passes NO perplexity or ai_search_enabled vars; deploy.md (lines 34, 41-43, 79, 101) still lists /clouder/perplexity/api_key, ai_search_enabled=true, perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter, PERPLEXITY_API_KEY as required, and a force-update example targeting beatport-prod-ai-search-worker. Confirmed ai_search_results tables were dropped in migration 20260518_21_drop_ai_search_results.py, no ai-search-worker Lambda exists in infra, and PERPLEXITY_API_KEY is read by nothing (only a stale VENDORS_ENABLED docstring default in registry.py mentions perplexity_label). So the doc is genuinely stale and an operator doing break-glass/manual ops would create dead SSM params and chase a non-existent function. Not refuted. However, the risk narrative is partly wrong: production deploys are fully automated by deploy.yml on push to main (nobody runs deploy.md step-by-step), and the doc's apply-then-migrate ordering (steps 3->4) actually MATCHES deploy.yml's real order (apply line 81, migrate line 102), so the claimed "masking risky apply-before-migrate ordering" is a non-issue. Net impact for this MVP (automated deploy, small DJ circle): docs-only staleness causing onboarding/break-glass confusion, no outage or data-loss path. P3 is the correct level.

Confirmed accurate against real code. (1) docs/ops/deploy.md:34 documents an SSM sync for /clouder/perplexity/api_key, but .github/workflows/deploy.yml:56-79 syncs gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify/ytmusic and never perplexity. (2) deploy.md:42-43 passes `-var=ai_search_enabled=true` and `-var=perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter=...`; grep for both in infra/ returns nothing — neither Terraform variable exists, so a literal copy-paste apply would error on unknown variables. The real workflow (deploy.yml:86-95) passes gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/ytmusic/vendor_match vars instead. (3) deploy.md:79 lists PERPLEXITY_API_KEY as a required production secret; perplexity is referenced only in experiments/ local sandboxes, never in infra/, src/collector/, or the workflow. (4) deploy.md:101 targets beatport-prod-ai-search-worker; no such function exists in infra/ (grep empty), and the ai_search_results table was dropped in alembic/versions/20260518_21_drop_ai_search_results.py. Reproducible path: an operator following the runbook creates a dead SSM param, runs an apply that fails on unknown vars, and hunts for a non-existent Lambda. Severity P3 is correct: DOCS-only staleness in a manual runbook with no automated/runtime impact (CI deploy.yml is itself correct); the bad vars surface as an immediate Terraform error rather than silent prod misconfiguration. Minor caveat: the risk note's claim that the doc gets the apply→migrate ordering wrong is overstated — deploy.md steps 3→4 (apply then migrate) actually match deploy.yml — but this does not affect the core stale-secrets/vars/worker finding.

title: Migration runbook omits the IAM-auth bootstrap chicken-and-egg and the manual one-time grant it depends on
evidence: deploy.yml runs the migration Lambda as AURORA_AUTH_MODE=iam connecting as clouder_migrator (DB user). But the clouder_migrator role and its rds_iam grant are themselves created by migrations 20260419_07/08, and ADR-0005:13,24 states the rds_iam grant 'cannot be self-granted' and 'must be granted by the master user once after cluster creation' as a manual step. docs/data/migrations.md's Migration-Lambda section (lines 56-74) documents both auth modes but never mentions this bootstrap dependency — i.e. the very first IAM-mode migration cannot run until a human has done the master-user grant, and the IAM flag may silently revert per ADR-0005:25.
risk: A fresh-cluster or post-cluster-rebuild deploy run with auth_mode=iam fails to authenticate (role/grant absent or IAM flag reverted) and the runbook gives no recovery procedure, stranding the deploy at the migration step.
recommendation: Add a Migrations-runbook section covering: the one-time master-user `GRANT rds_iam TO clouder_migrator`, verifying/forcing IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled after each apply, and the password-mode break-glass fallback for first-run bootstrap.

votes.reason: The finding's underlying technical observation is correct — the IAM-bootstrap chicken-and-egg is real (migration_handler.py:84-94 connects as clouder_migrator under IAM; that role and its rds_iam grant are created by migrations 07/08, which cannot run until the role can authenticate) and deploy.yml:96 sets migration_aurora_auth_mode=iam. BUT the finding's core claim — that this dependency and its recovery procedure are undocumented — is false. Every item the recommendation asks to "add" already exists in the ops docs: (1) the one-time master-user grant with exact SQL and two execution methods, plus the explicit "must be re-run if the cluster is rebuilt from scratch" note, is in docs/ops/aurora.md:61-83 ("Migrator role IAM grant"); (2) verifying/forcing IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled is documented at docs/ops/aurora.md:48-53 and ADR-0005:25; (3) the password-mode break-glass fallback is at docs/ops/deploy.md:107, and password is the Terraform default (infra/variables.tf:343) and handler default. It is also cross-referenced from docs/ops/env-vars.md:132 and docs/backend/gotchas.md:109. The finding scopes its complaint to docs/data/migrations.md alone, but migrations.md:74 explicitly directs the reader to ADR-0005, which (lines 13, 24-25) documents the self-grant constraint and silent flag revert. So the documented trail reaches the full procedure; the stated risk ("the runbook gives no recovery procedure, stranding the deploy") does not hold. At most this is a trivial nit to add a cross-link from migrations.md to docs/ops/aurora.md, not a real documentation gap.

The finding's core premise — that the docs "omit" the IAM-auth bootstrap dependency and "give no recovery procedure, stranding the deploy" — is factually wrong. Every item the recommendation asks to add already exists in the operational documentation:

1. The one-time master-user `GRANT rds_iam TO clouder_migrator`: fully documented in docs/ops/aurora.md:61-83, with two execution methods (RDS Query Editor and Data API) AND the explicit note "must be re-run if the cluster is rebuilt from scratch" (aurora.md:83) — i.e. the exact fresh-cluster/rebuild recovery the finding claims is absent. Also in docs/backend/gotchas.md:109, ADR-0005:24, docs/ops/env-vars.md:132.
2. Verify/force IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled after each apply: documented in docs/ops/aurora.md:34-57 (describe + modify-db-cluster --apply-immediately), docs/backend/gotchas.md:94-111, docs/ops/deploy.md:90-95, ADR-0005:25.
3. Password-mode break-glass fallback: docs/ops/deploy.md:107-113 ("Manual migration (break-glass — prefer the Lambda invoke path)" → alembic upgrade head); password mode is the documented default (migrations.md:71, env-vars.md).

docs/ops/aurora.md is the canonical Aurora ops runbook and the natural home for cluster-level bootstrap steps, not the Alembic reference doc. ADR-0005:29 explicitly cross-references both ../ops/aurora.md and ../data/migrations.md for this. migrations.md:74 routes the reader to ADR-0005, which in turn links to aurora.md — so the trail migrations.md → ADR-0005 → aurora.md leads an operator straight to the complete procedure.

The only true residual is narrow: the grant/flag-verify steps are not ALSO duplicated into docs/data/migrations.md, and migrations.md links to ADR-0005 but not directly to aurora.md. That is a trivial cross-reference nicety, not a missing recovery procedure. For this MVP (small DJ circle, rare fresh-cluster/rebuild, deploy run by the developer with full doc access), the documented-and-linked procedure makes "stranded deploy" implausible. The finding's stated risk does not hold.

The finding's core claim — that the bootstrap dependency, the manual master-user grant, the IAM-flag verification, and a break-glass fallback are undocumented and the runbook "gives no recovery procedure" — is false. All of it is documented, just not in docs/data/migrations.md:

- docs/ops/aurora.md:61-83 ("Migrator role IAM grant") documents the exact one-time master-user `GRANT rds_iam TO clouder_migrator`, two execution methods (RDS Query Editor + Data API using the master secret ARN), and explicitly states it "must be re-run if the cluster is rebuilt from scratch" — directly covering the fresh-cluster/rebuild scenario the finding names.
- docs/ops/aurora.md:34-53 ("IAM authentication") documents verifying `IAMDatabaseAuthenticationEnabled` (aws rds describe-db-clusters) and force-enabling it (aws rds modify-db-cluster --enable-iam-database-authentication --apply-immediately) — covering the "IAM flag may silently revert" point.
- docs/ops/deploy.md:86-113 ("Manual operations") repeats the IAM-toggle command and provides a password-mode break-glass `alembic upgrade head` path.
- ADR-0005:29 cross-references ../ops/aurora.md.

The underlying engineering hazard is real and confirmed in code: deploy.yml:96 sets migration_aurora_auth_mode=iam; migration_handler.py:84-99 authenticates as clouder_migrator via IAM token; that role and its rds_iam grant are created by migrations 20260419_07/08, which cannot run until a human performs the master-user grant (ADR-0005:13,24, aurora.md:63). So the chicken-and-egg exists. But the finding is framed as a DOCS gap, and the documentation gap it asserts is contradicted by aurora.md and deploy.md, which contain exactly the three procedures the recommendation asks to add.

What genuinely remains is a narrow cross-reference omission: docs/data/migrations.md:56-74 describes both auth modes but points only to ADR-0005 for rationale and does not link to docs/ops/aurora.md, where the bootstrap grant and IAM recovery live. A reader on migrations.md alone could miss the prerequisite. That is a real but trivial doc-linking nit, not an absent procedure, and falls below the finding's stated P3.

title: Aurora has no final snapshot, deletion protection off, and no explicit backup retention/PITR config
where: infra/rds.tf:18-19 (and absent backup config in the same resource)
evidence: aws_rds_cluster.aurora sets skip_final_snapshot = true and deletion_protection = false (rds.tf:18-19). The resource has no backup_retention_period, no preferred_backup_window, and no PITR/AWS Backup plan anywhere in infra (grep across infra/ for backup_retention/point_in_time/backtrack returns nothing). This is the single Aurora cluster holding the entire canonical catalogue, all user curation (categories/playlists/triage/tags), sessions, and KMS-encrypted vendor tokens. With backup_retention_period unset, the cluster falls back to the AWS default of 1-day automated backups; with skip_final_snapshot=true a `terraform destroy` (or accidental cluster replacement) takes no final snapshot, and deletion_protection=false does not block it.
risk: An accidental destroy/replace, a Terraform diff that forces cluster replacement, or any operator error permanently loses all tenant data with no final snapshot and at most a 1-day automated-backup window. This is irreversible data loss for a multi-tenant production store.
recommendation: Set deletion_protection = true; set skip_final_snapshot = false with a final_snapshot_identifier; set an explicit backup_retention_period (e.g. 7 days) and a preferred_backup_window. Aurora PITR is automatic within the retention window once retention is set. Optionally add an AWS Backup plan for longer-term copies.

votes.reason: Verified against the actual code: infra/rds.tf:18-19 literally sets `skip_final_snapshot = true` and `deletion_protection = false` on the sole `aws_rds_cluster.aurora`. The resource has no `backup_retention_period`, no `preferred_backup_window`, and a grep across all of infra/ for backup_retention/point_in_time/backtrack/aws_backup returns nothing (the only "retention" vars found are for CloudWatch logs and SQS queues, unrelated to the DB). The other infra files referencing aws_rds_cluster (alarms.tf, iam.tf, lambda.tf, outputs.tf, curation.tf, auth.tf) only consume the cluster's outputs/attributes; none add backup config. With backup_retention_period unset, the cluster falls back to the AWS default of 1 day, so the "at most a 1-day automated-backup window" claim is technically correct (PITR exists but only within that 1 day). With skip_final_snapshot=true and deletion_protection=false, an accidental `terraform destroy`, a forced-replacement diff, or operator error can permanently destroy the single Aurora cluster holding all canonical catalogue + per-tenant curation + sessions + KMS-encrypted vendor tokens with no final snapshot and nothing blocking it at the API level. The evidence, risk, and recommendation are all accurate. I could not find any guard or mitigation the finder missed (copy_tags_to_snapshot=true and storage_encrypted=true do not mitigate backup/deletion-protection). Severity P1 is correct: irreversible total data loss for the sole multi-tenant production datastore, but it is a latent durability/recoverability gap requiring a triggering event rather than an actively-firing data-loss bug, so it sits below P0 and well above P2.

Verified against infra/rds.tf:14-26: skip_final_snapshot = true and deletion_protection = false are exactly as claimed, and a grep across infra/ for backup_retention/preferred_backup/point_in_time/backtrack/aws_backup returns nothing — there is no explicit retention, PITR, or AWS Backup plan. ADR-0002 confirms the "one database" single-Aurora design holds all per-user curation (categories/playlists/triage/tags), sessions, and KMS-encrypted vendor tokens; docs/ops/runbook.md documents no backup/restore/disaster-recovery path. The accepted-behaviors list (cold-start 503, refresh-replay revocation, min_acu=0, YT weekly re-connect) does NOT cover backup posture, so this is not an accepted tradeoff. Impact is real for this MVP: unlike the canonical catalogue (re-ingestable from Beatport S3 snapshots), per-user curation, sessions, and vendor tokens are original, non-recreatable state and are the actual product value for the DJ circle. The trigger is plausible, not theoretical: deletion_protection=false plus skip_final_snapshot=true means any terraform destroy, any diff that forces cluster replacement (changing an immutable attribute like database_name/master_username/subnet group), or operator error wipes the sole copy with no final snapshot. The AWS default of 1-day automated backups bounds routine drift but does not protect against a destroy/forced-replace, which is the failure mode here. This is irreversible total loss of the only production data store via a realistic operator/Terraform path; the fix is small and correct. P1 stands.

Verified against real code, not refuted. infra/rds.tf:18-19 sets skip_final_snapshot = true and deletion_protection = false on aws_rds_cluster.aurora. A grep across infra/ for backup_retention/preferred_backup/point_in_time/backtrack/aws_backup returns ONLY those two lines — no backup_retention_period, no preferred_backup_window, no PITR/backtrack config, and no AWS Backup plan/module anywhere (no modules, no tfvars overriding it; terraform.tfvars.example is the only tfvars). This is the single production cluster: cluster_identifier = local.db_cluster_identifier = "${name_prefix}-aurora" where name_prefix = "${project}-${environment}" (documented prefix beatport-prod-*), holding the entire canonical catalogue, all per-user curation, sessions, and KMS-encrypted vendor tokens.

Concrete loss path: with deletion_protection=false, any apply that forces cluster replacement (a real Aurora footgun: changing an immutable attribute), a `terraform destroy`, or operator error destroys the cluster; skip_final_snapshot=true means no final snapshot is taken, so recovery is limited to the AWS default 1-day automated backups (Aurora retention cannot be 0; provider default is 1). The technical claims are all accurate.

Severity check: P1 is correct, not P0. There is no active/automatic data-loss path — destruction requires an explicit destructive action — and a 1-day automated backup still exists, so "total irreversible loss" is the worst case rather than the steady state. But for a multi-tenant production datastore on the DATA dimension, with a plausible forced-replacement trigger, near-zero remediation effort, and a recovery window of only ~1 day, it sits above P2. Severity unchanged.

title: Lambda Errors alarm map omits artist_enricher and auto_enrich_dispatch workers
where: infra/alarms.tf:14-22 (worker_lambdas local) feeding lambda_errors for_each at :29-30
evidence: local.worker_lambdas (alarms.tf:14-19) lists only canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, label_enricher. all_lambdas = merge(api_lambdas, worker_lambdas) (alarms.tf:21), and the lambda_errors alarm iterates for_each = local.all_lambdas (alarms.tf:30). The artist_enricher_worker (lambda.tf:239) and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker (lambda.tf:288) Lambdas exist and have log groups (logging.tf:46-54) but are absent from the map, so neither gets a Lambda Errors alarm.
risk: Failures in the artist enrichment worker and the auto-enrich dispatch worker (the fan-out entry point that enqueues label+artist enrichment per triage block) are invisible at the alarm layer. A broken dispatch worker silently stops all auto-enrichment with no signal; only DLQ depth would eventually catch it, after maxReceiveCount retries.
recommendation: Add artist_enricher and auto_enrich_dispatch to local.worker_lambdas so the existing lambda_errors alarm covers them, matching the log-group coverage already present in logging.tf.

votes.reason: Verified directly against the cited code. local.worker_lambdas (alarms.tf:14-19) lists only canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, label_enricher. The lambda_errors alarm iterates for_each = local.all_lambdas (alarms.tf:30), where all_lambdas = merge(api_lambdas, worker_lambdas) (alarms.tf:21), so both artist_enricher_worker (lambda.tf:239) and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker (lambda.tf:288) get no Lambda Errors alarm. A grep across all infra/*.tf confirms no error/metric alarm references either worker. Both have log groups (logging.tf:46-54), confirming the coverage asymmetry. The auto_enrich_dispatch_worker is indeed the fan-out entry point (it enqueues to LABEL_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL and ARTIST_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL, lambda.tf:304-305). The finding's own risk section correctly notes the one compensating control: DLQ depth alarms (logging.tf:58-67) DO cover artist_enrichment and auto_enrich_dispatch, so failures are delayed-visible (post maxReceiveCount), not permanently invisible. No misread line, no missed guard. P2 is appropriate: this is an observability gap that removes the fast-signal error alarm for two real workers (one a single point of failure for the whole auto-enrich path) but does not itself cause data loss or outage given DLQ retention. Fix is trivial and matches existing patterns.

Facts are accurate: artist_enricher_worker (lambda.tf:239) and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker (lambda.tf:288) exist and are genuinely absent from local.worker_lambdas (alarms.tf:14-19), so neither gets a lambda_errors alarm. But the finding's RISK ("silently stops... with no signal") is materially overstated, which is why it's refuted at this severity. Both queues DO have DLQ depth alarms: local.dlq_queues (logging.tf:58-67) includes artist_enrichment and auto_enrich_dispatch, and dlq_depth (logging.tf:69-90) fires on the FIRST message in the DLQ (threshold=0, evaluation_periods=1). With maxReceiveCount=3 (variables.tf:505,588), a persistently broken worker is caught within minutes — not "no signal." Moreover the lambda_errors alarm is itself documented as best-effort even where present: its comment (alarms.tf:24-28) says treat_missing_data=notBreaching means a fully-broken Lambda emitting zero invocations won't fire either, "acceptable for the single-tenant phase." So DLQ depth is the codebase's actual backstop, and the missing alarm is a redundant early-warning layer, not the primary detection mechanism. For these specific workers the DLQ alarm is arguably the better signal anyway: a single transient error (e.g., one Aurora cold-start 503 per ADR-0014) would trip the errors threshold (=1) but self-heals via SQS retry, so the missing alarm is partly noise reduction. Auto-enrichment is explicitly best-effort background fan-out (auto_enrich_dispatch variable doc, variables.tf:586) off the interactive path, so a few-minutes detection delay has zero user impact for a small DJ circle. Real but minor consistency gap; cheap to fix but not a meaningful MVP reliability risk. P2 is too high — at most P3.

Verified accurate against real code. local.worker_lambdas (infra/alarms.tf:14-19) lists only canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, label_enricher. all_lambdas = merge(api_lambdas, worker_lambdas) (alarms.tf:21) and the lambda_errors alarm iterates for_each = local.all_lambdas (alarms.tf:30), so artist_enricher_worker (lambda.tf:239) and auto_enrich_dispatch_worker (lambda.tf:288) get NO Errors alarm. I confirmed there are exactly 5 aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resources total (lambda_errors, lambda_duration_p95 [api-only], label_enricher_throttles, aurora_acu_near_max, dlq_depth) and none reference artist/dispatch by FunctionName. Both workers are real resources with log groups (logging.tf:46-54) and SQS event-source mappings (lambda.tf:272, :315), so the asymmetry vs log-group coverage is real. Concrete reproducible path: triage-finalize enqueues to the auto_enrich_dispatch SQS queue -> auto_enrich_dispatch_worker (handler collector.auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.lambda_handler) consumes and fans out to label+artist enrichment queues; if that handler regresses and raises on every invocation, the Lambda Errors metric climbs but no alarm watches it, so auto-enrichment silently stops. Severity stays P2 (not P1, not lower): there IS a partial safety net — dlq_depth (logging.tf:69) covers the auto_enrich_dispatch and artist_enrichment DLQs, so failures are eventually caught after maxReceiveCount retries (sqs.tf:100, :121). So it's a detection-latency/coverage-gap on a critical fan-out path during the single-tenant phase, not a total blind spot. The fix is trivial (two map entries) and the omission is clearly unintended given log-group and DLQ coverage already include both workers.

title: All CloudWatch alarms default to no notification target (alarm_sns_topic_arn empty)
where: infra/variables.tf:230-234; consumed at alarms.tf:47-48,72-73,98-99,119-120 and logging.tf:88-89
evidence: variable alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults to "" (variables.tf:233). Every alarm wires alarm_actions/ok_actions = var.alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" ? [...] : [] (e.g. alarms.tf:47-48 lambda errors, :72-73 duration p95, :119-120 Aurora ACU, logging.tf:88-89 DLQ depth). With the default empty value, all alarms are created but have zero actions — they change state in the console but page/notify nobody.
risk: DLQ-depth alarms, Lambda error alarms, and the Aurora ACU-near-max alarm fire into the void by default. The MVP can ship with no operator notified of a stuck worker, a DLQ filling up, or runaway DB capacity until someone manually opens the console.
recommendation: Provide a non-empty alarm_sns_topic_arn (an SNS topic with an email/Slack/PagerDuty subscription) as part of the standard prod tfvars, or create the SNS topic in-repo and default the alarms to it. Document it as a required deploy input.
