votes.reason: Verified all cited locations. infra/variables.tf:230-234 defines alarm_sns_topic_arn with default = "". Every alarm in infra/alarms.tf (lambda_errors :47-48, lambda_duration_p95 :72-73, label_enricher_throttles :98-99, aurora_acu_near_max :119-120) and the DLQ depth alarm in infra/logging.tf:88-89 (which fans out over 6 DLQs via for_each) wires alarm_actions/ok_actions = var.alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" ? [...] : []. No aws_sns_topic resource exists anywhere in infra/, and the only tfvars file (terraform.tfvars.example:8) ships alarm_sns_topic_arn = "" with the comment "Empty disables notifications." So with the default, all alarms are created but have zero actions — they change state in the console but notify/page nobody. The finding's behavioral claim is exactly correct. (Minor: cited line :98-99 is the throttles alarm, not a separately-named one, but it is still an empty-action alarm, so the claim holds.) Severity P2 is appropriate: the code comment at alarms.tf:27-28 calls it a deliberate single-tenant-phase trade-off, alarms remain console-visible, and the fix is a one-line tfvars input — a real ops-readiness gap, not a P1 defect/data-loss risk nor merely cosmetic.

Technically accurate (alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults to "" at variables.tf:233, and all alarm_actions/ok_actions degrade to [] — confirmed at alarms.tf:47-48,72-73,98-99,119-120 and logging.tf:88-89; terraform.tfvars.example:8 also ships ""). But this is a documented, deliberately accepted trade-off for the MVP, not an oversight. alarms.tf:25-28 explicitly states the no-paging behavior is "acceptable for the single-tenant phase, revisit when wiring real paging." The variable description ("Empty string disables alarm actions") and the tfvars comment ("Empty disables notifications") frame it as an intentional opt-out. The alarms are still created and remain console/dashboard-visible — they simply don't page, consistent with the MVP's manual-console operational posture (CLAUDE.md gotcha #7, ADR-0014, runbook.md treats the DLQ alarm as a state to inspect). For a small DJ circle where the operator is the deployer, real-time paging is a known acceptable gap, and the wiring is already fully in place: enabling it is a one-line tfvars change (the finding's own S effort). This matches the refute criterion of an accepted behavior / deliberately deferred capability rather than a defect.

Finding confirmed against real code, not refuted. Verified: (1) variable alarm_sns_topic_arn defaults to "" (infra/variables.tf:233). (2) All alarms gate actions on a non-empty value — lambda_errors (alarms.tf:47-48), duration_p95 (72-73), label_enricher_throttles (98-99), aurora_acu_near_max (119-120), and dlq_depth for all six DLQs (logging.tf:88-89) — using `var.alarm_sns_topic_arn != "" ? [...] : []`. (3) No aws_sns_topic resource exists anywhere in infra/ (grep returned nothing), so there is no in-repo topic to default to. (4) The variable is never assigned a non-empty value in any tracked file; the only tfvars is infra/terraform.tfvars.example which explicitly sets `alarm_sns_topic_arn = ""`. There are no *.auto.tfvars that could inject a value.

Reproducible deploy path: the documented deploy command (CLAUDE.md: `cd infra && terraform apply`) run with the example tfvars or no tfvars leaves alarm_sns_topic_arn = "". Terraform then renders alarm_actions=[] and ok_actions=[] on every alarm. The alarms are created and change ALARM/OK state in the console but publish to no SNS topic, so no email/Slack/page is sent. Concretely: a canonicalization worker timing out exhausts maxReceiveCount and lands messages in beatport-prod-canonicalization-dlq; the dlq_depth alarm transitions to ALARM but notifies nobody. This directly contradicts docs/ops/runbook.md:64-68, whose DLQ runbook is premised on "CloudWatch alarm fires on DLQ depth" reaching an operator — the notification leg does not exist by default.

Severity P2 is correct (unchanged): real operational/REL gap (no paging on stuck workers, filling DLQs, runaway Aurora ACU), but it is a deploy-config default rather than a code defect, has no data-loss or correctness impact, and is fixed by supplying a single variable; the alarm definitions themselves are correct and activate the moment a topic ARN is provided.

title: No Lambda dead_letter_config or event-invoke destination on any function; timed-out workers strand runs silently
where: infra/lambda.tf (all aws_lambda_function blocks, e.g. :1-34, :36-62, :288-313); grep for dead_letter_config/destination_config across infra/ returns nothing
evidence: No aws_lambda_function declares a dead_letter_config, and there are no aws_lambda_function_event_invoke_config / destination_config resources anywhere in infra. Failure handling relies entirely on the SQS redrive_policy → DLQ for the SQS-driven workers. Per the documented behavior (CLAUDE.md + worker_handler.py), a Lambda timeout is NOT a Python exception, so set_run_failed never runs and the ingest_runs row stays at RAW_SAVED indefinitely; the canonicalization queue still redrives the message (maxReceiveCount=5, sqs.tf:16) but there is no on-failure destination capturing the timed-out invocation context for diagnosis.
risk: A worker timeout (e.g. a large canonicalization or enrichment batch) leaves the run silently stuck at RAW_SAVED with partial data committed, no error_code, and no on-failure capture. For the API/auth/curation Lambdas (no SQS, no DLQ) an async or unhandled failure has no failure sink at all.
recommendation: Add an SQS/SNS on-failure destination (aws_lambda_function_event_invoke_config) for the workers, or a function-level dead_letter_config, so timed-out/failed invocations are captured. Pair with a Duration-near-timeout alarm on the long-running workers (currently only api_lambdas have a duration alarm, alarms.tf:54-55).

votes.reason:
The finding's headline ("strand runs silently", "no on-failure capture") and its primary recommendation are both materially wrong on the code as written.

What is true: grep confirms no dead_letter_config / event_invoke_config / destination_config anywhere in infra/ (exit 1, no matches). And on a Lambda TIMEOUT the worker_handler.py try/except (src/collector/worker_handler.py:139-180) never runs set_run_failed (a timeout is not a Python exception), so the ingest_runs row can remain at RAW_SAVED with no error_code. That narrow state-consistency gap is real.

What refutes the finding:
1) NOT silent. All six worker SQS queues have DLQ depth alarms wired to SNS (infra/logging.tf:56-90, dlq_depth for_each over canonicalization/spotify_search/vendor_match/label_enrichment/artist_enrichment/auto_enrich_dispatch). A timed-out message redrives maxReceiveCount times (sqs.tf) then lands in the DLQ and fires the alarm. The finding's own evidence concedes the redrive happens, so "captures the timed-out invocation context for diagnosis ... there is none" / "stranded silently" overstates the gap — there is an observable failure sink for every worker.
2) Recommendation is technically inapplicable. Both recommended fixes (function-level dead_letter_config and aws_lambda_function_event_invoke_config on_failure destinations) fire ONLY for ASYNCHRONOUS (Event) invocations. Every worker here is an SQS event-source-mapping (poll-based) invocation (infra/lambda.tf:97-101,140-144,181-186,223-235,272-284,315-323), and the API/auth/curation Lambdas are SYNCHRONOUS API Gateway invocations (infra/curation.tf:41-73, aws_apigatewayv2_integration; no async self-invokes found in src). Neither recommended mechanism would capture a timeout for ANY Lambda in this repo. The already-present SQS redrive→DLQ is the correct AWS mechanism.
3) "For the API/auth/curation Lambdas an async or unhandled failure has no failure sink at all" is false: those Lambdas are synchronous (errors return to API Gateway as 5xx) and are covered by the lambda_errors Errors alarm (infra/alarms.tf:29-49 includes collector/auth_handler/auth_authorizer/curation).

Net: the failure-handling machinery the finding claims is missing (DLQ + SNS alarms + Errors alarm) actually exists, and the headline recommended fix would not work for these invocation types. The genuine residual issue is only the narrow timeout-leaves-RAW_SAVED-without-error_code state gap, which is observable via the DLQ alarm and is a minor P3 observability/consistency nicety, not a P2 reliability gap. (Minor true sub-point: artist_enrichment and auto_enrich_dispatch workers are absent from the alarms.tf Errors/duration maps, but both still have DLQ depth alarms.)

The finding's literal grep claim is true (no dead_letter_config / event_invoke_config / destination_config in infra), but the remediation it proposes targets a mechanism that does not apply to this architecture, and the risk it describes is already covered.

1. dead_letter_config and aws_lambda_function_event_invoke_config / destination_config only fire on ASYNCHRONOUS (InvocationType=Event) Lambda invocations. This system has ZERO async invocations: grep for InvocationType / .invoke across src/collector/ returns nothing. Every worker is driven by aws_lambda_event_source_mapping (SQS poll), and collector/auth/curation are synchronous API Gateway invocations. For neither invocation type does a Lambda dead_letter_config get exercised by AWS. So adding it is effectively a no-op here.

2. For the SQS-driven workers, the correct on-failure sink IS the SQS redrive_policy → DLQ, which the finding itself admits every queue has (sqs.tf: 6 queues, all with deadLetterTargetArn). A timed-out invocation does not delete the message, so it redrives to maxReceiveCount and lands in the existing DLQ — the failure context the finding wants is captured there.

3. The claim that API/auth/curation Lambdas have "no failure sink at all" for async failures is moot — they have no async invocations; sync errors return to API Gateway and surface to the caller.

4. The genuine underlying risk (timeout strands ingest_runs at RAW_SAVED because a Lambda timeout is not a Python exception so set_run_failed never runs) is real but is a CODE concern, not fixed by dead_letter_config. And observability is already wired: alarms.tf lambda_errors alarm uses local.all_lambdas (includes all 4 workers) on the AWS/Lambda Errors metric, which a timeout increments → fires the SNS alarm. The alarm comments explicitly accept reduced paging fidelity "for the single-tenant phase."

For this MVP (small DJ circle, single-tenant phase per the in-repo alarm comments), existing DLQs plus the all-function error alarm already provide the failure capture the finding asks for, and the recommended Terraform change would attach config AWS never invokes in this topology. Refuted on impact and on remedy correctness.

Refuted on mechanism and on the core risk premise. (1) The recommended fix is inapplicable: every Lambda in the repo is invoked SYNCHRONOUSLY — all 6 workers via aws_lambda_event_source_mapping (SQS poller, lambda.tf:97,140,181,223,272,315) and all 4 API Lambdas (collector/auth_handler/auth_authorizer/curation) via API Gateway AWS_PROXY integrations (api_gateway.tf, auth.tf, curation.tf). There are no asynchronous invocations anywhere (no S3-event, EventBridge→Lambda, SNS→Lambda, or InvocationType=Event in code). Lambda dead_letter_config and aws_lambda_function_event_invoke_config apply ONLY to async invocations and are ignored for SQS-ESM and API Gateway, so the recommended remediation would have zero effect on any function here. (2) The finding's claim that the API/auth/curation Lambdas have 'no failure sink at all' for an 'async or unhandled failure' is false — they are never invoked async; API Gateway returns the 5xx to the caller, and they are covered by the lambda_errors alarm (alarms.tf:29-49, for_each=all_lambdas) plus duration p95 alarm (alarms.tf:54-74). (3) The residual true kernel — a worker TIMEOUT is not a Python exception, so set_run_failed (worker_handler.py:147) never runs and the run stays at RAW_SAVED while the message redrives — is NOT silent: infra/logging.tf:56-90 declares a dlq_depth alarm (for_each over all six worker DLQs, ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible>0, 'DLQ … has messages — worker failing') wired to the SNS topic. A repeatedly-timing-out message redrives 5x (sqs.tf:16) into the DLQ and immediately raises this alarm. The SQS redrive→DLQ is the only AWS-supported failure sink for synchronous SQS invocations and is present on every queue (sqs.tf). The finding's literally-true observation (no dead_letter_config/destination_config in infra) is immaterial because those constructs do not apply to this 100%-synchronous fleet, and the actual concern is already captured by SQS DLQs + DLQ-depth + Lambda-error alarms. The only genuinely uncovered item is the absence of a Duration-near-timeout alarm on long-running workers (p95 duration alarm is API-only), a minor observability nice-to-have (~P3), not a P2 reliability gap.

title: No backup/restore/DR procedure documented in docs/ops despite detailed scaling docs
where: docs/ops/aurora.md (covers scaling, IAM auth, master secret, cold-start — no backup section); docs/ops/deploy.md and README.md contain zero backup/snapshot/restore mentions
evidence: aurora.md documents Serverless scaling, IAM auth, migrator grant, master-secret retention, and cold-start in depth, but has no section on backup retention, PITR, snapshot restore, or disaster recovery. grep -ic for backup/snapshot/restore/disaster/recover across deploy.md and README.md returns 0. Combined with the infra gap (skip_final_snapshot=true, no backup_retention_period), there is no documented recovery path for the single production database.
risk: If the cluster is lost or corrupted, the operator has no documented procedure (restore from snapshot, PITR window, expected RPO/RTO). The undocumented 1-day default retention and missing final snapshot are not surfaced to whoever runs the deploy.
recommendation: Add a 'Backups & disaster recovery' section to docs/ops/aurora.md stating the configured retention/PITR window, the restore-from-snapshot and PITR-restore CLI steps, and the expected RPO/RTO. Do this alongside fixing the infra backup config so docs and reality agree.

(1) docs/ops/aurora.md: grep for backup|snapshot|restore|disaster|recover|PITR|RPO|RTO returns no matches (exit 1); reading the file confirms it has sections for Serverless v2 scaling, IAM auth, migrator-role grant, master-secret retention, and cold-start behaviour — and no backup/PITR/DR section, exactly as stated. (2) docs/ops/deploy.md and root README.md: zero backup/snapshot/restore mentions (grep exit 1 each); deploy.md headers are PR checks, deploy pipeline, frontend deploy, GitHub secrets, manual operations. (3) No operator-facing DR procedure anywhere in docs/ops or docs/ — runbook.md covers cold-start/FAILED_TO_QUEUE/DLQ/refresh-cookie/concurrency only; the only DR-adjacent mentions in docs/ are inside the review's own artifacts and an ADR rationale aside, not procedures. (4) Infra corroborates the risk: infra/rds.tf has skip_final_snapshot=true (line 18), deletion_protection=false (line 19), and no backup_retention_period/preferred_backup_window, so it relies on the AWS default 1-day retention. The finding is accurate; the line/file citations and logic all hold. Severity P3 is correct: pure DOCS gap, documentation-only, low immediate blast radius, small fix effort, consistent with P3 conventions; the infra config gap is tracked separately as its own DATA/REL finding.

Technically accurate: docs/ops/aurora.md covers scaling/IAM/master-secret/cold-start but has no backup/PITR/restore/DR section, and grep across deploy.md and README.md returns 0 backup/snapshot/restore mentions. infra/rds.tf confirms skip_final_snapshot=true, deletion_protection=false, and no backup_retention_period (Aurora defaults to a 1-day PITR window, undocumented). However, as a standalone DOCS finding it does not matter for THIS MVP. (1) It is a pure documentation-gap claim with no runtime trigger; the actual data-loss exposure lives entirely in the infra config (rds.tf), which is a separate finding — adding a markdown section changes nothing about recovery posture. (2) At small-DJ-circle scale the operator is the developer who authored the Terraform, so the assumed operator-vs-deployer knowledge gap is largely absent, and standard AWS PITR/snapshot CLI is usable without a bespoke runbook. (3) The canonical Beatport core is re-derivable from S3 raw snapshots (RAW_BUCKET_NAME); only the per-user overlay is irreplaceable, and protecting it is an infra-config decision, not a docs decision. The recommendation itself concedes the doc work should ride alongside fixing the infra backup config — confirming the load-bearing risk is the infra item, not the missing doc. P3 severity for the documentation gap is appropriate, but the gap is immaterial to MVP outcomes on its own.

(3) The infra gap is real and actually understated: infra/rds.tf sets skip_final_snapshot=true, deletion_protection=false, and defines no backup_retention_period (Aurora defaults to a 1-day PITR window) — so the single production cluster (beatport-prod-aurora) can be destroyed/replaced with no final snapshot and only a 1-day recovery window. P3 is correct for a pure docs gap that surfaces a real but separately-tracked infra risk.

title: Two independent /auth/refresh callers share no dedupe — concurrent refresh trips replay detection and revokes ALL of the user's sessions
evidence: There are two unrelated code paths that POST /auth/refresh with the same HttpOnly refresh cookie. (1) The fetch wrapper's silent 401 retry: client.ts:59 calls tryRefreshOnce(), which is guarded by the module-level `inflightRefresh` promise (client.ts:18). (2) AuthProvider.refresh() (AuthProvider.tsx:158) calls api('/auth/refresh') as a plain POST — it does NOT go through tryRefreshOnce, so it never touches `inflightRefresh`. AuthProvider.refresh() is invoked by the proactive expiry timer (AuthProvider.tsx:134), on bootstrap (AuthProvider.tsx:201), on SDK authentication_error (PlaybackProvider.tsx:314), and as `onAuthExpired` for EVERY Spotify Web API 401 (PlaybackProvider.tsx:114). The device picker polls getMyDevices({onAuthExpired}) every 30s (5s when open) and on every window focus while sdkReady (PlaybackProvider.tsx:657-660, usePolling.ts:16-26). When the Spotify access token expires, that poll gets a 401 and fires onAuthExpired -> AuthProvider.refresh() (the UNGUARDED path); if a normal CLOUDER API call hits a 401 in the same window it fires client.tryRefreshOnce() (the guarded path). Both POST /auth/refresh with the same cookie; per ADR-0015 the backend rotates the token on the first and sees a stale hash on the second -> replay detected -> revoke every session of the user. The bootstrapStarted guard (AuthProvider.tsx:115) only stops the StrictMode double-mount at bootstrap; it does nothing for timer-vs-401 or poll-vs-API-call races during normal use.
risk: During ordinary use (token nearing expiry while playback device polling is active, or a window-focus burst) two refreshes race and the backend revokes ALL of the user's sessions. The user is silently logged out everywhere and must do a fresh OAuth round-trip — and on a small DJ circle this is a recurring, hard-to-reproduce session-loss bug, not a rare edge.
recommendation: Route every /auth/refresh through a single shared in-flight dedupe. Make AuthProvider.refresh() delegate to the same `inflightRefresh` promise used by client.tryRefreshOnce() (export it / move it to a shared module), or have AuthProvider.refresh() call tryRefreshOnce() directly and rebuild state from the dispatched auth:refreshed event. The dedupe must be a process-wide singleton so the scheduled timer, bootstrap, SDK auth-error handler, and the fetch-wrapper retry all coalesce onto one network request.

votes.reason:
Verified against the cited code; the finding is accurate on every load-bearing claim.

TWO INDEPENDENT REFRESH PATHS (confirmed):
- Guarded: frontend/src/api/client.ts:58-59 calls tryRefreshOnce() on a 401, which is coalesced by the module-level `inflightRefresh` promise (client.ts:8,18,36). Note the POST to /auth/refresh inside api() (line 56) is itself a plain fetch — `inflightRefresh` is only consulted when some OTHER request 401s.
- Unguarded: AuthProvider.refresh() (AuthProvider.tsx:156-158) calls api('/auth/refresh', {method:'POST'}) as a direct POST. That POST never touches `inflightRefresh`. grep confirms `inflightRefresh` (client.ts) is the only refresh-dedupe primitive in frontend/src and refresh() does not reference it.

TRIGGERS (confirmed): refresh() is wired to the proactive expiry timer (AuthProvider.tsx:133-134), bootstrap (201), SDK authentication_error (PlaybackProvider.tsx:314 `void refresh()`), and onAuthExpired = ()=>refresh() (PlaybackProvider.tsx:114). The device poll calls getMyDevices({onAuthExpired}) every 30s (5s open) and on every window focus while sdkReady (PlaybackProvider.tsx:657-660; lib/usePolling.ts:16-26 — the finding's path `usePolling.ts` was slightly off, real path is lib/usePolling.ts, content matches). spotifyWebApi.ts:48-50 invokes opts.onAuthExpired() on any Spotify 401. So a Spotify-token-expiry poll-401 fires the UNGUARDED refresh(); a concurrent CLOUDER-API 401 fires the GUARDED tryRefreshOnce(); two unguarded refresh() calls (timer vs poll-401, or SDK auth_error vs poll-401) can also race each other. None coalesce.

BACKEND MECHANIC (confirmed exactly): auth_handler.py:_handle_refresh — get_active_session (410), inbound_hash=_sha256_hex(refresh_token) (414), and `if session.refresh_token_hash != inbound_hash: repo.revoke_all_user_sessions(...)` (415-416). rotate_session (464-466) updates the stored hash on the first request; a concurrent second request presenting the pre-rotation cookie mismatches → revoke ALL sessions. auth_repository.revoke_all_user_sessions (225-235) revokes every non-revoked session for the user. ADR-0015 and docs/frontend/auth.md:26,44 explicitly state the bootstrapStarted guard covers ONLY the StrictMode double-mount and that concurrent same-cookie refresh revokes all sessions.

REFUTATION ANGLES REJECTED: cookie Path=/auth/refresh (auth_handler.py:741) is sent on both calls; React does not batch independent setTimeout/fetch-callback/SDK-listener handlers; the race window is widened (not closed) by network/Aurora-cold-start latency per ADR-0014 because the original cookie is replayed until the first response's Set-Cookie lands.

Severity unchanged (P1): genuine concurrency race, but it requires true simultaneity (two refreshes in-flight before either response returns), so it is intermittent rather than deterministic — consistent with P1, not P0. Impact is severe and user-visible: silent logout across ALL devices/sessions, recoverable only via a full OAuth round-trip, recurring on the target small-DJ-circle workload.

Confirmed at code and impact level; cannot refute.

CODE (all verified):
- frontend/src/api/client.ts:8-40,58-74 — silent-401 retry routes through tryRefreshOnce(), guarded by module-level `inflightRefresh`.
- frontend/src/auth/AuthProvider.tsx:156-174 — refresh() calls api('/auth/refresh') as a plain POST; it NEVER touches inflightRefresh. The two dedupe pools are disjoint (grep confirms inflightRefresh exists only in client.ts; AuthProvider has no shared coalescing).
- AuthProvider.refresh() is invoked by the proactive expiry timer (line 133-134, fires at expiresAt-5min), bootstrap (line 201), SDK authentication_error (PlaybackProvider.tsx:314), and onAuthExpired (PlaybackProvider.tsx:114 = `() => refresh()`).
- frontend/src/features/playback/api/spotifyWebApi.ts:48-50 — EVERY Spotify Web API 401 calls opts.onAuthExpired(), i.e. the UNGUARDED refresh().
- frontend/src/features/playback/lib/usePolling.ts:16-26 — getMyDevices polls every 30s (5s open) AND on every window 'focus' while sdkReady (PlaybackProvider.tsx:657-660).
- Backend consequence verified: src/collector/auth_handler.py:414-417 — on inbound refresh-hash != rotated session hash, calls repo.revoke_all_user_sessions() and raises RefreshReplayDetectedError. So a concurrent same-cookie double-refresh revokes ALL sessions exactly as claimed.
- Timing verified: access-token TTL default 1800s (infra/variables.tf:380), REFRESH_LEEWAY_MS=5min, so the unguarded timer fires at the ~25-min mark.

IMPACT — does NOT qualify for any accepted-behavior exemption. ADR-0015 accepts replay-revocation as defense against a malicious/stale replayed cookie; here the app races ITSELF — two of its own legitimate refresh paths (unguarded timer / SDK auth-error / Spotify-401-onAuthExpired via 30s poll + window-focus, vs. the guarded fetch-wrapper 401) collide with the same cookie during ordinary use. That is a self-inflicted bug, not the accepted tradeoff.

PLAUSIBLE TRIGGER (not theoretical): a DJ leaving the Curate page open with playback polling active. When the access token nears expiry, a window-focus burst fires usePolling's focus handler (-> Spotify 401 -> unguarded refresh) and/or a re-fetch API 401 (guarded refresh) within the same window as the late-firing background-throttled 25-min timer. Three independent unguarded entry points + one guarded path, only two of which coalesce, and into disjoint pools. The team already knows this exact failure mode — the bootstrapStarted guard (AuthProvider.tsx:115) was added specifically for it — but it only covers the StrictMode double-mount, not timer-vs-401 or poll-vs-API races.

EFFECT: silent logout of every session, requiring a fresh OAuth round-trip; intermittent and hard to reproduce — a recurring session-loss bug for a small DJ circle. P1 stands (above P2 given trigger frequency x severity; below P0 since it needs a timing coincidence and causes no data loss / not simultaneous-all-users).

Every load-bearing claim is confirmed in real code, and I can construct a concrete reproducible trigger path.

Backend (auth_handler.py:_handle_refresh): on a stale-hash refresh the handler revokes ALL user sessions. Line 414 computes inbound_hash; line 415 `if session.refresh_token_hash != inbound_hash:` → line 416 `repo.revoke_all_user_sessions(claims.user_id, ...)` + RefreshReplayDetectedError. rotate_session (line 464-468) overwrites refresh_token_hash with the NEW hash on the first request, so a second concurrent request carrying the same (now-stale) cookie hits the revoke-all branch. Confirmed.

Frontend dedupe gap: the ONLY dedupe is the module-level `inflightRefresh` in client.ts (lines 8/18/36/39), used exclusively inside `tryRefreshOnce()`, which is reached ONLY from `api()`'s 401 retry (client.ts:58-59). AuthProvider.refresh() (AuthProvider.tsx:158) calls `api('/auth/refresh', {method:'POST'})` as a plain POST — it never passes through the 401-retry branch, so it never touches `inflightRefresh`. refresh() itself has NO in-flight guard (lines 156-174). Confirmed there is no other dedupe (grep found only the client.ts occurrences).

refresh() callers all bypass the guard: proactive timer (AuthProvider.tsx:133-134 → refreshRef.current()), bootstrap (line 201), SDK authentication_error (PlaybackProvider.tsx:314 `void refresh()`), and onAuthExpired = `() => refresh()` (PlaybackProvider.tsx:114) wired into every Spotify Web API call. spotifyWebApi.ts call() (line 47-51): on a 401 it invokes opts.onAuthExpired() → refresh(). usePolling (lib/usePolling.ts:18-21) ticks every 30s/5s AND on every window 'focus', driving refreshDevices → getMyDevices({onAuthExpired}).

Concrete reproducible path (no StrictMode needed): In the SDK 'ready' handler, transferMyPlayback (PlaybackProvider.tsx:193-197) and getMyDevices (PlaybackProvider.tsx:207-215) are fired as two separate un-awaited `void` blocks — they run concurrently. If the stored spotify_access_token is already expired at SDK-ready (e.g. the tab/app was idle/backgrounded, a normal DJ-tool pattern), BOTH Spotify calls return 401, BOTH independently invoke onAuthExpired() → refresh(), and BOTH POST /auth/refresh with the same HttpOnly cookie. Backend rotates on the first, sees the stale hash on the second → revoke_all_user_sessions → silent logout everywhere requiring a fresh OAuth round-trip. A second path: proactive timer firing near-simultaneously with a window-focus poll 401. Neither path is coalesced.

The bootstrapStarted guard (AuthProvider.tsx:115/196) only suppresses the StrictMode double-mount at bootstrap; it does nothing for timer-vs-401 or concurrent-Spotify-call races during normal use, exactly as the finding states.

Severity P1 is correct: severe blast radius (all sessions revoked, silent global logout, full re-OAuth) with a plausible, recurring, hard-to-reproduce concurrency trigger. It is a race (requires two near-simultaneous refresh triggers) rather than a guaranteed-on-every-request break, which keeps it below P0 but well above P2.

title: navigate('/auth/premium-required') targets an unregistered route — Spotify account_error dead-ends on the 404 page
evidence: On the Spotify Web Playback SDK `account_error` event the provider runs navigate('/auth/premium-required') (PlaybackProvider.tsx:318). No route with path '/auth/premium-required' is declared in router.tsx — the only premium handling is /login?error=premium_required (login.tsx:11-12) and inline copy in auth.return.tsx:63-67. The catch-all '*' route (router.tsx:135) renders NotFoundPage, so this navigation lands on the generic 404 instead of a 'Premium required' explanation.
risk: A non-Premium (or downgraded) Spotify user who triggers playback gets dumped on a meaningless 'Not Found' page with no guidance, instead of the premium-required message. The error is the SDK account_error, so it surfaces exactly when the user most needs the explanation.
recommendation: Either register a '/auth/premium-required' route that renders the premium-required copy, or change the navigation to the existing surface: navigate('/login?error=premium_required') (or render the LongOperationOverlay/banner inline). Add a test asserting account_error lands on a page that shows auth.premium_required.

So in production this navigation lands on the generic 404. The premium copy that exists (en.json auth.premium_required / playback.errors.premium_required_title; login.tsx:11-12; auth.return.tsx:64) is never wired to this path. Crucially, the sdk.error state set with kind:'account' (line 317) is never rendered as a premium message anywhere — the only UI consumers (PlaylistPlayerPanel.tsx:139, BucketPlayerPanel.tsx:60, CurateSession.tsx:65, CategoryPlayerPanel.tsx:180) react solely to kind === 'init', so there is no inline fallback. The test suite is green only because the harness renderApp.tsx:166 registers a fake PremiumRequiredStub at '/auth/premium-required' (integration.batch3.test.tsx asserts the stub's premium-required-page testid), which masks the missing production route. The risk (a non-Premium/downgraded Spotify user dead-ending on a meaningless 404 exactly when account_error fires) is accurate. Severity P2 is appropriate: real user-facing reliability/UX degradation, narrow trigger (non-Premium Spotify account reaching playback), no data loss or crash, small fix.

PlaybackProvider.tsx:318 calls navigate('/auth/premium-required'); router.tsx (lines 41-136) registers no such route — only /login, /auth/return, the authenticated AppShell children, and catch-all '*' → NotFoundPage. So the navigation lands on the generic 404. I also confirmed there is no inline fallback: the sdkError of kind 'account' is never rendered anywhere — only kind 'init' maps to a 'disconnected' UI state (CurateSession.tsx:65), so the navigation is the sole channel for surfacing this error, and it dead-ends. Notably the tests pass only because the test harness injects a PremiumRequiredStub at '/auth/premium-required' (renderApp.tsx:166, integration.batch3.test.tsx scenario 17, PlaybackProvider.test.tsx:585) — a route the production app lacks, so the tests mask the bug rather than catch it. Not an accepted/ADR-documented behavior.

Fix is trivial (redirect to the existing /login?error=premium_required surface).

No data loss, no security impact — purely a cosmetic guidance degradation in a low-probability path, which fits P3 (polish) better than P2 (moderate).

Notably the test 'Scenario 17' (integration.batch3.test.tsx:307, asserting pathname '/auth/premium-required' and premium-required-page) only passes because the test harness fabricates a stub route at renderApp.tsx:166 that does not exist in production — a false-green test masking the defect.

Severity P2 is correct: real REL/UX defect with a concrete path, but scoped to the non-Premium subset, no data loss/crash/security impact, and a working (if misdirecting) escape page. Not P1 (no broad/core-flow outage) and not P3 (more than cosmetic — actively misdirects at the moment of need and is hidden by a misleading test).

title: VITE_API_BASE_URL and lib/env.ts are dead at runtime — API client hardcodes window.location.origin, contradicting CLAUDE.md and dev-setup docs
evidence: The fetch wrapper derives baseUrl solely from window.location.origin (client.ts:5-6). Grep across src/ (excluding tests) shows VITE_API_BASE_URL is referenced only by the type decl (vite-env.d.ts:4) and the schema in env.ts; parseEnv() has no runtime caller (only lib/__tests__/env.test.ts). spotifyWebApi.ts hardcodes BASE='https://api.spotify.com'. So lib/env.ts is dead code and the env var drives nothing — yet CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 and the documented dev setup tell developers to put VITE_API_BASE_URL in frontend/.env.local to point requests at the API.
risk: A developer following the documented setup sets VITE_API_BASE_URL expecting it to route requests; it is silently ignored, and any non-same-origin deployment of the SPA (or local dev not proxied through the same origin) would break with no error. Misleading config surface and dead validation code.
recommendation: Either delete lib/env.ts + the vite-env decl and remove the gotcha-#9 guidance, or wire baseUrl to fall back to import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL when set. Pick one and make the docs match the code.

votes.reason: The finding's central thesis is false: it claims VITE_API_BASE_URL is "dead at runtime," "drives nothing," is "silently ignored," and "contradicts CLAUDE.md and dev-setup docs." The finder missed vite.config.ts. At vite.config.ts:31-32 the env var IS read via loadEnv and used as the dev-server proxy target (server.proxy, gated at line 58: `proxy: target ? proxy : undefined`). The same-origin design in client.ts (window.location.origin, confirmed at client.ts:5-6) is intentional and complementary: in dev the browser is same-origin with localhost:5173 and Vite's proxy (driven by VITE_API_BASE_URL) forwards /auth/*, /tracks, etc. to the prod API Gateway. This is exactly what the docs describe — README.md:120 ("The Vite proxy forwards every API request to ${VITE_API_BASE_URL}"), docs/frontend/features.md:67 ("The dev server proxies API calls to VITE_API_BASE_URL... Without the variable, proxy is undefined and all API routes fall through to the SPA, breaking /auth/login"), and CLAUDE.md gotcha #9. So the var is live, the docs are accurate (not contradicted), and a developer following the setup gets the documented behavior. The other code claims are individually true (client.ts uses window.location.origin; spotifyWebApi.ts:4 hardcodes BASE='https://api.spotify.com'; parseEnv is imported only by its test), but they do not support the headline. The only surviving residue is that the parseEnv helper/lib/env.ts validation is never called at runtime (vite.config.ts uses the raw loadEnv value without parseEnv) — a trivial unused-helper cleanup, not the claimed misleading/broken config surface, and far narrower than the finding asserts.

The finding's headline claim is factually wrong. VITE_API_BASE_URL is NOT dead — frontend/vite.config.ts:31-32,58 reads it via loadEnv and uses it as the dev-server proxy `target` (server.proxy is only defined when target is set). This is exactly the same-origin model CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 and docs/frontend/features.md:67 describe: client.ts uses window.location.origin (localhost:5173 in dev), and the Vite proxy forwards those same-origin requests to the cross-origin API Gateway via VITE_API_BASE_URL. So the documented developer workflow genuinely works — setting VITE_API_BASE_URL in .env.local does route requests, refuting the risk that "it is silently ignored" and "local dev would break." Code and docs are consistent, not contradictory. docs/archive/specs/2026-05-06-staging-frontend-host-design.md:25 explicitly records this intent. The only true residue is that lib/env.ts:parseEnv is referenced only by its own test (env.test.ts) — but the vite-env.d.ts type decl IS used (it types import.meta.env in vite.config.ts). One unused 11-line zod helper with a passing test has zero runtime/security/behavior impact for an MVP serving a small DJ circle. Worse, the finding's own alternative recommendation (fall back to import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL in client.ts) would double-apply the base URL and break the working proxy design. Headline false, risk false, residual issue immaterial.

REFUTED on its central claim. The finding says VITE_API_BASE_URL "drives nothing" and is "dead at runtime," and that the documented dev setup (CLAUDE.md gotcha #9, README, docs/frontend/features.md) contradicts the code. That is false. The env var IS consumed — by frontend/vite.config.ts:31-32 (`const env = loadEnv(mode, process.cwd(), 'VITE_'); const target = env.VITE_API_BASE_URL ?? '';`) and drives the dev-server proxy at line 58 (`server: { ..., proxy: target ? proxy : undefined }`). The finding's grep was deliberately scoped to `src/`, which excludes vite.config.ts (located at frontend/vite.config.ts), causing the miss.

The actual, intended design: the browser-side client.ts:5-6 uses window.location.origin (same-origin, e.g. http://127.0.0.1:5173 in dev) so the Vite dev server can intercept those requests and proxy them to VITE_API_BASE_URL (the prod API Gateway). This is exactly what the docs describe (docs/frontend/features.md:67; README.md:120 "Local dev hits prod backend... Vite proxy forwards every API request to ${VITE_API_BASE_URL}"). docs/archive/specs/2026-05-06 even explicitly notes apiClient uses window.location.origin by design and no VITE_API_BASE_URL is needed at build time. So there is NO contradiction, and the env var is not silently ignored — without it, server.proxy is undefined and dev API calls fall through to the SPA (the documented failure mode), which is the opposite of "drives nothing."

The finding's load-bearing risk ("config silently ignored; non-same-origin deployment breaks with no error") is wrong, and its recommendation to "wire baseUrl to fall back to import.meta.env.VITE_API_BASE_URL" would actually break the deliberate same-origin proxy architecture.

Residual truth: lib/env.ts's parseEnv() is genuinely dead — confirmed only referenced by itself and lib/__tests__/env.test.ts, never called from vite.config.ts (which uses loadEnv directly) or src/. But that is a trivial unused-export cleanup, not the architectural/documentation defect the finding describes. The finding as written mischaracterizes a working, documented design as a bug.

title: No test covers the concurrent-refresh / replay-revoke-all-sessions path
evidence: AuthProvider.test.tsx contains only happy-path single-refresh transitions (loading->authenticated, refresh-fail->unauthenticated, signOut, auth:expired/auth:refreshed event handling, spotify token roll). Grep for inflight/concurrent/replay/race in AuthProvider.test.tsx returns nothing. There is no test asserting that AuthProvider.refresh() and client.tryRefreshOnce() coalesce into a single /auth/refresh, nor that two near-simultaneous refresh triggers result in exactly one network call. The most security-sensitive auth behavior (the ADR-0015 replay -> all-sessions-revoked seam) is therefore entirely unguarded by tests.
risk: The dedupe regression in finding #1 can be introduced or worsened with zero test signal; refactors of the refresh paths cannot be safely verified.
recommendation: Add a test that fires the scheduled/AuthProvider refresh and a client 401-retry refresh concurrently (mock /auth/refresh and assert it is hit exactly once), and a test asserting both callers share one in-flight promise. Co-locate with the dedupe fix from finding #1.

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code; all claims hold. AuthProvider.test.tsx (frontend/src/auth/__tests__/AuthProvider.test.tsx:23-219) contains only single-refresh happy-path transitions: loading->authenticated (23), refresh-fail->unauthenticated (44), signOut (59), auth:expired (97), auth:refreshed (121), spotify callback (153), token roll (186), signOut-clears-spotify (219). A grep for inflight|concurrent|replay|race|coalesce|dedup|simultaneous across all frontend *.test.ts(x) returns zero hits in AuthProvider.test.tsx and client.test.ts (only unrelated features match). The dedupe seam the finding references genuinely exists and is the security-sensitive bit: client.ts:8 `let inflightRefresh`, coalesced inside tryRefreshOnce() (lines 17-40). No test asserts that two near-simultaneous refresh triggers (AuthProvider.refresh + client tryRefreshOnce) collapse into exactly one /auth/refresh call. client.test.ts:50 ("refreshes token on 401 and retries once") counts the /me `attempt` (toBe(2)) sequentially — it never issues concurrent calls and never asserts refresh-call coalescing. The only tryRefreshOnce-adjacent test (integration.batch4.test.tsx:395-414) deliberately uses a stub refresh, not the real client path, and is single-shot. The ADR-0015 replay->all-sessions-revoked hazard is real and documented in the code itself (AuthProvider.tsx:109-115 comment), guarded only by the untested bootstrapStarted ref. The finding's evidence, risk, and recommendation are all accurate.

Refuted on impact for this MVP. (1) The finding's core premise is factually wrong: AuthProvider.refresh() calls api('/auth/refresh') (the plain api() in client.ts), which does NOT route through tryRefreshOnce() and is NOT covered by the module-level `inflightRefresh` guard. Only client.ts's own 401-retry path uses inflightRefresh. So there is no cross-module "coalesce into a single /auth/refresh" mechanism — a test asserting "exactly one network call across both callers / shared in-flight promise" would assert behavior the code neither implements nor was designed to. (2) The security-sensitive seam it wants guarded (replay -> revoke-all-sessions) is an explicitly accepted behavior per ADR-0015; the ADR documents that the ONLY app-level guard is bootstrapStarted.current (against StrictMode double-mount), and tab-restore replay is deliberately unguarded ("users must re-log-in"). There is no frontend invariant "two near-simultaneous refreshes collapse to one" that the code promises. (3) The StrictMode double-mount — the case that actually bit — is already guarded by bootstrapStarted.current and exercised by the existing single-bootstrap happy-path tests. (4) Realistic concurrent trigger (scheduled ~25-min timer coinciding within ms of a 401 retry) is extremely narrow, and worst case is the accepted ADR-0015 all-sessions revocation + ~5s re-login. (5) The cross-referenced "finding #1 dedupe regression" is not anchored in committed code (no review-doc/source describes such a dedupe). This is a TEST-dimension coverage-gap note, not a live bug, for an accepted behavior on a small-DJ-circle MVP — does not matter here.

Confirmed accurate against real code. (1) Grep for inflight|concurrent|replay|race|coalesce|dedup|simultaneous in both AuthProvider.test.tsx and api/__tests__/client.test.ts returns nothing (exit 1). (2) AuthProvider.test.tsx (lines 23-256) holds only single-path cases: loading->authenticated, refresh-fail->unauthenticated, signOut, auth:expired, auth:refreshed, spotify token roll — no concurrency. (3) client.test.ts "refreshes token on 401 and retries once" (line 50) is a single sequential request; no test fires two concurrent api() 401s and asserts one /auth/refresh, and none asserts a shared in-flight promise (no Promise.all of refresh-triggers, no import of inflightRefresh — exit 1). (4) The two seams are genuinely separate and untested: client.ts dedupes only its own tryRefreshOnce via inflightRefresh (client.ts:8,17-39), while AuthProvider.refresh() calls api('/auth/refresh') directly (AuthProvider.tsx:158) on a DIFFERENT path that does not participate in inflightRefresh. Concrete unguarded path: scheduleRefresh's timer fires refreshRef.current() (AuthProvider.refresh -> POST /auth/refresh) at the same instant an in-flight request 401s and client.ts calls tryRefreshOnce -> a second POST /auth/refresh with the now-rotated stale cookie -> ADR-0015 replay detection revokes all sessions. Nothing tests these coalesce. (5) Scenario 7 in integration.batch4.test.tsx asserts refreshCalls===1 but uses a STUB refresh (lines 401-418), not the real coalescing path, and is sequential (single 401->refresh->retry), not two concurrent triggers — it does not cover the cross-path dedupe and so does not refute the finding. The gap is the absence of a regression guard for a security-sensitive path, not an active defect; P3 is correct.

title: account_error -> premium dead-end and other auth/visual-critical flows lack browser-test coverage
evidence: Only 16 *.browser.test.tsx files exist and they cover component visuals (PageHeader, TrackRow, TagPill, playlist panels, etc.). There is no browser or unit test exercising PlaybackProvider's account_error -> navigate('/auth/premium-required') branch (PlaybackProvider.tsx:316-319), so the broken navigation in finding #2 ships untested. The OAuth-return premium/error mapping (auth.return.tsx:63-67) and the login premium banner (login.tsx:11-12) likewise have no test asserting the user reaches a page that actually renders the premium copy.
risk: Auth failure UX (premium-required, oauth_failed) is the user's only feedback when sign-in or playback authorization fails; regressions like the unregistered route go undetected because no test asserts the user lands on a page showing the intended message.
recommendation: Add a test that dispatches the SDK account_error and asserts the rendered route shows the premium-required copy (not NotFound), plus tests for auth.return error mapping. Once finding #2 is fixed, this test pins the correct destination.

votes.reason: Core factual claim ("no browser or unit test exercising the account_error -> navigate('/auth/premium-required') branch" and "OAuth-return premium/error mapping... have no test") is false. Three active, passing tests cover exactly these flows: (1) frontend/src/features/playback/__tests__/PlaybackProvider.test.tsx:585 emits account_error and asserts navigate('/auth/premium-required') on PlaybackProvider.tsx:316-319; (2) frontend/src/features/playback/__tests__/integration.batch3.test.tsx:307 (Scenario 17) emits account_error, asserts router.location.pathname === '/auth/premium-required' and that the page renders; (3) frontend/src/routes/__tests__/auth.return.test.tsx:64 stubs a 403 account_error and asserts the '/Spotify Premium/i' copy renders — which is precisely the test the finding's recommendation asks to add. I ran all three: 27+1 tests pass, none skipped/only. The finding's framing that coverage only exists as 16 component-visual *.browser.test.tsx files conflates test harnesses; routing/navigation logic is correctly covered by jsdom integration/unit tests. One real nuance: the integration test asserts against a PremiumRequiredStub route fabricated in the test harness (frontend/src/test/renderApp.tsx:166), while the production router (frontend/src/routes/router.tsx, no /auth/premium-required route, falls through to '*' NotFound) differs — so that integration test does not pin the true production destination. But that is a fidelity defect in an existing test, not the "no coverage exists" gap the finding asserts, and the PlaybackProvider unit test independently pins the navigate target. The stated evidence is incorrect, so the finding does not stand as written.

The finding's central evidence is factually wrong. It claims the account_error branch "ships untested" and that the auth.return premium mapping "has no test asserting the user reaches a page that actually renders the premium copy." Both are refuted by existing tests:

1. PlaybackProvider account_error branch IS tested twice: a unit test at frontend/src/features/playback/__tests__/PlaybackProvider.test.tsx:585 ("SDK account_error navigates to /auth/premium-required") and an integration test at frontend/src/features/playback/__tests__/integration.batch3.test.tsx:307 that emits account_error and asserts screen.getByTestId('premium-required-page') renders.

2. The auth.return premium/error mapping IS tested with assertions on the actual rendered copy: frontend/src/routes/__tests__/auth.return.test.tsx:64 mocks a 403 account_error and asserts getByText(/Spotify Premium/i) is in the document; line 59 covers the validation-failure error path; line 43 the success path. This directly contradicts the finding's claim of "no test."

The only true sub-claim is that the login.tsx:11-12 premium banner (a static query-param-driven i18n render) has no dedicated test — trivial, and the same premium copy render is already proven by the auth.return test.

There is one real but narrow kernel: the PlaybackProvider integration test uses a fabricated stub /auth/premium-required route (frontend/src/test/renderApp.tsx:166) that the real router (frontend/src/routes/router.tsx, only /login and /auth/return registered) lacks, so it masks rather than catches the real-router NotFound bug. But that gap is entirely parasitic on the separate routing finding (#2); the recommendation itself concedes "Once finding #2 is fixed, this test pins the correct destination." It adds no independent actionable signal. For a TEST-dimension item on an error-path branch that is already covered in unit + integration form, on a small-DJ-circle MVP, this does not matter independently. The finding as written substantially misrepresents existing coverage, so it is refuted.

The finding's two primary evidence claims are factually false, contradicted by existing passing tests.

CLAIM 1 (FALSE): "There is no browser or unit test exercising PlaybackProvider's account_error -> navigate('/auth/premium-required') branch." Two tests exercise it: frontend/src/features/playback/__tests__/PlaybackProvider.test.tsx:585 ("SDK account_error navigates to /auth/premium-required", asserts navigateMock called with the path) and frontend/src/features/playback/__tests__/integration.batch3.test.tsx:307 (renders the full app router, emits account_error via the SDK mock, and asserts router.state.location.pathname === '/auth/premium-required' plus the rendered premium-required page testid). I ran both files: 27 tests pass.

CLAIM 2 (FALSE): "The OAuth-return premium/error mapping (auth.return.tsx:63-67)... [has] no test asserting the user reaches a page that actually renders the premium copy." frontend/src/routes/__tests__/auth.return.test.tsx:64-75 mocks a 403 account_error response and asserts screen.getByText(/Spotify Premium/i) — i.e. the rendered premium copy (auth.premium_required = "CLOUDER requires Spotify Premium."). It also tests the validation-failure fallback (line 59) and the success path (line 43). I verified ApiError.from (frontend/src/api/error.ts) maps error_code:'account_error' to ApiError.code, so the test genuinely hits the real err.code === 'account_error' branch. Test passes.

RESIDUAL (TRUE but narrow): Only login.tsx's premium banner (login.tsx:11-12) genuinely has no test — no login test file exists. And the underlying production route bug (router.tsx:41-136 never registers /auth/premium-required, so PlaybackProvider's navigate falls through to NotFoundPage at router.tsx:135) is not caught, because both playback tests assert against a STUB '/auth/premium-required' route defined in renderApp.tsx:166 (explicitly labeled a placeholder). But that is a property of the separate route-registration bug, not the missing-test claim made here.

The finding's headline ("lack browser-test coverage", "ships untested") and its two main evidence bullets are directly contradicted by existing, passing tests. The genuine gap reduces to a single low-risk login banner, far smaller than described.

title: Deploy applies new Lambda code before running DB migrations (schema-skew window in prod)
evidence: Step order is: `Terraform apply` (lambda.tf:1-34 etc. point every function at the freshly packaged dist/collector.zip, so `terraform apply -auto-approve` publishes the NEW Lambda code) at deploy.yml:81-100, THEN `Run DB migrations via Lambda` (`aws lambda invoke ... {"action":"upgrade","revision":"head"}`) at deploy.yml:102-132. There is no step ordering that holds traffic. Between apply completing and the migration Lambda returning, all API/worker Lambdas run new code against the old schema.
risk: A push whose new code depends on a not-yet-applied migration (new column/table) serves 500s or, worse for the canonicalization/curation writers, fails ON CONFLICT/INSERT against missing columns for the duration of the migration invoke. Aurora cold-start (min_acu=0) can make the migration invoke take tens of seconds, widening the window. No automated rollback exists (`-auto-approve`, no plan gate).
recommendation: Run forward migrations before deploying code that requires them (expand/contract: migrate-then-deploy for additive changes), or gate the apply so the migration Lambda is invoked before the rest of the stack flips to new code. At minimum document and enforce the expand/contract discipline so a deploy is never code-ahead-of-schema.

votes.reason: Verified all load-bearing claims; the finding is accurate. (1) Step ordering: deploy.yml runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` (lines 81-100) BEFORE the `Run DB migrations via Lambda` invoke (lines 102-132). (2) Apply publishes new code: every collector Lambda in infra/lambda.tf (collector, canonicalization_worker, spotify_search_worker, vendor_match_worker, label/artist enrichers, auto_enrich_dispatch — lines 1-323) uses `filename = local.lambda_zip_file` + `source_code_hash = filebase64sha256(local.lambda_zip_file)`; package_lambda.sh rebuilds dist/collector.zip from src/collector, so a code change changes the hash and apply flips all functions to the new code. (3) No traffic hold / no rollback: there are no `aws_lambda_alias`, no versioned/provisioned aliases, no CodeDeploy traffic-shifting on the collector Lambdas (the only `publish = true` hits are CloudFront functions in frontend.tf), and the apply is `-auto-approve` with no plan gate. Functions run at $LATEST, so new code is live immediately while the old schema persists until the migration Lambda returns. (4) Window-widening: `aurora_serverless_min_acu` defaults to 0 (variables.tf:127-131) with 300s auto-pause and is NOT overridden in the deploy workflow, so prod can be paused and the VPC-bound, psycopg-connecting migration Lambda incurs cold-start latency, extending the skew window. docs/ops/deploy.md:20-59 documents the apply-then-migrate order with no expand/contract discipline, confirming the gap is real and undocumented. Severity P2 is correct: the impact is conditional (only when a push contains code requiring a not-yet-applied, non-backward-compatible migration), bounded to a seconds-to-tens-of-seconds window, on a small-audience app — a moderate, conditional reliability defect with a clear known remediation (migrate-then-deploy / expand-contract), not an unconditional P1.

Mechanism confirmed and trigger is real, not theoretical. Verified in deploy.yml:81-132 that `terraform apply` updates all Lambda code via source_code_hash (infra/lambda.tf, curation.tf, auth.tf all point at local.lambda_zip_file = dist/collector.zip) BEFORE the migration Lambda is invoked. Git history shows this repo regularly ships additive schema migrations alongside the code that uses them (e.g. bfceed1 "add clouder_tracks key columns", 665e00e "add classification flags and FAV bucket", f166435 "add artist enrichment tables"), so the schema-skew window is a recurring condition, not a hypothetical. Aurora min_acu=0 (rds.tf:22, ADR-0014) genuinely widens the migration invoke when Aurora is paused, as claimed. The finding is therefore NOT refuted — it correctly identifies a real code-ahead-of-schema window with a plausible trigger, and no accepted-behavior ADR covers deploy ordering.

However, P2 overstates the impact for this MVP. Mitigating factors lower it to P3: (1) the condition is transient and self-correcting — bounded by migration duration, and API reads recover on the next request once the migration lands; (2) the most-cited victims, the canonicalization/curation SQS workers, fail into retry/DLQ rather than losing data, so a schema-skew INSERT/ON CONFLICT failure largely self-heals after the migration completes; (3) no persistent data corruption — additive migrations don't destroy data; (4) audience is a small DJ circle, so the probability of a real user hitting the affected endpoint during a tens-of-seconds deploy window is low. It is a genuine reliability gap worth the documented expand/contract discipline, but for this MVP it is a low-severity, transient, self-healing issue rather than a P2.

Not refuted — the finding is accurate and reproducible from real code. Verified mechanism: deploy.yml runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` at lines 81-100, THEN invokes the migration Lambda at lines 102-132 (confirmed in both the workflow and docs/ops/deploy.md, where apply is step 3 and migrate is step 4). Every aws_lambda_function in infra/lambda.tf uses `filename = local.lambda_zip_file` with `source_code_hash = filebase64sha256(local.lambda_zip_file)`, so a code change flips the hash and `apply` publishes NEW code for the API function (collector) and all SQS workers in step 3 — before any migration runs. The API/worker handlers do not self-migrate (grep for upgrade/alembic/migrat in handler.py and worker_handler.py returned nothing); migrations happen only via the dedicated migration Lambda invoked after code is already live. No step holds traffic and `-auto-approve` means no rollback gate.

Concrete reproducible path: a push containing migration alembic/versions/20260531_30_track_key.py (adds key_name/key_camelot columns to clouder_tracks) shipped with the code that uses them — repositories.py:618-690 (raw `INSERT (... key_name, key_camelot ...) VALUES (:key_name, :key_camelot ...)` and `UPDATE ... SET key_name = COALESCE(:key_name, key_name)`) and curation_handler.py:1307-1308 (SELECT of row.key_name/row.key_camelot). Between step 3 (new code live) and step 4 completing: a canonicalization-worker SQS message that upserts a track executes INSERT/UPDATE against not-yet-existing columns and fails; a curation API read of those columns 500s. The migration history (adding tables/columns alongside code that references them) shows this is a recurring shape, not a one-off.

Severity P2 is correct: the window is real and unguarded but transient (self-heals when the migration finishes, typically seconds; Aurora min_acu=0 cold-start can widen it to ~tens of seconds), affects a small low-traffic deploy, and causes no data corruption (failed SQS writes retry; reads just 500). Not P1 (no persistent outage or data loss) and not lower (genuine repeatable prod-facing skew window with no traffic-hold or rollback guardrail).

title: Production deploy runs no tests/lint/plan gate and is path-filter-blind
evidence: deploy.yml has no pytest, no pnpm test/typecheck/lint, and no `terraform plan` step — it goes straight to `terraform apply -auto-approve` and `aws lambda invoke` migrations. It relies entirely on PR checks. But pr.yml jobs are path-filtered (pr.yml:21-39): a frontend-only PR runs only `frontend`, skipping `terraform`/`tests`/`alembic-check`. Merging that PR triggers deploy.yml which ALWAYS runs full `terraform apply` + migration invoke against prod — infra/backend the PR never validated with `terraform plan` or pytest.
risk: Drift between main and the last-validated infra plan is applied to production unreviewed. A bad terraform change merged via a path that skipped the `terraform` job (or an out-of-band edit to main) is auto-applied with no plan diff visible and no rollback.
recommendation: Add a `terraform plan` + test gate inside deploy.yml before apply (fail-closed), or require the full PR matrix to pass regardless of path filter for merges to main. Consider a manual-approval environment gate before apply.

votes.reason:
Code facts verified and accurate. deploy.yml (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) triggers on push to main (lines 3-6) and contains NO test/lint/typecheck/`terraform plan` gate — its steps go straight to `terraform apply -auto-approve` (lines 81-100) and `aws lambda invoke` migrations (lines 102-132). All cited line ranges check out. pr.yml's jobs are indeed path-filtered via dorny/paths-filter: `alembic-check` and `tests` gate on `backend=='true'` (lines 43, 130), `terraform` on `infra=='true'` (line 83), `frontend` on `frontend=='true'` (line 153). So a frontend-only PR runs only the `frontend` job, and the subsequent push-to-main triggers the unconditional prod apply. The core technical claim is correct.

One nuance: a *frontend-only* PR by definition does not modify `infra/**` or `src/**`, so its deploy re-applies the same infra/backend that was previously validated — the drift risk materializes primarily via out-of-band edits to main, provider/state drift between plan-time and apply-time, or an infra-affecting merge whose checks were not enforced (no branch protection is visible in-repo). The finding mentions the out-of-band path, so the risk narrative holds even if the frontend-only-PR framing slightly overstates the most direct trigger.

The substantive defect is real and undisputed: a fail-open `-auto-approve` production deploy with no in-pipeline `terraform plan` diff and no test gate, relying entirely on path-filtered PR checks. This is a legitimate CI reliability gap. Note deploy.yml uses `environment: production` (line 18), which *could* carry a GitHub environment approval gate configured outside the repo, but that is not visible in code and the recommendation already suggests adding one. P2 is the correct severity: a genuine fail-open prod-apply anti-pattern, but requiring a triggering condition rather than a guaranteed-broken or data-loss path (not P1), and more than cosmetic (not P3).

Factual claims verified and accurate: deploy.yml (lines 81-132) runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` + migration Lambda invoke on every push to main with no pytest, no pnpm test/typecheck/lint, no `terraform plan`, and no manual gate. pr.yml (lines 41-180) is path-filtered: a frontend-only PR runs only the `frontend` job, skipping `terraform`/`tests`/`alembic-check`. I also confirmed two aggravating facts the finding didn't cite: main has NO branch protection (`gh api .../branches/main/protection` → 404, so PR checks are not required to pass before merge and direct pushes are possible), and the `production` GitHub environment has empty `protection_rules` (no approval/reviewers), so `environment: production` provides zero gating today.

However, on the MVP-impact lens the risk is over-stated. The headline scenario — "frontend-only PR merges → terraform apply applies unvalidated infra" — is essentially a no-op: a frontend-only PR changes no `.tf` files, so `terraform apply` computes no infra diff and the migration invoke (`upgrade head`) is a no-op when head is already applied. No drift is introduced by that merge. The *normal* infra-change flow IS covered: an infra PR sets `infra==true`, which runs the `terraform` job with fmt/validate/plan. The only genuinely uncovered case is the abnormal one — merging an infra PR despite a failed/absent terraform job, or editing prod state out of band — both of which require an operator to override their own signals. For this MVP (small DJ circle, effectively solo maintainer tarodo), that is a self-discipline/process-maturity matter, not a defect with a plausible everyday trigger. Auto-apply of PR-validated Terraform is a common, accepted pattern at this scale. This is a legitimate hardening recommendation (add a plan gate / required checks / environment approval) but not an MVP-blocking reliability bug. Refuting on impact; if tracked at all it belongs at P3, not P2.

Verified against the two referenced files. deploy.yml (triggers on push to main, lines 3-6, no path filter) runs straight to `terraform apply -auto-approve` (line 83) over the full infra/ state, then `aws lambda invoke` DB migrations (line 105), then frontend deploy — with NO pytest, NO `terraform plan`, NO pnpm test/typecheck/lint anywhere (grep for those terms in deploy.yml returns nothing). pr.yml jobs are individually path-filtered: terraform `if: needs.changes.outputs.infra == 'true'` (82-83), tests/alembic `if: ...backend == 'true'` (42-43,129-130). A frontend-only PR (frontend/** only) runs just the `frontend` job and skips terraform/tests/alembic. There is no aggregate 'all-checks' gate job and no always()-run job in pr.yml, and no branch-protection-as-code/rulesets in .github (only the two workflow files exist). Reproducible path: any merge to main (even docs/frontend-only) triggers deploy.yml, which applies the entire current infra/ state to prod without a plan diff — so any drift, an infra change that landed via a path that skipped the `terraform` job, or a direct push to main is auto-applied unreviewed, plus DB migrations are invoked unconditionally. The risk is conditional (requires drift/out-of-band change) and not externally triggerable, but the blast radius (unreviewed auto-apply to prod infra + DB migrations) is real — consistent with P2. Not refuted; severity unchanged.

title: deploy.yml lacks a concurrency block — concurrent main pushes race terraform apply + migration + frontend
evidence: Neither deploy.yml nor pr.yml declares a `concurrency:` group (grep returns nothing). Two pushes to main within a short window start two `deploy` jobs in parallel. The DynamoDB state lock (init at deploy.yml:44-51) serializes `terraform apply`, but the `aws lambda invoke` migration step (102-132), SSM secret sync (53-79), and `scripts/deploy_frontend.sh` S3 sync + CloudFront invalidation (146-148) are not lock-protected.
risk: Interleaved deploys can apply older code/migration after newer (lost-update on Lambda version, frontend S3 `--delete` sync from a stale build clobbering the newer one, or migration of an older revision running last). With `-auto-approve` and no rollback, recovery is manual.
recommendation: Add `concurrency: { group: deploy-production, cancel-in-progress: false }` to deploy.yml so deploys queue and run strictly serially.

votes.reason:
Verified all cited claims against the actual files; finding is correct.

1. No `concurrency:` block: `grep -rn "concurrency"` over .github/workflows/ returns NO MATCHES. Confirmed deploy.yml header (lines 1-17) and pr.yml have no `concurrency:` key. Absent this, GitHub Actions starts a new run per push, so two main pushes in a short window run two `deploy` jobs in parallel.

2. Line references are accurate: DynamoDB lock backend-config at deploy.yml:50 (init block 44-51); SSM `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` calls at 53-79 (not lock-protected); `aws lambda invoke` migration at 102-132 (not lock-protected); `scripts/deploy_frontend.sh` at 146-148 (not lock-protected). The Terraform DynamoDB lock serializes only init/plan/apply — the migration, SSM sync, and frontend steps run outside any mutual exclusion, exactly as the finding states.

3. The clobbering risk is backed by the real script: scripts/deploy_frontend.sh runs `aws s3 sync dist/ "s3://$BUCKET/" --delete` followed by a CloudFront invalidation. A stale build syncing after a newer one would `--delete` the newer hashed assets — a genuine lost-update.

No misread logic, no missed guard. The race is real and the recommended `concurrency: { group: deploy-production, cancel-in-progress: false }` is the correct standard fix.

Severity P2 (unchanged) is well-calibrated: it is a latent reliability gap requiring two pushes within the multi-minute deploy window (uncommon for this single-maintainer project), with manual-only recovery. Not P1 (no active outage/data-loss on the common path), not P3 (it is a recognized CI/CD anti-pattern with real interleaving consequences against prod infra/migrations).

Verified: deploy.yml (and pr.yml) have no `concurrency:` block, deploy triggers on push to main, and the SSM sync (53-79), migration invoke (102-132), and frontend `--delete` S3 sync (deploy_frontend.sh) run outside the DynamoDB terraform lock. The trigger is plausible, not theoretical: the single maintainer's merge history shows PRs landing 11-23 min apart in bursts (e.g. 2026-05-31 PRs #165-#168), and a full deploy run (Lambda package + terraform apply against possibly cold Aurora per ADR-0014 + migration + pnpm build + S3 sync + CloudFront) realistically takes 5-15+ min, so two deploys can overlap. The finding stands as a real, cheaply-fixable (2-line) gap.

However, the stated severity (P2) overstates the blast radius. Two of the three claimed harms are largely neutralized: (a) terraform apply — and thus the Lambda code version/infra/API contract — IS serialized by the DynamoDB lock (the finding concedes this); (b) the migration is `upgrade head`, which in alembic is monotonic and idempotent — it only moves forward, so the claimed "migration of an older revision running last" cannot downgrade or apply a stale target; both concurrent invokes converge to head. SSM sync writes fixed GitHub-secret values (not build-derived), so interleaving is a no-op. The only genuine residual is the frontend S3 `--delete` sync race: an older build could land after a newer one, leaving the SPA one revision stale. That window is self-healing on the very next push, index.html is invalidated each deploy, and there is no data loss, auth/security impact, or API-contract break. For a small DJ-circle MVP with a single maintainer, that transient, self-correcting stale-frontend window is P3, not P2.

Verified against real files. deploy.yml (read in full) has no `concurrency:` key; grep across .github/workflows/ returns nothing, confirming pr.yml lacks one too. The single `deploy` job runs all steps sequentially in one job: package_lambda → terraform init (DynamoDB lock at lines 44-51, `dynamodb_table` at line 50) → SSM secret sync (53-79) → terraform apply -auto-approve (81-100) → migration via `aws lambda invoke` (102-132) → `scripts/deploy_frontend.sh` (148). The DynamoDB state lock only serializes the `terraform apply` step, NOT the migration invoke, SSM sync, or the frontend S3 `--delete` sync + CloudFront invalidation in deploy_frontend.sh (confirmed: `aws s3 sync dist/ ... --delete`).

Reproducible path: two pushes to `main` within a short window (e.g. merging a stack of PRs back-to-back, or a fast-follow hotfix) each fire the Deploy workflow. Job A (older commit) and Job B (newer commit) run in parallel. The lock prevents concurrent `apply` but does NOT order them — if A's apply or A's `deploy_frontend.sh` s3 sync lands last, production ends up on the OLDER Lambda code / OLDER frontend bundle (lost update), with `-auto-approve` and no rollback. `environment: production` (line 18) does NOT refute this — a bare environment reference provides no run serialization absent a concurrency block or configured protection rules.

One sub-claim is overstated: "migration of an older revision running last" — Alembic `upgrade head` is forward-only and idempotent, so the older job's migration is a no-op rather than a regression; the real migration risk is stale Lambda code running against newer schema. But this is only one of three listed mechanisms; the Lambda-version and frontend-S3 lost-update mechanisms are sound and sufficient to sustain the finding.

Severity P2 is correct: real reliability gap, but harm is conditional on concurrent pushes (not every deploy), detectable, and operationally recoverable by re-running the latest deploy. Not a runtime data-integrity bug. The recommended `concurrency: { group: deploy-production, cancel-in-progress: false }` is the standard fix.

title: docs/ops/deploy.md is stale vs deploy.yml — wrong secrets, wrong vendor vars, missing redirect/admin vars
evidence: deploy.md documents syncing `/clouder/perplexity/api_key`, `/clouder/spotify/client_id`, `/clouder/spotify/client_secret` (3 secrets) and applying `-var ai_search_enabled=true -var perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter=...`. The actual deploy.yml syncs 8 SSM params (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek api keys + spotify client_id/secret + ytmusic client_id/secret, :56-79) and passes gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/ytmusic vars plus `vendor_match_enabled=true`, `spotify_oauth_redirect_uri`, `admin_spotify_ids`, `allowed_frontend_redirects` (:84-100). `perplexity`/`ai_search` do not exist anywhere in infra/ (grep empty). The GitHub Secrets table (deploy.md:77-84) still lists `PERPLEXITY_API_KEY` and omits GEMINI/OPENAI/TAVILY/DEEPSEEK/YTMUSIC_* secrets the workflow requires.
risk: An operator following deploy.md to set up secrets/rotate keys provisions the wrong SSM params and never sets the four LLM keys + two ytmusic keys the deploy actually consumes, producing enrichment/publish failures that are hard to diagnose. The manual-ops section (deploy.md:97-105) references a non-existent `beatport-prod-ai-search-worker` Lambda.
recommendation: Regenerate deploy.md from the current deploy.yml: list all 8 synced SSM params, the real `-var` set, the full secret/variable inventory (incl. ALLOWED_FRONTEND_REDIRECTS, ADMIN_SPOTIFY_IDS, SPOTIFY_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI), and remove perplexity/ai-search references.

votes.reason: Verified all cited claims against deploy.md and deploy.yml directly; every factual assertion holds. deploy.md:32-35 documents 3 synced SSM params; deploy.yml:55-79 actually syncs 8 (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek api keys + spotify client_id/secret + ytmusic client_id/secret). deploy.md:43 documents `ai_search_enabled` and `perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter` vars; deploy.yml:84-100 instead passes gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/ytmusic SSM vars plus vendor_match_enabled, spotify_oauth_redirect_uri, admin_spotify_ids, allowed_frontend_redirects. grep confirms `perplexity` and `ai_search`/`ai_search_enabled` appear nowhere in infra/ (or any tf/py/yml). The manual-ops snippet (deploy.md:102) references `beatport-prod-ai-search-worker`, which does not exist — infra/lambda.tf defines canonicalization/migration/spotify_search/vendor_match/label_enrichment/artist_enrichment/auto_enrich_dispatch workers only. The real infra vars (vendor_match_enabled, gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/ytmusic SSM params) are confirmed present in infra/variables.tf and infra/lambda.tf. The finding is accurate and the operator-confusion risk is real but documentation-only.

Finding is fully verified against both files. deploy.md:34-36 documents syncing only 3 SSM params (/clouder/perplexity/api_key, spotify client_id, client_secret) while deploy.yml:56-79 syncs 8 (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify x2/ytmusic x2). deploy.md:43 passes -var ai_search_enabled and perplexity_api_key_ssm_parameter; deploy.yml:84-100 passes none of those and instead passes gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/ytmusic SSM vars + vendor_match_enabled + spotify_oauth_redirect_uri + admin_spotify_ids + allowed_frontend_redirects. grep confirms perplexity/ai_search/ai-search-worker have ZERO hits in infra/ and src/, while gemini/ytmusic/vendor_match vars are real (infra/variables.tf, iam.tf, terraform.tfvars.example). The bite is only on rare manual operator actions (key rotation / secret re-provisioning) in a small-DJ-circle MVP, where the doc misleads by naming an unconsumed secret and omitting six real ones — recoverable enrichment/publish failures, not catastrophic. This is documentation drift with low blast radius and no plausible automated-path trigger. Correctly classified as P3; neither refuted nor worth elevating.

Finding confirmed against actual code. (1) deploy.md:32-35 documents syncing 3 SSM params incl. /clouder/perplexity/api_key; deploy.yml:56-79 actually syncs 8 (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek + spotify id/secret + ytmusic id/secret), no perplexity.
