title: Third-party GitHub Actions pinned to mutable major tags, not commit SHAs
evidence: All actions use floating major tags: `actions/checkout@v4`, `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4`, `actions/setup-python@v5`, `hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3`, `pnpm/action-setup@v4`, `actions/setup-node@v4` (deploy.yml), and `dorny/paths-filter@v3` (pr.yml:21). None are pinned to a full commit SHA.
risk: deploy.yml assumes the OIDC AWS role (id-token: write, full terraform apply + SSM put-parameter on production credentials). A compromised or retagged third-party action (especially the AWS-credentials and setup-terraform actions, or dorny/paths-filter which sees the diff) would execute with that production AWS access. Mutable tags make a supply-chain push silently land on the next deploy.
recommendation: Pin every third-party action to a full-length commit SHA (with a comment noting the version), and enable Dependabot for GitHub Actions to track upgrades. First-party `actions/*` can stay on tags if policy allows, but the AWS/terraform/paths-filter actions should be SHA-pinned.

votes.reason: Verified all cited lines directly. The core claim is accurate: every third-party GitHub Action is pinned to a floating major tag, not a commit SHA. Confirmed in deploy.yml — actions/checkout@v4 (L24), aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 (L27), actions/setup-python@v5 (L33), hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 (L42), pnpm/action-setup@v4 (L135), actions/setup-node@v4 (L140); and in pr.yml — checkout@v4 (L20), dorny/paths-filter@v3 (L21), configure-aws-credentials@v4 (L93), setup-terraform@v3 (L99), action-setup@v4 (L160), setup-node@v4 (L163). The risk is genuine: deploy.yml has permissions id-token: write (L10), assumes the AWS OIDC role (L29), runs terraform apply -auto-approve (L83), and writes production secrets via aws ssm put-parameter (L56-79). A retagged/compromised third-party action would execute with that production AWS access on the next push to main. Minor imprecisions that do not refute: two pr.yml line numbers in the 'where' (33, 42) point at filter-glob content rather than action pins, and the dorny/paths-filter risk wording slightly overstates its AWS exposure (it runs in the changes job without assuming the role, though it does have id-token: write available). These are cosmetic; the substantive finding holds. Severity P3 is correct — this is a defense-in-depth hardening gap requiring a maintainer-compromise/tag-hijack to exploit, not an active vulnerability.

Verified accurate against both workflow files. deploy.yml uses floating major tags for all third-party actions (actions/checkout@v4 L24, aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 L27, actions/setup-python@v5 L33, hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 L42, pnpm/action-setup@v4 L135, actions/setup-node@v4 L140) and pr.yml adds dorny/paths-filter@v3 (L21). The privileged deploy job genuinely has id-token: write (L10), assumes the OIDC AWS role (L29), and runs terraform apply (L83) plus aws ssm put-parameter on production secrets (L56-79). A retag/compromise of an upstream action would execute with that production access on the next push to main. This is not a purely theoretical class: SHA-pinning of third-party actions is standard supply-chain guidance (GitHub, OWASP, SLSA), and the attack has occurred in the wild against privileged deploy workflows (tj-actions/changed-files, March 2025), so it has a plausible real-world trigger and is not refuted. Severity P3 is correct: the named actions are reputable (AWS/HashiCorp/pnpm/GitHub maintained), the one individual-maintained action (dorny/paths-filter) runs only in the low-privilege `changes` job not the AWS-credentialed job, the privileged path is already gated to push-on-main (requires merge access, no fork-PR trigger), and the fix is low-effort defense-in-depth hardening rather than an active exploitable defect. Appropriately a nice-to-have hardening item for this small-DJ-circle MVP.

Verified the finding against the actual workflow files. All claims are accurate. grep confirms every third-party action across .github/workflows/deploy.yml and pr.yml uses a floating major tag (actions/checkout@v4, aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4, actions/setup-python@v5, hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3, pnpm/action-setup@v4, actions/setup-node@v4, dorny/paths-filter@v3); zero are SHA-pinned (grep for @<40-hex> returns nothing). No .github/dependabot.yml exists either. The line numbers in the finding match exactly.

Reproducible path: a push to main triggers deploy.yml, which holds permissions id-token: write, resolves aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 to whatever commit the mutable tag currently points at, assumes the production OIDC role (secrets.AWS_GITHUB_ROLE_ARN), then runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` (deploy.yml:81-100) and `aws ssm put-parameter ... SecureString` writing production secrets (deploy.yml:53-79). A malicious retag of any of these third-party actions would execute in that production AWS context. This is the documented tj-actions/changed-files class of supply-chain attack. The risk and recommendation are sound.

Severity unchanged at P3: this is a legitimate defense-in-depth/supply-chain hardening gap aligned with GitHub hardening guidance and OpenSSF Scorecard, but exploitation depends on an external event (compromise/retag of a reputable upstream action) that is not currently occurring and is not directly attacker-reachable through this system. pr.yml uses pull_request (not pull_request_target), so a fork PR cannot trivially exfiltrate the role without maintainer interaction. The mitigation is cheap (effort S) and blast radius high, so it should not be dismissed — P3 is correct.

title: Secret-sync step overwrites 8 SSM SecureStrings on every push to main
evidence: The `Sync GitHub secrets to SSM Parameter Store` step runs unconditionally on every push to main and issues 8 `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` calls (gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify×2/ytmusic×2). There is no change-detection — values are re-written identically each deploy. Combined with `manage`-style overwrite, every merge creates a new SSM parameter version.
risk: Minor but unbounded: each deploy bumps 8 SSM parameter versions (SSM keeps up to 100 versions per advanced param / overwrites standard), generates put-parameter API calls, and (since secrets are passed as `--value "${{ secrets.X }}"`) relies entirely on GitHub log-masking for non-exposure. No cost blowup, but the unconditional rewrite is noise and a rotation-audit confounder.
recommendation: Make the sync conditional (only when secrets changed) or move it out of the per-push path into a separate manually/secret-rotation-triggered workflow. Confirm the params are standard-tier so version accumulation is bounded.

votes.reason: Verified the cited code at .github/workflows/deploy.yml:53-79. All factual claims hold: (1) the "Sync GitHub secrets to SSM" step has no `if:` guard and the workflow triggers only on push to main (lines 3-6), so it runs unconditionally on every merge. (2) It issues exactly 8 `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite --type SecureString` calls for gemini/openai/tavily/deepseek/spotify×2/ytmusic×2 (lines 56-79). (3) grep across .github/infra/scripts confirms no change-detection/diff logic and no tier specification anywhere. (4) The `--overwrite` semantics claim is technically correct — AWS SSM does NOT deduplicate identical values; each put-parameter --overwrite bumps the parameter version unconditionally. (5) Secrets are indeed passed as `--value "${{ secrets.X }}"`, relying on GitHub log-masking. The finding's own risk text is honest and appropriately modest ("No cost blowup," "Minor but unbounded," "noise and a rotation-audit confounder"). Standard-tier SSM params are free, cap at 100 retained versions (older silently dropped, no error/cost), and put-parameter calls are free — so the only real impact is version-history noise and rotation-audit confounding. That is exactly a P3-level housekeeping observation. Nothing is overstated; the finding is accurate and the recommendation (conditional/out-of-path sync) is reasonable. Not refutable on correctness grounds.

Verified the finding is technically accurate: deploy.yml:53-79 runs an unconditional secret-sync step on every push to main with 8 `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` calls. However, the impact does not matter for this MVP. (1) The parameters are created via CLI `put-parameter` with no `--tier advanced` flag, so they are standard-tier: standard parameters have ZERO storage cost and PutParameter is free within SSM standard throughput, so 8 idempotent re-writes per merge cost nothing even at hundreds of deploys/month for a small DJ circle. (2) The finding's own risk text concedes "No cost blowup" — it is self-defeating on its stated COST dimension. (3) Version accumulation is inherently bounded: AWS auto-prunes standard parameters at 100 versions (oldest discarded on the 101st write), so there is no unbounded growth and no cleanup burden. (4) Passing secrets as `--value "${{ secrets.X }}"` is the standard, universally-used GitHub Actions secret-injection pattern (log-masked, stored as KMS-encrypted SecureString) — not a novel exposure. (5) The "rotation-audit confounder" is cosmetic noise the finding itself labels "noise"; nobody audits rotation on this MVP. This is a purely theoretical tidiness concern with no plausible cost, security, or operational trigger, which the rubric classifies as refuted.

Finding is accurate and reproducible. In .github/workflows/deploy.yml the workflow triggers on every push to main (lines 3-6). The job `deploy` runs the step `Sync GitHub secrets to SSM Parameter Store` (lines 53-79) unconditionally — there is no `if:`, no diff/change-detection anywhere in the file (verified via grep). The step issues exactly 8 `aws ssm put-parameter ... --type SecureString --overwrite` calls: gemini, openai, tavily, deepseek, spotify client_id, spotify client_secret, ytmusic client_id, ytmusic client_secret (lines 56-79). Concrete repro: merge any PR to main -> workflow runs -> all 8 put-parameter calls execute with identical values -> each creates a new parameter version. The values come from GitHub secrets via --value "${{ secrets.X }}", so non-exposure relies on GitHub log masking, as stated. The params are created only by these CLI calls (Terraform merely references them by name via *_ssm_parameter variables; no aws_ssm_parameter resource exists for them), and the calls omit --tier, so they default to Standard tier — which auto-prunes the oldest version at 100, making version accumulation bounded and harmless. The finding itself states there is no cost blowup; the actual impact is CI noise and a rotation-audit confounder. P3 is the correct, honest severity — this is hygiene, not a bug, security hole, or correctness/cost problem.

title: OpenAPI contract can silently drift from handlers/gateway — CI verifies schema.d.ts against the committed yaml but never regenerates the yaml from generate_openapi.py
evidence: The contract lives in three independent places (system map gotcha #8): infra route_key declarations, the handler dispatch tables (handler.py / curation_handler._ROUTE_TABLE), and scripts/generate_openapi.py:ROUTES (which produces docs/api/openapi.yaml). The frontend CI job (pr.yml:169-176) runs `pnpm api:types` and fails if frontend/src/api/schema.d.ts diverges from the committed docs/api/openapi.yaml. But grep confirms no CI step ever runs `generate_openapi.py` and diff-checks its output against the committed yaml (only references are inside the script itself). docs/api/openapi.yaml is a 163k checked-in artifact. So the verified invariant is 'schema.d.ts matches the committed yaml', not 'the yaml matches the actual routes'. A developer who adds a route to infra + the handler but forgets to run generate_openapi.py leaves the yaml (and therefore schema.d.ts) stale, and all of CI stays green.
risk: Frontend types and the documented API contract drift from the real backend surface with no automated detection. The SPA can be coded against a route shape that no longer matches the handler, surfacing only at runtime. The 'three places' invariant has no machine enforcement at the yaml-vs-code seam.
recommendation: Add a CI job (backend filter) that runs `PYTHONPATH=src python scripts/generate_openapi.py` to a temp file and `git diff --exit-code` against docs/api/openapi.yaml, mirroring the existing schema.d.ts freshness check. This closes the generator->yaml seam so the existing yaml->schema.d.ts check becomes transitively sound.

votes.reason:
Verified all cited anchors against the actual code; the finding holds.

1. pr.yml:169-176 runs `pnpm api:types` (which per frontend/package.json:22 is `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml -o src/api/schema.d.ts`) and fails only if schema.d.ts diverges from the COMMITTED yaml. So the enforced invariant is exactly "schema.d.ts matches committed yaml", as claimed.

2. No CI/hook/test ever regenerates the yaml from generate_openapi.py and diff-checks it. Confirmed: grep for `generate_openapi` across all .yml/.yaml/.sh/.toml/.ini/.cfg returns only the two self-referential header lines inside docs/api/openapi.yaml itself. deploy.yml has zero openapi references. No .pre-commit-config.yaml, no .githooks, no .git/hooks. The one test hit (test_label_enrichment_repository.py:312) is a JSONB-parsing regression test whose docstring merely mentions openapi.yaml as rationale — it does not regenerate or diff the yaml.

3. The "three places" drift seam is real: generate_openapi.py docstring (lines 10-12) explicitly says ROUTES must be "kept in sync with infra/api_gateway.tf and infra/auth.tf when adding new endpoints" — manual sync, no machine enforcement. ROUTES is at line 1222; yaml is 163119 bytes (matches "163k checked-in artifact").

Therefore a route added to infra + handler but not regenerated leaves yaml (and transitively schema.d.ts) stale while all CI stays green. The mechanism, evidence, and risk are accurate. The recommendation (backend-filtered CI job: regenerate to temp file + `git diff --exit-code`) correctly closes the generator→yaml seam, making the existing yaml→schema.d.ts check transitively sound.

Severity P2 is appropriate: genuine machine-enforcement gap in the ARCH/CI dimension, but it is latent (requires developer error to trigger), impact is contract/type drift surfacing at runtime rather than data loss/security exposure, and there is partial mitigation (documented manual-regeneration workflow + "do not edit by hand" yaml header). Not P1 (no live defect, no immediate user/data impact) and not P3 (it is a real soundness gap in a guarded invariant, not cosmetic).

Factual claims verified and correct, so the finding is NOT false. pr.yml:169-176 runs `pnpm api:types` (= openapi-typescript on the committed docs/api/openapi.yaml) and diffs only schema.d.ts; nothing in pr.yml or deploy.yml ever runs scripts/generate_openapi.py to regenerate openapi.yaml from its Python source. grep confirms no generate_openapi reference in .github/. The check is therefore circular (both sides derive from the same committed yaml), and no backend test gates yaml freshness (the only openapi mention in tests is an unrelated comment). The drift scenario is real. The recommended fix (run generate_openapi.py to temp + git diff --exit-code, effort S) is valid.

However, severity P2 overstates impact for THIS MVP, so it should be P3: (1) No live drift exists — I regenerated openapi.yaml and got a zero-byte git diff; the committed contract is in sync today. (2) The repo has effectively 1-2 contributors (tarodo 45/50, Roman 5/50 of recent commits) who follow CLAUDE.md gotcha #8 and demonstrably bundle the regenerated openapi.yaml + ROUTES + schema.d.ts in the same commit (e.g. 92ba864). Triggering the gap requires a maintainer to forget the documented regen step. (3) The failure is benign: a stale yaml yields types MISSING a route, not WRONG types — and pnpm typecheck IS gated in CI (pr.yml:177), so tsc fails the moment frontend code calls the new endpoint, surfacing in CI at first use rather than shipping broken. (4) Zero runtime/data/security impact: the real API surface is Terraform routes + Lambda handlers deployed independently; openapi.yaml is purely codegen/doc input. This is genuine but low-impact CI/defense-in-depth hardening with no present bug and a small trusted audience — P3, not P2.

Verified against real code. CI job in .github/workflows/pr.yml:169-176 runs `pnpm api:types` (defined in frontend/package.json:22 as `openapi-typescript ../docs/api/openapi.yaml -o src/api/schema.d.ts`) and `git diff --quiet src/api/schema.d.ts`. This treats the committed docs/api/openapi.yaml as INPUT, so it can only detect schema.d.ts drift from the yaml — never yaml drift from the actual routes. grep over .github/ (both pr.yml and deploy.yml), scripts/, Makefile/justfile (none exist), .pre-commit-config.yaml (none exists), and tests/ confirms NO step ever runs scripts/generate_openapi.py and diff-checks its output; the only `generate_openapi` references are inside the script's own header/docstring and the unrelated docstring at tests/unit/test_label_enrichment_repository.py:316. docs/api/openapi.yaml is a 163k git-tracked artifact (git ls-files confirms) produced from scripts/generate_openapi.py:ROUTES (line 1222). Reproducible path: a developer adds a route to infra/*.tf route_key + the handler dispatch table but forgets to run generate_openapi.py — the yaml stays stale, `pnpm api:types` regenerates schema.d.ts from the stale yaml (no diff), and all CI passes green while the documented contract and frontend types lag the real backend surface. The 'three places' invariant (system map gotcha #8) has no machine enforcement at the generator->yaml seam. Severity P2 is appropriate: this is a missing CI guard for a docs/type-gen artifact whose drift surfaces as frontend type mismatch at integration/runtime, not a direct production-breaking defect, with the only current safety net being manual adherence to gotcha #8.

title: Single dist/collector.zip bundles all heavyweight deps (google-genai, openai, ytmusicapi, sqlalchemy, psycopg, cryptography) into every Lambda including the 256MB/5s auth_authorizer
evidence: package_lambda.sh produces one artifact (dist/collector.zip) by `pip install -r requirements-lambda.txt` then copying all of src/collector. requirements-lambda.txt pulls google-genai, openai, ytmusicapi, sqlalchemy, alembic, cryptography, psycopg[binary]. All 11 Lambdas deploy this same zip (infra variables.tf:49-53). The latency-critical authorizer (auth_authorizer, 256MB memory, 5s timeout, on the hot path of every authenticated request, cached only 300s) imports only PyJWT-level code (auth_authorizer.py:9-11) yet ships the full multi-hundred-MB dependency tree. psycopg[binary] is in the runtime artifact even though only the VPC migration Lambda imports it (migration_handler.py:96,113) — it is never imported by a Data-API handler, so the CLAUDE.md 'no psycopg at runtime' rule holds, but the binary still inflates every package.
risk: Larger package = slower cold-start init for every function, worst on the small/fast authorizer where init time is added to first-request latency under the 5s timeout. A vulnerable transitive dep in any vendor SDK widens the attack surface of unrelated Lambdas (e.g. the public auth Lambda carries the LLM SDKs). Per-function right-sizing is impossible with one shared artifact.
recommendation: Split packaging: a minimal artifact for auth_authorizer/auth/curation/api (PyJWT, pydantic, boto3, cryptography) and a worker artifact carrying the enrichment SDKs; or move heavy SDKs to a shared Lambda layer attached only to the enrichment/search workers. At minimum drop psycopg from requirements-lambda.txt and install it only into the migration build path.

votes.reason: All factual claims verified against the cited code. package_lambda.sh (lines 6,14-27) produces a single dist/collector.zip via `pip install -r requirements-lambda.txt` then copies all of src/collector. requirements-lambda.txt confirms the heavyweight deps: google-genai, openai, ytmusicapi, sqlalchemy, alembic, cryptography, psycopg[binary], pydantic. Exactly 11 aws_lambda_function resources exist (auth.tf x2, lambda.tf x7, curation.tf x1 = wait, count is 11 per grep), all referencing local.lambda_zip_file — so every function ships the identical artifact. auth_authorizer is 256MB/5s (variables.tf defaults timeout=5/memory=256, wired in auth.tf:118-119). auth_authorizer.py imports only auth_settings/jwt_utils/logging_utils — no LLM SDKs or psycopg, confirming it ships hundreds of MB it never uses. psycopg is referenced only in migration_handler.py:96,113 (DB URL strings), confirming the finding's own caveat that the no-runtime-psycopg rule holds while the binary still inflates every package. The finding is accurate and the architectural concern (cold-start init cost on the hot-path authorizer, widened attack surface, no per-function right-sizing) is legitimate. Severity P3 is correct: this is an efficiency/hygiene/attack-surface issue, not a functional bug or active vulnerability; the performance impact is real but bounded and the security angle is speculative.

The packaging fact is true (one dist/collector.zip from requirements-lambda.txt deployed to all 11 Lambdas via lambda_zip_path, with psycopg[binary] bundled though only migration_handler.py imports it), but the stated RISK does not materialize for this MVP. The headline concern — added cold-start latency on the 256MB/5s auth_authorizer — rests on a false premise: Python imports are lazy, and the authorizer's actual import chain (verified) is auth_settings (stdlib), jwt_utils (PyJWT), logging_utils (structlog/settings) — it never imports google-genai, openai, ytmusicapi, sqlalchemy, or psycopg. Those SDKs sit dormant on disk and add ~0 to the authorizer's module-init time. The only real effect of a bigger zip is marginally longer one-time artifact download/unpack, which is trivial next to the already-accepted Aurora cold-start 503 (ADR-0014) and is moot once the authorizer's 300s key cache / warm container is in play. JWT verification is pure CPU against a cached key, so there is no plausible path to the 5s timeout. The 'widened attack surface' point concerns code that is never imported into the authorizer process — not reachable code, and purely theoretical for a small DJ circle. Per-function right-sizing is a build-ergonomics nicety, not an MVP correctness or operational issue. Net: a valid but cosmetic architectural cleanup whose concrete risks don't bite this MVP. P3 is the right level if kept as a future optimization, but it does not matter now.

Verified all load-bearing claims against real code. package_lambda.sh (lines 14-16) builds exactly one artifact, dist/collector.zip, by `pip install -r requirements-lambda.txt -t BUILD_DIR` then copying all of src/collector. requirements-lambda.txt confirms google-genai>=0.4.0, openai>=1.50.0, ytmusicapi>=1.7, sqlalchemy>=2.0, alembic, cryptography>=44, and psycopg[binary] are pulled in. infra/main.tf:28 defines local.lambda_zip_file = abspath(../dist/collector.zip) and variables.tf:52 defaults lambda_zip_path to dist/collector.zip. grep confirms every one of the 11 aws_lambda_function resources (curation.tf x1, auth.tf x2, lambda.tf x8) uses `filename = local.lambda_zip_file` — single shared artifact, no per-function packaging. auth_authorizer (auth.tf:112-131) defaults to 256MB memory / 5s timeout (variables.tf:401-411) and imports only auth.jwt_utils + auth_settings + logging_utils (auth_authorizer.py:9-11), i.e. PyJWT-level code, yet ships the full tree. psycopg appears in src/collector only in migration_handler.py:96,113 as a SQLAlchemy connection-string scheme for the VPC migration Lambda; no Data-API handler imports it, so the CLAUDE.md no-psycopg-at-runtime rule holds while the binary still inflates every package — exactly as stated.

Concrete reproducible path: any authenticated request hits API Gateway -> invokes auth_authorizer (cache TTL 300s, so periodic cold starts). On a cold start Lambda must fetch and unzip the shared multi-hundred-MB collector.zip carrying enrichment SDKs the authorizer never imports, adding init overhead under its 5s budget. Path is fully constructible from real code.

Severity assessment confirms P3 rather than higher: the heavy SDKs (openai/google-genai/ytmusicapi) are all lazily imported inside functions (label_enrichment_handler.py:24, gemini.py:80, ytmusic/lookup.py:22, etc.), so they are NOT loaded into the authorizer's interpreter at module init — the cost is on-disk artifact size / unzip time, not Python module-init CPU. Real but modest cold-start impact, plus a latent defense-in-depth attack-surface concern; no security boundary broken and the Data-API rule still holds. Accurate, reproducible, and correctly rated. Severity unchanged.

title: Tenant-identity extraction reimplemented independently in 6+ handlers with inconsistent None-handling — tenant-isolation drift surface
where: src/collector/curation_handler.py:171; label_enrichment/routes.py:61; label_enrichment/auto_routes.py:31; artist_enrichment/routes.py:61; artist_enrichment/auto_routes.py:31; auth_handler.py:537; handler.py:89-95
evidence: The identical body parsing `event.requestContext.authorizer.lambda.user_id` is copy-pasted as `_extract_user_id`/`_user_id_or_none`/`_authorizer_context` in at least six modules. They diverge in failure behavior: curation `_user_id_or_none` returns None and the caller 401s (curation_handler.py:447-449); enrichment routes are inconsistent within the same file — handle_put_label_preference raises 'user_id is required' when None (routes.py:275-277) but handle_get_label_user passes `_extract_user_id(event)` straight into the query allowing None (routes.py:233). Tenant isolation is a per-query discipline (no gateway guarantee, per the system map), so each copy is load-bearing for multi-tenant data separation.
risk: A behavior change (claim-name change, admin-claim addition, None-vs-401 policy) must be hand-replicated across all copies; missing one creates an isolation gap or an inconsistent 401/200 contract. A None user_id reaching a WHERE user_id=... query can return cross-tenant or null-scoped rows depending on the SQL, and the divergence already exists between routes in the same file.
recommendation: Extract one shared `require_user_id(event)`/`optional_user_id(event)` helper (e.g. in a small auth context module) and have every handler import it; make the 'missing identity' decision (raise vs None) explicit and uniform per route category. Add a test asserting all user-scoped routes 401 on a token-less / user_id-less context.

votes.reason:
The structural claims are accurate and verified: 6 independent reimplementations of the authorizer.lambda.user_id extraction path with no shared helper and no cross-imports — _user_id_or_none (curation_handler.py:171, validates isinstance(uid, str) and uid), four identical _extract_user_id copies (label_enrichment/routes.py:61, label_enrichment/auto_routes.py:31, artist_enrichment/routes.py:61, artist_enrichment/auto_routes.py:31, all returning raw ctx.get('user_id')), _authorizer_context (auth_handler.py:537), and _require_admin (handler.py:89-95). Divergent None-handling is real: curation returns None then 401s (curation_handler.py:447-449); enrichment routes are internally inconsistent — handle_put_label_preference raises on None (routes.py:275-277) but handle_get_label_user passes None into the query (routes.py:233), same in artist routes. So the drift-surface / inconsistent-contract core is NOT refuted. But severity is overstated. The worst-case 'None reaching a WHERE user_id=... query returns cross-tenant rows' does not hold at the cited site: get_label_info_for_user (repository.py:944-996) uses user_id only in a LEFT JOIN ON ulp.user_id=:user_id, coalesced via 'user_id or \"\"', with the main WHERE on the shared canonical label_id — None yields a null my_preference, not cross-tenant data; same for list_labels. No divergent read site feeds None into a WHERE user_id= filter. Also the authorizer (auth_authorizer.py:39-65) either denies the request ({'isAuthorized': False}) or returns a populated user_id, so the None branch is not gateway-reachable in practice. This is genuine copy-paste drift / inconsistent 401-vs-200 contract maintainability risk, not a present tenant-isolation vulnerability — P3, not P2.</reason>
</invoke>

The duplication is real (the ~4-line authorizer parse helper is reimplemented as `_extract_user_id`/`_user_id_or_none`/`_authorizer_context` across label_enrichment/routes.py:61, auto_routes.py:31, artist_enrichment/routes.py:61, auto_routes.py:31, curation_handler.py:171, auth_handler.py:537, handler.py:89-95). But the load-bearing risk — "tenant-isolation drift," "None user_id reaching a WHERE user_id=... query can return cross-tenant rows" — does not hold for this codebase, so the P2 severity is unjustified.

(1) The None case is not reachable through the live API. The gateway Lambda authorizer (auth_authorizer.py:39-66) returns {"isAuthorized": False} for any request lacking a valid JWT, and verify_access_token (jwt_utils.py:73-84,110) requires the `sub` claim and sets user_id=str(payload["sub"]). Every request that reaches a handler carries a non-empty user_id. The gateway IS a guarantee here, directly contradicting the finding's premise that there is "no gateway guarantee." The recommended test (assert routes 401 on a token-less/user_id-less context) tests a state the gateway makes unreachable.

(2) The one "inconsistent" route the finding relies on — handle_get_label_user (routes.py:233) passing user_id straight into get_label_info_for_user — is structurally incapable of cross-tenant leakage. Both query branches (repository.py:958-996) select the row by WHERE li.label_id=:id / WHERE lbl.id=:id (shared canonical, admin-ingested label data accessible to all authenticated users per the tenancy model) and only LEFT JOIN the caller's OWN preference overlay on :user_id, with `user_id or ""`. A None/empty user_id yields my_preference=null — the documented intended fallback — never another tenant's row. The finding's "cross-tenant or null-scoped" collapses to just "null-scoped," which is correct behavior, not a bug.

(3) The genuinely user-scoped routes already 401 on None (handle_put_label_preference routes.py:275-277; handle_get_my_label_preferences routes.py:319-321), and their queries are WHERE p.user_id=:user_id, which return/affect zero rows on None — never cross-tenant.

So the divergence is cosmetic, not a security/isolation gap. The residual substance is a low-impact DRY/maintainability smell (one shared helper would be nicer) with no plausible isolation trigger for this small DJ-circle MVP — P3 at most, not the P2 the tenant-isolation framing implies.

The structural finding is real but its severity is overstated; it is a maintainability/architecture concern, not a present tenant-isolation gap.

VERIFIED (existence holds):
- 7 distinct helpers walk requestContext.authorizer.lambda across 6 modules: 4 byte-identical `_extract_user_id` (label_enrichment/routes.py:61, label_enrichment/auto_routes.py:31, artist_enrichment/routes.py:61, artist_enrichment/auto_routes.py:31), plus curation_handler.py:171 `_user_id_or_none`, auth_handler.py:537 `_authorizer_context`. These diverge in None-handling: `_user_id_or_none` requires a non-empty str; the four `_extract_user_id` return ctx.get("user_id") raw.
- The intra-file caller divergence is real: routes.py:275-277 raises on None user_id, but routes.py:233 passes the raw value into get_label_info_for_user (same split in artist_enrichment routes.py:222 vs 264).

WHY SEVERITY IS LOWER (P3 not P2):
1. Worst-case data-leak claim is unsupported on the cited path. I traced get_label_info_for_user (repository.py:944-996): user_id is coalesced to "" (`user_id or ""`) and used ONLY in a LEFT JOIN to fetch the caller's own preference; the row data comes from shared canonical tables (clouder_label_info / clouder_labels). A None user_id yields my_preference: null, never cross-tenant rows. No isolation breach materializes.
2. The None precondition is not reachable from real traffic. Every route uses authorization_type = "CUSTOM" with the JWT authorizer (infra: all CUSTOM, zero NONE). auth_authorizer.py returns isAuthorized:False for missing/invalid tokens and on success always sets user_id: claims.user_id, where claims.user_id = str(payload["sub"]) and `sub` is a required JWT claim (jwt_utils.py:79,110). So an authenticated request always carries a non-empty user_id; nothing drives None into these handlers. The finding's premise "no gateway guarantee" is inaccurate for the live path.
3. One citation is mislabeled: handler.py:89-95 is `_require_admin` (checks is_admin), not user-id extraction; it does not support the None-handling-divergence claim.

Net: genuine copy-paste/contract-drift surface worth the recommended shared-helper refactor, but the actual impact is an inconsistent 401/200 contract and a future-change hazard, not active tenant-isolation drift. Correct level is P3.

title: Auto-enrichment block fan-out has no architectural size cap — uncapped per-dispatch LLM multiplier gated only by claim-dedup
evidence: `_dispatch_labels` calls `claim_labels(sorted(set(label_ids)))` then enqueues one SQS message per claimed label in batches of _SQS_BATCH=10, with no upper bound on len(label_ids) derived from a triage block (label_ids_for_triage_block / artist_ids_for_triage_block). Each enqueued item then costs vendors × (1 generate + 1 merge) LLM calls (the Tavily adapter is internally 3 HTTP calls). The only limiter is claim_labels dedup, which prevents re-dispatching already-claimed labels but does not cap a single dispatch. The auto path fires on every triage-block finalize.
risk: A large finalized block (or first-ever enrichment of many labels) issues an unbounded burst of LLM calls — the largest uncapped cost multiplier in the system — with no architectural throttle. maximum_concurrency=8 on the worker ESM bounds parallelism but not total spend; SQS just serializes the same total volume.
recommendation: Add a per-dispatch cap (e.g. max labels/artists per auto-run, with remainder deferred) and/or a daily enrichment budget gate in claim_labels/create_run. Surface the cap as a config var so a runaway block cannot translate directly into thousands of vendor calls.

votes.reason:
Verified every cited fact against the code; the finding holds.

(1) No per-dispatch size cap. `_dispatch_labels` (src/collector/label_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:57-135) and `_dispatch_artists` (src/collector/artist_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:55-130) call `claim_labels(sorted(set(label_ids)))` / `claim_artists(...)` with no upper bound on len(label_ids). The triage-block source query `label_ids_for_triage_block` (auto_repository.py:236-248) is `SELECT DISTINCT a.label_id ... WHERE ct.source_triage_block_id = :block_id` — count scales with the block's distinct labels, unbounded.

(2) claim_labels is dedup-only. auto_repository.py:108-181 reclaims failed/stale-queued and inserts brand-new ids, excluding completed/in-progress/already-searched labels. `_IN_CHUNK=500` and `_SQS_BATCH=10` are SQL/SQS batching loops, NOT total caps — a first-ever enrichment of N new labels claims all N. The finding states this correctly.

(3) Per-item LLM multiplier is real. batch_size=1 (variables.tf: "one label per invocation") → one SQS message = one full enrichment. enrich_label_for_run / run_vendors_parallel (orchestrator.py:34-70) issue one adapter call per vendor plus a merge client call, matching "vendors × (1 generate + 1 merge)".

(4) Fires on every finalize. enqueue_block_auto_enrich at curation_handler.py:1534 is called unconditionally on every triage-block finalize (async via dispatch worker, PR #183 — consistent with the finding's own note).

(5) No total-spend throttle. maximum_concurrency (lambda.tf:232/281/321) caps parallelism only — the infra comments say so explicitly; SQS serializes the same total volume. Worker reserved_concurrent_executions defaults to -1 (unlimited) since enable_lambda_reserved_concurrency defaults false.

No guard was missed and no line was misread. Severity P2 is appropriate: this is a latent cost/blast-radius architectural concern (requires a large or first-ever block to manifest; parallelism and dedup partially mitigate), not an active correctness defect — exactly the P2 band. Severity unchanged.

Technically true that there is no explicit per-dispatch numeric cap, but the impact framing ("uncapped cost multiplier," "thousands of vendor calls," "largest uncapped cost multiplier in the system") is overstated for this MVP. Five refuting facts: (1) Opt-in by default — auto_enrich_config.enabled defaults to false (alembic 20260525_25_auto_enrich.py:29); the auto path is dormant until an admin deliberately enables it via upsert_config, so an admin owns the cost decision. (2) Permanent dedup bounds total spend by catalog size, not finalize frequency: the claim INSERT guards on NOT EXISTS clouder_label_info (auto_repository.py:170-173) plus NOT EXISTS state row, and retries cap at _MAX_ATTEMPTS=2 — each distinct label is enriched at most ~twice in the system's lifetime. Total auto-enrichment spend is bounded by (distinct labels in catalog) × vendors × ~2, a converging one-time backfill, NOT a recurring per-finalize multiplier. The finding's own worst case ("first-ever enrichment of many labels") is the one scenario that cannot repeat. (3) Steady state is zero new claims — once the catalog is enriched, every subsequent finalize hits the claimed=0 skip path, so the burst self-extinguishes. (4) Small-DJ-circle scale: distinct labels/artists per block come from SELECT DISTINCT over one block's promoted tracks — realistically tens, not thousands; no mechanism injects thousands of NEW labels in a single finalize. (5) No request-path or rate-limit blowout: dispatch runs off-path in the auto-enrich-dispatch worker, fully _safe-wrapped (never breaks curation), and maximum_concurrency=8 + SQS serialization bound parallelism; the only consequence is bounded background spend. The legitimate residual (no hard daily/per-run budget gate) is a real cost-governance backlog note, but bounded, opt-in, and converging — not a P2 architectural risk. P3 at most.

Confirmed reproducible from real code; could not refute. Concrete trigger path: POST /triage/blocks/{id}:finalize -> _finalize_triage_block (curation_handler.py:1534) -> enqueue_block_auto_enrich (curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py) -> AUTO_ENRICH_DISPATCH_QUEUE -> auto_enrich_dispatch_handler.lambda_handler -> try_dispatch_for_triage_block / try_dispatch_artists_for_triage_block -> _dispatch_labels / _dispatch_artists.

Preconditions: auto_enrich_config row for kind 'labels'/'artists' has enabled=true (admin-toggled), and the finalized block's tracks resolve to many distinct labels/artists not previously enriched.

No size cap verified: AutoEnrichRepository.label_ids_for_triage_block (auto_repository.py:236) is `SELECT DISTINCT a.label_id ... WHERE ct.source_triage_block_id=:block_id` with NO LIMIT. A triage block is populated by a date-range classify query over an entire style's releases in create_block (triage_repository.py:259+), not bounded by TRACK_IDS_MAX=1000 (that constant only guards the move/transfer endpoints). So a single block can span hundreds/thousands of distinct labels+artists. _dispatch_labels enqueues one SQS message per claimed id in _SQS_BATCH=10 chunks with no upper bound on len(resolved).

Only limiter is dedup: claim_labels (auto_repository.py:108) excludes ids with an existing state row or a clouder_label_info row — so on FIRST-ever enrichment of a large catalog the entire distinct set is claimed and dispatched. No budget/quota/throttle exists in claim_labels, create_run (repository.py:619; computes cells_total = requested_labels * len(vendors) purely for accounting, never caps), or the worker.

Cost multiplier confirmed: orchestrator.run_label_enrichment runs all configured vendors in parallel (1 generate call each) + 1 merge call per claimed label (orchestrator.py:34-128); Tavily adapter is internally several HTTP calls. maximum_concurrency=8 on the worker ESM (infra/lambda.tf) bounds parallelism but not total spend; SQS serializes the same volume.

Severity stays P2: blast radius is cost/spend, not availability or data integrity — it is config-gated (enabled flag), the dedup makes the worst burst essentially one-time per large catalog, and concurrency caps prevent a vendor-rate-limit outage. Not P1 (no outage/data loss, requires auto-enrich enabled). Not P3 (a single normal user action can translate one finalize into thousands of paid vendor calls — the largest uncapped cost multiplier in the system). Recommendation (per-dispatch cap + daily budget gate as config) is sound and the where/evidence are accurate.

title: Configuration access is split between the typed settings layer and ad-hoc os.environ reads, fragmenting the settings contract
where: src/collector/settings.py:133-220 (typed queue-url fields); label_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:51, routes.py:55; artist_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:49, routes.py:55; curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py:23
evidence: Queue URLs are defined as typed Pydantic settings fields (settings.py: canonicalization_queue_url, spotify_search_queue_url, vendor_match_queue_url, label_enrichment_queue_url, artist_enrichment_queue_url) and read that way in handler.py:887 and curation_handler.py:404. But the enrichment/dispatch modules bypass the settings layer and read raw os.environ.get('LABEL_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL') / 'ARTIST_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL' / 'AUTO_ENRICH_DISPATCH_QUEUE_URL' directly, raising bare RuntimeError on absence. So the same configuration surface has two readers with different validation, defaulting, and error semantics.
risk: The Pydantic settings classes are no longer the single source of truth for configuration; an env-name typo or rename is caught by the typed layer for some consumers and only at runtime (RuntimeError) for others. Aliases/defaults defined in settings.py (e.g. the CANONICALIZATION_QUEUE_URL/CANONICALIZE_QUEUE_URL dual alias) are not honored on the os.environ path.
recommendation: Route all queue-URL access through the settings layer (add the enrichment/dispatch queue URLs to the appropriate Settings class and read them via get_*_settings()), removing the direct os.environ.get calls so configuration validation is uniform.

votes.reason: All cited code verified accurate. Typed Pydantic queue-URL fields exist in settings.py (canonicalization_queue_url:133 with AliasChoices dual-alias CANONICALIZATION_QUEUE_URL/CANONICALIZE_QUEUE_URL, spotify_search_queue_url:140, vendor_match_queue_url:141, label_enrichment_queue_url:204, artist_enrichment_queue_url:220) and are read via the settings layer in handler.py:887 (settings.canonicalization_queue_url) and curation_handler.py:404 (get_api_settings().vendor_match_queue_url). The five enrichment/dispatch modules confirmed to bypass settings and read raw os.environ with bare RuntimeError: label_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:51, label_enrichment/routes.py:55, artist_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:49, artist_enrichment/routes.py:55, curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py:23. So LABEL_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL and ARTIST_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL genuinely have two readers with different validation/error semantics; the dual-alias on canonicalization is real and would not be honored on the os.environ path. Minor imprecision: AUTO_ENRICH_DISPATCH_QUEUE_URL has no corresponding typed field, so that one is env-only rather than a true split-reader — but it is still a valid instance of the same ad-hoc-env-read pattern the finding describes. No functional bug (Terraform sets the env vars in prod, both paths work), so this is a maintainability/consistency concern. P3 (ARCH, low impact) is correct.

Finding is factually true (config access is split between typed Pydantic settings and ad-hoc os.environ.get in the enrichment/dispatch modules), but its concrete risk doesn't materialize and it has no plausible failure trigger in this MVP. (1) The headline risk — that the CANONICALIZATION_QUEUE_URL/CANONICALIZE_QUEUE_URL dual alias isn't honored on the os.environ path — is moot: that dual-aliased field is the canonicalization queue, which is read ONLY via the settings layer (handler.py:887). The os.environ-reading modules never touch a dual-aliased field, and CANONICALIZE_QUEUE_URL is referenced nowhere except its own alias definition (not set in infra, no consumer). (2) The affected queues' typed fields (label_enrichment_queue_url, artist_enrichment_queue_url) use a SINGLE alias byte-identical to the env name the os.environ path reads, so a typo/rename breaks both readers identically — the typed layer adds no protection here. (3) AUTO_ENRICH_DISPATCH_QUEUE_URL is not a typed field anywhere; it has exactly one reader, so no fragmentation. (4) infra/curation.tf and lambda.tf set all three env vars to match the code exactly, and dedicated unit tests (test_auto_dispatch.py, test_curation_auto_enrich_dispatch_enqueue.py, test_artist_auto_dispatch.py) cover the enqueue paths, so a missing-env regression surfaces in CI. This is pure architectural tidiness (DRY settings contract) with zero behavioral/operational risk for a small-DJ-circle MVP. Refuted on impact.

Finding verified against real code; all factual claims hold. Typed Pydantic queue-URL fields exist in settings.py: canonicalization_queue_url with the dual alias AliasChoices("CANONICALIZATION_QUEUE_URL","CANONICALIZE_QUEUE_URL") (L133-138), spotify_search_queue_url (L140), vendor_match_queue_url (L141), label_enrichment_queue_url (L203-205), artist_enrichment_queue_url (L219-221). Handlers read via the settings layer: handler.py:887 (settings.canonicalization_queue_url) and curation_handler.py:404 (get_api_settings().vendor_match_queue_url). The dispatch/route modules bypass settings with raw os.environ.get(...) + bare RuntimeError: label_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:51 and routes.py:55 read LABEL_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL; artist_enrichment/auto_dispatch.py:49 and routes.py:55 read ARTIST_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL; curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py:23 reads AUTO_ENRICH_DISPATCH_QUEUE_URL — which has NO typed field anywhere (present only in infra/curation.tf:25). So the same config surface genuinely has two readers with divergent validation/defaulting/error semantics, and the dual-alias precedent (CANONICALIZATION_QUEUE_URL/CANONICALIZE_QUEUE_URL) would not be honored on the os.environ path. Notably, the typed label_enrichment_queue_url and artist_enrichment_queue_url fields are never read by any consumer (grep for .label_enrichment_queue_url/.artist_enrichment_queue_url returns nothing) — they are effectively dead, which slightly strengthens the fragmentation point. Concrete path: a finalize request enqueues a block via curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.enqueue_block_auto_enrich -> _queue_url() reads AUTO_ENRICH_DISPATCH_QUEUE_URL raw; the auto-enrich-dispatch worker then calls label_enrichment.auto_dispatch._dispatch_labels -> _queue_url() reads LABEL_ENRICHMENT_QUEUE_URL raw — both off the typed layer. Severity P3 is correct: infra (lambda.tf, curation.tf) sets exactly the env names the os.environ readers expect, so there is no live functional bug today; this is a maintainability/consistency (ARCH) concern where a future rename/typo is validated inconsistently across consumers. Effort S is reasonable. Not refuted.

title: SQS workers loop over event Records with per-record continue/raise but no ReportBatchItemFailures — correctness depends entirely on the batch_size=1 infra setting
evidence: worker_handler.lambda_handler iterates `for record in event['Records']`, using `continue` to drop permanent-error messages and `raise` to redrive transient ones (worker_handler.py:52,55,67,177-180). No ESM in infra/lambda.tf sets function_response_types=['ReportBatchItemFailures'], and the worker returns {'processed': n}, not a batchItemFailures shape. This is only correct because every ESM uses batch_size=1 (the loop processes exactly one record). The code shape (a loop with per-record drop/raise) implies multi-record handling that the infra does not actually support safely.
risk: If any *_batch_size var is ever raised above 1 (these are configurable terraform vars, not constants), a single transient `raise` re-delivers the whole batch — re-processing already-committed records (canonicalization is idempotent so data is safe, but vendor/LLM calls and Spotify searches would re-fire, multiplying cost) — while there is no per-record partial-failure reporting to limit the blast radius. A latent coupling between code semantics and an infra constant.
recommendation: Either pin the batch_size vars to 1 with a comment that the workers are not partial-batch-safe, or implement ReportBatchItemFailures (set function_response_types on each ESM and return {'batchItemFailures': [...]}) so the loop is genuinely multi-record safe before batch_size is ever increased.

votes.reason: Verified all factual claims against the cited code; they hold. worker_handler.py:50 iterates `for index, record in enumerate(records)`; permanent errors `continue` (lines 52, 55, 67, 179), transient errors `raise` (line 180); handler returns `{"processed": processed}` (line 182), not a batchItemFailures shape. grep across the repo confirms zero occurrences of `function_response_types`, `ReportBatchItemFailures`, or `batchItemFailures` — so no ESM enables partial-batch reporting. All six ESMs (lambda.tf:100,143,184,226,275,318) bind `batch_size` to terraform vars, each defaulting to 1 (variables.tf:82,209,283,476,529,606), and three already warn "Keep at 1". So the loop-with-per-record-drop/raise is indeed only safe because batch_size=1, and raising any var would redrive a whole batch with no partial-failure reporting — exactly as described. The finding does not overclaim: it correctly notes data is idempotent-safe and frames the impact as cost amplification of vendor/LLM/Spotify re-fires gated on a manual config change. Severity P3 is correctly calibrated — latent coupling requiring an operator override to trigger, no current incorrect behavior, and partially already mitigated by existing "Keep at 1" comments on three workers. Cannot refute.

The finding's core technical observation is accurate (workers loop over Records with continue/raise and do not implement ReportBatchItemFailures, so correctness relies on batch_size=1), but it is refuted on two grounds. First, its own recommendation is already implemented: all six *_batch_size vars default to 1 in infra/variables.tf (lines 82, 209, 283, 476, 529, 606), and the three highest-cost workers carry exactly the comment the finding asks for — label/artist say "Keep at 1 — one label/artist per invocation" and auto_enrich_dispatch says "Keep at 1 so a partial-batch retry can't re-dispatch already-processed blocks," naming the precise failure mode. Second, there is no plausible trigger: no .tfvars files exist anywhere in the repo, so the deployed value is the default 1; the issue only manifests if an operator deliberately raises a documented "keep at 1" var while ignoring the inline warning. Even then, the finding concedes data stays safe (canonicalization is idempotent) and the worst case is duplicated vendor/LLM/Spotify cost, not corruption. For this MVP (small DJ circle, admin-driven ingest) this is a latent, already-documented coupling with no current trigger — a purely theoretical issue whose mitigation is already in the code.

Verified accurate and reproducible as a latent coupling. Confirmed in real code: worker_handler.py:50-182, spotify_handler.py:112-171, vendor_match_handler.py:43-66, label_enrichment_handler.py, and artist_enrichment_handler.py all loop `for record in records` using per-record `continue` (drop permanent errors) and `raise` (redrive transient). All return `{"processed": n}`, none return a `{"batchItemFailures": [...]}` shape. Grep confirms NO `function_response_types = ["ReportBatchItemFailures"]` exists anywhere in infra/. Correctness depends entirely on batch_size=1: all six vars (canonicalization_batch_size, spotify_search_batch_size, vendor_match_batch_size, label_enrichment_batch_size, artist_enrichment_batch_size, auto_enrich_dispatch_batch_size) default to 1 in variables.tf and have no tfvars override, but are plain `type = number` vars with no validation block pinning them to 1.

Concrete trigger path: operator sets var.spotify_search_batch_size = 5; ESM delivers a 5-record batch; spotify_handler processes records 1-3 successfully (committing Spotify search results), record 4 raises a transient error at line 169; the raise propagates out of the loop, the invocation fails, and without ReportBatchItemFailures SQS makes the WHOLE batch visible again — re-firing the already-completed Spotify API searches for records 1-3 on redrive. Real, constructible path.

The finding correctly frames this as latent (not currently triggerable at defaults; data is safe due to idempotent canonicalization; impact is cost amplification of vendor/LLM/Spotify calls under a non-default config). It also correctly notes a real inconsistency: label/artist/auto-enrich vars carry explicit "Keep at 1" guard comments, but canonicalization/spotify/vendor_match vars do not. P3 (low-severity latent arch coupling / defensive hardening) is the correct level — not an active bug, not cosmetic.

title: ADR-0008 describes a removed Perplexity/ai_search_results subsystem as the live AI-flag mechanism
evidence: ADR-0008 (Status: Accepted, not superseded; README index line 41) states is_ai_suspected is set by `propagate_ai_flag` reading `ai_search_results` (Perplexity JSONB results) on labels/artists/tracks. Reality: migration 20260518_21 drops the `ai_search_results` table; `propagate_ai_flag` has ZERO definitions/callers in src/collector (grep returns nothing); `search_handler.py`, `src/collector/search/`, and `perplexity_client.py` do not exist. Perplexity survives only in a stale docstring (providers/registry.py:4) and a comment (settings.py:19), not as a live provider. The flag is now mirrored from the multi-vendor LLM enrichment via `_mirror_ai_content` (label_enrichment/repository.py:817-836: `UPDATE clouder_labels SET is_ai_suspected = :value` gated on `merged.confidence >= threshold` and `merged.ai_content in (SUSPECTED, CONFIRMED)` / clears on NONE_DETECTED) — i.e. ADR-0016's pipeline, not Perplexity.
risk: An agent following ADR-0008 will look for a Perplexity client, an `ai_search_results` table, and a `propagate_ai_flag` function that were all deleted, and may wire AI-flag work to the wrong (dead) subsystem. The threshold/no-op/clear semantics happen to survive in the new code, but the entire described data path and table are gone.
recommendation: Mark ADR-0008 `Superseded by ADR-0016` (and update the README index status), or rewrite its Decision/Consequences to describe the current source: `is_ai_suspected` mirrored from `merged.ai_content` in the label-enrichment consensus, confidence-gated, no `ai_search_results` table.

votes.reason: Verified all core claims against the cited code. ADR-0008 is Status: Accepted (line 2) and README index line 41 lists it un-superseded. It describes is_ai_suspected as set by propagate_ai_flag reading the ai_search_results Perplexity JSONB table. Reality confirmed: migration alembic/versions/20260518_21_drop_ai_search_results.py drops the table; grep for propagate_ai_flag, ai_search_results, and ai_content returns ZERO hits in src/; no perplexity_client.py / search_handler.py / src/collector/search exist (Perplexity survives only in experiments/ sandbox, a registry.py:4 docstring, and a settings.py:19 comment). The flag is now set by project_ai_suspected (label_enrichment/repository.py:811-835, called from label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:128 and artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:124), gated on confidence >= threshold with SUSPECTED/CONFIRMED -> true, NONE_DETECTED -> clear, else no-op — i.e. ADR-0016's enrichment pipeline. The ADR genuinely misdirects an agent toward deleted code. One cosmetic slip in the finding: it names the live function _mirror_ai_content when it is actually project_ai_suspected, but it quotes the real SQL, the real docstring text, and the correct gating semantics, and line range 817-836 does contain that code — substance is correct. P3 DOCS severity is appropriate (docs-only, misleading, no runtime impact).

Verified: ADR-0008 (Status: Accepted, README index line 41, no superseded marker) describes is_ai_suspected as set by propagate_ai_flag reading the ai_search_results table from Perplexity. Reality confirms the finding: migration 20260518_21 drops ai_search_results; grep finds ZERO definitions/callers of propagate_ai_flag in src; perplexity_client.py, search_handler.py, and src/collector/search/ do not exist. The live mechanism is repository.project_ai_suspected (label_enrichment/repository.py:811, called from orchestrator.py:128; mirrored in artist_enrichment) gated on confidence>=threshold with SUSPECTED/CONFIRMED->true, NONE_DETECTED->false. So the entire described data path (table + function + provider) is dead while a stale ADR claims it is live. This is a genuine, non-theoretical doc-staleness issue with a real trigger: an agent doing AI-flag work from ADR-0008 will hunt for deleted artifacts (propagate_ai_flag/ai_search_results/Perplexity client) and find nothing. Not an accepted behavior, so not refuted on impact grounds. Two notes that keep this low severity (P3 correct, unchanged): (1) the finding misnames the live method as _mirror_ai_content — the actual name is project_ai_suspected (minor error in the finding's own evidence, not load-bearing); (2) the risk is partially mitigated because ADR-0016 already cross-references ADR-0008 (line 56) and states is_ai_suspected is "re-populated as a denormalized projection of the new pipeline" (lines 33-34, 51-52), and the behavioral semantics (threshold/clear/no-op) survive verbatim in the new code — so an agent gets pointed toward the right mechanism and would still implement correct behavior. Pure DOCS accuracy, no runtime/data/security impact, small fix. P3 is right.

Verified against real files. ADR-0008 (docs/adr/0008-ai-suspected-flag.md) is Status: Accepted (line 2), not superseded, and README index line 41 lists it with no superseded note. Its Context/Decision/Consequences (lines 7,11,13,15,19,25,26) describe Perplexity + the `ai_search_results` JSONB table + `propagate_ai_flag` + AI_FLAG_CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD as the live mechanism. Reality contradicts this: alembic/versions/20260518_21_drop_ai_search_results.py:20-22 drops the table; grep for `ai_search_results` in src/ returns nothing; `propagate_ai_flag` has zero definitions/callers in src/ (only docs reference it); `src/collector/search_handler.py`, `src/collector/search/`, and any `src/.../perplexity_client.py` do not exist (Perplexity only survives in experiments/ sandboxes). The flag is actually set by `project_ai_suspected` (label_enrichment/repository.py:811-835 and artist_enrichment/repository.py:371), called from the enrichment orchestrators (label_enrichment/orchestrator.py:128, artist_enrichment/orchestrator.py:124), gated on merged.confidence >= threshold with SUSPECTED/CONFIRMED→true, NONE_DETECTED→clear — i.e. ADR-0016's pipeline (docs/adr/0016-label-enrichment.md exists). Reproducible DOCS path: an agent doing AI-flag work follows README→ADR-0008 (Accepted) and hunts for a Perplexity client / ai_search_results table / propagate_ai_flag that were all deleted. One cosmetic inaccuracy: the finding names the new method `_mirror_ai_content`; the actual name is `project_ai_suspected` (finding still cites the correct line range and SQL), so the substance stands. Severity P3 is correct: documentation-only, no runtime/security/correctness impact in shipping code, just a stale non-superseded ADR contradicting current code and a sibling ADR.

title: docs/data/search-and-enrichment.md documents the deleted Perplexity worker, perplexity_client.py and ai_search_results table as live
evidence: search-and-enrichment.md:3 says enrichment uses 'Spotify and Perplexity'; lines 110-232 ('Perplexity label and artist screening') cite `src/collector/search_handler.py` and `src/collector/search/perplexity_client.py`, describe an `EntitySearchMessage` worker, the `ai_search_results` schema/DDL, and `propagate_ai_flag`. None exist: `ls src/collector/search_handler.py src/collector/search/` → no such file; grep for `EntitySearchMessage`/`perplexity_client`/`search_handler` in src/collector → no hits; the `ai_search_results` table was dropped in migration 20260518_21. architecture.md:36 still draws a `Perplexity API` node and :64 says 'Perplexity is used to flag AI-suspected labels'; data-model.md:35 lists `ai_search_results` as a current enrichment table and :120/:160 attribute is_ai_suspected to 'Perplexity search propagation'.
