title: ADR-0019 Decision states YT Music publish uses ytmusicapi, but publish goes through the YouTube Data API v3
evidence: ADR-0019 Decision (lines 36-37) says publish 'Uses `ytmusicapi` authenticated + Google device-flow OAuth'. Actual publish client is `YoutubeDataApiClient` whose module docstring (youtube_data_api_client.py:1-9) explicitly states: "ytmusicapi's OAuth (device-flow) path is broken upstream for write operations ... We therefore publish via the official YouTube Data API v3, reusing the SAME Google OAuth bearer token and `youtube` scope" (base `https://www.googleapis.com/youtube/v3`). `ytmusic_publish_service.py` imports/uses `YoutubeDataApiClient`, not ytmusicapi. Matching (lookup) still correctly uses unauthenticated ytmusicapi per the ADR; only the publish transport drifted.
risk: The ADR misattributes the publish transport. An agent trusting it would debug or extend publish against ytmusicapi's broken OAuth write path — the exact failure mode the code was rewritten to avoid. Device-flow OAuth and the token reuse are still accurate, so the drift is narrow but on the load-bearing 'how do we publish' detail.
recommendation: Amend ADR-0019's Decision bullet (or add a follow-up note/ADR) to state that publish uses the official YouTube Data API v3 (`YoutubeDataApiClient`) over the same Google OAuth token/scope, because ytmusicapi's OAuth write path is broken upstream; ytmusicapi remains only for unauthenticated match search.

votes.reason: Verified the finding against the cited code; it is accurate. ADR-0019's Decision bullet (docs/adr/0019-youtube-music-vendor.md:35-36) states publish "Uses `ytmusicapi` authenticated + Google device-flow OAuth." The actual production publish path does NOT use ytmusicapi for the publish transport: curation_handler.py:1154 calls _build_ytmusic_user_client, which at line 1889 returns a YoutubeDataApiClient (base https://www.googleapis.com/youtube/v3), and that client is injected into YtmusicPublishService at line 1159. The YoutubeDataApiClient module docstring (youtube_data_api_client.py:1-9) explicitly states ytmusicapi's OAuth device-flow write path is broken upstream (HTTP 400 on create/add via OAuth, ytmusicapi #676/#921) and that publish therefore goes through the official YouTube Data API v3 reusing the same Google OAuth bearer token and `youtube` scope. The finding's nuances also check out: device-flow OAuth and token reuse are still accurate (ytmusic_oauth.py uses Google device-flow with scope https://www.googleapis.com/auth/youtube, the same token the Data API client reuses), and matching still correctly uses unauthenticated ytmusicapi (providers/ytmusic/lookup.py:22-24 builds YTMusic() unauthenticated). So the ADR contradiction is real and narrowly scoped to the publish-transport bullet. Severity P3 is appropriate: this is pure documentation drift with no runtime impact, the OAuth scaffolding is still correctly described, and the code's own docstring documents the true transport so a code-reading agent is corrected — but the inaccuracy sits on the load-bearing "how do we publish" detail, justifying a real (low) severity rather than refutation.

Verified accurate. ADR-0019 Decision (docs/adr/0019-youtube-music-vendor.md:35-36) says publish "Uses ytmusicapi authenticated + Google device-flow OAuth." Actual production wiring in src/collector/curation_handler.py:1868,1889 instantiates YoutubeDataApiClient (YouTube Data API v3, base https://www.googleapis.com/youtube/v3), and both youtube_data_api_client.py:1-9 and curation_handler.py:1856-1857 explicitly state publish uses the official Data API "not ytmusicapi" because ytmusicapi's OAuth write path returns HTTP 400. So the ADR genuinely contradicts shipped code on the publish transport — the drift is real and on a meaningful detail.

Not refuted: this is a true doc/code contradiction, not theoretical and not an accepted-behavior carve-out. P3 is correct: the rest of the ADR (device-flow OAuth, token reuse, testing-mode weekly re-connect) stays accurate, so the drift is narrow; and the code is loudly self-documenting (module docstring + handler comment both name the correct transport and the reason), so an agent touching publish lands in code that already warns against the broken ytmusicapi path. Low real-world risk, but a legitimate docs-hygiene fix worth a one-line ADR amendment — exactly P3.

Confirmed accurate, not refuted. ADR-0019 Decision bullet (docs/adr/0019-youtube-music-vendor.md:35-36) states publish "Uses `ytmusicapi` authenticated + Google device-flow OAuth." The real production publish path contradicts this: the route handler `_handle_publish_ytmusic` (src/collector/curation_handler.py:1148-1163) calls `_build_ytmusic_user_client` (curation_handler.py:1868-1892), which constructs and returns a `YoutubeDataApiClient`, NOT ytmusicapi. `YtmusicPublishService` (ytmusic_publish_service.py:159 wiring) receives that client and calls create_playlist/add_items/edit_meta on it. `YoutubeDataApiClient` hits the official YouTube Data API v3 base `https://www.googleapis.com/youtube/v3` (youtube_data_api_client.py:18), and its module docstring (lines 1-9) explicitly states ytmusicapi's OAuth device-flow path is broken upstream for write operations (HTTP 400, issues #676/#921), which is why publish was moved to Data API v3 reusing the same Google OAuth token/youtube scope. Concrete reproducible path: POST publish-to-YT-Music route → `_handle_publish_ytmusic` → `_build_ytmusic_user_client` returns `YoutubeDataApiClient` → service.publish() issues playlists.insert/playlistItems.insert against googleapis.com/youtube/v3. Git history reinforces the drift: the Data-API-v3 client was committed 2026-05-31, three days BEFORE ADR-0019 was authored (2026-06-04), so the ADR documented the superseded spec intent rather than the already-implemented reality. The finding correctly scopes the drift to publish transport only (matching via unauthenticated ytmusicapi, device-flow OAuth, and token reuse remain accurate). The risk is real: an agent trusting the ADR would debug/extend publish against ytmusicapi's broken OAuth write path — the exact failure the code was rewritten to avoid. P3 (DOCS, doc-only, no runtime impact, but load-bearing 'how do we publish' detail) is the correct severity.

title: CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 claims VITE_API_BASE_URL drives frontend API requests; it is dead at runtime
where: CLAUDE.md gotcha #9 (and the dev-setup command block); contradicted by frontend/src/api/client.ts:5-6 and frontend/src/lib/env.ts
evidence: Gotcha #9 says `pnpm dev` 'Requires `frontend/.env.local` with `VITE_API_BASE_URL=$(cd infra && terraform output -raw api_endpoint)`', and gotcha #8 / docs imply the SPA reads it. But api/client.ts:5-6 sets `baseUrl = window.location.origin` (same-origin), never reading the env var. The only non-test references to `VITE_API_BASE_URL` are the type decl (vite-env.d.ts:4) and `lib/env.ts`, whose `parseEnv` is imported by nothing in runtime code (grep for importers returns only its own definition; sole caller is lib/__tests__/env.test.ts). So requests ignore VITE_API_BASE_URL entirely.
risk: A developer/agent following the gotcha will set VITE_API_BASE_URL expecting it to point the SPA at a remote API, but the dev server only ever calls same-origin — leading to confusing 'requests go to localhost:5173 not the API' debugging. The documented requirement is false.
recommendation: Update gotcha #9 to state the runtime client uses `window.location.origin` (same-origin via the Vite/CloudFront proxy) and that `VITE_API_BASE_URL` is currently unused at runtime (only validated in tests); fix the dev-setup instructions accordingly, or wire the env var into client.ts if same-origin dev is not actually intended.

votes.reason: The finding's central claim — that VITE_API_BASE_URL is "dead at runtime" and "requests ignore VITE_API_BASE_URL entirely" — is false. The finder grepped only frontend/src ("The only non-test references...are the type decl and lib/env.ts"), which excluded frontend/vite.config.ts, where VITE_API_BASE_URL is the dev-server proxy target: `const target = env.VITE_API_BASE_URL ?? ''` and `proxy: target ? proxy : undefined`. The mechanism: client.ts:5-6 sets baseUrl = window.location.origin (during pnpm dev that's 127.0.0.1:5173), so api('/tracks') hits 127.0.0.1:5173/tracks same-origin; vite.config.ts then proxies /tracks, /auth/login, etc. to VITE_API_BASE_URL (the real API Gateway). If VITE_API_BASE_URL is unset, there is NO proxy (`target ? proxy : undefined`) and the SPA reaches nothing. Therefore gotcha #9's requirement to set frontend/.env.local with VITE_API_BASE_URL is correct and load-bearing, not false. The stated risk is also backwards: setting the var is precisely what routes the localhost:5173 same-origin requests to the remote API. The var also appears in frontend/.env.example and frontend/README.md. The only true sub-point — lib/env.ts parseEnv is consumed only by tests — is a minor unused-validation-helper observation, not evidence the env var is unused at runtime, and does not make the gotcha inaccurate.

The finding's central premise is false. It claims VITE_API_BASE_URL is "dead at runtime" and gotcha #9's dev-setup requirement is wrong, but it only inspected frontend/src/ and missed the actual consumer: frontend/vite.config.ts:31-32 reads VITE_API_BASE_URL via loadEnv and uses it as the dev-server proxy target (vite.config.ts:48-58). client.ts:5-6 correctly uses window.location.origin so the SPA makes same-origin requests to localhost:5173, and the Vite proxy forwards /auth, /me, /tracks, /categories, etc. to the remote API GW — the standard same-origin-proxy pattern that also makes cookie-based auth (credentials:'include', cookieDomainRewrite:'') work. Crucially, vite.config.ts:58 sets `proxy: target ? proxy : undefined`, so WITHOUT VITE_API_BASE_URL the proxy is disabled and dev API calls go nowhere. Thus gotcha #9's requirement is correct and load-bearing, not "false." frontend/.env.example even documents this ("Vite proxy forwards /auth, /me, /categories..."). The only true sub-point — that lib/env.ts parseEnv is test-only dead code — is a separate trivial observation unrelated to what gotcha #9 documents; gotcha #9 never claims parseEnv reads the value. The recommendation to "wire the env var into client.ts" would actively break the same-origin cookie auth flow. No doc change is warranted, and the proposed fix is harmful.

Refuted. The finding's central claim ("requests ignore VITE_API_BASE_URL entirely", "dead at runtime") is false. The evidence section enumerated VITE_API_BASE_URL references but MISSED frontend/vite.config.ts:32, where `const target = env.VITE_API_BASE_URL ?? ''` feeds the Vite dev-server proxy (server.proxy, lines 48-58). The actual mechanism: client.ts:5-6 sets baseUrl=window.location.origin so the browser fires SAME-ORIGIN requests (e.g. http://127.0.0.1:5173/tracks); the Vite dev server then proxies every backend prefix (BACKEND_ONLY_PREFIXES/SPA_AWARE_PREFIXES) to target=VITE_API_BASE_URL. Crucially, vite.config.ts:58 is `proxy: target ? proxy : undefined` — if VITE_API_BASE_URL is unset, target='' and the proxy is DISABLED, so API calls 404 against the dev server. That is precisely the failure the gotcha's requirement prevents, making the documented `.env.local` requirement correct and load-bearing, not false. README.md:117-121 confirms this is intentional ("The Vite proxy forwards every API request to ${VITE_API_BASE_URL}"). The finding inverted the design: same-origin in client.ts is the front half of a same-origin-plus-proxy setup, not proof the var is unused. The only true sub-claim — that lib/env.ts:parseEnv is test-only dead code — is a separate trivial observation that does not support the headline. Adopting the recommendation would make CLAUDE.md less accurate and could lead an agent to drop the .env.local requirement, breaking local dev.

title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in triage_repository
evidence: bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) flags 14 hardcoded SQL expressions built via string formatting in this file
risk: If any interpolated fragment carries user input not passed as a Data API parameter, SQL injection / tenant-isolation bypass is possible
recommendation: Verify every flagged statement passes user-controlled values as Data API named parameters; only static identifiers may be interpolated

votes.reason: Verified every f-string SQL site in src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py. Bandit B608 does fire here (the f-strings are real), but the finding's risk condition — an interpolated fragment carrying user input not passed as a Data API parameter — holds nowhere. All dynamic interpolation is one of two safe forms: (1) loop-index-based placeholder NAMES only (e.g. select_placeholders = ", ".join(f":t{i}" ...) at lines 443/559; tech/staging VALUES rows f"(:tid_{i}, :block_id, ...)" at 205/244; insert rows f"(:to_id, :t{i}, :now)" at 476/583) — values are never concatenated; or (2) fixed static SQL literal fragments toggled by internal booleans (sql_filter = " AND tb.status = :status" at 867/950; search_clause = " AND t.normalized_title ILIKE :search" at 1055; the classify CASE WHEN fav_when/disliked_when/compilation_when fragments at 264-359). Every user-controlled value — user_id, style_id, status, all track_ids, and the only free-text input `search` (bound as params["search"] = term at line 1054, with `term` never reaching the SQL string) — is passed via the params dict, which DataAPIClient.execute (data_api.py lines 41-44) converts into typed, out-of-band `parameters` on execute_statement, separate from the `sql` text. There is no injectable site and no tenant-isolation bypass. The recommendation ("verify user values are passed as named parameters") is already satisfied everywhere. This is a classic B608 false positive (placeholder-name interpolation / static-fragment toggling that the rule cannot distinguish from value concatenation). Real exploitable security risk is zero; appropriate handling is a `# nosec B608` annotation, not a security fix.

Refuted. I inspected all 14 bandit B608 f-string SQL sites in src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py and performed exactly the verification the finding recommends. Every interpolated fragment is either (a) a parameter-placeholder NAME with a loop index (e.g. f":t{i}", f"(:tid_{i}, :block_id, :btype_{i}, NULL, FALSE, :now)") whose actual values are bound via Data API named params, or (b) a static SQL fragment built only from hard-coded literals / module constants (fav_when, disliked_when, compilation_when at lines 281-340; sql_filter = " AND tb.status = :status" at lines 867/950; search_clause = " AND t.normalized_title ILIKE :search" at line 1055). The only two user-controlled inputs reaching these dynamic queries — the search term and the status filter — are both bound as named parameters (:search at line 1054, :status), never concatenated into SQL text. TECHNICAL_BUCKET_TYPES is a tuple of static constants, and even those are bound as :btype_{i} params rather than interpolated. The finding's own guard condition ("IF any interpolated fragment carries user input not passed as a parameter") is unmet at every site, so no SQL-injection or tenant-isolation-bypass vector exists. These are textbook bandit false positives (bandit cannot tell placeholder-name f-strings from value-bearing ones). At most this is a linter-suppression/hygiene note, not a security issue.

No reachable SQL-injection path exists; the finding's alleged risk is a false positive of bandit B608's known limitation (it flags any f-string SQL regardless of whether user data is interpolated). I traced all 14 flagged f-strings in src/collector/curation/triage_repository.py. Every interpolated fragment is one of: (1) loop-index-derived bind-parameter tokens like :t{i}, :tid_{i}, :cid_{i}, :sid_{i}, :btype_{i} where i ranges over range(len(...))/enumerate(...) — integers, never user strings (lines 205,244,443,476,559,583); or (2) static, hardcoded SQL fragments selected by boolean toggles — fav_when/disliked_when/compilation_when (lines 264-375), sql_filter=" AND tb.status = :status" (lines 867,950), search_clause=" AND t.normalized_title ILIKE :search" (line 1055). No user-controlled value ever enters the SQL string body.

Concrete adversarial path traced: GET /triage/blocks/{id}/buckets/{bucket_id}/tracks?search=<payload> is the only route with free-text reaching this file. Handler curation_handler.py:1421 passes search to list_bucket_tracks; line 1053-1055 wraps it as term="%"+search.strip().lower()+"%" and stores it in params["search"], while the f-string interpolates only the constant search_clause containing the :search placeholder. A payload like "'; DROP TABLE--" lands as a bound parameter value, not SQL text.

Structural backstop confirmed in data_api.py:41-44: DataAPIClient.execute() passes params via the RDS Data API "parameters" field (named params bound server-side), never via string interpolation. track_ids/bucket_ids (schemas.py:63-83) are likewise all bound as :t{i}/:from_id/:to_id/:tgt_id. The (f-string) error-message lines 155,461,517,540,578,628,663,856,1043 are exception text, not SQL.

The finding's own recommended invariant ("user-controlled values passed as named parameters; only static identifiers interpolated") is already fully satisfied by the code as written. There is no defect to fix.

title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in repositories.py
evidence: bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) flags 11 hardcoded SQL expressions built via string formatting in this file
risk: User input interpolated into SQL rather than passed as a Data API parameter would allow injection
recommendation: Confirm all dynamic values use Data API parameters; restrict interpolation to whitelisted static identifiers/ORDER BY columns

votes.reason: The security claim is refuted: no user input reaches the SQL string. I read all 11 B608 sites in src/collector/repositories.py (confirmed exactly 11 f-string SQL sites via grep, matching the finding's count). Every interpolated fragment is code-controlled: `{where}`/`{where_extra}` are static string literals (e.g. line 872 `where = "WHERE t.normalized_title LIKE :search"`), and `{placeholders}` (line 846) is `", ".join(f":id_{i}" for i in range(len(track_ids)))` — generated bind-parameter NAMES, never user content. The user-controlled `search` (from `query_params.get("search")`, handler.py:854) flows only into `params["search"] = f"%{search.lower()}%"` (line 764 etc.), which is the VALUE dict, not the SQL text. data_api.py:28-49 `DataAPIClient.execute` passes `params` as the RDS Data API `parameters` field — genuine bound parameters transmitted separately from `sql`. So injection cannot occur; the finding's risk is conditional ("user input interpolated... would allow injection") and that condition is false. The finding's own recommendation ("confirm all dynamic values use Data API parameters") resolves to a no-op — they all do. What remains is a true-but-benign bandit false-positive signal, worth at most a nosec annotation, not a P2 data-access security risk.

Verified all 11 B608 sites in src/collector/repositories.py. Every f-string SQL interpolates ONLY static, code-controlled fragments: `{where}`/`{where_extra}` are literal strings the code itself assigns (e.g. "WHERE t.normalized_title LIKE :search"), and `{placeholders}` is built from range(len(track_ids)) yielding `:id_0, :id_1, ...` — independent of value content. All user-controlled values (search, track_ids, limit, offset) are passed via the params dict and bound server-side as RDS Data API named parameters by data_api.py's execute()/batch_execute() (_to_parameter -> request["parameters"]), never interpolated into SQL text. Grep for dynamic ORDER BY column / table / sort-direction / sort_by / order_by interpolation from input returned nothing — no such pattern exists. The finding's own recommendation (use Data API parameters; restrict interpolation to static identifiers) is already fully satisfied. The injection risk described does not occur; these are classic bandit false positives (it flags any f-string passed as a SQL arg regardless of what is interpolated). No plausible trigger for the claimed vulnerability. Refuted as actionable.

Refuted: no reachable SQL injection. I traced every f-string SQL site in src/collector/repositories.py and the concrete route GET /tracks?search=<x> → handler.py _parse_pagination_params → repository.list_tracks → execute. In all 10 list/count methods, the interpolated `{where}`/`{where_extra}` variable is assigned ONLY from hardcoded string literals (e.g. "WHERE t.normalized_title LIKE :search"). The user-controlled `search` value is placed into params["search"] = f"%{search.lower()}%" and bound as an RDS Data API parameter via DataAPIClient.execute → _to_parameter (request["parameters"]); it is never concatenated into the SQL text. The 11th site, propagate_release_type_to_albums, interpolates `placeholders` = ", ".join(f":id_{i}" for i in range(len(track_ids))) — parameter placeholder NAMES derived from integer range(), with values bound via params[f"id_{i}"]; the ids are internal clouder track UUIDs from just-upserted rows (spotify_handler.py), not raw user input. There is no path where user input reaches interpolated SQL, so injection is not constructible. This is a textbook bandit B608 false positive (B608 flags any f-string containing SQL irrespective of taint). The code already satisfies the finding's own recommendation (interpolation restricted to static fragments / generated placeholders), so no change is warranted. The finding asserts an exploitable injection (P2 SEC) that does not exist; at most this is lint noise.

title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in label_enrichment repository
evidence: bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) flags 7 hardcoded SQL expressions in this file
risk: Injection if interpolated fragments include user/vendor-controlled input not parameterized
recommendation: Audit each statement; parameterize all dynamic values via the Data API

votes.reason:
Refuted on correctness of the claimed risk. The finding's count is accurate (bandit B608 flags the 7 f-string SQL execute calls at lines 104, 120, 223, 271, 359, 406, 447), but the asserted risk "injection if interpolated fragments include user/vendor-controlled input not parameterized" does not hold for any of them. I traced every interpolation source:

1. get_labels_by_ids (L105) / derive_styles_for_labels (L132): the interpolated `placeholders` is `", ".join(f":t{i}" ...)` built from a range() integer counter — these are bind-placeholder NAMES (:t0, :t1, ...), not values. The actual label_id values are passed in the `params` dict as bound parameters.
2. list_labels (L224, L272): interpolates `ctes` (built from module constant `_STYLE_SLUG_EXPR`), `where_sql` (`" AND ".join(where)` where every `where` entry is a hardcoded SQL literal — L170-171,175,184,186,188), and `order_by` (a boolean branch between two constant strings). User inputs `style`/`q` are bound as `:style`/`:q`. `sort` is enum-validated at route L373; `my` is validated at route L376 and in repo L163-164.
3. list_backlog (L360, L407): interpolates `ctes`, static `where` literals, and `stale_clause`. `stale_clause` interpolates `str(int(staleness_days))` — integer-coerced AND not caller-exposed (route L264 never passes staleness_days; default 180). `status` is matched against literal branches and validated at route to _BACKLOG_STATUSES; cursor parts are int()-coerced / bound as :cur_count, :cur_id.
4. list_runs (L448): `where` entries are static literals (`"status = :status"` etc.); values bound.

No user- or vendor-controlled string is ever interpolated into SQL text — all dynamic values traverse the params dict as bound Data API parameters. This is the well-known B608 false-positive pattern (interpolating placeholder names/static fragments). No exploitable injection exists, so as a P2 security finding it is refuted; at most it is scanner noise / code-hygiene.

Verified all 7 bandit B608 sites in src/collector/label_enrichment/repository.py are false positives. The f-string interpolations are exclusively: (1) generated bind-parameter NAMES, not values — `placeholders = ", ".join(f":t{i}" ...)` from a loop index (lines 102, 118), with the actual values bound via `params` dict; (2) a module-level constant SQL literal `_STYLE_SLUG_EXPR` (lines 208, 345) and the `ctes` block built from it; (3) static WHERE-clause strings joined with `' AND '` (lines 239, 279, 375, 412, 453) where every dynamic value is a `:param` placeholder (`:style`, `:q`, `:cur_count`, etc.); (4) `order_by` chosen between two hardcoded constants (line 240); (5) `stale_clause` interpolating `str(int(staleness_days))` — `int()`-coerced, so non-numeric input raises before reaching SQL (lines 304-306). No user- or vendor-controlled STRING is ever concatenated into SQL text; all data flows through the RDS Data API's `:name` parameter binding. The finding's own risk premise ("if interpolated fragments include user/vendor-controlled input not parameterized") is not met anywhere in the file. Defense-in-depth confirms no MVP impact: the fragment-selecting inputs are all whitelist-validated at the route boundary (routes.py: `sort` ∈ {name,recent} line 373-374; `status` ∈ _BACKLOG_STATUSES line 251; `my` validated in list_labels line 163-164), and `staleness_days` is not route-reachable (default 180). No injection vector exists.

Bandit B608 false positive. I traced all 7 f-string SQL sites in src/collector/label_enrichment/repository.py (lines 105, 121, 224, 272, 360, 407, 448 — AST-confirmed as exactly the execute() first-arg f-strings, matching the "7 expressions" claim) and found no reachable injection path.

Every dynamic VALUE reaches Postgres through Data API bind parameters (:name in the params dict), never via string interpolation:
- Lines 105/121 interpolate `placeholders` = ", ".join(f":t{i}") — generated bind-parameter NAMES from a range index, not values; the values go in params={f"t{i}": v}.
- Lines 224/272/360/407 interpolate `ctes` (built from module-level constant _STYLE_SLUG_EXPR), `where_sql`/`order_by`, and joined `where` lists — all of whose elements are hardcoded literal clause strings (e.g. "LOWER(lbl.name) LIKE :q", "status = :status"). Request values (style, q, status, source, user_id, cursor fields) are bound via params.
- Line 448 (list_runs) joins only literal clause strings.
- Discriminators sort/my/status select between hardcoded literal clauses in Python (if sort=="recent", if my=="liked"); the raw strings are never interpolated, and sort/my are allowlist-validated in routes.py:373-374 and repository.py:163-164.

The ONLY literally-concatenated scalar is staleness_days at line 305: "... INTERVAL '" + str(int(staleness_days)) + " days'". This is injection-proof two ways: (a) str(int(...)) coercion raises on any non-numeric input, and (b) grep confirms no caller ever passes staleness_days — list_backlog is invoked with the default 180 (routes.py:264-266), with no request binding. No route/queue message can supply it.

Conclusion: no constructible path from any route or queue message to SQL injection. The code is already parameterized exactly as the finding's own recommendation prescribes. The risk premise ("interpolated fragments include user/vendor-controlled input not parameterized") is false for all 7 sites.

title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in tags_repository
evidence: bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) flags 7 hardcoded SQL expressions in this file
risk: Injection / cross-tenant access if user-supplied tag data is interpolated into SQL
recommendation: Parameterize all dynamic values; verify user_id predicates are present

votes.reason: False positive. I read every f-string SQL expression in src/collector/curation/tags_repository.py (lines 112-119, 128, 175-179, 239, 310-313, 387-393, 421-433). In all 7 cases the interpolated content is either (a) hardcoded SQL literal fragments — search_clause/count_clause are the constant " AND normalized_name LIKE :search ", and the `sets`/`value_clauses` lists are built only from string constants like "name = :name" and "color = :color" — or (b) index-derived placeholder NAMES (f":tg{i}", f":t{i}" where i comes from range()/enumerate()). No user-supplied VALUE is ever concatenated into SQL text. All values (tag names, ids, search term, color) flow through the `params` dict and are bound server-side as typed RDS Data API parameters — confirmed in data_api.py:execute (sql and parameters sent as separate fields) and _to_parameter/_to_field (lines 28-49, 123-162), which emit structured {name, value} parameter objects, not concatenation. The search value is :search-bound (line 111/126), not interpolated. The secondary 'cross-tenant access' risk is also unfounded: every query carries a user_id predicate (lines 116, 128, 140, 177, 196, 213, 239, 260, 293/311, 345, 371, 391, 423/429), matching the module's tenancy docstring. The recommendation is already fully satisfied by the existing code. B608 fires on any f-string in a SQL string regardless of whether values are interpolated; this is textbook scanner noise, not a real injection or tenancy defect. At most it's a P3 hygiene/suppression note, not a P2 SEC issue.

All 7 bandit B608 flags in src/collector/curation/tags_repository.py are false positives. I read the full file and verified DataAPIClient.execute (src/collector/data_api.py) passes params to the RDS Data API as named parameter bindings (server-side bound), not string substitution. Every f-string SQL site interpolates ONLY non-user data: (a) hardcoded SQL literal fragments — search_clause/count_clause are constant strings "AND normalized_name LIKE :search", the rename_tag `sets` list items are constants like "name = :name"; or (b) index-based placeholder names generated from range(len(...)) integer counters — :tg0/:t0 etc. No user-supplied value (tag name, search string, tag id, track id) is ever placed into SQL text; all such values are bound via :param placeholders. The finding's risk premise ("if user-supplied tag data is interpolated into SQL") never occurs. The cross-tenant concern is also refuted: every query carries a WHERE user_id = :user_id (or c.user_id/tt.user_id) predicate bound as a parameter — the file docstring's tenancy invariant holds at every call site. The recommendation is already fully satisfied by the existing code. No injection or cross-tenant trigger exists, even theoretically, so this does not matter for the MVP.

No injection or cross-tenant path is constructible from real code; all 7 bandit B608 hits are false positives.

Mechanism: src/collector/data_api.py DataAPIClient.execute() passes every value through the RDS Data API `parameters` list (`:name` named binds, _to_parameter/_to_field), so values are NEVER concatenated into SQL text. The 7 f-strings in tags_repository.py interpolate ONLY structural tokens, never user values:
- list_tags (L113) / count (L128): interpolate the CONSTANT clause `" AND normalized_name LIKE :search "`; the user search term is bound via `:search` (params["search"], L111).
- rename_tag (L175): interpolates `", ".join(sets)` where `sets` is a closed allowlist of fixed fragments (`updated_at=:updated_at`, `name=:name`, `normalized_name=:normalized_name`, `color=:color`); all values bound.
- _assert_tag_ids_owned (L239), set_track_tags INSERT (L310), list_tags_for_tracks (L387), cleanup_orphaned_track_tags (L421): interpolate placeholder strings (`:tg0,:tg1,...` / `:t0,:t1,...`) generated purely from range(len(list)); the tag/track id strings are bound via params[f"tgN"]/params[f"tN"].

Traced concrete path: PUT /tracks/{track_id}/tags -> _handle_set_track_tags (curation_handler.py:1709) validates tag_ids (list of non-empty str, count<=MAX, no dupes, L1716-1725) -> set_track_tags. A malicious tag_id like `x'); DROP TABLE user_tags;--` is transported as a stringValue parameter, not interpolated; the f-string only inserts the loop index integer. No injection.

Cross-tenant: every site includes `user_id = :user_id` in WHERE/INSERT (L116,128,140,177,196,213,239,260,293,311/345,371,391,423/429), plus _assert_tag_ids_owned and _assert_track_in_any_active_category ownership guards before writes. The finding's own recommendation ("verify user_id predicates present") is already satisfied throughout.

This is the standard B608 static heuristic false positive (flags f-string near SQL keyword regardless of value-vs-placeholder). Correct level is informational lint-noise, not a P2 security finding.

title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in artist_enrichment repository
evidence: bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) flags 6 hardcoded SQL expressions in this file
risk: Injection if interpolated fragments include unparameterized input
recommendation: Audit and parameterize all dynamic values via the Data API

votes.reason: Verified by reading repository.py and tracing every f-string/concatenated SQL site (lines 102, 512, 738/865 _STYLE_SLUG_EXPR, 752/799/878/925 ctes, 767/806/893 where_sql, 768 order_by, 830/844/846/885 stale_clause) plus the route handlers in routes.py. The descriptive claim is correct: the file builds SQL via f-strings/concatenation, and bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) fires on exactly this pattern, so the scanner signal and file location are accurate (could not re-run bandit — not installed in the venv — but the trip-pattern is unambiguously present). However, the security risk is NOT realized. Every interpolated fragment is an internal constant (_STYLE_SLUG_EXPR, ctes), index-generated placeholders (f':t{i}' with values bound in params), a static where-list entry using bound placeholders (:style, :q, :status, :source, :cur_*), a ternary between two hardcoded order_by strings selected by a whitelisted sort value, or stale_clause = str(int(staleness_days)) which is integer-cast AND never passed by any caller (routes.py:253 and label routes.py:264 both omit it; default 180). ALL user-controlled values (style, q, status, my, sort, limit, page, cursor, ids) reach the DB exclusively via the Data API's bound params dict, and routes additionally whitelist enum inputs. The finding even hedges with 'if interpolated fragments include unparameterized input' — and none do. So it is a true-positive lint signal but a false-positive vulnerability. Not refuted on the literal factual claim, but P2 (SEC) overstates a non-exploitable pattern; correct level is P3 informational/defense-in-depth.

False positive. I read the entire file (src/collector/artist_enrichment/repository.py) and traced every dynamic SQL site bandit B608 flags. None interpolates unparameterized user input. Every data value — status, source, style, q, cursor, my, user_id, artist_ids, limit, page — is passed as a bound :name parameter, never string-formatted into SQL. The interpolated f-string fragments are only: (1) the module-level constant _STYLE_SLUG_EXPR; (2) static WHERE-clause string literals assembled from a Python list (e.g. "status = :status", "source = :source"); (3) bind-variable NAMES generated from loop counters (f":t{i}" in get_artists_by_ids); and (4) order_by/where_sql/ctes built only from those static pieces (order_by is selected via == comparison, not interpolation). The sole numeric interpolation, stale_clause at line 830, embeds str(int(staleness_days)): int() coercion makes injection impossible (a malicious string raises ValueError), and staleness_days is never passed by either route caller — both use the hardcoded default 180 (verified: src/collector/artist_enrichment/routes.py:253-255 and label_enrichment/routes.py omit the arg). So there is no attacker-reachable path and no unparameterized value. The recommendation ('parameterize all dynamic values') is already fully satisfied. B608 is a well-known noisy check that fires on any f-string-built SQL regardless of taint; this is a baseline-suppressible FP with no plausible trigger for this MVP.

Traced every interpolated SQL fragment in src/collector/artist_enrichment/repository.py against its callers in artist_enrichment/routes.py. No reachable path puts unparameterized user input into interpolated SQL text:

1) get_artists_by_ids (L102): {placeholders} are machine-generated ":tN" names from range(len(chunk)); the artist_id values are bound via params. Safe.
2) list_runs (L511): {' AND '.join(where)} joins only static literal fragments ("status = :status", "source = :source", the cursor tuple-compare). status/source are additionally validated against allowlists in routes.py (L340, L350); all values bound. Safe.
3) list_artists (L751/L798): {ctes} is built from module constant _STYLE_SLUG_EXPR; {where_sql} is static fragments; {order_by} is one of two hardcoded strings chosen by `sort` which routes.py validates to name|recent (L362). style/q/user_id all bound. Safe.
4) list_backlog (L877/L924): {ctes} constant; {where} joins static clauses chosen by `status` validated against _BACKLOG_STATUSES (routes.py L240); {stale_clause} interpolates str(int(staleness_days)). staleness_days is the only truly interpolated dynamic value, but the sole caller (routes.py L253) never passes it (defaults to 180), and it is int-coerced so any non-numeric input raises ValueError before reaching SQL. Not caller-controlled and not injectable.

B608 here is MEDIUM confidence and is a textbook false positive: f-strings assembling hardcoded SQL constants with bound ":param" placeholders. The asserted P2 SEC injection vulnerability is not reachable from any route/queue/input. At most this is informational SQL-hygiene (P3); it is not a real security defect.

title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in playlists_repository

votes.reason:
Refuted as stated. I read all 5 B608-flagged sites in src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py and the binding layer in src/collector/data_api.py. The finding's risk — "Injection / IDOR if playlist ids or names are interpolated rather than parameterized" — does not hold. In every flagged site the ONLY interpolated content is (a) fixed placeholder tokens generated from range(), e.g. ", ".join(f":t{i}" for i in range(len(track_ids))) producing :t0, :t1, ... (lines 446, 836-841, 920, 969), or (b) hardcoded literal clause fragments like " AND p.status = :status" (lines 292, 304). No track id, playlist id, name, or any user-supplied value ever reaches the SQL string — all such values go into the params dict and are bound by the RDS Data API as a separate parameters array (data_api.py lines 41-44, _to_parameter), i.e. genuine parameterized binding, not string interpolation. So injection is unreachable. IDOR is also covered: every owner-scoped method filters user_id = :user_id (get/list_all/patch/soft_delete/append_tracks/remove_track/reorder_tracks/list_tracks/set_cover/clear_cover/set_publish_state/set_ytmusic_publish_state), and validate_tracks_in_scope enforces c.user_id/p.user_id/uit.user_id = :user_id. The dynamic-placeholder pattern is the documented workaround for the Data API rejecting Python lists as array params (comments lines 77-81, 444-445). Classic bandit B608 false positive for safe parametric IN-lists; the recommendation is already satisfied. Not a P2; at most a P3 lint-suppression note.</parameter>
</invoke>

Refuted as a real issue. I read src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py in full and inspected every dynamic-SQL site bandit B608 flags (lines ~270/296/308 in get/list_all, 452 in append_tracks, 841 in validate_tracks_in_scope, 925 in fetch_unmatched_match_inputs, 975/983 in fetch_ytmusic_status). In ALL cases the only thing interpolated into the SQL text is either (a) a sequence of bind-placeholder tokens ":t0, :t1, ..." produced by `", ".join(f":t{i}" for i in range(len(track_ids)))` — i.e. integer loop indices, never the track-id values — or (b) a static literal clause chosen by an if-branch (status_clause/count_clause are fixed strings), or (c) non-SQL strings (exception messages, a YouTube URL). The actual user-supplied values (track ids, names, user_id, status, limit, offset) are ALWAYS passed via the Data API parameter map as bound :params. So SQL injection is not reachable. The IDOR half is also unfounded: every CRUD/owner query carries `AND user_id = :user_id AND deleted_at IS NULL` as a bound param (lines 219, 271, 296, 308, 359, 399, 419, 509, 552, 599, 726, 750, 783, 814), all track-mutating methods run an explicit owner-check before touching playlist_tracks, and the scope-check template enforces user_id on all three EXISTS branches. The parametric IN-list is a documented necessity (Aurora Data API rejects Python lists as text[] arrays, lines 77-81). This is the canonical B608 false positive: it cannot distinguish a generated placeholder list from value interpolation. The finding's own recommendation ("parameterize values; verify user_id scoping") is already fully implemented. No plausible trigger, no exposure for the MVP. Severity should drop accordingly.

Refuted: no injection or IDOR path can be constructed from real code. I enumerated all 5 dynamic-SQL sites in src/collector/curation/playlists_repository.py (lines 296/308 list_all, 452 append_tracks, 841 validate_tracks_in_scope, 925/941 fetch_unmatched_match_inputs, 975/988 fetch_ytmusic_status). Every f-string/.format() interpolates ONLY (a) auto-generated bind-parameter NAMES `:t{i}` derived from `range(len(track_ids))` (the integer index, never the value), or (b) a fixed string literal clause (`status_clause`/`count_clause` = " AND p.status = :status"). The actual values — track ids, playlist id, name, status — are ALWAYS passed as named bind parameters: `params[f"t{i}"]=tid`, and DataAPIClient.execute (src/collector/data_api.py:42-44) forwards them via `_to_parameter` into the RDS Data API `parameters` array, where the server substitutes them (no client-side string concatenation of values). Concrete path trace for the only scalar clause: GET playlists list route -> curation_handler.py:798-802 reads `status` from query params, allowlist-validates it (`status not in ("active","completed") -> ValidationError`), then passes it to list_all where it is bound as `:status`, not interpolated. No playlist id or name is ever concatenated into SQL anywhere in the file. IDOR is independently mitigated: every method includes `AND user_id = :user_id`/owner-check in WHERE. The scanner OUTPUT is real (B608 is a textual heuristic that fires on any f-string-built SQL regardless of taint), but the asserted SEC risk is a textbook false positive — there is no exploitable injection or IDOR. Verified the actual code at all three bandit B608 sites in src/collector/label_enrichment/auto_repository.py (claim_labels lines 132-176, attach_run lines 195-204). title: bandit: hardcoded SQL string construction in categories_repository
evidence: bandit B608 (MEDIUM/MEDIUM) flags 2 hardcoded SQL expressions in this file
risk: Injection if interpolated fragments include unparameterized input
recommendation: Parameterize all dynamic values via the Data API

votes.reason:
Read the cited file (src/collector/curation/categories_repository.py) and traced every f-string SQL interpolation. None inject unparameterized user input, so the finding's own stated risk condition ("Injection if interpolated fragments include unparameterized input") is false.

The only SQL f-string interpolations are:

- order_by (line 741, used at 777): built from column = _SORT_COLUMNS[sort] (line 738) and direction = _ORDER_DIRS[order] (line 739). These are whitelist dict lookups returning hardcoded literals ("t.title", "ct.added_at", "ASC", "DESC"); any unknown value raises KeyError. The handler (curation_handler.py:669-676) additionally pre-validates sort/order against _SORT_VALUES/_ORDER_VALUES before the call.
- search_clause/tag_clause/fresh_clause (lines 773-775, 830-832): static literal clause strings; the user values inside them (search, tag ids, tag_count, user_id) are all bound as named params (:search, :tag0, :tag_count, :user_id).
- Line 529 (f"Track(s) not found: {missing[0]}") is an error message, not SQL.

Bandit B608 is a syntactic heuristic that cannot tell that the interpolated fragments are parameter placeholders / whitelisted identifiers, so these are known false positives. The recommendation "parameterize all dynamic values" is already satisfied — every user-controlled VALUE flows through Data API named-parameter binding; only fixed identifiers/placeholder-names are interpolated, which is the correct and safe pattern (identifiers cannot be parameterized in SQL). No injection vector exists.

(Note: bandit itself is not installed in this env — verified by reading the code directly rather than re-running the scanner, which is the authoritative check for this correctness claim.)

Refuted as a real vulnerability — bandit B608 here is a false positive. I traced every f-string SQL construction bandit flags in categories_repository.py (lines 521, 556, 705, 715, 743, 796, 806, 825). In all of them, the only interpolated tokens are: (a) generated named-parameter placeholders (e.g. `placeholders = ", ".join(f":t{i}" ...)`, `value_rows = f"(:category_id, :tid_{i}, ...)"`, `tag_placeholders = f":tag{i}"`) whose actual values are bound through the Data API params dict, and (b) fixed string-literal clause fragments (search_clause/tag_clause/fresh_clause). The one place a non-placeholder identifier is interpolated is `order_by = f"{column} {direction}{nulls}"` (line 741), where `column = _SORT_COLUMNS[sort]` and `direction = _ORDER_DIRS[order]` are DICT LOOKUPS — an unexpected key raises KeyError, it cannot inject. Defense-in-depth: the sole HTTP caller `_handle_list_tracks` in curation_handler.py (lines 669-676) validates `sort` against the allowlist {"title","spotify_release_date","added_at"} and `order` against {"asc","desc"} BEFORE calling the repo, raising BadQueryParamError otherwise. `search` is bound as a parameter (`%...%`), never interpolated. No user-controlled value ever reaches SQL text unparameterized; there is no plausible injection trigger. The finding's "2 expressions" count is also off (bandit flags 8), but that's immaterial. The recommendation ("parameterize all dynamic values") is already fully satisfied. For this MVP this is non-actionable noise.

Traced every concrete path. Inside the repo there is a second gate: column=_SORT_COLUMNS[sort] and direction=_ORDER_DIRS[order] are dict lookups (categories_repository.py:738-739) that KeyError on anything unmapped, so only the hardcoded literals 't.title'/'ct.added_at'/'ASC'/'DESC' can ever reach the SQL text. (e.g. :t0, :tagN, :tid_N); the actual track_id/tag_id/search values are passed as Data API bound parameters, never concatenated into SQL. search is bound as :search. The categories repo's list_tracks has no caller other than this validated handler (the publish services call the separate playlists_repository.list_tracks).
