title: bandit: urllib urlopen with audited scheme in vendor/oauth clients
evidence: bandit B310 (MEDIUM/HIGH) at spotify_client.py:411,449; beatport_client.py:113; auth/spotify_oauth.py:57; auth/ytmusic_oauth.py:74 — urllib.urlopen permits file:/ftp: schemes
risk: If any opened URL is influenced by external input, SSRF or local-file read could result; low risk if URLs are static vendor endpoints
recommendation: Confirm all URLs are constructed from static vendor bases; prefer requests/httpx or validate scheme is https

votes.reason:
Verified all five cited urlopen call sites. Every URL is a static hardcoded https constant or a config-supplied base, and no scheme/host is ever derived from external input — so the SSRF/local-file (file:/ftp:) risk the finding warns about does not exist:
- spotify_client.py:411 → url=TOKEN_URL = "https://accounts.spotify.com/api/token" (module constant, line 22).
- spotify_client.py:449 → _request(url) where url = f"{API_BASE_URL}/search?..." and API_BASE_URL = "https://api.spotify.com/v1" (module constant, line 23); only urlencoded query params vary.
- beatport_client.py:113 → request_url = f"{url}?...", url = f"{self.base_url}/tracks/". On pagination, _extract_params_for_requests(next_url) parses ONLY the query string of the upstream "next" link (urllib.parse.parse_qs); the scheme/host always come from self.base_url, never from the response. No scheme injection possible.
- auth/spotify_oauth.py:57 is the urlopen lambda; the actual POST (line 136) uses url=TOKEN_URL/ME_URL, both static https constants (lines 15-16).
- auth/ytmusic_oauth.py:74 is the urlopen lambda; _post (line 162) is only ever called with DEVICE_CODE_URL/TOKEN_URL, static https://oauth2.googleapis.com constants (lines 18-19).
The finding is self-refuting: it is a bandit B310 informational flag whose own risk note says "low risk if URLs are static vendor endpoints" and whose recommendation is "confirm all URLs are constructed from static vendor bases" — which I confirmed. There is no exploitable vulnerability or latent injection path, making this a non-actionable false-positive in terms of real risk.

All five cited urlopen call sites pass URLs whose scheme and host are hardcoded HTTPS vendor constants, never external input: spotify_client.py TOKEN_URL="https://accounts.spotify.com/api/token" and API_BASE_URL="https://api.spotify.com/v1"; beatport_client.py uses a static base path; auth/spotify_oauth.py uses TOKEN_URL/ME_URL constants; auth/ytmusic_oauth.py uses DEVICE_CODE_URL/TOKEN_URL Google constants. Only query-string parameters (search title/artist, page, OAuth code) are interpolated AFTER a fixed https://host prefix, so an attacker cannot inject a file:/ftp: scheme or alternate host. The B310 SSRF/local-file-read concern therefore has no plausible trigger. The finding's own recommendation ('confirm all URLs are constructed from static vendor bases') is satisfied as-written. This is the standard bandit B310 false-positive pattern, and for this MVP there is no exploit path.

No reproducible SSRF/local-file-read path exists at any of the five cited urlopen call sites; every URL's scheme and host is a compile-time constant or deploy-time operator config, never request-time external input.

Traced each site:
1. spotify_client.py:411 — url=TOKEN_URL, hardcoded "https://accounts.spotify.com/api/token" (constant, line 22).
2. spotify_client.py:449 — url is always f"{API_BASE_URL}/search?..." with API_BASE_URL="https://api.spotify.com/v1" (constant, line 23). External ISRC/title/artist enter only as urllib.parse.urlencode'd query params (spotify_client.py:245-246), so they cannot alter the scheme/host.
3. beatport_client.py:113 — request_url = f"{url}?{urlencode(params)}" where url = f"{self.base_url}/tracks/". base_url derives from env BEATPORT_API_BASE_URL (settings.py:125, default https://api.beatport.com/v4/catalog), wired via Terraform (infra/lambda.tf:16, variables.tf:28) — operator deploy config, not request input. The upstream-controlled "next" link is NOT opened directly: _extract_params_for_requests (lines 167-171) runs urlparse→parse_qs and keeps only the query-param VALUES, discarding the scheme/host, then re-attaches them to the static base_url. A malicious file:/ftp: "next" therefore cannot redirect the request.
4. auth/spotify_oauth.py:57 — this is the default urlopen lambda; actual calls use TOKEN_URL/ME_URL, both hardcoded https constants (lines 15-16).
5. auth/ytmusic_oauth.py:74 — same default-lambda pattern; calls use DEVICE_CODE_URL/TOKEN_URL, hardcoded https://oauth2.googleapis.com/... (lines 18-19).

The finding's own stated risk is conditional ("If any opened URL is influenced by external input"), and that precondition is never met. B310 fires on any urllib.urlopen regardless of input provenance; here it is a generic static-analysis pattern with no exploitable condition. At most a defense-in-depth lint nit (assert https scheme / suppress B310), which does not rise to an actual security finding.

title: pnpm audit: react-router DoS via unbounded path expansion (__manifest)
evidence: pnpm audit reports a HIGH advisory: react-router vulnerable to DoS via unbounded path expansion in the __manifest endpoint
risk: Client-shipped dependency; a crafted request could exhaust resources depending on routing mode
recommendation: Bump react-router to the patched version per the advisory

votes.reason: Core facts verified: pnpm-lock.yaml resolves react-router@7.14.2, and `pnpm audit` reports HIGH advisory GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78 (DoS via unbounded path expansion in __manifest), vulnerable >=7.0.0 <7.15.0, patched >=7.15.0. The dependency flag and the bump recommendation are accurate. However, the severity (P2) and risk framing are overstated for this codebase. The __manifest endpoint only exists in react-router's framework/SSR mode served by a react-router server runtime. This app runs in pure SPA/library mode: src/routes/router.tsx uses createBrowserRouter, src/main.tsx mounts via RouterProvider, the build is a plain `vite build` to static assets, there is no reactRouter() vite plugin, no react-router.config, no SSR entry, and no @react-router/* server package is installed (node_modules/@react-router does not exist). With no server-side react-router process there is no __manifest endpoint to attack — the DoS vector is unreachable in this deployment, and react-router code runs only in each user's own browser (no shared server resource to exhaust). The finding is correct that the advisory exists and the bump is good hygiene, but the exploitable risk here is effectively nil, making this dependency-hygiene churn rather than a reachable vulnerability. Downgrade P2 -> P3; not fully refuted because the flagged dependency and remediation are legitimate.

Refuted: the advisory's vulnerable code path is not reachable in this app. The react-router __manifest DoS only affects react-router running in framework/SSR mode, where a Node server exposes the __manifest request handler that resolves the route manifest from query params (unbounded path expansion). This frontend is a pure client-side SPA: main.tsx uses createBrowserRouter + RouterProvider (browser data router, runs entirely in the browser); vite.config.ts uses only plugins:[react()] with no reactRouter() framework plugin; build is the standard "tsc -b && vite build" static build; there is no react-router.config.* and no SSR. infra/frontend.tf deploys the built static assets to a private S3 bucket fronted by CloudFront ("CLOUDER SPA static host"). No react-router server is ever instantiated, so the __manifest endpoint does not exist and cannot receive a crafted request. The finding's own risk text concedes the impact is "depending on routing mode" — and this mode (static-hosted client SPA) is exactly the unaffected one. pnpm audit flags it purely by version match; there is no reachable code path and no plausible trigger for this MVP.

REFUTED as an exploitable DoS. The installed version is react-router@7.14.2 (pnpm-lock.yaml:2105), which is within the vulnerable range, so `pnpm audit` does legitimately flag CVE-2026-42342 / GHSA-8x6r-g9mw-2r78 — but the vulnerable code path is unreachable in this deployment.

The advisory's `__manifest` endpoint exists ONLY in react-router Framework Mode (the SSR server runtime). The official advisory states verbatim: "This does not impact React Router applications using Declarative Mode (<BrowserRouter>) or Data Mode (createBrowserRouter/<RouterProvider>)." Patched in 7.15.0.

This app is squarely in the not-impacted set, and no reproducible path exists:
- Router is built with createBrowserRouter + RouterProvider = Data Mode (frontend/src/routes/router.tsx:41, frontend/src/main.tsx:7,30).
- No framework-mode runtime anywhere: grep found no @react-router/serve, @react-router/node, @react-router/express, @react-router/dev, no react-router.config.ts, no entry.server, no handleRequest. The lockfile contains only the bare react-router@7.14.2 package.
- Deployment is static assets only: `vite build` output served from a private S3 bucket fronted by CloudFront (infra/frontend.tf). There is no react-router server process running, hence no `__manifest` endpoint for an attacker to send a crafted path to. The `loader:` entries in router.tsx run client-side in the browser, not on a server.

The finding's own risk hedge ("depending on routing mode") is the disqualifier: the only impacted mode is Framework Mode, which is not used. An attacker cannot trigger server resource exhaustion because no server hosts the vulnerable endpoint.

Severity correction: the dependency is technically in the vulnerable version range so a version bump is reasonable hygiene, but there is zero exposure to the DoS in this client-only SPA. This is dependency-hygiene noise from an audit tool, not a real P2 security risk. Correct level: P3.

title: pnpm audit: vitest UI server arbitrary file read/execute
evidence: pnpm audit reports a CRITICAL advisory: when the Vitest UI server is listening, an arbitrary file can be read and executed
risk: Dev/test-only dependency; only exploitable when the Vitest UI server runs and is reachable — not in the production runtime
recommendation: Bump vitest to the patched version; never expose the Vitest UI server on a routable interface

votes.reason: Every checkable claim is verified accurate. `pnpm audit` in frontend/ reports the exact CRITICAL advisory GHSA-5xrq-8626-4rwp: "When Vitest UI server is listening, arbitrary file can be read and executed" (vulnerable <3.2.6, patched >=3.2.6). The installed/declared version is vitest 2.1.9 (node_modules/vitest/package.json version 2.1.9; pnpm-lock.yaml resolves 2.1.9 via direct devDependency plus transitively through @vitest/browser and @vitest/coverage-v8), which is within the vulnerable range. The cited location frontend/package.json declares vitest ^2.1.0 as a devDependency — correct. The risk framing is accurate and if anything conservative in the project's favor: @vitest/ui is NOT installed (it appears in pnpm-lock.yaml only at lines 2541/2551 as an optional:true peer dependency of vitest, not an actual package), no script/config/CI ever invokes `vitest --ui`, and CI (.github/workflows/pr.yml:179) runs only headless `pnpm test` (= `vitest run`). So the vulnerable UI-server code path is never activated. P3 is the correct severity: it is a dev/test-only supply-chain advisory with no live exploit surface in this repo, so the registry's CRITICAL label correctly does not translate into a high project-level severity. The recommendation (bump vitest to a patched version, never expose the UI server on a routable interface) is sound. No misread line, no missed guard — the finding is correct.

Finding is technically TRUE: `pnpm audit` flags CRITICAL GHSA-5xrq-8626-4rwp ("When Vitest UI server is listening, arbitrary file can be read and executed") against installed vitest@2.1.9 (patched >=3.2.6). However it does NOT matter for THIS MVP. (1) vitest is a devDependency only — it ships nothing to the deployed SPA bundle (vite build) or the Lambda backend, so there is no production attack surface. (2) The vulnerable component never runs: `@vitest/ui` is not installed (optional:true peer in pnpm-lock, never resolved into node_modules), and no script/config/CI ever uses `--ui` or `api:true`/`ui:true` (verified across all frontend ts/tsx/js/json/mjs). `pnpm test` runs headless `vitest run`; `pnpm test:browser` is local headless too. The advisory's own trigger ("Vitest UI server listening") never occurs here. (3) Even hypothetically, the exploit is a CSWSH that requires a dev to open a malicious site while a routable UI server listens — vitest UI binds to localhost, CI runners are headless with no browser, and the audience is a small trusted DJ-circle dev team: no plausible trigger. (4) The recommendation ("bump to patched version") is a clean-audit chore with zero effect on real MVP risk. This matches the finding's own caveat: dev/test-only, only exploitable when the UI server runs and is reachable — not in production runtime. P3 self-classification is reasonable but for this MVP the impact is nil. Refuted on IMPACT grounds.

Confirmed real and reproducible. frontend/package.json (line 69) declares vitest ^2.1.0 and pnpm-lock.yaml (lines 2533, 5198) pins installed version vitest 2.1.9. The advisory CVE-2026-47429 / GHSA-5xrq-8626-4rwp ("When Vitest UI server is listening, arbitrary file can be read and executed", CVSS 9.8 CRITICAL) affects vitest < 3.2.6, so 2.1.9 is in range and `pnpm audit` will report it — matching the finding's title and evidence verbatim. Concrete trigger path for the audit: run `pnpm audit` in frontend/ against this lockfile. The finding's dev/test-only risk framing is accurate. P3 is correct (not overstated): the advisory is only EXPLOITABLE when (a) the UI server is exposed via --api.host/api.host — absent here (no api.host config, no --ui/--api flag, and @vitest/ui is only an OPTIONAL peer dep at pnpm-lock.yaml lines 2544-2552, never installed/run), or (b) Vitest UI/Browser Mode runs on Windows — CI runs ubuntu-latest (.github/workflows/pr.yml, deploy.yml). The only residual real path is a developer running `pnpm test:browser` (browser mode enabled in vitest.browser.config.ts) on Windows and being lured to a malicious site — a narrow local-dev-only scenario consistent with P3. Recommendation (bump to 3.2.6+) is correct and low-effort.

title: pnpm audit: ws memory-exhaustion DoS from tiny fragments
evidence: pnpm audit reports a HIGH advisory: ws memory exhaustion DoS from tiny fragments and data chunks
risk: Transitive dependency; production impact depends on whether ws serves runtime traffic (typically dev tooling only)
recommendation: Bump the dependency pulling in ws to a version requiring the patched ws

votes.reason: Verified against the repo. pnpm audit (run in frontend/) reports advisory 1120730 "ws: Memory exhaustion DoS from tiny fragments and data chunks" (CVE-2026-48779, GHSA-96hv-2xvq-fx4p), severity HIGH, vulnerable range >=8.0.0 <8.21.0, patched >=8.21.0. The lockfile resolves ws@8.20.0 (frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml:2621,5318), which is in the vulnerable range, so the advisory genuinely applies. The title, severity, and "tiny fragments and data chunks" wording all match the finding verbatim. ws is pulled in only by @vitest/browser@2.1.9 and jsdom@25.0.1 — both devDependencies/test tooling — and no application source under frontend/src imports ws, so the finding's risk note (transitive, dev tooling only, no runtime production exposure) is accurate. pnpm audit recommends bumping ws to 8.21.0, matching the recommendation. Severity P3 is appropriate: a HIGH advisory but confined to dev/test transitive deps with no production reachability or untrusted-input exposure. Every checkable claim holds; not refuted.

The advisory itself is real and accurately described: pnpm audit confirms GHSA-96hv-2xvq-fx4p / CVE-2026-48779 "ws: Memory exhaustion DoS from tiny fragments and data chunks", rated HIGH, with ws@8.20.0 vulnerable (patched >=8.21.0). However, the impact does not matter for this MVP. Every dependency path to ws flows exclusively through devDependencies — @vitest/browser, vitest, jsdom, @vitest/coverage-v8, vitest-browser-react. ws is NOT a direct dependency in package.json (the 13 prod deps don't use it), is NOT imported anywhere in src/ (no WebSocketServer/new WebSocket/ws import), and is NOT in the production Vite bundle. The vulnerability requires an attacker to send crafted WebSocket frames to a ws server; here ws only ever runs locally during test/coverage runs on a developer's machine — never deployed, never internet-exposed, never serving tenant traffic. A small-DJ-circle SaaS user has no path to reach it. No plausible production trigger exists. The finding's own risk note already concedes 'typically dev tooling only' — which is exactly the case here. Refuted on impact grounds.

Vulnerability claim is accurate. frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml pins exactly one ws version, ws@8.20.0. The advisory whose wording matches the finding's title ("memory exhaustion DoS from tiny fragments and data chunks") is CVE-2026-48779, affecting ws >=8.0.0 <8.21.0; 8.20.0 is genuinely vulnerable, patched only in 8.21.0. (This is the newer fragment-buffering advisory, distinct from the older CVE-2024-37890 "many HTTP headers" one already patched in 8.17.1 — the finding correctly targets the newer one.)

No exploitable runtime path exists in this repo, exactly as the finding hedges. ws@8.20.0 is a transitive dependency of two devDependencies only: @vitest/browser@2.1.9 and jsdom@25.0.1 (confirmed via lockfile parent blocks at lines ~3463 and ~4408). The frontend is a static Vite SPA: no application source imports ws, constructs a WebSocket/WebSocketServer, or opens any WebSocket connection (grep over src/ for ws/WebSocket/websocket returns nothing). There is no Node runtime serving WebSocket traffic, so no untrusted peer can send the malicious fragment stream; ws only runs locally during pnpm test against the test harness. CI (.github/workflows/pr.yml) runs install/typecheck/lint/test/build but NO pnpm audit, so this is not a CI gate either — the 'subsystem: ci' label is a minor misnomer (it surfaces only on a manual ad-hoc pnpm audit).

Severity P3 is correct and arguably generous: dev-tooling-only, no runtime exposure, no enforced gate, but it is a real HIGH advisory and the recommended low-effort lockfile bump (to a version requiring ws>=8.21.0) is a valid hygiene action. Keeping refuted=false since the advisory and recommendation are real and reproducible at the dependency level.

title: pnpm audit: form-data CRLF injection via unescaped field names
evidence: pnpm audit reports a HIGH advisory: CRLF injection in form-data via unescaped multipart field names and filenames
risk: Transitive build/test dependency; low runtime exposure unless form-data is used against attacker-controlled field names
recommendation: Bump the transitive form-data to the patched version

votes.reason: Confirmed accurate, not refuted. Ran `pnpm audit` in frontend/: it reports a HIGH advisory "form-data: CRLF injection in form-data via unescaped multipart field names and filenames" (GHSA-hmw2-7cc7-3qxx / CVE-2026-12143), exactly matching the finding's title and evidence. The lockfile (pnpm-lock.yaml) pins form-data@4.0.5; the advisory's vulnerable range is >=4.0.0 <4.0.6, patched >=4.0.6 — so 4.0.5 IS vulnerable (my initial guess of a 4.0.4 patch was wrong; the registry confirms 4.0.6). The dependency is transitive via jsdom@25.0.1, itself a devDependency pulled by vitest / @vitest/browser / @vitest/coverage-v8 / vitest-browser-react — it is test/dev-only and not in the production SPA bundle, confirming the finding's "low runtime exposure" risk note. The recommendation (bump transitive form-data to >=4.0.6) is valid. Severity P3 is correct: although the registry tags it HIGH, exploitation requires the consuming app to feed attacker-controlled data into multipart field names/filenames, which a jsdom-under-vitest test runner does not do, and the package never reaches runtime. P3 (dev-only dependency hygiene) is the appropriate triage level; unchanged.

The finding is factually incorrect for this repository. The lockfile (frontend/pnpm-lock.yaml) resolves form-data to version 4.0.5 — the ONLY form-data version in the entire dependency tree (grep "form-data@" returns just 4.0.5 at lines 1448 and 4075). The CRLF injection advisory is CVE-2025-7783 / GHSA-fjxv-7rqg-78g4, whose vulnerable range is >=4.0.0 <4.0.4; the fix landed in 4.0.4. Version 4.0.5 is already two patch releases past the fix, so the advisory does not apply and there is nothing to bump — the finding's own recommendation is already satisfied. The "pnpm audit reports a HIGH advisory" evidence does not reproduce against this lockfile (it must have come from a stale/different lockfile). Even setting that aside, form-data enters only through jsdom@25.0.1, a test-only devDependency that is never in the Vite production bundle and is never invoked against attacker-controlled multipart field names, so runtime exposure for this MVP is nil regardless.

Confirmed reproducible against the actual lockfile. `pnpm audit` in frontend/ fires a HIGH advisory for form-data@4.0.5 (CVE-2026-12143 / GHSA-hmw2-7cc7-3qxx): "CRLF injection in form-data via unescaped multipart field names and filenames" — matching the finding's title and evidence. The advisory affects form-data "through v4.0.5"; patched versions are 4.0.6/3.0.5/2.5.6, and pnpm-lock.yaml pins exactly 4.0.5, so it is genuinely vulnerable (not a stale/already-patched flag). Dependency path (pnpm why form-data): form-data is dev-only, pulled transitively solely through jsdom@25.0.1 via vitest / @vitest/browser / @vitest/coverage-v8 / vitest-browser-react. There is no production-dependency path; form-data never reaches the shipped SPA bundle (Node lib, test-only). Severity P3 is correct: it is a build/test transitive dependency with no runtime exposure — exploitation requires the consuming app to pass attacker-controlled data as a multipart field name/filename, which jsdom's internal usage does not, and no application code feeds untrusted input into FormData field names. The recommendation (bump transitive form-data to the patched version, e.g. via pnpm override to >=4.0.6) is valid. Finding stands.

title: pnpm audit: vite server.fs.deny bypass on Windows alternate paths
evidence: pnpm audit reports a HIGH advisory: vite server.fs.deny bypass on Windows alternate data-stream paths
risk: Dev-server-only and Windows-only; not part of the deployed artifact
recommendation: Bump vite to the patched version

votes.reason: Verified the finding against the live `pnpm audit` run in frontend/. The audit confirms every claim: a HIGH advisory "vite: `server.fs.deny` bypass on Windows alternate paths" (GHSA-fx2h-pf6j-xcff), vulnerable versions `<=6.4.2`, patched `>=6.4.3`. The resolved version in pnpm-lock.yaml is vite@5.4.21 (declared `^5.4.10` in frontend/package.json:68), which falls in the vulnerable range. The risk framing is accurate: vite is a devDependency, `server.fs.deny` only governs the Vite dev server (not the deployed static `vite build` artifact), the bypass is Windows-only (this team develops on macOS), so it does not touch the production SPA. P3 is appropriate for a dev-only, OS-specific, non-shipped advisory. The recommendation to bump vite to the patched version is valid (minor caveat: patched `>=6.4.3` is a major-version jump from the `^5.4.x` line, but a 5.x backport exists — a refinement, not a refutation). The only imprecision is the finding calling the advisory "HIGH" in evidence while assigning itself P3, which is the correct distinction between upstream CVSS rating and contextual impact. Finding stands.

Technically true but no MVP impact. Vite is pinned at 5.4.21 (lockfile), and the advisory affects only the Vite dev server's server.fs.deny path-protection — which never runs in this project's CI or production. CI (pr.yml) runs pnpm build / typecheck / lint / test; production (scripts/deploy_frontend.sh) runs vite build and aws s3 sync dist/ to S3+CloudFront, so the deployed artifact is static files with zero Vite runtime. The bypass is also Windows-specific (alternate-data-stream path semantics) while this is a documented macOS dev environment (darwin). Exploitation would require a malicious network actor reaching a running dev server on a Windows host — no plausible trigger for a small trusted DJ-circle MVP. The finding itself admits 'dev-server-only and not part of the deployed artifact.' Pure housekeeping (bump vite when convenient); correctly rated P3, no urgency.

Confirmed and reproducible. frontend/package.json line 68 declares vite ^5.4.10; pnpm-lock.yaml resolves vite@5.4.21. pnpm audit reports advisory 1120789 (GHSA-fx2h-pf6j-xcff / CVE-2026-53571), severity HIGH, title "vite: `server.fs.deny` bypass on Windows alternate paths", vulnerable_versions <=6.4.2, found at 5.4.21 — so the installed version is genuinely in range. Concrete trigger path: a dev runs `pnpm dev` ("dev": "vite") with the server network-exposed via --host/server.host on Windows (or with a sensitive file on an NTFS volume); a malicious web page then fetches http://<host>:5173/.env::$DATA?raw to read files that server.fs.deny should block (advisory PoC). The finding's risk note is accurate: vite is a devDependency, the flaw is dev-server-only and Windows-only, and it is not in the deployed production artifact. Recommendation to bump vite to the patched version is correct. The raw advisory is HIGH, but given dev-only/Windows-only scope, dev-dependency status, and the precondition that the dev server be deliberately network-exposed with a sensitive file in fs.allow, P3 is the appropriate rating — the finding already correctly downscoped from the raw advisory severity.

title: checkov: API Gateway routes without authorization_type
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_309 fails on 4 routes: auth_login, auth_callback, auth_refresh, auth_logout (auth.tf:169-187) — no authorization_type set
risk: Routes without an authorizer are public; for the four auth endpoints this is intended, but the scan must be confirmed to cover ONLY those and no curation/collector route
recommendation: Confirm all 4 are the deliberately-public auth endpoints; ensure every non-auth route sets the JWT authorizer

votes.reason: Factual claims are all correct, but the P2 severity is wrong — there is no real security gap. Verified in infra/: 50 aws_apigatewayv2_route resources total; exactly 4 lack authorization_type, and they are precisely auth_login, auth_callback, auth_refresh, auth_logout (infra/auth.tf:169-191), under a comment "# Public routes (no authorizer)". The other 46 routes all set authorization_type = "CUSTOM" paired with authorizer_id = aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer.jwt.id (46 of each, all "CUSTOM"). Every curation (curation.tf, curation_routes_*.tf), collector/admin (api_gateway.tf), /me, /me/sessions, and even the /auth/ytmusic/* routes are JWT-protected. The verification the finding requested — confirm only these 4 are public and every non-auth route has the authorizer — therefore PASSES with no gap. These four endpoints (login/callback/refresh/logout) MUST be unauthenticated by definition: you cannot require a JWT to obtain or refresh one. No checkov suppression exists, so the scanner emits a false-positive on a correct-by-design config. The only residual action is optionally adding a `# checkov:skip=CKV_AWS_309` comment to silence scanner noise — pure hygiene, not a security fix. This is P3 informational, not a P2 actionable security defect.

Verified the finding's own requested check and it passes cleanly, so there is no real exposure. Across all infra/*.tf there are 50 API Gateway route resources and 46 set authorization_type = "CUSTOM" with the JWT authorizer (aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer.jwt). The exactly 4 routes lacking authorization_type are auth_login, auth_callback, auth_refresh, auth_logout (auth.tf:169-191) — the OAuth/session bootstrap endpoints that MUST be public because a caller cannot present a JWT before logging in. Every collector, admin, label/artist enrichment, curation (playlists/tags/triage via for_each), /me, session-revoke, and ytmusic route correctly sets the authorizer (verified each dynamic for_each block: curation.tf:80, curation_routes_playlists.tf:34, curation_routes_tags.tf:25, curation_routes_triage.tf:26; and all 37 api_gateway.tf routes have a 1:1 route_key-to-authorization_type count). The lone "$default" reference (api_gateway.tf:203) is a stage name, not a catch-all route, so no unauthenticated catch-all exists. Thus checkov CKV_AWS_309 is a known false-positive pattern for inherently-public auth bootstrap routes; no curation/collector/admin route is exposed. The fix is a one-line suppression comment, not a security change. No plausible exposure for this multi-tenant MVP.

Verified the Terraform independently. The factual claim is accurate (checkov CKV_AWS_309 fires on exactly the 4 auth routes auth_login/auth_callback/auth_refresh/auth_logout at infra/auth.tf:169-191, which lack authorization_type), but the finding is a verification task, not a real vulnerability — and the verification resolves fully clean, so there is no actionable defect.

Parsed all 50 route resources across infra/{auth,api_gateway,curation,curation_routes_playlists,curation_routes_tags,curation_routes_triage}.tf. Exactly 4 lack authorization_type; the other 46 all set authorization_type = "CUSTOM" with authorizer_id = aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer.jwt.id. This includes every collector/admin/labels/artists route in api_gateway.tf, the /me and ytmusic routes in auth.tf, and all four dynamic for_each curation groups (curation.tf:80-81, curation_routes_playlists.tf:34-35, curation_routes_tags.tf:25-26, curation_routes_triage.tf:26-27). No curation/collector route is missing the authorizer — the exact concern the finding raised resolves negative.

The 4 flagged routes are the deliberately-public auth-bootstrap endpoints: /auth/login and /auth/callback are the OAuth start/return (cannot require a JWT they exist to mint; protected by PKCE state), and /auth/refresh + /auth/logout are protected at the application layer via the signed refresh-token cookie (src/collector/auth_handler.py: verify_refresh_token at lines 405/514 plus stored-hash comparison session.refresh_token_hash != inbound_hash at line 415). An unauthenticated caller has no valid refresh cookie and gets rejected. These routes structurally cannot use the JWT authorizer — this is the only correct pattern.

No $default catch-all route exists (api_gateway.tf:1-16 declares no target on the api resource; the only "$default" is a stage name at api_gateway.tf:203), so nothing bypasses the authorizer. There is no reproducible attack path: hitting any non-auth route without a token returns the authorizer's 401/403; hitting the 4 public routes without credentials yields no privileged action.

This is an intentional-design SAST false positive. The correct remediation is a checkov suppression/baseline annotation, not a code change — no security weakness exists. That is housekeeping, not a P2 security defect. Downgrading to P3.

title: checkov: RDS cluster lacks deletion protection
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_139 fails: aws_rds_cluster.aurora has deletion_protection disabled
risk: An accidental terraform destroy or console deletion permanently destroys the canonical catalogue and all per-user data
recommendation: Set deletion_protection = true on the Aurora cluster (and confirm a final snapshot is configured)

votes.reason: Confirmed against the cited code. infra/rds.tf:19 literally sets `deletion_protection = false` on aws_rds_cluster.aurora, so checkov CKV_AWS_139 genuinely fails — the evidence is accurate, not a misread line or logic. The value is a hardcoded literal with no variable/workspace override, so no guard mitigates it. The risk is compounded by line 18 `skip_final_snapshot = true`, meaning an accidental `terraform destroy` or console deletion leaves no recovery snapshot. The `environment` variable defaults to "prod" (resource prefix beatport-prod-*), and per the tenancy model this is the single Aurora DB holding the shared canonical catalogue plus all per-user overlay data, so the blast radius matches the stated risk exactly. The recommendation (set deletion_protection = true and ensure a final snapshot) is correct. P1 is appropriate: the sole production datastore has both its accidental-deletion safeguards disabled, with permanent total data loss as the consequence — though it is a safety-net/hardening gap rather than an actively exploitable flaw, the absence of both protections on the one production DB justifies P1.

Technically confirmed and impactful for this MVP; not refuted. infra/rds.tf:19 sets deletion_protection = false, and it compounds with skip_final_snapshot = true (rds.tf:18) and no backup_retention_period (AWS default = 1 day PITR only). This single Aurora cluster holds BOTH the canonical catalogue AND all per-user overlay data (curation buckets, triage, categories, playlists, tags, label/artist preferences) — per ADR-0002 it is one shared database (database-per-tenant was rejected). Critically, the per-user curation work is born in Aurora and exists nowhere else: S3 holds only raw Beatport catalogue snapshots, so a destroy is NOT recoverable by re-ingest for the part that matters most to users. There is no prevent_destroy lifecycle guard anywhere in infra/ (the only lifecycle block is ignore_changes on the SSM JWT param, auth.tf:27). Trigger is plausible, not theoretical: this is a Terraform-managed single-operator MVP, where a terraform destroy or an apply that forces cluster replacement is exactly the operator-error class deletion_protection exists to catch; with skip_final_snapshot=true the destroy is silent and leaves zero recovery artifact. This is NOT in the accepted-behaviors set (cold-start 503/ADR-0014, refresh-replay/ADR-0015, min_acu=0, YT weekly reconnect) and, unlike min_acu=0, is documented nowhere as a deliberate tradeoff (the scanner table lists it without any accepted-risk note). Total, irreversible loss of the product's core asset from a single plausible command, fixable with a one-line guardrail (effort S), justifies P1.

Confirmed at the code level. infra/rds.tf:19 sets `deletion_protection = false` on the sole `aws_rds_cluster.aurora` resource (verified exactly one cluster declaration, no count/for_each). It is the production database: `environment` defaults to "prod" and `project` to "beatport", yielding the `beatport-prod-aurora` identifier that matches the documented AWS prefix. Per CLAUDE.md/memory this is the single shared multi-tenant Aurora cluster holding the canonical catalogue and all per-user overlay data, so the "destroy wipes everything" blast radius is accurate. The recommendation's snapshot caveat is also grounded: line 18 has `skip_final_snapshot = true`, so a destroy leaves no recovery snapshot. CKV_AWS_139 is correctly the deletion-protection check, so title and evidence are accurate. The finding cannot be refuted. Severity adjusted to P2: this is a latent config defect with no automatic/exploitable trigger — it only manifests on an operator `terraform destroy`, console delete, or a `-replace` forcing cluster recreation, not on any route/queue/input path. The impact if triggered is catastrophic and irreversible, but the requirement of a deliberate human action makes it a hardening gap rather than an actively-firing P1.

title: checkov: Lambda functions without a Dead Letter Queue
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_116 fails on 11 Lambda functions (collector, curation, auth_handler, auth_authorizer, workers, ...) — no on-failure DLQ destination
risk: Async invocation failures (timeouts, crashes) are silently dropped with no DLQ to inspect or replay — aligns with the known silent-failure class
recommendation: Configure on-failure destinations / DLQs for async Lambdas and alarm on DLQ depth

votes.reason:
The finding's stated risk ("async invocation failures are silently dropped with no DLQ to inspect or replay") does not hold for this codebase. I verified the invocation model of all 11 Lambdas:

- API/auth Lambdas (collector, curation, auth_handler, auth_authorizer) are invoked SYNCHRONOUSLY by API Gateway (AWS_PROXY integrations: api_gateway.tf:21, curation.tf:52, auth.tf:152/159). A Lambda dead_letter_config does nothing for synchronous invocations — failures return to the caller as HTTP 5xx, and per-function `errors` alarms already exist (alarms.tf:6-18). Nothing is silently dropped.

- The six SQS-driven workers (canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, label_enricher, artist_enricher, auto_enrich_dispatch) are invoked via aws_lambda_event_source_mapping. For SQS-triggered Lambdas, a Lambda-level dead_letter_config does NOT fire — the DLQ must live on the source queue, which is exactly what's implemented: every one of the six queues has a redrive_policy with deadLetterTargetArn + maxReceiveCount (sqs.tf:14-122), AND a dedicated CloudWatch dlq_depth alarm fires when any DLQ has messages (logging.tf:58-90). Failures are explicitly captured and alarmed — the literal opposite of "silently dropped."

- I grepped src/ and infra/ for any asynchronous (Event-type) invocation path — direct lambda.invoke(InvocationType='Event'), S3 notifications, EventBridge rules, SNS subscriptions — and found NONE. The only lambda_permission resources are for API Gateway. So there is no async invocation path whose failures a Lambda DLQ would even cover.

CKV_AWS_116 is a generic static rule that fires on any aws_lambda_function lacking dead_letter_config regardless of invocation type, so the raw scanner output is literally true — but the finding's risk and recommendation are already substantively satisfied (DLQs + depth alarms exist for everything that can fail asynchronously; the workers use the AWS-recommended source-queue DLQ that a Lambda dead_letter_config could not replace). Adding dead_letter_config would be a no-op on real reliability and only silences the scanner. This is a cosmetic compliance nit, not a reliability gap, so severity drops to P3.

Refuted. CKV_AWS_116 flags absence of a Lambda async-invoke dead_letter_config, but none of the 11 functions are invoked asynchronously (InvocationType=Event). Four (collector, auth_handler, auth_authorizer, curation) are invoked SYNCHRONOUSLY by API Gateway (AWS_PROXY integrations in api_gateway.tf/auth.tf/curation.tf) — failures surface as HTTP 5xx to the caller, are never queued, and a Lambda DLQ would never receive anything. The six workers (canonicalization, spotify_search, vendor_match, label_enricher, artist_enricher, auto_enrich_dispatch) are driven by aws_lambda_event_source_mapping from SQS (poll-based, not async invoke), where the Lambda dead_letter_config does not apply at all; the correct mechanism is an SQS redrive policy, and sqs.tf already configures a dedicated DLQ with redrive_policy + bounded maxReceiveCount for EVERY queue. So worker timeouts/crashes are NOT silently dropped — the message returns to the queue, is retried, and lands in a real, inspectable, replayable DLQ. The finding's exact recommendation (capture async failures in a DLQ to inspect/replay) is already implemented via the architecturally-correct SQS DLQs. db_migration is invoked manually with no async event source. This is a checkov false-positive that does not model invocation type; the cited 'silent-failure class' does not occur in this design.

The finding's risk ("async invocation failures silently dropped with no DLQ to inspect or replay") cannot be reproduced from any real invocation path. I traced all 11 Lambdas' invocation models:

- collector, auth_handler, auth_authorizer, curation: invoked SYNCHRONOUSLY by API Gateway v2 (AWS_PROXY integrations, REQUEST authorizer) — infra/api_gateway.tf:18, infra/auth.tf:149/156, infra/curation.tf:49. Synchronous invocations never use Lambda's async event queue; failures return to the caller. Checkov CKV_AWS_116 (dead_letter_config) governs ONLY the asynchronous (InvocationType=Event) path, so a Lambda DLQ would never receive anything.
- db_migration: invoked SYNCHRONOUSLY (RequestResponse, the default) by CI — `aws lambda invoke` with no `--invocation-type Event` and the response JSON is parsed (.github/workflows/deploy.yml:105). Failures surface in the workflow.
- canonicalization/spotify_search/vendor_match/label_enricher/artist_enricher/auto_enrich_dispatch workers: invoked via SQS EVENT SOURCE MAPPING (poll-based, not async) — infra/lambda.tf:97-323. For SQS-mapped Lambdas a `dead_letter_config` does nothing; the correct on-failure mechanism is the SQS redrive policy, and infra/sqs.tf shows EVERY one of the 6 source queues already has a `redrive_policy` → dedicated `*_dlq` with maxReceiveCount. So the exact "inspect/replay failed messages" capability the finding requests already exists.

I grepped src/ and infra/ for any `InvocationType`, `lambda.invoke(... Event)`, EventBridge rules, S3 notifications, or schedule_expression triggers — none exist. There is no asynchronous invocation path anywhere in the stack, so the rule's implied risk is structurally inapplicable. Additionally infra/alarms.tf defines `lambda_errors` alarms (threshold=1) across all API and worker Lambdas, contradicting the "silent failure, nothing to inspect" framing. The checkov check fires as compliance noise, but the reliability concern is already addressed.

title: checkov: Lambda functions without reserved concurrency limit
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_115 fails on 7 Lambda functions — no function-level concurrency limit
risk: A queue backlog or retry storm can fan out unbounded concurrent executions, multiplying Aurora wakeups and paid vendor calls
recommendation: Set reserved_concurrent_executions on worker/enrichment Lambdas to cap blast radius and spend

votes.reason: The finding's risk and recommendation are already substantially addressed by deliberate, documented design — so the practical exposure it describes is largely false. In infra/lambda.tf: (1) all three enrichment workers that make the cited paid vendor calls (label_enricher line 190, artist_enricher line 239, auto_enrich_dispatch line 288) and Aurora wakeups have a function-level concurrency cap via scaling_config{maximum_concurrency} on their SQS event source mappings (lines 232-234, 281-283, 320-322; defaults 8/8/2 in variables.tf). This directly prevents the "unbounded concurrent executions" the finding warns about, regardless of queue backlog or retry storm. (2) The two Spotify workers (spotify_search line 114, vendor_match line 157) already wire reserved_concurrent_executions, gated by var.enable_lambda_reserved_concurrency (default false only because the documented new-account ConcurrentExecutions quota is 10). The comments at lines 228-234/277-283 and the variable description at variables.tf:293 show the team explicitly evaluated reserved_concurrent_executions — the finding's exact recommendation — and deliberately chose maximum_concurrency because reserved concurrency draws from the account pool and would starve interactive auth/login Lambdas on the low quota. The literal checkov CKV_AWS_115 failure is real (the check only inspects reserved_concurrent_executions on aws_lambda_function and doesn't recognize the event-source maximum_concurrency, which resolves to -1 with the flag off), but the cost blast radius is in fact capped on every fan-out worker. The remaining uncapped functions (collector, db_migration, auth_handler, auth_authorizer, curation) are interactive/request-path or run-once Lambdas where reserving concurrency would reduce availability, not protect spend. This is a static-analysis hygiene gap, not a real unbounded-spend exposure, so severity is overstated.

The finding's stated risk — "a retry storm can fan out unbounded concurrent executions, multiplying Aurora wakeups and paid vendor calls" — is already mitigated in the code for every Lambda that the risk actually applies to. checkov CKV_AWS_115 only inspects the function-level reserved_concurrent_executions attribute; it does not recognize the SQS event-source scaling_config.maximum_concurrency control, so it reports false positives. In infra/lambda.tf, every queue-driven worker that makes paid vendor calls or wakes Aurora already has an explicit cap: label_enricher_worker and artist_enricher_worker (the Gemini/OpenAI/Tavily/DeepSeek callers) each set scaling_config.maximum_concurrency (default 8), with code comments stating this exists precisely to cap blast radius and protect the account pool; auto_enrich_dispatch_worker likewise; spotify_search_worker and vendor_match_worker support optional reserved_concurrent_executions plus SQS batch_size and are bounded by the shared Spotify token bucket (≤5 combined). The only uncapped functions are the API collector (bounded by the API Gateway 29s timeout and human-driven request volume for a small DJ circle), canonicalization_worker (no paid vendor calls — pure canonicalization, bounded by SQS batch_size), and db_migration (manually invoked, never queue-driven). So the unbounded-fan-out/cost scenario the finding describes cannot actually trigger on any paid-vendor path. The recommendation ("set reserved_concurrent_executions on worker/enrichment Lambdas") is already implemented via an equivalent-or-better mechanism that checkov simply can't detect. This is a scanner false positive against already-mitigated risk, with negligible real cost impact for this MVP.

The finding's COST risk is materially refuted by the actual code in infra/lambda.tf. The risk claims "a queue backlog or retry storm can fan out unbounded concurrent executions, multiplying Aurora wakeups and paid vendor calls," and recommends setting concurrency caps on "worker/enrichment Lambdas." But those caps already exist via the AWS-recommended mechanism for SQS-driven workers:

- The two paid-vendor enrichment workers — label_enricher_worker (lambda.tf:223-235) and artist_enricher_worker (lambda.tf:272-284), which make the Gemini/OpenAI/Tavily/DeepSeek "paid vendor calls" the risk cites — have scaling_config { maximum_concurrency = var.*_worker_max_concurrency } on their SQS event-source mappings, default 8 each (variables.tf:479-488, 532-541). A backlog physically cannot fan them past 8 concurrent.
- auto_enrich_dispatch_worker has maximum_concurrency = 2 (lambda.tf:315-323, default variables.tf:609-613).
- spotify_search_worker and vendor_match_worker have batch_size=1 plus conditional reserved_concurrent_executions (lambda.tf:114,157); vendor_match's mapping is disabled by default (vendor_match_enabled=false, variables.tf:262-266).

I cannot construct a concrete path where a queue backlog drives unbounded enrichment fan-out / multiplied vendor spend — maximum_concurrency on the event-source mapping is exactly the control that prevents it, and it caps spend WITHOUT consuming the small account ConcurrentExecutions pool (documented inline at lambda.tf:228-234 and in the variable descriptions). The reserved_concurrent_executions = -1 default (enable_lambda_reserved_concurrency=false) is a deliberate, documented trade-off because new AWS accounts ship with quota 10.

What remains true is only the literal checkov result: CKV_AWS_115 still fires because reserved_concurrent_executions resolves to -1 on those functions, and the API/auth/collector/curation/migration Lambdas have no function-level cap. But those are API-Gateway/S3/migration-driven (not the "worker/enrichment" Lambdas the recommendation targets) and make no paid vendor calls. So the P2 cost-blast-radius justification does not hold; this is at most a cosmetic scanner-hygiene nit (P3): the actual cost/concurrency blast radius is already bounded.

title: checkov: IAM policy allows '*' resource for restrictable actions
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_356 fails: aws_iam_policy_document.collector_lambda grants actions on Resource '*'
risk: Over-broad permissions widen the blast radius if the collector Lambda is compromised
recommendation: Scope each action to specific resource ARNs where AWS supports resource-level permissions

votes.reason: Verified by reading infra/iam.tf and running checkov CKV_AWS_356 directly. The check genuinely FAILS on data.aws_iam_policy_document.collector_lambda (no skip annotation). The evidence "grants actions on Resource '*'" is accurate: two statements use "*" — line 138 (secretsmanager:GetSecretValue via try(...master_user_secret[0].secret_arn, "*")) and line 238 (AllowLambdaVpcNetworking, ec2 ENI actions, resources = ["*"]). I confirmed cloudsplaining's all_allowed_unrestricted_actions (the engine behind this check) returns exactly: secretsmanager:GetSecretValue, ec2:CreateNetworkInterface, ec2:DeleteNetworkInterface, ec2:AssignPrivateIpAddresses, ec2:UnassignPrivateIpAddresses — so the "restrictable actions" framing is correct (ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces is correctly NOT flagged). The cited line infra/iam.tf:19 is the data-block anchor checkov reports for the whole resource; the actual "*" literals are at 138/238, which is how checkov anchors it (not a misread). Nuances that temper but do not refute: the secretsmanager "*" is a degenerate try-fallback that resolves to a specific ARN in practice (rds.tf:8 manage_master_user_password=true), and the EC2 VPC "*" mirrors AWS's own AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole managed policy. The rest of the policy is already tightly scoped. Finding is factually accurate; severity P2 is defensible (P3 also reasonable, but kept unchanged).

Refuted. The collector_lambda policy document has exactly two `*` resource usages, and neither is the over-broad grant the finding implies. (1) infra/iam.tf:138 `try(aws_rds_cluster.aurora.master_user_secret[0].secret_arn, "*")` — the `*` is only a Terraform plan-time fallback; at apply it resolves to the specific Aurora master-user secret ARN (confirmed by RDS-managed secret wiring in outputs.tf:73 and AURORA_SECRET_ARN env vars). Checkov flags the static HCL token, a false positive on the deployed policy. (2) infra/iam.tf:228-239 `AllowLambdaVpcNetworking` with `resources=["*"]` for ec2 ENI actions — this is the canonical AWS-published Lambda-VPC policy (identical to AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole). The finding's OWN recommendation says scope 'where AWS supports resource-level permissions' — but ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces is a Describe* action that AWS forbids ARN-scoping (must be `*`), and ec2:CreateNetworkInterface targets dynamically-created ENIs the Lambda service manages. So the recommendation is already satisfied; the residual `*` is exactly the un-scopable carve-out it acknowledges. Every other statement (logs, S3, SQS, rds-data, secretsmanager, ssm, kms, rds-db:connect) is already tightly ARN-scoped — this policy is a least-privilege model. Impact for this MVP: the stated risk (blast radius on compromise) does not materialize — ENI describe/create/delete grants cannot exfiltrate tenant data or escalate privilege; they are Lambda-service plumbing. There is no actionable remediation and no real risk. Even granting the concern, severity is overstated: a `*` on EC2 ENI plumbing actions in a single serverless app is at most informational (P3).

Finding is factually accurate but severity is overstated. Verified infra/iam.tf:19 defines aws_iam_policy_document.collector_lambda, which contains Resource = ["*"] at line 238 (AllowLambdaVpcNetworking) plus a try(..., "*") fallback at line 138. Checkov CKV_AWS_356 would flag the line-238 statement because some EC2 ENI actions (CreateNetworkInterface, DeleteNetworkInterface, Assign/UnassignPrivateIpAddresses) support resource-level constraints, so the finding is NOT refuted.

However the reproducible scope is narrow. The only "*" in the data-plane is the EC2 ENI lifecycle statement, which exists because the Lambdas are VPC-attached (confirmed infra/lambda.tf:75-77: vpc_config with subnet_ids + security_group_ids). This is the exact pattern of AWS's managed AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole, and ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces cannot be scoped at all (EC2 Describe* actions never support resource-level permissions), so "*" is unavoidable for at least one action in that statement. The remaining ENI actions are a well-known CKV_AWS_356 false-positive for Lambda VPC roles.

All actual data-plane permissions are already tightly scoped: S3 to specific bucket+prefixes (lines 46-78), SQS to enumerated queue ARNs (89-116), rds-data to the specific cluster ARN (129), KMS to the specific user_tokens key (207), SSM/Secrets to specific parameter/secret ARNs (147-217). The line-138 "*" only resolves when aws_rds_cluster.aurora.master_user_secret[0] is absent (an edge fallback, not the normal apply path).

Exploit path: requires the collector Lambda to already be compromised; the only extra capability "*" grants over a scoped policy is ENI manipulation within the account — a minimal blast-radius increase versus the data access CKV_AWS_356 targets, which is not over-broad here. Real-but-minor hardening item, not P2.

title: checkov: IAM policy allows unconstrained write access
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_111 fails: aws_iam_policy_document.collector_lambda allows write actions without resource constraints
risk: Compromise of the collector role permits broad writes beyond its intended scope
recommendation: Constrain write actions to specific ARNs / add conditions

votes.reason:
Verified against infra/iam.tf. The finding is correct on the facts. The `aws_iam_policy_document.collector_lambda` block (declared at line 19) contains one statement — "AllowLambdaVpcNetworking" (lines 228-239) — that pairs write-level EC2 actions (ec2:CreateNetworkInterface, ec2:DeleteNetworkInterface, ec2:AssignPrivateIpAddresses, ec2:UnassignPrivateIpAddresses) with `resources = ["*"]` (line 238). That is exactly what CKV_AWS_111 ("IAM policies must not allow write access without constraints") flags, so the check genuinely fails and the cited location is accurate. The only other wildcard (line 138, secretsmanager:GetSecretValue) is a read action used as a try() fallback and would not trigger CKV_AWS_111. Cannot refute on correctness.

However, the severity is overstated. The sole offending statement is the standard Lambda-in-VPC ENI-management permission set — the same actions AWS's own managed AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole grants on Resource:"*", because these ENI-provisioning actions cannot be meaningfully resource-scoped at creation time (the ENI doesn't exist yet). Every other write action in the policy (S3 PutObject, SQS Send/Delete, rds-data writes, KMS GenerateDataKey, logs) is already tightly ARN-scoped. The risk narrative "permits broad writes beyond its intended scope" implies cross-service write exposure that does not exist; real blast radius is limited to ENI create/delete in the account. This is a defense-in-depth hardening nit (optionally add subnet/vpc/region conditions), not a P2. Correct severity is P3.

Verified infra/iam.tf directly. The only `resources = ["*"]` in the entire infra dir (line 238) is the AllowLambdaVpcNetworking statement — ec2:CreateNetworkInterface/DeleteNetworkInterface/Assign/UnassignPrivateIpAddresses. CKV_AWS_111 fires on this because checkov classifies these EC2 ENI actions as "write" against Resource "*". Every actual data-plane write in the policy is already tightly ARN-scoped: S3 PutObject (bucket prefixes), SQS SendMessage/DeleteMessage (6 specific queue ARNs), RDS Data API writes (Aurora cluster ARN), KMS GenerateDataKey (user_tokens key ARN), CloudWatch Logs (specific log groups), rds-db:connect (specific dbuser). The finding's evidence ("write actions without resource constraints") and risk ("broad writes beyond its intended scope") are misleading: there is exactly ONE unscoped statement and it grants only ENI lifecycle, not data writes. ec2:CreateNetworkInterface cannot be scoped to a resource ARN for Lambda-in-VPC (the ENI doesn't exist at creation time); AWS's own managed AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole uses Resource "*" for these exact actions. The recommendation ("constrain write actions to specific ARNs") is therefore not achievable here. This is a checkov false positive from coarse action classification — a one-line skip-annotation cosmetic with zero security improvement. No plausible "broad write" blast radius exists; refuted for this MVP. (Note: a sibling finding CKV_AWS_356 at the same line covers the identical wildcard.)

Reproducibility: The only write-action statement paired with `resources = ["*"]` in `aws_iam_policy_document.collector_lambda` is `AllowLambdaVpcNetworking` (infra/iam.tf:228-239: ec2:CreateNetworkInterface/DeleteNetworkInterface/AssignPrivateIpAddresses/UnassignPrivateIpAddresses/DescribeNetworkInterfaces). So checkov CKV_AWS_111 does fire on the document — the scanner trigger is real. But the finding mischaracterizes the rest: 12 of 13 statements are tightly scoped to specific ARNs/prefixes (S3 PutObject -> bucket prefixes, SQS SendMessage -> specific queues, logs -> specific log groups, rds-data -> cluster ARN, KMS -> specific key). The policy is a least-privilege document, not one with "unconstrained write access."

Mischaracterization of risk: The flagged `*` is on EC2 ENI-management actions only. This is the AWS-published `AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole` pattern; AWS itself uses Resource:"*" here because ENI IDs are unpredictable per cold-start and CreateNetworkInterface/DeleteNetworkInterface cannot be ARN-constrained. It is the canonical CKV_AWS_111 false-positive case. A compromised role gains only narrow ENI create/delete capability in the account — it cannot exfiltrate S3, write the DB, drain queues, or read secrets, all of which ARE properly scoped in the same policy. The finding's "broad writes beyond its intended scope" is overstated.

Recommendation accuracy: "Constrain write actions to specific ARNs" is not achievable for the actually-flagged actions; only condition-key hardening (e.g., ec2:Subnet/ec2:AuthorizedService) is valid, which the finding lists only secondarily.

Severity: Not P2. This is a single VPC-networking statement where `*` is AWS's required pattern; the only real improvement is optional condition-key defense-in-depth. Downgrade to P3 rather than full refute, since the scanner genuinely fires on a real `*` and condition-key hardening is a legitimate minor improvement.

title: checkov: Lambda environment variables not encrypted with CMK
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_173 fails on 11 Lambda functions — env vars use the default key, not a customer-managed KMS key
risk: Env vars are encrypted at rest by AWS default key; CMK is defense-in-depth. Relevant only if any secret/token is stored in env vars
recommendation: Confirm no token/secret lives in Lambda env vars (use Secrets Manager); optionally enable a CMK

votes.reason: Verified accurate. Confirmed exactly 11 aws_lambda_function resources (lambda.tf x8, curation.tf x1, auth.tf x2) and zero kms_key_arn attributes anywhere in infra/ (grep returned NONE FOUND), so every Lambda environment block uses the AWS-managed default key and CKV_AWS_173 fails on all 11 — exactly as stated. The risk framing is also correct and self-limiting: it conditions impact on whether a real secret lives in env vars. I checked every env block; they contain only references (e.g. JWT_SIGNING_KEY_SSM_PARAMETER, SPOTIFY_CREDENTIALS_SECRET_ARN, AURORA_SECRET_ARN, KMS_USER_TOKENS_KEY_ARN) and non-sensitive config (TTLs, IDs, redirect URIs, queue URLs). No raw token/secret/password value is materialized into any env var; secrets are externalized to SSM/Secrets Manager and fetched at runtime, matching the recommendation. P3 is the right level: pure defense-in-depth (data already encrypted with AWS-managed key, no plaintext secret exposed). Minor imprecision only: where points at lambda.tf:1 though 3 of the 11 functions live in curation.tf/auth.tf — not enough to refute.

Technically true (checkov CKV_AWS_173 flags missing CMK on Lambda env vars), but no impact for this MVP. (1) AWS already encrypts env vars at rest with the AWS-managed KMS key — these are encrypted, not plaintext; CMK is pure defense-in-depth (rotation/audit/cross-account isolation), irrelevant for a single-account, single-tenant-DB MVP. (2) The recommendation's own precondition is already satisfied: I verified every env var in lambda.tf, auth.tf, and curation.tf — all secrets live in SSM Parameter Store / Secrets Manager, and only POINTERS appear in env vars (e.g. SPOTIFY_CREDENTIALS_SECRET_ARN, *_SSM_PARAMETER, AURORA_SECRET_ARN, JWT_SIGNING_KEY_SSM_PARAMETER, KMS_USER_TOKENS_KEY_ARN). 37 secret-related env entries are all names/ARNs; zero literal secret values. The most sensitive token (bp_token) is never persisted per policy. (3) No plausible trigger: reading env vars needs lambda:GetFunctionConfiguration on the account, and anyone with that can already read SSM/Secrets Manager — a CMK doesn't change the threat model. This is a benign scanner nit where the real control (no secrets in env) is already correctly implemented.

Confirmed accurate, not refuted. infra/ contains exactly 11 aws_lambda_function resources (lambda.tf x8: collector, canonicalization_worker, db_migration, spotify_search_worker, vendor_match_worker, label_enricher_worker, artist_enricher_worker, auto_enrich_dispatch_worker; auth.tf x2: auth_handler, auth_authorizer; curation.tf x1: curation). None sets kms_key_arn, so checkov CKV_AWS_173 flags all 11 — the count and rule applicability are correct. The finding is a static-scan/config-hardening item, not a runtime exploit path; there is no route/queue message that "triggers" it. I verified the finding's own risk caveat ("relevant only if a secret/token lives in env vars"): all env vars across the 11 functions are references only — ARNs (AURORA_*_ARN, KMS_USER_TOKENS_KEY_ARN, SPOTIFY_CREDENTIALS_SECRET_ARN), SSM SecureString parameter NAMES (JWT_SIGNING_KEY_SSM_PARAMETER, *_API_KEY_SSM_PARAMETER, SPOTIFY_OAUTH_*_SSM_PARAMETER), SQS URLs, bucket names, flags, and tuning numbers. Actual secrets are correctly externalized: JWT key is an SSM SecureString (auth.tf:21-30), AI/Spotify keys fetched at runtime by SSM name, Aurora creds in Secrets Manager by ARN. So no plaintext secret/token sits in any Lambda env var. P3 is the correct severity: env vars are already encrypted at rest by the AWS-managed key, CMK is pure defense-in-depth, and the one real risk condition (secrets in env) does not hold. The recommendation's confirmation step is already satisfied by the existing SSM/Secrets Manager design.

title: checkov: SQS queues not encrypted at rest
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_27 fails on 12 SQS queues — no server-side encryption configured
risk: Queue payloads (ingest/enrichment messages) sit unencrypted at rest; low risk if no token/PII is in message bodies
recommendation: Enable SSE-SQS (sqs_managed_sse_enabled) on all queues; verify no token is ever placed in a message body

votes.reason: Verified directly against infra/sqs.tf. Exactly 12 aws_sqs_queue resources are defined (lines 1-123), and a repo-wide grep across infra/ for sqs_managed_sse_enabled, kms_master_key_id, and kms_data_key_reuse_period_seconds returns zero matches — so none of the queues configure server-side encryption. SQS SSE must be set per-queue (no provider-level default exists), the queues are plain resources with no module wrapper, and there is no checkov skip for CKV_AWS_27. The finding's evidence (CKV_AWS_27 fails on 12 SQS queues, no SSE) is accurate. Severity P3 is correct: this is a defense-in-depth at-rest hardening item, not an active exploit path (data is TLS-encrypted in transit), and per CLAUDE.md gotcha #5 bp_token is never persisted, so practical exposure is low. Recommendation (enable sqs_managed_sse_enabled, effort S) is correct.

Finding is technically accurate: infra/sqs.tf defines 12 queues (6 main + 6 DLQ) and none set sqs_managed_sse_enabled or kms_master_key_id, so checkov CKV_AWS_27 correctly fails. But it does not matter for this MVP. I traced every SQS send site and the message bodies contain only non-sensitive job descriptors: label/artist enrichment use {run_id, label_id/artist_id, name, style}; canonicalization uses {run_id, source, s3_key, style_id, iso_year, iso_week, attempt}; spotify follow-up uses {batch_size}; vendor_match dumps a track/vendor-matching pydantic model. No bp_token, access_token, refresh_token, password, or PII ever reaches a MessageBody — the token-handling code lives only in the auth/token-resolver paths, never the enqueue paths. The finding's own stated low-risk precondition ('low risk if no token/PII is in message bodies') is therefore satisfied. There is no compliance regime in scope for a small DJ-circle MVP that mandates SSE-SQS, and no triggerable exploit: SSE-SQS only defends against AWS-internal disk-level compromise, while any actual attacker needs IAM access to the queue and would read messages directly regardless. This is a generic checkov hygiene flag (cheap to fix as routine hardening) with no material consequence for this product. Severity P3 is already the lowest band and appropriate as residual, so unchanged.

Confirmed accurate and reproducible. infra/sqs.tf declares exactly 12 aws_sqs_queue resources (6 functional + 6 DLQs); none set sqs_managed_sse_enabled or kms_master_key_id (grep across all of infra/ returns nothing), so AWS creates them unencrypted at rest and checkov CKV_AWS_27 deterministically fails on all 12. These are real applied infra, wired into Lambda event sources and IAM (lambda.tf:98, iam.tf:90, outputs.tf:53), not fixtures. Severity P3 is correct: I traced every send_message/send_message_batch call site (handler.py:921, worker_handler.py:197, spotify_handler.py:380, vendor_match/enqueue.py:59, curation/auto_enrich_dispatch.py:34, label_enrichment/routes.py:181, artist_enrichment/routes.py:171) and the payloads contain only IDs/metadata (run_id, s3_key, style_id, block_id, user_id, label_id, batch_size, ISRCs) — no bp_token, no credentials, no Bearer/authorization, no PII. So data-at-rest exposure is genuinely low and this is a defense-in-depth/compliance hardening item. Minor note: 6 of the 12 are DLQs (count still correct); the 'verify no token in body' part of the recommendation is already satisfied, leaving only the trivial sqs_managed_sse_enabled=true addition (effort S accurate).

title: checkov: RDS cluster has no AWS Backup plan
evidence: checkov CKV2_AWS_8 fails: aws_rds_cluster.aurora is not covered by an AWS Backup plan
risk: Recovery depends solely on automated snapshots/PITR; verify retention window is adequate for MVP RPO
recommendation: Confirm backup_retention_period and PITR meet the recovery target; optionally add an AWS Backup plan

votes.reason: Verified against the cited code: aws_rds_cluster.aurora begins at infra/rds.tf:1, and a grep across all of infra/ for aws_backup / backup_plan / backup_selection / backup_vault returns nothing — there is no AWS Backup plan, vault, or selection covering the cluster, so checkov CKV2_AWS_8 genuinely fails. The cited location (rds.tf:1) and evidence are correct, and no guard was missed (single unconditional aws_rds_cluster resource, no modules, no tfvars override; grep for module/backup_retention/preferred_backup/point_in_time/backtrack all return exit 1). The finding's risk framing is also fair: backup_retention_period is unset, so recovery relies on the AWS default ~1-day automated snapshots/PITR. The claim is technically accurate, not refuted. Severity P2 is appropriate for this finding as written: it makes a narrow, correctly-hedged defense-in-depth claim (absence of an AWS Backup plan, with PITR/automated snapshots still present, "optionally add a plan"). Note that it is a deduped scanner echo (survived=False) of richer human-authored findings [36]/[87] which were rated P1 because they capture the more severe destroy-without-final-snapshot / deletion_protection=false exposure on the sole multi-tenant store; but judged on its own narrower AWS-Backup-plan claim with PITR present, P2 stands.

The finding is a compliance-scanner nudge (checkov CKV2_AWS_8) that is largely refuted on impact for this MVP. Aurora's automated backups + point-in-time-recovery are always enabled and cannot be disabled — the finding itself concedes "recovery depends solely on automated snapshots/PITR," which means data is in fact recoverable without an AWS Backup plan. CKV2_AWS_8's intent (have a backup mechanism) is functionally met by native Aurora backups. An AWS Backup plan would add a redundant vault + IAM role + plan/selection resources and cost, and the finding marks it "optional." The only substantive residual is that backup_retention_period is unset in infra/rds.tf, so it defaults to 1 day (24h PITR window). There is non-re-derivable per-user curation/overlay data (ADR-0002, docs/data/data-model.md), so a too-short window is a real but minor consideration — yet the finding doesn't assert 1 day is inadequate; it only asks to "verify." For a small-DJ-circle MVP with low write volume, a 24h PITR window is acceptable and certainly not a P2 data-loss risk. The actionable residual is at most "set an explicit backup_retention_period," a config-hardening nit. Adding the AWS Backup plan is over-engineering at this scale. Severity should drop to P3.

Finding is factually accurate but severity is overstated. VERIFIED: infra/rds.tf defines aws_rds_cluster.aurora with no aws_backup_plan/aws_backup_vault/aws_backup_selection anywhere in infra/ (grep returns nothing), and no explicit backup_retention_period/preferred_backup_window, so it relies on the AWS default 1-day automated backups + PITR. The cluster is unconditionally the prod cluster (environment defaults to "prod", project to "beatport" -> beatport-prod-aurora; main.tf:24, variables.tf:4,10), with no count/for_each/workspace gating and no prevent_destroy lifecycle, and it is the sole multi-tenant data store. So checkov CKV2_AWS_8 genuinely fails as stated — NOT refuted on the facts or reproducibility (this is a static-config check whose "path" is the scanner evaluating the rendered resource and finding no aws_backup_selection covering it, which the code confirms).

However P2 is too high for THIS standalone item. (1) The recommendation itself concedes the load-bearing action is "confirm backup_retention_period and PITR meet the recovery target; OPTIONALLY add an AWS Backup plan" — AWS Backup is optional for Aurora since native automated backups + PITR already provide recovery; a separate plan only adds longer-retention/centralized copies. (2) The actual data-loss teeth (skip_final_snapshot=true at rds.tf:18 + deletion_protection=false at rds.tf:19, which enable a destroy/forced-replace with no final snapshot) are tracked as a SEPARATE, stronger finding (findings-verified.json:2266, adjudicated P1/P2). As a standalone "no AWS Backup plan" item — with native 1-day PITR already active and an explicitly-optional fix — this is a recoverability-hardening recommendation from a static scanner, not a P2 durability defect. Downgrade to P3.

title: checkov: CloudWatch log groups retain logs under 1 year
evidence: checkov CKV_AWS_338 fails on 11 log groups — retention below 1 year (or unset)
risk: If retention is UNSET, logs never expire and storage cost grows unbounded; if intentionally short, that is a cost choice, not a defect
recommendation: Confirm every log group sets an explicit bounded retention_in_days (not unset)

votes.reason: Refuted on correctness of the core risk. All 11 log groups in infra/logging.tf explicitly set `retention_in_days = var.log_retention_days`. The variable (infra/variables.tf:43-47) defaults to 30 (days), and there are no *.tfvars files anywhere in the repo overriding it, so retention is always an explicit, bounded 30 days — never unset. The finding's primary risk branch ("if retention is UNSET, logs never expire and storage cost grows unbounded") is factually impossible here. The only remaining condition is that 30 days < 1 year, which trips checkov's CKV_AWS_338 (requires >=365 days), but the finding itself explicitly states that an intentionally-short retention "is a cost choice, not a defect." The recommendation ("confirm every log group sets an explicit bounded retention_in_days (not unset)") is already fully satisfied by the code. For a small-DJ-circle MVP, 30-day bounded retention is a deliberate cost-minimizing choice, not a defect. Severity overstated: this is informational at most, not P3.

Refuted. All 11 CloudWatch log groups in infra/logging.tf set retention_in_days = var.log_retention_days, and that variable (infra/variables.tf:43-47) has an explicit default of 30 days. Retention is therefore NOT unset — logs expire after 30 days and storage cost is bounded. The finding's only genuine cost risk ("if retention is UNSET, logs never expire and storage cost grows unbounded") does not apply. The remaining case is the finding's own words: "if intentionally short, that is a cost choice, not a defect" — which is exactly the situation here. checkov CKV_AWS_338 fires only because 30 < 365 days, a compliance preference for 1-year retention, not a bug. The recommendation ("confirm every log group sets an explicit bounded retention_in_days, not unset") is already satisfied. For a small-DJ-circle MVP, a deliberate 30-day bounded retention is a sound cost decision in the COST direction this finding cares about. No plausible unbounded-cost trigger exists.

Refuted. All 11 CloudWatch log groups in infra/logging.tf explicitly set retention_in_days = var.log_retention_days. variables.tf:43-47 defines that variable as type=number with default=30 and there is no override in the only tfvars file (terraform.tfvars.example has no log_retention entry). So retention resolves to a bounded 30 days for every group — it is NOT unset, and logs DO expire. The finding's primary risk path ("if retention is UNSET, logs never expire and storage cost grows unbounded") is impossible: there is no concrete code path producing an unset retention. The recommendation ("confirm every log group sets an explicit bounded retention_in_days, not unset") is already satisfied. No other CloudWatch log groups exist (API Gateway has no access_log_settings). checkov CKV_AWS_338 does fire because it demands >=365 days and 30<365, so the raw scanner evidence is accurate — but that rule targets long-term auditability/compliance, not cost; a 30-day retention is the cost-minimizing choice, the opposite of an unbounded-cost defect. By the finding's own wording this is "a cost choice, not a defect." At most it is an informational compliance note, not a P3 COST finding.
